commit_message:
Prevent null dereference read in `GetInitOp`.

We have a map of maps. We test that the key exists in the first map but then we don't have any validation that this also means the second map has the needed key. In the scenarios where this is not the case, we'll dereference a nullptr, if we don't have this check

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408739325
Change-Id: If9bb7ed759aba1f3b56a34913f209508dbaf65ce
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `GetInitOp` is vulnerable to a crash caused by dereferencing a null pointer. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
array.c: fix `mrb_ary_shift_m` initialization bug.

The `ARY_PTR` and `ARY_LEN` may be modified in `mrb_get_args`.
cve_desc: mruby is vulnerable to NULL Pointer Dereference

commit_message:
Prevent `CHECK`-fail when building reference tensor.

The tensor constructor does not allow reference dtypes, as these should not show up explicitly. However, when passed these invalid types instead of building an invalid object the constructor crashes via a `CHECK`-fail. We have a static builder that properly handles this case but is not applicable given current usage.

Instead, before calling the constructor, we can check that the dtype is not a reference type and return an error otherwise, given that the dtype is user controlled so malicious users can trigger denial of service.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409662503
Change-Id: I5892f831fde7f276cd7ab34519cf6b8061c71a59
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. A malicious user can cause a denial of service by altering a `SavedModel` such that Grappler optimizer would attempt to build a tensor using a reference `dtype`. This would result in a crash due to a `CHECK`-fail in the `Tensor` constructor as reference types are not allowed. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Avoid Segfault for scalar shapes.

Calling tensor::FromElementsOp with an empty vector of elements and no type
causes a segfault. We need to let the FromElementsOp know which scalar type it
should have.
Also add back the DynamicBroadcastInDimOp canonicalization patterns, which
previously prevented this bug from happening.
Add a regression test that demonstrates the bug.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 417561444
Change-Id: I6d1d6cfb71aabbad6102422625a00bbe253ac95a
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The `simplifyBroadcast` function in the MLIR-TFRT infrastructure in TensorFlow is vulnerable to a segfault (hence, denial of service), if called with scalar shapes. If all shapes are scalar, then `maxRank` is 0, so we build an empty `SmallVector`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. This is the only affected version.

commit_message:
Revert "NFSv4: Handle the special Linux file open access mode"

This reverts commit 44942b4e457beda00981f616402a1a791e8c616e.

After secondly opening a file with O_ACCMODE|O_DIRECT flags,
nfs4_valid_open_stateid() will dereference NULL nfs4_state when lseek().

Reproducer:
  1. mount -t nfs -o vers=4.2 $server_ip:/ /mnt/
  2. fd = open("/mnt/file", O_ACCMODE|O_DIRECT|O_CREAT)
  3. close(fd)
  4. fd = open("/mnt/file", O_ACCMODE|O_DIRECT)
  5. lseek(fd)

Reported-by: Lyu Tao <tao.lyu@epfl.ch>
Signed-off-by: ChenXiaoSong <chenxiaosong2@huawei.com>
Signed-off-by: Trond Myklebust <trond.myklebust@hammerspace.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in fs/nfs/dir.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.5. If an application sets the O_DIRECTORY flag, and tries to open a regular file, nfs_atomic_open() performs a regular lookup. If a regular file is found, ENOTDIR should occur, but the server instead returns uninitialized data in the file descriptor.

Instead, before calling the constructor, we can check that the dtype is not a reference type and return an error otherwise, given that the dtype is user controlled so malicious users can trigger denial of service.

[lite] Add check for bias_size is zero to avoid division by zero. This shouldn't happen for properly converted models. Just safety check

If ConfigProto is not used, then use the default settings which is to allow all devices.

commit_message:
Handle invalid inputs instead of crashing.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409549744
Change-Id: I7f5935b34b53f7e426a5462fcc027bdbf5dcda24
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. TensorFlow's type inference can cause a heap out of bounds read as the bounds checking is done in a `DCHECK` (which is a no-op during production). An attacker can control the `input_idx` variable such that `ix` would be larger than the number of values in `node_t.args`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. This is the only affected version.

commit_message:
KVM: nSVM: always intercept VMLOAD/VMSAVE when nested (CVE-2021-3656)

If L1 disables VMLOAD/VMSAVE intercepts, and doesn't enable
Virtual VMLOAD/VMSAVE (currently not supported for the nested hypervisor),
then VMLOAD/VMSAVE must operate on the L1 physical memory, which is only
possible by making L0 intercept these instructions.

Failure to do so allowed the nested guest to run VMLOAD/VMSAVE unintercepted,
and thus read/write portions of the host physical memory.

Fixes: 89c8a4984fc9 ("KVM: SVM: Enable Virtual VMLOAD VMSAVE feature")

Suggested-by: Paolo Bonzini <pbonzini@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Maxim Levitsky <mlevitsk@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Paolo Bonzini <pbonzini@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the KVM's AMD code for supporting SVM nested virtualization. The flaw occurs when processing the VMCB (virtual machine control block) provided by the L1 guest to spawn/handle a nested guest (L2). Due to improper validation of the "virt_ext" field, this issue could allow a malicious L1 to disable both VMLOAD/VMSAVE intercepts and VLS (Virtual VMLOAD/VMSAVE) for the L2 guest. As a result, the L2 guest would be allowed to read/write physical pages of the host, resulting in a crash of the entire system, leak of sensitive data or potential guest-to-host escape.

commit_message:
Replace faulty overflow check with a builder for `TensorShape`.

Prevents an integer overflow that was not caught before.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 415381595
Change-Id: I76585ddedc912bd9f4a390aeafa8e2ced1a28863
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `AddManySparseToTensorsMap` is vulnerable to an integer overflow which results in a `CHECK`-fail when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, an assert failure based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fix memory leak when a graph node is invalid.

If a graph node is invalid but a kernel is created then we set the kernel back to `nullptr` but we forget to delete it. Hence, we get a memory leak.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408968108
Change-Id: I1d8a9d0d8988ed5e08be8b9f2004ce1b4cd11b7c
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. If a graph node is invalid, TensorFlow can leak memory in the implementation of `ImmutableExecutorState::Initialize`. Here, we set `item->kernel` to `nullptr` but it is a simple `OpKernel*` pointer so the memory that was previously allocated to it would leak. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:

Prevent a crash due to heap OOB write in grappler.

commit_message:
Handle typeof applied to empty in InstSimplify

Summary:
Do not simplify `typeof` if it is applied to an invalid type. This
handles a case like the one in the added test, where `typeof` is called
on a literal empty in unreachable code.

Reviewed By: kodafb

Differential Revision: D31000173

fbshipit-source-id: 2d7f69cbcc9c1bb0a916585c07171089444c85dc
cve_desc: A type confusion vulnerability could be triggered when resolving the "typeof" unary operator in Facebook Hermes prior to v0.10.0. Note that this is only exploitable if the application using Hermes permits evaluation of untrusted JavaScript. Hence, most React Native applications are not affected.

commit_message:
Eliminate debug `CHECK`-fail from `function.cc`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409416119
Change-Id: I8376ee464d434e9b970ff0ad49edfdaa2a273cfe
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. A malicious user can cause a denial of service by altering a `SavedModel` such that assertions in `function.cc` would be falsified and crash the Python interpreter. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent copying uninitialized data in `AssignOp`.

This prevents harder to debug undefined behaviors that cannot be traced back to the original tensor after assignments occur earlier in the graph execution. Several of these undefined behaviors are just reference bindings to null pointers, which are caught when running under ubsan/asan.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408654780
Change-Id: Iad2ec40d43f5fd7ea016c20283356c12d5ddeab1
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `AssignOp` can result in copying uninitialized data to a new tensor. This later results in undefined behavior. The implementation has a check that the left hand side of the assignment is initialized (to minimize number of allocations), but does not check that the right hand side is also initialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent null pointer dereference in constant folding.

Under certain conditions, an invalid protobuf saved model with invalid nodes would be loaded. During optimization phase, Grappler optimizer will then dereference a null pointer.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409683530
Change-Id: I1f10340a7ec384bc9bc587300390f1078cf5caa0
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. Under certain scenarios, Grappler component of TensorFlow can trigger a null pointer dereference. There are 2 places where this can occur, for the same malicious alteration of a `SavedModel` file (fixing the first one would trigger the same dereference in the second place). First, during constant folding, the `GraphDef` might not have the required nodes for the binary operation. If a node is missing, the correposning `mul_*child` would be null, and the dereference in the subsequent line would be incorrect. We have a similar issue during `IsIdentityConsumingSwitch`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: Fix incorrect value reading in JIT.
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the compile_xclass_matchingpath() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This involves a unicode property matching issue in JIT-compiled regular expressions. The issue occurs because the character was not fully read in case-less matching within JIT.

commit_message:
Validate `proto.dtype()` before calling `set_dtype()`.

This prevents a `DCHECK`-fail when the proto contains an invalid dtype for a tensor shape with 0 elements or for an incomplete tensor shape.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408369083
Change-Id: Ia21a3e3d62a90d642a4561f08f3b543e5ad00c46
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. When decoding a tensor from protobuf, a TensorFlow process can encounter cases where a `CHECK` assertion is invalidated based on user controlled arguments, if the tensors have an invalid `dtype` and 0 elements or an invalid shape. This allows attackers to cause denial of services in TensorFlow processes. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: codegen.c: fixed a bug in hash code generation with `!val`.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in Homebrew mruby prior to 3.2.

commit_message: fixed #1904
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a null pointer dereference vulnerability in the gf_isom_get_payt_count function in hint_track.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: The pointer 'hmhd' is dereferenced (hmhd->type) before it is checked for NULL. If trak->Media->information->InfoHeader is NULL, accessing hmhd->type causes a null pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: hmhd is assigned from trak->Media->information->InfoHeader, which could be NULL.
hmhd->type is accessed to compare against GF_ISOM_BOX_TYPE_HMHD, dereferencing the NULL pointer before the NULL check on line 4.
The later check 'if (!hmhd || !hmhd->subType)' that guards against a NULL hmhd is never reached because the dereference already occurred.

commit_message: codegen.c: fixed a bug in hash code generation with `!val`.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in Homebrew mruby prior to 3.2.

commit_message:
codegen.c: need to pack argument when `n==13` too.

Because we have extra 2 arguments coming (kw and rhs).
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in mrb_get_args in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

commit_message:
Add a check for Key being scalar tensor for MapStage and OrderedMapStage ops.

According to documentation[1][2], key must be int64 value, but this wasn't enforced and the ops would fail with check failure for non-scalar key value.

[1]https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/cc/class/tensorflow/ops/ordered-map-stage

vulnerabilities.description: When compiler_for_platform fails with a status code other than NOT_FOUND, the error is silently ignored and execution continues past the error check. This means the function proceeds to use the platform and attempt to create a client even though compilation support is broken, potentially leading to undefined behavior or crashes downstream.

step_by_step_execution.description: compiler_for_platform returns an error status with a code that is NOT error::NOT_FOUND (e.g., INTERNAL, UNAVAILABLE, etc.)
The inner if-check for NOT_FOUND does not match, so the return statement is skipped. Execution falls through the entire if-block without returning an error.
Execution continues to code that assumes a valid compiler/platform setup, proceeding to create client_options and use the platform, despite the compiler error.

commit_message:
Fix Segfault in Concat V2 shape function.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 412120654
Change-Id: I3ff915faea694f9ad8b00024e9af2de9909011be
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of shape inference for `ConcatV2` can be used to trigger a denial of service attack via a segfault caused by a type confusion. The `axis` argument is translated into `concat_dim` in the `ConcatShapeHelper` helper function. Then, a value for `min_rank` is computed based on `concat_dim`. This is then used to validate that the `values` tensor has at least the required rank. However, `WithRankAtLeast` receives the lower bound as a 64-bits value and then compares it against the maximum 32-bits integer value that could be represented. Due to the fact that `min_rank` is a 32-bits value and the value of `axis`, the `rank` argument is a negative value, so the error check is bypassed. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent null dereference read in `GetInitOp`.

We have a map of maps. We test that the key exists in the first map but then we don't have any validation that this also means the second map has the needed key. In the scenarios where this is not the case, we'll dereference a nullptr, if we don't have this check

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408739325
Change-Id: If9bb7ed759aba1f3b56a34913f209508dbaf65ce
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `GetInitOp` is vulnerable to a crash caused by dereferencing a null pointer. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: When the signature_def map contains `kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey`, the code looks up the same key in the `outputs()` map of that signature definition. The result of `find()` is never checked against `end()` before being dereferenced. If the outputs map does not contain `kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey`, the iterator returned by `find()` equals `end()`, and dereferencing it is undefined behavior (typically a null/invalid pointer dereference or out-of-bounds read).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a `meta_graph_def` whose `signature_def()` map contains an entry keyed by `kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey`.

state.value: valid iterator pointing to the signature def entry

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `init_op_sig_it != sig_def_map.end()` passes, so execution enters the if-block.

`init_op_sig_it->second.outputs()` returns the outputs map of the found SignatureDef. `.find(kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey)` is called on this outputs map. If the outputs map does not contain `kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey`, the returned iterator equals `outputs().end()`.
The code immediately dereferences this past-the-end iterator via `->second.name()`, causing undefined behavior (read of invalid memory).

commit_message:
Handle invalid inputs instead of crashing.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409549744
Change-Id: I7f5935b34b53f7e426a5462fcc027bdbf5dcda24
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. TensorFlow's type inference can cause a heap out of bounds read as the bounds checking is done in a `DCHECK` (which is a no-op during production). An attacker can control the `input_idx` variable such that `ix` would be larger than the number of values in `node_t.args`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. This is the only affected version.

vulnerabilities.description: The bounds check on `ix` against `node_t.args_size()` is performed via DCHECK, which is compiled out in release/optimized builds. If `ix` (derived from `edge->src_output()`) is greater than or equal to `node_t.args_size()`, the subsequent call to `node_t.args(ix)` results in an out-of-bounds read on the protobuf repeated field.

step_by_step_execution.description: Vectors `input_nodes` and `input_idx` are populated from incoming edges. `input_idx[i]` is set to `edge->src_output()`.
The code checks that the input node has `experimental_type` set and its `type_id` is not `TFT_UNSET`, entering the branch that accesses args.
The variable `ix` is set from `input_idx[i]`. In release builds, DCHECK is a no-op, so no bounds check is enforced.
node_t.args(ix) is called with ix out of bounds, causing an out-of-bounds read on the protobuf repeated field.

commit_message:
Ensure that bundles have metadata on install

If we have a bundle without metadata we wouldn't properly present
the permissions in the transaction.
cve_desc: Flatpak is a Linux application sandboxing and distribution framework. Prior to versions 1.12.3 and 1.10.6, Flatpak doesn't properly validate that the permissions displayed to the user for an app at install time match the actual permissions granted to the app at runtime, in the case that there's a null byte in the metadata file of an app. Therefore apps can grant themselves permissions without the consent of the user. Flatpak shows permissions to the user during install by reading them from the "xa.metadata" key in the commit metadata. This cannot contain a null terminator, because it is an untrusted GVariant. Flatpak compares these permissions to the *actual* metadata, from the "metadata" file to ensure it wasn't lied to. However, the actual metadata contents are loaded in several places where they are read as simple C-style strings. That means that, if the metadata file includes a null terminator, only the content of the file from *before* the terminator gets compared to xa.metadata. Thus, any permissions that appear in the metadata file after a null terminator are applied at runtime but not shown to the user. So maliciously crafted apps can give themselves hidden permissions. Users who have Flatpaks installed from untrusted sources are at risk in case the Flatpak has a maliciously crafted metadata file, either initially or in an update. This issue is patched in versions 1.12.3 and 1.10.6. As a workaround, users can manually check the permissions of installed apps by checking the metadata file or the xa.metadata key on the commit metadata.

Fix segfault on OOM in Conv2D.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 404655317
Change-Id: I33588dbd3f5d0fef980e3c908bf5515a9ee09ce7
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions several TensorFlow operations are missing validation for the shapes of the tensor arguments involved in the call. Depending on the API, this can result in undefined behavior and segfault or `CHECK`-fail related crashes but in some scenarios writes and reads from heap populated arrays are also possible. We have discovered these issues internally via tooling while working on improving/testing GPU op determinism. As such, we don't have reproducers and there will be multiple fixes for these issues. These fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Division by zero if filter.dim_size(2) (patch_depth) is zero. There is no validation that patch_depth is non-zero before the division.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads in_depth from the input tensor and patch_depth from the filter tensor.

state.value: filter.dim_size(2), could be 0 if attacker controls filter shape

step_by_step_execution.description: Division is performed without checking that patch_depth != 0, causing undefined behavior / crash.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on the explicit_paddings vector. When padding == EXPLICIT, indices 2 through 5 are accessed without checking that the vector has at least 6 elements.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives explicit_paddings as a vector of int64_t with no size validation.
If padding == EXPLICIT and num_groups >= 1, the loop body accesses explicit_paddings at indices 2-5 without bounds checking.
std::vector operator[] does not perform bounds checking, leading to out-of-bounds memory read.

state.value: 2, 3, 4, 5 — all out of bounds if size < 6

commit_message:
Remove a `DCHECK`-fail, log an error instead.

`DCHECK` in debug mode results in crashes. TensorFlow has had multiple vulnerabilities due to this.

Outside of debug mode, `DCHECK` is a no-op.

A better alternative is to report an error to the log buffer and continue. This should happen both in debug mode and in prod mode.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408375925
Change-Id: Id5b3e19c73f3fbe0cc4bba26ca44ff9607bb6356
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. An attacker can trigger denial of service via assertion failure by altering a `SavedModel` on disk such that `AttrDef`s of some operation are duplicated. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
libpq: reject extraneous data after SSL or GSS encryption handshake.

libpq collects up to a bufferload of data whenever it reads data from
the socket.  When SSL or GSS encryption is requested during startup,
any additional data received with the server's yes-or-no reply
remained in the buffer, and would be treated as already-decrypted data
once the encryption handshake completed.  Thus, a man-in-the-middle
with the ability to inject data into the TCP connection could stuff
some cleartext data into the start of a supposedly encryption-protected
database session.

This could probably be abused to inject faked responses to the
client's first few queries, although other details of libpq's behavior
make that harder than it sounds.  A different line of attack is to
exfiltrate the client's password, or other sensitive data that might
be sent early in the session.  That has been shown to be possible with
a server vulnerable to CVE-2021-23214.

To fix, throw a protocol-violation error if the internal buffer
is not empty after the encryption handshake.

Our thanks to Jacob Champion for reporting this problem.

Security: CVE-2021-23222
cve_desc: A man-in-the-middle attacker can inject false responses to the client's first few queries, despite the use of SSL certificate verification and encryption.

Flatpak compares these permissions to the *actual* metadata, from the "metadata" file to ensure it wasn't lied to. However, the actual metadata contents are loaded in several places where they are read as simple C-style strings. Thus, any permissions that appear in the metadata file after a null terminator are applied at runtime but not shown to the user. So maliciously crafted apps can give themselves hidden permissions. This issue is patched in versions 1.12.3 and 1.10.6. commit_message:
Prevent null pointer dereference in constant folding.

Under certain conditions, an invalid protobuf saved model with invalid nodes would be loaded. During optimization phase, Grappler optimizer will then dereference a null pointer.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409683530
Change-Id: I1f10340a7ec384bc9bc587300390f1078cf5caa0
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. Under certain scenarios, Grappler component of TensorFlow can trigger a null pointer dereference. There are 2 places where this can occur, for the same malicious alteration of a `SavedModel` file (fixing the first one would trigger the same dereference in the second place). First, during constant folding, the `GraphDef` might not have the required nodes for the binary operation. If a node is missing, the correposning `mul_*child` would be null, and the dereference in the subsequent line would be incorrect. We have a similar issue during `IsIdentityConsumingSwitch`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: conv_left_child and conv_right_child are obtained from node_map_->GetNode() which can return nullptr, but they are immediately dereferenced without null checks. Earlier in the function, the same pattern for mul_left_child and mul_right_child correctly checks for nullptr, but this check is missing for the convolution's children.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a node that is a Mul operation with 2 non-control inputs.
mul_left_child and mul_right_child are fetched and verified non-null. One is constant, the other is a Conv2D/Conv3D node.

state.value: points to a Conv2D or Conv3D node

step_by_step_execution.description: Device check passes. conv_node->input_size() >= 2, output count check passes, and conv_node is not in nodes_to_preserve_.
conv_left_child and conv_right_child are fetched via node_map_->GetNode(). If the graph has a malformed or missing node reference, GetNode returns nullptr.

state.value: nullptr (if conv_node->input(0) references a non-existent node)
nullptr (if conv_node->input(1) references a non-existent node)

step_by_step_execution.description: IsReallyConstant(*conv_left_child) dereferences the nullptr, causing undefined behavior (null pointer dereference / crash).

patch_depth is assigned from filter.dim_size(2).

commit_message: fixed #1895
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the ilst_box_read function in box_code_apple.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

vulnerabilities.description: Unsigned integer underflow on `size`. If the initial `size` value is between 1 and 3 (inclusive), the subtraction `size -= 4` wraps around to a very large value (close to UINT32_MAX) because `size` is a `u32`. This causes the `while (size)` loop to iterate far beyond the actual available data, leading to out-of-bounds reads from the bitstream and excessive memory allocations.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a `size` argument of, e.g., 3. The check `if (!size)` is false, so execution continues.
4 bytes (32 bits) of header fields are read from the bitstream (marker, version, seq_profile, etc.). This already reads beyond the intended `size` bytes.
The validation check on `reserved`, `marker`, and `version` passes (the bitstream contains valid values).
4 more bits are read for initial_presentation_delay fields. Total header consumed is 4 bytes.
`size -= 4` is executed. Since size is u32 (unsigned) and equals 3, this wraps around to UINT32_MAX - 0 = 4294967295.
The `while (size)` loop begins with the wrapped value, causing the loop to iterate many times, parsing OBUs and performing reads and allocations far beyond the originally intended data boundary.

add explicit format specifier to printf() invocations

cve_desc: Depending on the way the format strings in the card label are crafted it's possible to leak kernel stack memory. There is also the possibility for DoS due to the v4l2loopback kernel module crashing when providing the card label on request (reproduce e.g. with many %s modifiers in a row).

commit_message: fixed #1900
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability exists in Gpac through 1.0.1 via a malformed MP4 file in the svc_parse_slice function in av_parsers.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

vulnerabilities.description: The value `si->pps->sps_id + GF_SVC_SSPS_ID_SHIFT` is used to index into the `avc->sps` array without any bounds check. The `sps_id` field comes from a previously parsed PPS structure and its value (after adding the shift) could exceed the bounds of the `avc->sps` array, leading to an out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: A PPS ID is read from the bitstream and validated to be <= 255.
The PPS entry is retrieved from the avc->pps array using pps_id as index. This PPS was populated by a prior parsing step and contains an sps_id field.

state.value: arbitrary value set during prior PPS parsing, not validated here

step_by_step_execution.description: The code checks si->pps->slice_group_count is nonzero, which only validates the PPS was initialized, not the sps_id value.

The sps_id from the PPS is added to GF_SVC_SSPS_ID_SHIFT and used to index into avc->sps without any bounds validation. If sps_id + GF_SVC_SSPS_ID_SHIFT exceeds the size of the avc->sps array, this results in an out-of-bounds access.
The out-of-bounds pointer is then dereferenced to read log2_max_frame_num and potentially other fields, causing out-of-bounds reads.

commit_message:
Remove a `DCHECK`-fail, log an error instead.

`DCHECK` in debug mode results in crashes. TensorFlow has had multiple vulnerabilities due to this.

Outside of debug mode, `DCHECK` is a no-op.

A better alternative is to report an error to the log buffer and continue. This should happen both in debug mode and in prod mode.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408375925

We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

Since `n_state` is populated by `avc_parse_slice`, if the parsed slice references an SPS index that hasn't been loaded, `n_state.sps` could be NULL, leading to a crash when `n_state.sps->poc_type` is accessed.

step_by_step_execution.description: NAL unit type is parsed from bitstream. Suppose it is GF_AVC_NALU_NON_IDR_SLICE or GF_AVC_NALU_IDR_SLICE, entering the slice-parsing case.

`avc_parse_slice(bs, avc, idr_flag, &n_state)` is called, which parses the slice header. This function sets `n_state.sps` based on the SPS ID referenced by the slice's PPS. If the referenced SPS has not been loaded/parsed, `n_state.sps` is set to NULL.

state.value: NULL (SPS not found for the referenced SPS ID)

step_by_step_execution.description: The code checks `if (!avc->s_info.sps) return -1;` — this passes because `avc->s_info.sps` (from a *previous* valid slice) is non-NULL.
The code dereferences `n_state.sps->poc_type` in the comparison `if (avc->s_info.sps->poc_type == n_state.sps->poc_type)`, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

In standalone mode (i.e., acting as a proxy or a transparent proxy), with SSL MITM enabled, e2guardian, if built with OpenSSL v1.1.x, did not validate hostnames in certificates of the web servers that it connected to, and thus was itself vulnerable to MITM attacks.

vulnerabilities.description: If SSL_new(ctx) returns NULL (e.g., due to memory allocation failure), the code proceeds to call SSL_set_options, SSL_set_mode, SSL_set_connect_state, SSL_set_fd, and SSL_set_tlsext_host_name on a NULL pointer without any NULL check, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

SSL_new(ctx) is called but returns NULL due to an internal error or memory exhaustion.
SSL_set_options(ssl, SSL_OP_ALL) is called with ssl=NULL, dereferencing a NULL pointer.

vulnerabilities.description: The function never calls SSL_CTX_set_verify(ctx, SSL_VERIFY_PEER, ...). By default, OpenSSL client connections use SSL_VERIFY_NONE, meaning the server's certificate is never validated against the loaded CA certificates. An attacker performing a man-in-the-middle attack can present any certificate and the connection will succeed, despite the code going through the effort of loading verify locations and setting X509 params.

state.value: valid SSL object with verify mode SSL_VERIFY_NONE

step_by_step_execution.description: SSL_connect is called. The TLS handshake completes. Even if the server presents an invalid or self-signed certificate, verification result is ignored because the mode is SSL_VERIFY_NONE.
Function returns 0 (success), establishing a connection to a potentially impersonating server.

cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability exists in Gpac through 1.0.1 via a malformed MP4 file in the svc_parse_slice function in av_parsers.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

vulnerabilities.description: The value `si->pps->sps_id` is read from a previously parsed PPS structure (selected by attacker-controlled `pps_id`). It is used with an additive offset `GF_SVC_SSPS_ID_SHIFT` to index into the `avc->sps` array without any bounds validation. If `sps_id + GF_SVC_SSPS_ID_SHIFT` exceeds the size of the `avc->sps` array, this results in an out-of-bounds read/write.

The PPS entry is looked up: `si->pps = &avc->pps[pps_id]`. The `sps_id` field within this PPS was set during prior PPS parsing from attacker-controlled input.

The SPS is accessed as `avc->sps[si->pps->sps_id + GF_SVC_SSPS_ID_SHIFT]`. With GF_SVC_SSPS_ID_SHIFT typically being 16, and sps_id potentially being a large value, the computed index can exceed the bounds of the `avc->sps` array (typically 32 entries).
An out-of-bounds pointer is stored in `si->sps`, and subsequent accesses (e.g., `si->sps->log2_max_frame_num`) read from out-of-bounds memory.

commit_message: fixed #1902
cve_desc: A Segmentation fault caused by a null pointer dereference vulnerability exists in Gpac through 1.0.1 via the gf_avc_parse_nalu function in av_parsers.c when using mp4box, which causes a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: In release builds where NDEBUG is defined, assert() compiles to a no-op. If avc->s_info.sps is NULL, the subsequent dereference avc->s_info.sps->poc_type causes a NULL pointer dereference. Additionally, n_state.sps is never checked for NULL at all before being dereferenced as n_state.sps->poc_type.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a bitstream containing a NAL unit. nal_hdr is read from the bitstream.

state.value: value read from bs, e.g. with nal_unit_type = GF_AVC_NALU_NON_IDR_SLICE (1)

step_by_step_execution.description: n_state is initialized as a copy of avc->s_info. avc->s_info.sps may be NULL if no SPS has been parsed yet.
The switch falls into the GF_AVC_NALU_NON_IDR_SLICE case. slice is set to 1. avc_parse_slice is called and returns >= 0.
The first condition check on avc->s_info.nal_unit_type evaluates to false (it's within the valid slice range), so we don't break early.
frame_num, field_pic_flag, and nal_ref_idc comparisons all pass without setting ret=1, so execution continues to the assert.
assert(avc->s_info.sps) is reached. In release builds (NDEBUG defined), this is a no-op, so execution continues even if avc->s_info.sps is NULL.
avc->s_info.sps->poc_type is dereferenced, causing a NULL pointer dereference. Even if avc->s_info.sps were non-NULL, n_state.sps could also be NULL and is dereferenced without any check.

commit_message:
Replace faulty overflow check with a builder for `TensorShape`.

Prevents an integer overflow that was not caught before.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 415381595
Change-Id: I76585ddedc912bd9f4a390aeafa8e2ced1a28863
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `AddManySparseToTensorsMap` is vulnerable to an integer overflow which results in a `CHECK`-fail when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, an assert failure based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The overflow check uses an `int` (32-bit) variable `new_num_elements` to store the result of `MultiplyWithoutOverflow`, which returns `int64_t`. When the 64-bit result is positive but exceeds INT_MAX (e.g., 2^32), the truncation to `int` can wrap to a small non-negative value, bypassing the `< 0` overflow check. This allows construction of a TensorShape with very large dimensions, potentially leading to out-of-bounds memory access or other undefined behavior downstream.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads input_shape from user input. An attacker provides a sparse_shape vector with large dimension values, e.g., {2, 2^32} for a rank-2 tensor.
The overflow check loop begins. `new_num_elements` is initialized as `int` with value 1.
First iteration (i=0): MultiplyWithoutOverflow(1, 2) returns int64_t value 2. Truncated to int, still 2. Check 2 < 0 is false.

Second iteration (i=1): MultiplyWithoutOverflow(2, 4294967296) returns int64_t value 8589934592 (2^33). When this is truncated to 32-bit int, the value wraps to 0. Check 0 < 0 is false, so overflow is NOT detected.

The overflow check passes (overflow_ocurred is false). TensorShape is constructed with the original large dimensions [2, 4294967296], and subsequent operations (e.g., tensor allocation, memory access) proceed with these oversized dimensions, potentially causing out-of-bounds access or memory corruption.

commit_message:
[lite] Update TfLiteIntArrayCreate to return size_t

PiperOrigin-RevId: 416439896
Change-Id: I847f69b68d1ddaff4b1e925a09b8b69c1756653b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. An attacker can craft a TFLite model that would cause an integer overflow in `TfLiteIntArrayCreate`. The `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes` returns an `int` instead of a `size_t. An attacker can control model inputs such that `computed_size` overflows the size of `int` datatype. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow when computing the allocation size. The `size` parameter is a signed int and is used in an arithmetic expression without validation. A large positive value of `size` can cause `sizeof(dummy.data[0]) * size` to overflow, resulting in a small or negative `computed_size`. Since the return value is typically used to allocate memory, this leads to a too-small allocation followed by out-of-bounds writes when the caller populates the array.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a large `size` value, e.g. size = 0x40000000 (on a platform where sizeof(int)==4).
The expression `sizeof(dummy.data[0]) * size` is computed. sizeof(dummy.data[0]) is 4, so the multiplication is 4 * 0x40000000 = 0x100000000, which overflows a 32-bit int to 0.
computed_size is set to sizeof(dummy) + 0, which is a small value (e.g., 8 bytes).
The small computed_size is returned to the caller, who will allocate a buffer far too small for the requested array of 0x40000000 elements, enabling subsequent out-of-bounds memory writes.

commit_message: fixed #1895
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the ilst_box_read function in box_code_apple.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

vulnerabilities.description: When the initial `size` parameter (or the value obtained from `gf_bs_available`) is between 1 and 3 (inclusive), the subtraction `size -= 4` causes an unsigned integer underflow on the u32 variable, wrapping it to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF). This causes the `while (size)` loop to iterate far beyond the actual available data, leading to out-of-bounds reads and potentially large memory allocations based on garbage data.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `size` parameter equal to a small value, e.g., 3. Alternatively, `gf_bs_available(bs)` returns a small value like 3 when size is 0.
The check `if (!size)` passes since size is non-zero.

cfg is allocated and 4 bytes (32 bits) of header fields are read from the bitstream. If the bitstream has at least 4 bytes available (size was from the caller, not necessarily reflecting actual stream size), the reads succeed. Even if the stream is short, many bitstream implementations return 0-padded values.
Assuming the validation check (reserved==0, marker==1, version==1) passes, execution continues past the validation.
The line `size -= 4` is executed. Since size is u32 (unsigned) and equals 3, this underflows: 3 - 4 = 0xFFFFFFFF.

The `while (size)` loop begins. size is non-zero (very large), so the loop body executes. `gf_av1_parse_obu` is called, potentially reading beyond the real data in the bitstream, and `obu_size` may be set to an arbitrary/garbage value.

state.value: arbitrary value from parsing garbage data

step_by_step_execution.description: `gf_malloc((size_t)obu_size)` allocates memory based on potentially garbage obu_size, and `gf_bs_read_data` reads into that buffer, performing out-of-bounds reads from the bitstream. The loop continues iterating with the corrupted size value.

state.value: still very large after subtraction of obu_size, or further underflow

commit_message:
Prevent use after free in `DecodePng` kernel.

We are cleaning up the memory in `decode` and then we are using an `OP_REQUIRES` to check an invariant on the `decode` data.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409299145
Change-Id: I4eb93aaca52483eb202e89b78df07fbb2f6cb254
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. A malicious user can cause a use after free behavior when decoding PNG images. After `png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode)` gets called, the values of `decode.width` and `decode.height` are in an unspecified state. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
std::unique_ptr<uint16[]> buffer(
          new uint16[height * width * decode.channels]);
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the allocation size for the temporary uint16 buffer in the DT_FLOAT path. `height`, `width`, and `decode.channels` are all ints. The checks guarantee `width < (1<<27)`, `height < (1<<27)`, and `width*height < (1<<29)`, but do not account for multiplication by `decode.channels` (up to 4). The product `height * width * decode.channels` is computed in 32-bit int arithmetic and can overflow (e.g., (1<<29 - 1) * 4 > INT_MAX), resulting in a much smaller allocation than intended. `CommonFinishDecode` then writes the full image data into the undersized heap buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted PNG image is provided with large dimensions (e.g., width=32768, height=16384) and 4 channels. decode.width and decode.height are set accordingly.

width and height are cast to int and the size checks are evaluated. total_size = 32768 * 16384 = 536870912 = 2^29, but the check is `total_size >= (1LL << 29)` so let's use width=32767, height=16384 giving total_size=536854528 < 2^29. All checks pass.

data_type_ is DT_FLOAT, so the float path is taken. The allocation `new uint16[height * width * decode.channels]` is computed in 32-bit int arithmetic: 16384 * 32767 * 4 = 2147418112 which is close to INT_MAX. With slightly larger values the multiplication overflows, wrapping to a small or negative number. For instance width=32768, height=16384 gives 32768*16384*4 = 2^31 = -2147483648 as a signed int, which when cast to size_t becomes a very large value on 64-bit or a small/zero value on 32-bit, causing UB.
The undersized buffer is allocated. CommonFinishDecode then writes the full decoded image (height * width * channels * sizeof(uint16) bytes) into this buffer, overflowing the heap allocation.

state.value: much smaller than required due to integer overflow
full image size, larger than allocated buffer

Fix empty resource handle vulnerability.

Some ops that attempt to extract a resource handle from user input
can lead to nullptr dereferences.  This returns an error in such
a case.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445571938
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, multiple TensorFlow operations misbehave in eager mode when the resource handle provided to them is invalid. In graph mode, it would have been impossible to perform these API calls, but migration to TF 2.x eager mode opened up this vulnerability. If the resource handle is empty, then a reference is bound to a null pointer inside TensorFlow codebase (various codepaths). This is undefined behavior. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message: Fix overflow of triggered topics
cve_desc: uWebSockets 18.11.0 and 18.12.0 has a stack-based buffer overflow in uWS::TopicTree::trimTree (called from uWS::TopicTree::unsubscribeAll). NOTE: the vendor's position is that this is "a minor issue or not even an issue at all" because the developer of an application (that uses uWebSockets) should not be allowing the large number of triggered topics to accumulate

vulnerabilities.description: The buffer overflow check at the top of the function only drains when numTriggeredTopics is exactly 64 at function entry. However, within a single invocation of the function (and its recursive calls), numTriggeredTopics can be incremented multiple times without any bounds check — once per loop iteration for terminatingWildcardChild, once at the end for an exact match, and additionally within recursive calls via wildcardChild. This allows writing past the end of the triggeredTopics[64] array.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with numTriggeredTopics == 63. The initial check (numTriggeredTopics == 64) is false, so drain() is not called.
First loop iteration: iterator has a terminatingWildcardChild that is not yet triggered. The line `triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++]` writes at index 63 and increments to 64.

The loop continues (stop != npos). In the next iteration, iterator again has a terminatingWildcardChild that is not yet triggered. The line `triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++]` writes at index 64, which is out of bounds for a 64-element array.

Alternatively, even with a single loop iteration: after incrementing to 64 for terminatingWildcardChild, the loop exits and the exact match at the end also triggers `triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++]`, writing at index 64 (out of bounds).

commit_message:
run: Don't allow chroot()

If we don't allow pivot_root() then there seems no reason why we should
allow chroot().

Partially fixes GHSA-67h7-w3jq-vh4q.

Signed-off-by: Simon McVittie <smcv@collabora.com>
cve_desc: Flatpak is a system for building, distributing, and running sandboxed desktop applications on Linux. In versions prior to 1.10.4 and 1.12.0, Flatpak apps with direct access to AF_UNIX sockets such as those used by Wayland, Pipewire or pipewire-pulse can trick portals and other host-OS services into treating the Flatpak app as though it was an ordinary, non-sandboxed host-OS process. They can do this by manipulating the VFS using recent mount-related syscalls that are not blocked by Flatpak's denylist seccomp filter, in order to substitute a crafted `/.flatpak-info` or make that file disappear entirely. Flatpak apps that act as clients for AF_UNIX sockets such as those used by Wayland, Pipewire or pipewire-pulse can escalate the privileges that the corresponding services will believe the Flatpak app has. Note that protocols that operate entirely over the D-Bus session bus (user bus), system bus or accessibility bus are not affected by this. This is due to the use of a proxy process `xdg-dbus-proxy`, whose VFS cannot be manipulated by the Flatpak app, when interacting with these buses. Patches exist for versions 1.10.4 and 1.12.0, and as of time of publication, a patch for version 1.8.2 is being planned. There are no workarounds aside from upgrading to a patched version.

code_snippets:
if (r < 0 && r == -EFAULT /* unknown syscall */)
        return flatpak_fail_error (error, FLATPAK_ERROR_SETUP_FAILED, _("Failed to block syscall %d"), scall);
vulnerabilities.description: The error check after seccomp_rule_add only fails if the return code is exactly -EFAULT. Any other negative error code (e.g., -EINVAL, -ENOMEM, -EDOM) is silently ignored, meaning seccomp rules can fail to be installed without any error being reported. This allows blocked syscalls to remain unblocked, weakening the sandbox. The same flawed pattern appears for both the main blocklist and the nondevel blocklist loops.

code_snippets:
if (r < 0 && r == -EFAULT /* unknown syscall */)
        return flatpak_fail_error (error, FLATPAK_ERROR_SETUP_FAILED, _("Failed to block syscall %d"), scall);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function initializes a seccomp filter context and iterates over syscall_blocklist to add rules blocking dangerous syscalls.

For a given blocklist entry (e.g., SCMP_SYS(clone) with an arg filter), seccomp_rule_add is called. Suppose it fails with an error code other than -EFAULT, such as -EINVAL (invalid argument) or -ENOMEM (out of memory).
The error check evaluates: r < 0 is true, but r == -EFAULT is false. The condition short-circuits to false, so the error is silently ignored and execution continues.

The loop continues to the next syscall, but the rule for the failed syscall (e.g., clone with CLONE_NEWUSER) was never installed in the seccomp filter. The sandbox proceeds without blocking that syscall.

state.value: missing rule for the failed syscall

step_by_step_execution.description: The incomplete seccomp filter is exported and applied to the sandbox via bwrap, leaving dangerous syscalls (like clone with CLONE_NEWUSER, or mount, or others) unblocked.

state.value: exported to BPF with missing rules
weaker than intended — blocked syscalls are accessible

The `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes` returns an `int` instead of a `size_t. An attacker can control model inputs such that `computed_size` overflows the size of `int` datatype. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: If `size` is a negative integer or a very large positive integer, the multiplication `sizeof(dummy.data[0]) * size` can overflow or wrap around. Since `size` is a signed `int`, a negative value will be implicitly converted to `size_t` (unsigned), resulting in a very large value. The resulting `computed_size` wraps around, producing a small allocation size. A caller using this return value to allocate memory and then filling `size` elements would write out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a negative or very large `size` parameter, e.g. size = -1.

state.value: -1 (or any negative int / large int causing overflow)

step_by_step_execution.description: The expression `sizeof(dummy.data[0]) * size` is evaluated. `size` (int) is implicitly converted to `size_t` (unsigned). For size=-1, this becomes SIZE_MAX, and the multiplication yields a huge value that wraps around.

state.value: wraps around due to unsigned overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: The wrapped-around product is added to sizeof(dummy), producing a small computed_size value.

state.value: a small value, e.g. sizeof(dummy) - 4 on 64-bit systems when size=-1

step_by_step_execution.description: The small computed_size is returned. A caller allocating this many bytes and writing `size` (interpreted as a large count) elements into the buffer will perform a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: small size_t value, much smaller than needed for `size` elements

commit_message:
Fix empty resource handle vulnerability.

Some ops that attempt to extract a resource handle from user input
can lead to nullptr dereferences.  This returns an error in such
a case.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445571938
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, multiple TensorFlow operations misbehave in eager mode when the resource handle provided to them is invalid. In graph mode, it would have been impossible to perform these API calls, but migration to TF 2.x eager mode opened up this vulnerability. If the resource handle is empty, then a reference is bound to a null pointer inside TensorFlow codebase (various codepaths). This is undefined behavior. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
Validate `proto.dtype()` before calling `set_dtype()`.

This prevents a `DCHECK`-fail when the proto contains an invalid dtype for a tensor shape with 0 elements or for an incomplete tensor shape.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 408369083
Change-Id: Ia21a3e3d62a90d642a4561f08f3b543e5ad00c46
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. When decoding a tensor from protobuf, a TensorFlow process can encounter cases where a `CHECK` assertion is invalidated based on user controlled arguments, if the tensors have an invalid `dtype` and 0 elements or an invalid shape. This allows attackers to cause denial of services in TensorFlow processes. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in shape.num_elements(). If the TensorShape dimensions are individually valid but their product overflows int64_t, N can wrap to a small positive value. This causes a small buffer to be allocated (for N elements) while the stored shape_ implies a much larger tensor, leading to heap out-of-bounds reads/writes when the tensor is subsequently accessed using its shape dimensions.

code_snippets:
CASES_WITH_DEFAULT(proto.dtype(), p = Helper<T>::Decode(a, content, N),
                         dtype_error = true, dtype_error = true);

CASES_WITH_DEFAULT(proto.dtype(), p = FromProtoField<T>(a, proto, N),
                         dtype_error = true, dtype_error = true);

step_by_step_execution.description: TensorShape::IsValid checks the proto shape. If it does not verify that the product of all dimensions fits in int64_t without overflow, a shape with large dimensions (e.g., [2^31, 2^31, 2]) passes validation.

step_by_step_execution.description: TensorShape is constructed from the proto shape, storing the large dimensions.
shape.num_elements() computes the product of dimensions. The multiplication overflows int64_t and wraps, potentially yielding a small positive value.

state.value: small positive value due to int64_t overflow (e.g., 0 or a small number depending on exact dims)

step_by_step_execution.description: The condition N > 0 && proto.dtype() is true (assuming the overflow yields a small positive N). A buffer is allocated sized for only N elements via Helper<T>::Decode or FromProtoField<T>.

state.value: pointer to buffer allocated for a small number of elements

step_by_step_execution.description: shape_ is set to the original (overflowed) shape, dtype is set, and buf_ is set to the undersized buffer p. Any subsequent code that iterates over the tensor using shape_.num_elements() or individual dimension sizes will access memory far beyond the allocated buffer.

pointer to small heap buffer (N elements)

commit_message:
run: Don't allow chroot()

If we don't allow pivot_root() then there seems no reason why we should
allow chroot().

Partially fixes GHSA-67h7-w3jq-vh4q.

Signed-off-by: Simon McVittie <smcv@collabora.com>
cve_desc: Flatpak is a system for building, distributing, and running sandboxed desktop applications on Linux. In versions prior to 1.10.4 and 1.12.0, Flatpak apps with direct access to AF_UNIX sockets such as those used by Wayland, Pipewire or pipewire-pulse can trick portals and other host-OS services into treating the Flatpak app as though it was an ordinary, non-sandboxed host-OS process. They can do this by manipulating the VFS using recent mount-related syscalls that are not blocked by Flatpak's denylist seccomp filter, in order to substitute a crafted `/.flatpak-info` or make that file disappear entirely. Flatpak apps that act as clients for AF_UNIX sockets such as those used by Wayland, Pipewire or pipewire-pulse can escalate the privileges that the corresponding services will believe the Flatpak app has. Note that protocols that operate entirely over the D-Bus session bus (user bus), system bus or accessibility bus are not affected by this. This is due to the use of a proxy process `xdg-dbus-proxy`, whose VFS cannot be manipulated by the Flatpak app, when interacting with these buses. Patches exist for versions 1.10.4 and 1.12.0, and as of time of publication, a patch for version 1.8.2 is being planned. There are no workarounds aside from upgrading to a patched version.

code_snippets:
if (r < 0 && r == -EFAULT /* unknown syscall */)
        return flatpak_fail_error (error, FLATPAK_ERROR_SETUP_FAILED, _("Failed to block syscall %d"), scall);
vulnerabilities.description: The error-checking condition after seccomp_rule_add is logically inverted. The check `r < 0 && r == -EFAULT` only treats EFAULT as a fatal error, silently ignoring all other failures (e.g., -EINVAL, -ENOMEM, -EDOM). This means if seccomp_rule_add fails for any reason other than EFAULT, the corresponding syscall will not be blocked in the sandbox, but execution continues as if it succeeded. The likely intent was `r < 0 && r != -EFAULT` (fail on all errors except unknown syscall). This applies to both the main blocklist loop and the nondevel blocklist loop, potentially leaving dangerous syscalls (like mount, unshare, clone with CLONE_NEWUSER, ptrace, etc.) unblocked.

code_snippets:
if (r < 0 && r == -EFAULT /* unknown syscall */)
        return flatpak_fail_error (error, FLATPAK_ERROR_SETUP_FAILED, _("Failed to block syscall %d"), scall);

if (r < 0 && r == -EFAULT /* unknown syscall */)
            return flatpak_fail_error (error, FLATPAK_ERROR_SETUP_FAILED, _("Failed to block syscall %d"), scall);

step_by_step_execution.description: seccomp_rule_add is called to add a blocklist rule (e.g., for SCMP_SYS(mount) or SCMP_SYS(unshare)).
seccomp_rule_add returns a negative error code other than -EFAULT, such as -EINVAL or -ENOMEM, indicating the rule was NOT added.
The condition `r < 0 && r == -EFAULT` is evaluated. Since r is not -EFAULT, the condition is false, and the error is silently ignored.
The loop continues to the next syscall. The seccomp filter is missing the rule for the failed syscall (e.g., mount), but no error is reported.

state.value: missing rule for the syscall that failed to be added

step_by_step_execution.description: The incomplete seccomp filter is exported and applied. The sandboxed process can now invoke syscalls that should have been blocked (e.g., mount, unshare, clone with CLONE_NEWUSER), escaping sandbox restrictions.

state.value: syscalls that should be blocked are allowed through

commit_message:
Prevent memory leak in decoding PNG images.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409300653
Change-Id: I6182124c545989cef80cefd439b659095920763b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. When decoding PNG images TensorFlow can produce a memory leak if the image is invalid. After calling `png::CommonInitDecode(..., &decode)`, the `decode` value contains allocated buffers which can only be freed by calling `png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode)`. However, several error case in the function implementation invoke the `OP_REQUIRES` macro which immediately terminates the execution of the function, without allowing for the memory free to occur. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
std::unique_ptr<uint16[]> buffer(
          new uint16[height * width * decode.channels]);
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in buffer allocation for the DT_FLOAT path. The multiplication `height * width * decode.channels` is performed using int arithmetic. Since height and width can each be up to 2^27-1 and total_size up to 2^29-1, multiplying by decode.channels (up to 4) can overflow a 32-bit int (e.g., total_size ~= 2^29-1, channels = 4 yields ~2^31 which wraps). This results in a small heap allocation, but CommonFinishDecode then writes the full image data into it, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function receives a crafted PNG image. channel_bits is set to 16 (data_type_ == DT_FLOAT path will use 16-bit decoding).
png::CommonInitDecode succeeds, populating decode with large width, height, and channels from the PNG header.
width and height are cast to int. total_size is computed as int64 and passes the size checks (total_size = 16384*32767 = 536854528 < 2^29 = 536870912).
Output tensor is allocated successfully. Code reaches the DT_FLOAT branch.

Buffer allocation computes height * width * decode.channels in 32-bit int arithmetic: 32767 * 16384 * 4 = 2147418112, but if values are slightly larger (e.g. height=32768, width=16384, channels=4), the product is 2^31 = -2147483648 as signed int, wrapping to a negative or small value when converted to size_t on some platforms, or a very large value causing different issues. With carefully chosen values just under the checks, overflow occurs.

state.value: overflowed value, potentially small positive number

step_by_step_execution.description: A small buffer is allocated via new uint16[small_value]. CommonFinishDecode then writes the full decoded image (decode.channels * width * sizeof(uint16) bytes per row, for height rows) into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message: Improve handling of Gopher responses (#1022)
cve_desc: In Squid 3.x through 3.5.28, 4.x through 4.17, and 5.x before 5.6, due to improper buffer management, a Denial of Service can occur when processing long Gopher server responses.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one stack buffer overflow when writing the null terminator. The code writes '\0' to line[llen + 1] instead of line[llen]. When the overflow check caps llen, llen can reach TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1, causing line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE] to be written, which is one byte past the end of the stack-allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function allocates a stack buffer 'line' of size TEMP_BUF_SIZE via LOCAL_ARRAY.
The while loop processes input. Assume gopherState->len is 0 and the input contains a line that is very long (>= TEMP_BUF_SIZE characters before the newline).
The overflow check triggers: gopherState->len + llen >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE. llen is capped to TEMP_BUF_SIZE - gopherState->len - 1 = TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 0 - 1 = TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1.
Since lpos is not NULL (a newline was found) and gopherState->len == 0, the code copies llen bytes into 'line': memcpy(line, pos, llen). This writes TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1 bytes, which fits.
The null terminator is written at line[llen + 1] = line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1 + 1] = line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE]. This is one byte past the end of the buffer, causing a stack-based out-of-bounds write.

state.value: written with '\0', but buffer valid indices are 0 to TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1

commit_message:
Cleanup and remove duplicate validation in `SparseCount`.

We have valdiation that is duplicated, checking different conditions, in different formats and failing to capture all cases. This should fix all the previous bugs.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 414886981
Change-Id: Ibf0bba0beb057b76d505324bb9487565daf95f01
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `SparseCountSparseOutput` is vulnerable to a heap overflow. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable `batch` is derived from a user-controlled int64_t index value but only checked against an upper bound (`batch >= num_batches`). There is no check that `batch >= 0`. If the sparse index has a negative value in dimension 0, `batch` will be negative, the upper-bound check will pass, and `per_batch_counts[batch]` will perform an out-of-bounds access on the underlying container (likely a std::vector).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads the indices tensor, which is a user-provided 2D matrix of int64_t values.
shape is provided such that is_1d is false (shape has 2 elements), and num_batches is set from shape_vector(0), e.g. 5.
The loop iterates over values. For a given idx, batch is computed from indices_values(idx, 0). If the attacker provides -1 as the batch index in the sparse indices tensor, batch becomes -1.
The check `batch >= num_batches` evaluates to `-1 >= 5` which is false, so the OP_REQUIRES error is NOT triggered. Execution continues.
Assuming value >= 0, the code accesses per_batch_counts[batch], i.e., per_batch_counts[-1], which is an out-of-bounds access on the vector/container of size num_batches (5).

vulnerabilities.description: Both `num_batches` and `num_values` are declared as `int` but assigned from `int64_t` / platform-dependent size values. If shape_vector(0) exceeds INT_MAX, the value is truncated, potentially resulting in a small or negative `num_batches`. This undermines all subsequent bounds checks that rely on `num_batches`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The shape tensor is provided by the user with shape_vector(0) set to a large value, e.g. 0x0000000100000001 (4294967297).
num_batches is assigned via implicit int64_t-to-int truncation. 4294967297 truncated to 32-bit int becomes 1.

per_batch_counts is created with size 1. However, sparse indices can reference batch indices up to 4294967296, and since the batch check uses the truncated num_batches=1, large batch indices that should be valid are rejected, but batch=0 is accepted. Alternatively, a shape_vector(0) that truncates to a negative int means num_batches is negative, and the for loop over batches doesn't execute, skipping shape validation, while the batch bounds check `batch >= num_batches` with a negative num_batches will always trigger — though this still represents an integer truncation bug that can lead to unexpected behavior.

cve_desc: Buffer overflow in the send_control_msg function in hw/char/virtio-serial-bus.c in QEMU before 2.4.0 allows guest users to cause a denial of service (QEMU process crash) via a crafted virtio control message.

vulnerabilities.description: The function copies `len` bytes into `elem.in_sg[0].iov_base` without verifying that the destination buffer (whose size is `elem.in_sg[0].iov_len`) is large enough to hold `len` bytes. A guest can provide a virtqueue buffer smaller than the control message, leading to a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The virtqueue `c_ivq` is checked for readiness; assume it is ready.
virtqueue_pop() succeeds, populating `elem` with guest-provided scatter-gather descriptors. The guest controls the size of the in_sg buffers.

state.value: guest-controlled size, can be smaller than `len`, e.g. 1 byte

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy copies `len` bytes into elem.in_sg[0].iov_base without checking elem.in_sg[0].iov_len >= len, writing beyond the allocated buffer.

state.value: len (e.g. 8+ bytes written into a 1-byte buffer)

commit_message:
Prevent overflow in sparse dense cwise ops.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementations of `Sparse*Cwise*` ops are vulnerable to integer overflows. These can be used to trigger large allocations (so, OOM based denial of service) or `CHECK`-fails when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, assert failures based denial of service). commit_message:
Ensure num_streams >= 0 in tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateQuantileStreamResource

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387452765
Change-Id: I9990c760e177fabca6a3b9b4612ceeaeeba51495
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can cause a denial of service in `boosted_trees_create_quantile_stream_resource` by using negative arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantile_ops.cc#L96) does not validate that `num_streams` only contains non-negative numbers. In turn, [this results in using this value to allocate memory](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantiles/quantile_stream_resource.h#L31-L40). However, `reserve` receives an unsigned integer so there is an implicit conversion from a negative value to a large positive unsigned. This results in a crash from the standard library. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 8a84f7a2b5a2b27ecf88d25bad9ac777cd2f7992. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent nullptr deref in SparseTensorSliceDataset

The arguments must determine a valid sparse tensor. This means that when indices are empty then the values must be empty too (and the reverse).

Also added test, by modifying existing test with empty sparse tensor to now run with an invalid sparse tensor input.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388562757
Change-Id: Id8b54cd7c2316025b4f9a77292c8fb5344d17609
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When a user does not supply arguments that determine a valid sparse tensor, `tf.raw_ops.SparseTensorSliceDataset` implementation can be made to dereference a null pointer. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L240-L251) has some argument validation but fails to consider the case when either `indices` or `values` are provided for an empty sparse tensor when the other is not. If `indices` is empty, then [code that performs validation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L260-L261) (i.e., checking that the indices are monotonically increasing) results in a null pointer dereference. If `indices` as provided by the user is empty, then `indices` in the C++ code above is backed by an empty `std::vector`, hence calling `indices->dim_size(0)` results in null pointer dereferencing (same as calling `std::vector::at()` on an empty vector). We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 02cc160e29d20631de3859c6653184e3f876b9d7. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.
