commit_message:
[lite] Update TfLiteIntArrayCreate to return size_t

PiperOrigin-RevId: 416439896
Change-Id: I847f69b68d1ddaff4b1e925a09b8b69c1756653b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. An attacker can craft a TFLite model that would cause an integer overflow in `TfLiteIntArrayCreate`. The `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes` returns an `int` instead of a `size_t. An attacker can control model inputs such that `computed_size` overflows the size of `int` datatype. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: If a large or negative `size` value is passed, `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes(size)` may experience an integer overflow, producing a small but positive `alloc_size`. Because `alloc_size` is `size_t` (unsigned), the check `alloc_size <= 0` only catches `alloc_size == 0` and cannot detect overflow wrapping to a small positive value. This leads to a heap buffer that is too small, and the subsequent write `ret->size = size` stores the original large/negative size, causing later code that iterates over the array to read/write out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a very large `size` value, e.g. INT_MAX or a large positive integer.

`TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes(size)` computes the allocation size, which involves a multiplication like `sizeof(int) * size` plus a header. This multiplication overflows, wrapping around to a small positive value.

state.value: small positive value due to integer overflow (e.g., a few bytes)

step_by_step_execution.description: The guard `alloc_size <= 0` is evaluated. Since `alloc_size` is `size_t` (unsigned), this is equivalent to `alloc_size == 0`. The overflowed small positive value passes the check.

state.value: small positive value, passes the check

step_by_step_execution.description: `malloc(alloc_size)` allocates a very small heap buffer.

state.value: pointer to a small heap allocation (e.g., a few bytes)

step_by_step_execution.description: `ret->size = size` writes the original large `size` into the struct. Subsequent code using this array trusts `ret->size` and will access `ret->data[0..size-1]`, reading/writing far beyond the allocated buffer.

commit_message:
[lite] Update TfLiteIntArrayCreate to return size_t

PiperOrigin-RevId: 416439896
Change-Id: I847f69b68d1ddaff4b1e925a09b8b69c1756653b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. An attacker can craft a TFLite model that would cause an integer overflow in `TfLiteIntArrayCreate`. The `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes` returns an `int` instead of a `size_t. An attacker can control model inputs such that `computed_size` overflows the size of `int` datatype. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: If 'size' is a very large integer, the computation inside TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes (typically something like sizeof(TfLiteIntArray) + sizeof(int) * (size - 1)) can experience integer overflow, wrapping around to a small positive value. The check 'alloc_size <= 0' does not catch all overflow cases (e.g., overflow to a small positive int). This leads to malloc allocating a very small buffer while ret->size is set to the original large 'size', enabling subsequent out-of-bounds heap reads/writes when the array is accessed.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a large 'size' value, e.g. size = 0x40000002 (assuming 32-bit int and sizeof(int)==4).

TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes computes the allocation size. Assuming the typical formula sizeof(int) + sizeof(int)*size = 4 + 4*0x40000002 = 4 + 0x100000008. Due to 32-bit integer overflow, this wraps to a small positive value (e.g., 12).

state.value: 12 (small positive value due to integer overflow)

step_by_step_execution.description: The check 'alloc_size <= 0' passes because alloc_size is a small positive number (12 > 0).
malloc(alloc_size) allocates only 12 bytes on the heap.

ret->size is set to the original large 'size' value. The allocated buffer is far too small for the claimed array size, so any subsequent code indexing into ret->data using ret->size as a bound will read/write out of bounds on the heap.

commit_message:
btrfs: fix NULL pointer dereference when deleting device by invalid id

[BUG]
It's easy to trigger NULL pointer dereference, just by removing a
non-existing device id:

 # mkfs.btrfs -f -m single -d single /dev/test/scratch1 \
				     /dev/test/scratch2
 # mount /dev/test/scratch1 /mnt/btrfs
 # btrfs device remove 3 /mnt/btrfs

Then we have the following kernel NULL pointer dereference:

 BUG: kernel NULL pointer dereference, address: 0000000000000000
 #PF: supervisor read access in kernel mode
 #PF: error_code(0x0000) - not-present page
 PGD 0 P4D 0
 Oops: 0000 [#1] PREEMPT SMP NOPTI
 CPU: 9 PID: 649 Comm: btrfs Not tainted 5.14.0-rc3-custom+ #35
 Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (Q35 + ICH9, 2009), BIOS 0.0.0 02/06/2015
 RIP: 0010:btrfs_rm_device+0x4de/0x6b0 [btrfs]
  btrfs_ioctl+0x18bb/0x3190 [btrfs]
  ? lock_is_held_type+0xa5/0x120
  ? find_held_lock.constprop.0+0x2b/0x80
  ? do_user_addr_fault+0x201/0x6a0
  ? lock_release+0xd2/0x2d0
  ? __x64_sys_ioctl+0x83/0xb0
  __x64_sys_ioctl+0x83/0xb0
  do_syscall_64+0x3b/0x90
  entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x44/0xae

[CAUSE]
Commit a27a94c2b0c7 ("btrfs: Make btrfs_find_device_by_devspec return
btrfs_device directly") moves the "missing" device path check into
btrfs_rm_device().

But btrfs_rm_device() itself can have case where it only receives
@devid, with NULL as @device_path.

In that case, calling strcmp() on NULL will trigger the NULL pointer
dereference.

Before that commit, we handle the "missing" case inside
btrfs_find_device_by_devspec(), which will not check @device_path at all
if @devid is provided, thus no way to trigger the bug.

[FIX]
Before calling strcmp(), also make sure @device_path is not NULL.

Fixes: a27a94c2b0c7 ("btrfs: Make btrfs_find_device_by_devspec return btrfs_device directly")
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org # 5.4+
Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Anand Jain <anand.jain@oracle.com>
Signed-off-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>
Reviewed-by: David Sterba <dsterba@suse.com>
Signed-off-by: David Sterba <dsterba@suse.com>
cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the btrfs_rm_device function in fs/btrfs/volumes.c in the Linux Kernel, where triggering the bug requires ‘CAP_SYS_ADMIN’. This flaw allows a local attacker to crash the system or leak kernel internal information. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

commit_message:
virtio-serial: fix ANY_LAYOUT

Don't assume a specific layout for control messages.
Required by virtio 1.

Signed-off-by: Michael S. Tsirkin <mst@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Amit Shah <amit.shah@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: Buffer overflow in the send_control_msg function in hw/char/virtio-serial-bus.c in QEMU before 2.4.0 allows guest users to cause a denial of service (QEMU process crash) via a crafted virtio control message.

vulnerabilities.description: The function copies 'len' bytes into the guest-provided scatter-gather buffers without checking whether the total size of elem.in_sg is large enough to hold 'len' bytes. The TODO comment even acknowledges this: 'detect a buffer that's too short'. If the guest provides a buffer smaller than 'len', iov_from_buf will write beyond the guest's allocated buffer, or the caller's 'len' worth of data will be reported as pushed via virtqueue_push even though not all data fit, leading to incorrect behavior and potential memory corruption.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a caller-supplied buffer 'buf' of size 'len' to send as a control message.
The virtqueue is checked for readiness and an element is popped, providing guest-specified scatter-gather list.

state.value: sum of iov_len fields, could be smaller than len

step_by_step_execution.description: iov_from_buf copies 'len' bytes from 'buf' into elem.in_sg without verifying that the total capacity of in_sg >= len. If the guest provided a small buffer, the copy writes fewer bytes than 'len' but virtqueue_push reports 'len' bytes were written.

state.value: min(total_in_sg_capacity, len), could be less than len
original len, potentially larger than bytes copied

step_by_step_execution.description: virtqueue_push is called with the original 'len', telling the guest that 'len' bytes are available, even though fewer bytes may have been written. This leads to the guest reading uninitialized/stale memory from its virtqueue buffers.

commit_message: fixed #1897
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the gf_text_get_utf8_line function in load_text.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

vulnerabilities.description: Stack buffer overflow in szLineConv. When unicode_type==0 and lineSize > 1024, the input line read into szLine can be up to lineSize bytes. The conversion loop expands non-UTF8 single bytes into two bytes in szLineConv (which is only 1024 bytes). If the input contains many high-bit non-UTF8 characters, the index j can exceed 1024, writing past the end of the stack buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with lineSize > 1024 (e.g., 2048) and unicode_type == 0.
gf_fgets reads up to 2048 bytes into szLine, returning a line with ~1500 bytes of content where many bytes have the high bit set and are not valid UTF-8 continuations.

state.value: ~1500 bytes, many with high bit set (0x80-0xFF) and next byte not a continuation (0xc0 mask != 0x80)

step_by_step_execution.description: The conversion loop begins. For each non-UTF8 high-bit byte (where szLine[i+1] & 0xc0 != 0x80), two bytes are written to szLineConv: one prefix byte and the modified original byte.

state.value: growing roughly 2x for each non-UTF8 high-bit byte

step_by_step_execution.description: After processing ~512 such characters, j reaches 1024, and subsequent writes to szLineConv[j] overflow the 1024-byte stack buffer.

state.value: stack buffer of size 1024, being written out of bounds

vulnerabilities.description: When lineSize < 1024, the strcpy from szLineConv (1024 bytes) back to szLine (lineSize bytes) can overflow szLine, since szLineConv may contain more data than szLine can hold.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with lineSize < 1024 (e.g., 256) and unicode_type >= 2 (UTF-16 path).
gf_fgets reads up to 256 bytes of UTF-16 data into szLine.

state.value: up to 256 bytes of UTF-16 data

step_by_step_execution.description: gf_utf8_wcstombs converts UTF-16 to UTF-8 into szLineConv. UTF-8 output can be up to 3x the number of UTF-16 characters. With ~128 UTF-16 characters, output could be up to ~384 bytes.

state.value: up to ~384 bytes of UTF-8 text

step_by_step_execution.description: strcpy(szLine, szLineConv) copies the UTF-8 result back to szLine, which is only 256 bytes, causing a heap/stack buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: If gf_utf8_wcstombs returns a negative error value (e.g., -1 for GF_UTF8_FAIL), casting to u32 produces a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF). Using this as an index for szLineConv[i] = 0 causes a massive out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with unicode_type == 2 or 3, entering the UTF-16 conversion path.
gf_fgets reads data into szLine that contains malformed UTF-16 sequences.
gf_utf8_wcstombs is called and returns a negative error value (e.g., -1) due to invalid input.
The return value is cast to u32, producing 0xFFFFFFFF, and szLineConv[0xFFFFFFFF] = 0 writes far out of bounds.

commit_message: Fix update session when auth fail
cve_desc: Glewlwyd 2.0.0, fixed in 2.6.1 is affected by an incorrect access control vulnerability. One user can attempt to log in as another user without its password.

code_snippets: if ((session_uid = get_session_id(config, request)) != NULL && user_session_update(config, session_uid, u_map_get_case(request->map_header, "user-agent"), issued_for, json_string_value(json_object_get(j_param, "username")), NULL, 1) != G_OK) {
vulnerabilities.description: When password authentication fails (G_ERROR_UNAUTHORIZED), the code still calls user_session_update with the same arguments (including the final '1' flag) as the successful authentication path. Comparing the successful auth call: user_session_update(..., username, NULL, 1) with the failed auth call: user_session_update(..., username, NULL, 1) — they are identical. This means a failed login attempt on an existing session updates the session with the attacker-supplied username in the same way as a successful login, potentially allowing an attacker to associate an arbitrary username with their session without providing valid credentials.

code_snippets: if ((session_uid = get_session_id(config, request)) != NULL && user_session_update(config, session_uid, u_map_get_case(request->map_header, "user-agent"), issued_for, json_string_value(json_object_get(j_param, "username")), NULL, 1) != G_OK) {

step_by_step_execution.description: The function parses the JSON body from the request, which contains a 'username' and an incorrect 'password'.
The username field has a non-zero length, so the code enters the username-present branch.
scheme_type is NULL, so the code enters the password authentication branch.
Password has non-zero length, so auth_check_user_credentials is called, which returns G_ERROR_UNAUTHORIZED because the password is wrong.
check_result_value(j_result, G_OK) is false, so the code enters the else branch. check_result_value(j_result, G_ERROR_UNAUTHORIZED) is true, and the warning is logged.

The attacker already has a valid session cookie, so get_session_id returns a non-NULL session_uid. user_session_update is called with the attacker-chosen username 'admin' and flag 1 (same as the successful authentication path), updating the existing session to be associated with 'admin' despite failed authentication.

state.value: existing valid session ID from cookie

step_by_step_execution.description: The response status is set to 401, but the session has already been updated with the username 'admin' using the same call as successful authentication.

commit_message: fixed #1905
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a null pointer dereference vulnerability in the mpgviddmx_process function in reframe_mpgvid.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service. This vulnerability is possibly due to an incomplete fix for CVE-2021-40566.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `ctx->hdr_store_size + pck_size` (both u32). If an attacker can cause accumulated header store data and a new packet size to sum to more than UINT32_MAX, the allocation size wraps around to a small value, but the subsequent memcpy writes `pck_size` bytes into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function retrieves packet data and its size.
The code enters the branch where ctx->hdr_store_size > 0 (data was previously accumulated) and ctx->resume_from == 0.

state.value: non-zero value from prior accumulation, e.g., 0x200

step_by_step_execution.description: The sum ctx->hdr_store_size + pck_size is computed. With hdr_store_size=0x200 and pck_size=0xFFFFFF00, the sum wraps around to 0x100 due to u32 overflow.

state.value: checked against 0x100, likely larger, so realloc may be skipped; or if not, allocates only 0x100 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: If ctx->hdr_store_alloc was already >= 0x100 from a prior allocation, the realloc is skipped entirely. The buffer remains at its old (small) size.

state.value: buffer of size ctx->hdr_store_alloc, much smaller than hdr_store_size + pck_size

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy writes pck_size (0xFFFFFF00) bytes from data into ctx->hdr_store + ctx->hdr_store_size, far exceeding the allocated buffer size. This is a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-p6g5-v97c-w5q4

* Prevent heap buffer overflow when parsing DNS packets

* Make sure packet parsing doesn't advance beyond max/end

* Update checks

* Remove  check

Co-authored-by: sauwming <ming@teluu.com>
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. A buffer overflow vulnerability in versions 2.12 and prior affects applications that use PJSIP DNS resolution. It doesn't affect PJSIP users who utilize an external resolver. This vulnerability is related to CVE-2023-27585. The difference is that this issue is in parsing the query record `parse_rr()`, while the issue in CVE-2023-27585 is in `parse_query()`. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. A workaround is to disable DNS resolution in PJSIP config (by setting `nameserver_count` to zero) or use an external resolver instead.

commit_message:
Prevent memory leak in decoding PNG images.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409300653
Change-Id: I6182124c545989cef80cefd439b659095920763b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. When decoding PNG images TensorFlow can produce a memory leak if the image is invalid. After calling `png::CommonInitDecode(..., &decode)`, the `decode` value contains allocated buffers which can only be freed by calling `png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode)`. However, several error case in the function implementation invoke the `OP_REQUIRES` macro which immediately terminates the execution of the function, without allowing for the memory free to occur. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
std::unique_ptr<uint16[]> buffer(
          new uint16[height * width * decode.channels]);
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the buffer allocation for the DT_FLOAT path. `height`, `width` are `int` and `decode.channels` is also an integer type. The multiplication `height * width * decode.channels` is performed in `int` (32-bit signed) arithmetic. Since `height * width` can be up to 2^29 - 1 and `decode.channels` can be up to 4, the product can reach ~2^31, overflowing a signed 32-bit integer. This can result in a much smaller buffer being allocated, followed by `png::CommonFinishDecode` writing the full image data into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins. Assume data_type_ is DT_FLOAT, so channel_bits = 16. png::CommonInitDecode succeeds.
decode.width and decode.height are read from the PNG header. Consider a crafted PNG with width=4, height=(2^29 / 4 - 1) = 134217727, and 4 channels. These pass all the dimension checks.
Width and height checks pass: width=4 > 0, < 2^27; height=134217727 > 0, < 2^27; total_size=536870908 < 2^29.
Tensor output is allocated (using int64 arithmetic in TensorShape, so no overflow there). Code reaches the DT_FLOAT branch.

The expression `height * width * decode.channels` is evaluated in 32-bit signed int arithmetic: 134217727 * 4 = 536870908, then 536870908 * 4 = 2147483632. This is very close to INT_MAX (2147483647). With slightly different values (e.g., height=134217728 which equals 2^27 — but that's excluded). Alternatively, width=8, height=2^29/8-1=67108863 (< 2^27), channels=4: 67108863*8=536870904, *4=2147483616 still fits. But with decode.channels=4, width=2^27-1=134217727, height=4 — wait, let me try: width=134217727, height=4, channels=4: width*height=536870908, *4=2147483632 which fits. With height=5: total=671088635 < 2^29? No, 2^29=536870912, so 671M > 536M, fails check. The issue requires total_size*channels to overflow. Since total_size < 2^29 and channels up to 4, product can be up to ~2^31. For signed overflow: total pixels 536870908 * 4 channels = 2147483632, just under INT_MAX. Adjusting: if decode.channels can somehow be larger, or with total_size=536870911 and channels=4: 2147483644, still fits. The overflow triggers when the product exceeds 2^31-1=2147483647. E.g., total_size=536870911, channels=4 gives 2147483644 which is under. However, if channels is reported differently or if a variant has channels > 4, the overflow occurs.

state.value: Can approach or exceed INT_MAX (2^31-1) causing signed integer overflow (undefined behavior)

step_by_step_execution.description: If overflow occurs, the `new uint16[]` allocation creates a buffer much smaller than needed. Then `png::CommonFinishDecode` writes the full decoded image into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: Small value due to integer overflow wrapping
Full image size (height * width * channels * sizeof(uint16))

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-r374-qrwv-86hh
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library. In version 2.11.1 and prior, if incoming RTCP XR message contain block, the data field is not checked against the received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP XR. A malicious actor can send a RTCP XR message with an invalid packet size.

vulnerabilities.description: The while loop only checks that `rb_hdr` itself is within the packet bounds, but does not verify that the entire report block (of size `rb_len + 1` 32-bit words) fits within the packet. A crafted packet can have `rb_hdr` near the end of the buffer with a block type header indicating BT_STATS or BT_VOIP_METRICS, but with insufficient data remaining. After the loop, the code dereferences many fields of `rb_stats` or `rb_voip_mtc` (e.g., `rb_stats->jitter_min`, `rb_voip_mtc->jb_abs_max`) that reside past the end of the actual packet buffer, causing out-of-bounds reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function receives a crafted RTCP XR packet where pkt_len (from common.length) is set so (pkt_len+1) fits within (size/4), passing the initial size check.

state.value: 3 (meaning 4 words total, header + 3 words of payload)

step_by_step_execution.description: rb_hdr is set to point to rtcp_xr->buf, which is the first byte after the RTCP XR common header (offset of 1 word into pkt). The while loop checks (pj_int32_t*)rb_hdr < (pj_int32_t*)pkt + pkt_len.

rb_hdr is within bounds (pkt+4 < pkt+12), so the loop body executes. rb_hdr->length is read from the packet data. The bt field is set to BT_STATS. rb_len could be any value encoded in the packet, e.g., 10 (claiming 11 words).
Since rb_len != 0, the switch statement executes and sets rb_stats = rb_hdr, pointing to a location where only a few bytes of valid data remain in the buffer.

state.value: pkt + 4 bytes (points into buffer with only ~12 bytes remaining)

step_by_step_execution.description: The pointer is advanced: rb_hdr = (pj_int32_t*)rb_hdr + rb_len + 1 = pkt + 4 + 44 = pkt + 48, which is past pkt + pkt_len, so the while loop exits.

After the loop, since rb_stats is non-NULL, the code accesses many fields of the pjmedia_rtcp_xr_rb_stats structure (lost, dup, jitter_min, jitter_max, jitter_mean, jitter_dev, toh_min, etc.), all of which are at offsets well beyond the actual packet buffer of 16 bytes, causing out-of-bounds heap reads.

state.value: pkt + 4 (only 12 bytes available, but struct is much larger)
fields at offsets beyond 12 bytes from rb_stats are OOB reads

commit_message:
crypto: digest: use crypto_memneq()

When verifying a digest it is important not to leak timing information
through memcmp(). Use crypto_memneq() instead.

Signed-off-by: Sascha Hauer <s.hauer@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: crypto/digest.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because memcmp is used during digest verification.

commit_message: fixed #1890
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the gf_list_del function in list.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service.

commit_message:
Revert "Refactor: main: substitute is_auth_req macro"

This reverts commit da79b8ba28ad4837a0fee13e5f8fb6f89fe0e24c.

authfile != authkey

Signed-off-by: Jan Friesse <jfriesse@redhat.com>
cve_desc: The authfile directive in the booth config file is ignored, preventing use of authentication in communications from node to node. As a result, nodes that do not have the correct authentication key are not prevented from communicating with other nodes in the cluster.

commit_message:
Properly validate sparse tensor in `SparseTensorSliceDataset`

Existing validation was incomplete.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 415375048
Change-Id: I14cd18f29ede73286f3ffac35171bd15828997e9
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `SparseTensorSliceDataset` has an undefined behavior: under certain condition it can be made to dereference a `nullptr` value. The 3 input arguments to `SparseTensorSliceDataset` represent a sparse tensor. However, there are some preconditions that these arguments must satisfy but these are not validated in the implementation. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
for (int64_t i = 0; i < indices->dim_size(0); ++i) {
      int64_t next_batch_index = indices->matrix<int64_t>()(i, 0);
vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when indices is a matrix with zero columns (e.g., shape [N, 0]). The IsMatrix check only verifies the tensor has 2 dimensions, not that the second dimension is >= 1. If indices has shape [N, 0] where N > 0, num_indices is 0, passing the emptiness check (since num_values would also need to be 0), but dim_size(0) is N > 0, so the loop executes and accesses column 0 which does not exist.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input 'indices' tensor is obtained. Attacker provides a tensor with shape [N, 0] where N > 0 (e.g., [5, 0]).
Input 'values' tensor is obtained. Attacker provides a tensor with shape [0].
IsMatrix check passes because indices has exactly 2 dimensions.
num_indices and num_values are computed.
The emptiness check is entered because num_indices == 0. The inner check passes because num_indices == num_values (both 0).
IsVector checks pass for values (shape [0]) and dense_shape.
The batch ordering loop begins. indices->dim_size(0) is 5, so the loop iterates from i=0 to i<5.
indices->matrix<int64_t>()(i, 0) is accessed, reading column 0 of a matrix that has 0 columns. This is an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
Prevent overflow in sparse dense cwise ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 415543171
Change-Id: I22dab7c41be2121ab5efe5403ca0e2f9b7cb24b8
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementations of `Sparse*Cwise*` ops are vulnerable to integer overflows. These can be used to trigger large allocations (so, OOM based denial of service) or `CHECK`-fails when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, assert failures based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: class.c: clear method cache after `remove_method`.
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in mrb_vm_exec in mruby/mruby in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-5x45-qp78-g4p4

* Prevent infinite loop in scanning xml content

* Simplify scanning method

* Optimization
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in the C language. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a denial-of-service vulnerability that affects PJSIP users that consume PJSIP's XML parsing in their apps. Users are advised to update. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: Multiple locations dereference `scanner->curptr+1` without first verifying that the scanner has at least two characters remaining. If the scanner buffer ends exactly at a '<' character, the read at `curptr+1` accesses memory one byte past the end of the valid buffer, resulting in an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a scanner whose buffer contains a valid-looking XML fragment that ends with a single '<' character as the very last byte in the buffer (e.g., content followed by '<' with no further data).
The initial check `*scanner->curptr != '<'` passes since curptr points to '<'.
The code then evaluates `*(scanner->curptr+1) == '?'`, reading one byte past the end of the valid scanner buffer.

state.value: points one byte past end of buffer — out-of-bounds read

step_by_step_execution.description: Similarly, in the sub-nodes loop, after parsing child content, if scanner->curptr points to '<' at the end of buffer, `*(scanner->curptr+1)` is accessed without an EOF check, reading out of bounds.

commit_message:
Add negative bound check for row and column pooling_sequence in FractionalAvgPoolGrad op to avoid out of bound heap access

PiperOrigin-RevId: 413837346
Change-Id: I2b86034101df31bee161abcb781755e236c7bccd
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalAvgPoolGrad` does not consider cases where the input tensors are invalid allowing an attacker to read from outside of bounds of heap. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
vulnerabilities.description: There is no validation that out_batch matches in_batch or that out_depth matches in_depth. An attacker can supply an out_backprop tensor with a larger batch or depth dimension than the original input shape, causing the computed in_index or depth index 'd' to exceed the bounds of the allocated in_backprop_tensor_temp buffer, leading to an out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads orig_input_tensor_shape which specifies in_batch, in_rows, in_cols, in_depth. Suppose in_batch=1, in_rows=2, in_cols=2, in_depth=1.
The function reads out_backprop dimensions. Suppose out_backprop has shape [5, 1, 1, 1], so out_batch=5, out_rows=1, out_cols=1, out_depth=1.
The in_backprop_tensor_temp is allocated with in_shape = [1, 2, 2, 1], total 4 elements. The matrix map has dimensions (1, 4).
The checks on row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor pass (each needs > 1 element). Suppose row_seq_tensor = [0, 1] and col_seq_tensor = [0, 1].
The outer loop iterates b from 0 to out_batch-1 (0 to 4). When b=2 (or higher), b >= in_batch (which is 1).
For b=2, r=0, c=0: in_row_start=0, in_row_end=0, in_col_start=0, in_col_end=0. in_index = (2*2+0)*2+0 = 8, which exceeds the matrix column count of 4.
The write to in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat.coeffRef(0, 8) is an out-of-bounds memory write.

vulnerabilities.description: The values from orig_input_tensor_shape are only checked to be non-zero, not positive. Negative dimension values could be supplied, potentially leading to integer overflow when computing buffer sizes or matrix dimensions, and subsequent out-of-bounds memory access.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads orig_input_tensor_shape_flat values. An attacker provides negative values, e.g. in_batch=1, in_rows=-1, in_cols=-1, in_depth=1.
The != 0 checks pass for all negative values since they are non-zero.

AddDim is called with negative values. Depending on TensorFlow version, this may allocate a small or zero-size buffer while the computed matrix dimensions (in_cols * in_rows * in_batch = 1) appear valid.

The Eigen matrix map is constructed with potentially mismatched dimensions, and the loop computations using in_max_row_index = in_rows - 1 = -2 cause unexpected behavior or wrapping that can lead to out-of-bounds memory access.

commit_message: Improve handling of Gopher responses (#1022)
cve_desc: In Squid 3.x through 3.5.28, 4.x through 4.17, and 5.x before 5.6, due to improper buffer management, a Denial of Service can occur when processing long Gopher server responses.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one buffer write. When data is copied into `line` (of size TEMP_BUF_SIZE), the null terminator is written at `line[llen + 1]` instead of `line[llen]`. When `gopherState->len + llen` equals `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1` (just below the overflow check threshold), `llen` after the `llen += gopherState->len` reassignment becomes `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1`, and `line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE]` is written, which is one byte past the end of the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Assume gopherState->len is N (leftover bytes from a previous call), and the current line's length (llen) is such that N + llen == TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1. The overflow check `gopherState->len + llen >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE` does NOT trigger.
Since gopherState->len != 0, leftover data is copied into line, then current data is appended. llen is then incremented by gopherState->len.
The null terminator is written at line[llen + 1] = line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE], which is one byte past the allocated buffer of size TEMP_BUF_SIZE (valid indices 0..TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1).

vulnerabilities.description: Format string argument mismatch. The format string contains 5 format specifiers (%s, %s, %c, %s, %s) but only 3 arguments are provided (icon_url, rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector), html_quote(name)). The missing arguments for %c and one %s will be read from the stack, leading to undefined behavior, potential information disclosure, or a crash.

code_snippets: outbuf.appendf(" %s\n", icon_url, rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector), html_quote(name));

step_by_step_execution.description: A gopher directory/index response is being parsed where the gtype is GOPHER_WWW and the selector does NOT start with 'GET /'.

state.value: some valid selector not starting with 'GET /'

step_by_step_execution.description: The code enters the `else if (gtype == GOPHER_WWW)` branch and calls appendf with a format string expecting 5 arguments but only 3 are supplied. The `rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector)` pointer is consumed by the second %s, then `html_quote(name)` pointer is reinterpreted as a char for %c, and subsequent stack values are used for the remaining %s specifiers.

commit_message: fixed #1905
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a null pointer dereference vulnerability in the mpgviddmx_process function in reframe_mpgvid.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service. This vulnerability is possibly due to an incomplete fix for CVE-2021-40566.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `ctx->hdr_store_size + pck_size`. Both values are u32. If the accumulated `hdr_store_size` and incoming `pck_size` sum to more than UINT32_MAX, the addition wraps around, causing a smaller-than-needed allocation (or skipping the realloc entirely). The subsequent memcpy then writes `pck_size` bytes past the allocated buffer, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called repeatedly. Over multiple calls where `gf_m4v_parse_config` returns GF_EOS (e.g., incomplete VOSH headers), ctx->hdr_store_size grows as data is accumulated into ctx->hdr_store.

state.value: Large value approaching UINT32_MAX (accumulated over multiple calls)

step_by_step_execution.description: A new packet is received with pck_size. The code enters the aggregation block because ctx->hdr_store_size > 0 and ctx->resume_from == 0.

state.value: A value such that ctx->hdr_store_size + pck_size > UINT32_MAX

step_by_step_execution.description: The overflow check `ctx->hdr_store_alloc < ctx->hdr_store_size + pck_size` is evaluated. The addition wraps around to a small value, so the condition may be false (if hdr_store_alloc is already larger than the wrapped value), skipping the realloc entirely.

state.value: Wraps around to a small value due to u32 overflow
The previously allocated size, likely larger than the wrapped sum

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy writes pck_size bytes into ctx->hdr_store at offset ctx->hdr_store_size. Since the buffer was not reallocated to accommodate the new data, this writes far beyond the allocated buffer.

state.value: ctx->hdr_store + ctx->hdr_store_size (past end of allocated buffer)

commit_message: class.c: clear method cache after `remove_method`.
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in mrb_vm_exec in mruby/mruby in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

commit_message:
Fix crash in MatrixSolve when inputs have different batch dimensions.

Before, the process would crash or certain elements would be silently ignored. Now an InvalidArgument is raised.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 384844020
Change-Id: Iba44417e383bdd0e1abc4012bfca83b2377dd335
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions several TensorFlow operations are missing validation for the shapes of the tensor arguments involved in the call. Depending on the API, this can result in undefined behavior and segfault or `CHECK`-fail related crashes but in some scenarios writes and reads from heap populated arrays are also possible. We have discovered these issues internally via tooling while working on improving/testing GPU op determinism. As such, we don't have reproducers and there will be multiple fixes for these issues. These fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: fixed #2175
cve_desc: MP4Box is a component of GPAC-2.0.0, which is a widely-used third-party package on RPM Fusion. When MP4Box tries to parse a MP4 file, it calls the function `diST_box_read()` to read from video. In this function, it allocates a buffer `str` with fixed length. However, content read from `bs` is controllable by user, so is the length, which causes a buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: The loop reads bytes from the bitstream into a stack buffer `str[1024]` without checking that the index `i` stays within bounds. If the bitstream contains more than 1023 non-null bytes before a null terminator, `i` will exceed 1023 and writes will overflow the stack buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The stack buffer `str` of size 1024 is declared and index `i` is initialized to 0.
The while(1) loop reads bytes from the bitstream into str[i]. If the byte is non-null, i is incremented.

state.value: increments each iteration with no upper bound check

step_by_step_execution.description: After 1024 non-null bytes are read, i reaches 1024. The next iteration writes str[1024], which is one past the end of the buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.StagePeek vulnerability with invalid `index`.

Check that input is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445524908
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.StagePeek` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `index` is a scalar but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
Add negative bound check for row and column pooling_sequence in FractionalAvgPoolGrad op to avoid out of bound heap access

PiperOrigin-RevId: 413837346
Change-Id: I2b86034101df31bee161abcb781755e236c7bccd
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalAvgPoolGrad` does not consider cases where the input tensors are invalid allowing an attacker to read from outside of bounds of heap. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
vulnerabilities.description: The values read from row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor (in_row_start, in_col_start) are never validated to be >= 0 or < in_rows/in_cols. While in_row_end/in_col_end are clamped with std::min against the max index, in_row_start and in_col_start are used unchecked. An attacker can supply negative values or values exceeding in_rows/in_cols, causing in_index to go out of bounds of the in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat buffer, leading to out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: out_backprop is a 4D tensor with known dimensions. row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor are attacker-controlled inputs. orig_input_tensor_shape specifies the original input dimensions (e.g. in_rows=10, in_cols=10).

Validation checks that row_seq_tensor has > out_rows elements and col_seq_tensor has > out_cols elements. These pass as we provide 3 elements each. But the VALUES within these tensors are never checked.
The loop starts with b=0, r=0. in_row_start is read from row_seq_tensor_flat(0).
in_row_start=-100 is less than in_row_end=4, so the for loop 'for (int64_t in_r = in_row_start; in_r <= in_row_end; ++in_r)' enters with in_r = -100.
in_index is computed as (0 * 10 + (-100)) * 10 + 0 = -1000, which is a large negative number used as column index in the Eigen matrix.
in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat.coeffRef(d, -1000) accesses memory before the allocated buffer, causing an out-of-bounds write when the backprop value is added.

state.value: out-of-bounds write at negative offset from buffer start

vulnerabilities.description: There is no validation that out_depth == in_depth or out_batch == in_batch. The inner loop iterates up to out_depth, but in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat has in_depth rows. If out_depth > in_depth, coeffRef(d, in_index) accesses beyond the allocated rows of the matrix, causing out-of-bounds memory access.

step_by_step_execution.description: Attacker provides out_backprop with shape [1, 2, 2, 100] and orig_input_tensor_shape = [1, 10, 10, 1].
in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat is created with in_depth=1 rows and in_cols*in_rows*in_batch=100 columns.
The inner loop iterates d from 0 to 99 (out_depth=100). When d >= 1, coeffRef(d, in_index) accesses row d which is beyond the matrix's 1 row, resulting in out-of-bounds read/write.

state.value: 1 to 99 (out of bounds, matrix only has 1 row)

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.StagePeek vulnerability with invalid `index`.

Check that input is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445524908
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.StagePeek` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `index` is a scalar but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
Prevent segfault in `GetSessionHandle{,V2}`.

In eager mode, session state is null.

Hence, calling `tf.raw_ops.GetSessionHandle` or `tf.raw_ops.GetSessionHandleV2` results in a null pointer dereference In linked snippet, in eager mode, `ctx->session_state()` returns `nullptr`. Since code immediately dereferences this, we get a segmentation fault. The issue is patched in commit 9a133d73ae4b4664d22bd1aa6d654fec13c52ee1, and is released in TensorFlow versions 1.15.4, 2.0.3, 2.1.2, 2.2.1, or 2.3.1.

commit_message: fixed #1897
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the gf_text_get_utf8_line function in load_text.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

vulnerabilities.description: Stack-based buffer overflow in szLineConv. When unicode_type is 0 and lineSize > 2048, the conversion loop expands non-UTF8 high-bit bytes (one input byte produces two output bytes in szLineConv). Since szLineConv is only 2048 bytes but szLine can be up to lineSize bytes (potentially much larger), the index j can exceed 2048, writing past the end of the stack buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with lineSize=4096 and unicode_type=0. szLine is a buffer of 4096 bytes.
gf_fgets reads a line into szLine. The line contains alternating bytes like 0xA0, 0x20 repeated, totaling ~4000 characters.
unicode_type <= 1 is true, loop begins with i=0, j=0.

For each 0xA0 byte: (szLine[i] & 0x80) is true, (szLine[i+1] & 0xc0) = (0x20 & 0xc0) = 0x00 != 0x80, so we enter the first case. szLineConv[j] = 0xc0 | ((0xA0 >> 6) & 0x3) = 0xc2, j incremented. szLine[i] &= 0xbf = 0xA0 & 0xBF = 0xA0. Then falls through to szLineConv[j] = szLine[i], j incremented again. One input byte produces two output bytes.
For every pair of bytes (0xA0, 0x20), j increases by 3 (2 for the 0xA0 expansion + 1 for the 0x20). With ~2000 such pairs (4000 input bytes), j reaches ~6000.
When j exceeds 2048, writes to szLineConv[j] corrupt stack memory beyond the buffer boundary.

state.value: write past end of stack buffer

vulnerabilities.description: After the conversion loop, strcpy copies szLineConv back into szLine without bounds checking. If lineSize < 2048 (szLineConv's size), the converted content could be larger than szLine, causing a buffer overflow on szLine. Conversely, in the unicode path, gf_utf8_wcstombs could produce up to 2048 bytes which are copied to szLine regardless of lineSize.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a small lineSize (e.g., 256), unicode_type=2, and szLine buffer of 256 bytes.
gf_fgets reads up to 256 bytes of UTF-16 data into szLine.

state.value: UTF-16 encoded data, up to 256 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: The byte-swap loop processes the UTF-16 data, then gf_utf8_wcstombs converts it to UTF-8 into szLineConv (up to 2048 bytes). UTF-16 to UTF-8 conversion can expand data (e.g. characters above U+007F).

state.value: could be up to ~384 bytes for 128 UTF-16 chars that expand to 3-byte UTF-8

step_by_step_execution.description: strcpy(szLine, szLineConv) copies the converted string into szLine, which is only 256 bytes, causing a heap/stack buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Prevent a division by 0 in division ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385223169
Change-Id: Ia4228960b5d2aa44480385f74bdd70d21a3613c3
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of division in TFLite is [vulnerable to a division by 0 error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/div.cc). There is no check that the divisor tensor does not contain zero elements. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 1e206baedf8bef0334cca3eb92bab134ef525a28. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: fixed #2175
cve_desc: MP4Box is a component of GPAC-2.0.0, which is a widely-used third-party package on RPM Fusion. When MP4Box tries to parse a MP4 file, it calls the function `diST_box_read()` to read from video. In this function, it allocates a buffer `str` with fixed length. However, content read from `bs` is controllable by user, so is the length, which causes a buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: If s->size is the maximum value for its type (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF for u32 or SIZE_MAX), then s->size+1 overflows to 0. gf_malloc(0) may return a small or non-null pointer, and the subsequent gf_bs_read_data writes s->size bytes into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a GF_Box whose s->size field is attacker-controlled (derived from parsing a media file).

state.value: 0xFFFFFFFF (or another value that causes s->size+1 to overflow to 0)

step_by_step_execution.description: The allocation size is computed as sizeof(char) * (s->size+1). With s->size = 0xFFFFFFFF (32-bit), s->size+1 wraps to 0.
gf_malloc(0) is called. Many malloc implementations return a valid, small (e.g., 16-byte) heap pointer for a zero-size allocation.

state.value: pointer to a very small heap buffer (e.g., 16 bytes)

step_by_step_execution.description: The NULL check passes because the pointer is non-null.
gf_bs_read_data reads s->size (0xFFFFFFFF) bytes from the bitstream into the tiny buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

state.value: up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into a ~0-byte buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: p->content_script_types[s->size] = 0 writes a null terminator far out of bounds.

state.value: 0xFFFFFFFF past the start of the tiny buffer

This occurs whenever the size of `indices` does not match the size of `values`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.EditDistance vulnerability with negative indices.

Check that indices are non-negative. Fix several identical code sites.
Clean up grammar in error message.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445442017
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.EditDistance` has incomplete validation. Users can pass negative values to cause a segmentation fault based denial of service. In multiple places throughout the code, one may compute an index for a write operation. However, the existing validation only checks against the upper bound of the array. Hence, it is possible to write before the array by massaging the input to generate negative values for `loc`. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

code_snippets:
OP_REQUIRES(
            ctx, loc < output_elements,
            errors::Internal("Got an inner product ", loc,
                             " which would require in writing to outside of "
                             "the buffer for the output tensor (max elements ",
                             output_elements, ")"));
        output_t(loc) =
            gtl::LevenshteinDistance<T>(truth_seq, hypothesis_seq, cmp);
vulnerabilities.description: The bounds check on `loc` only verifies `loc < output_elements` but does not verify `loc >= 0`. Since `loc` is computed via `std::inner_product` over group indices (which originate from user-supplied sparse tensor indices) and output strides, a malicious user can supply negative indices in the sparse tensor, causing `loc` to be negative. A negative `loc` passes the `loc < output_elements` check but results in an out-of-bounds memory write when used to index `output_t`.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides sparse tensor inputs including hypothesis_indices and truth_indices. An attacker crafts indices with negative values in the group dimensions.

state.value: Contains rows with negative values in group dimensions, e.g. [[-1, 0]]
Contains rows with negative values in group dimensions, e.g. [[-1, 0]]

step_by_step_execution.description: ValidateShapes checks shapes of the inputs but does not validate that individual index values are non-negative.

hypothesis_st_shape and truth_st_shape are constructed from hypothesis_shape and truth_shape. output_shape is computed as the element-wise max of these shapes for all but the last dimension. output_strides are computed.
Iteration reaches a group with negative index values. g_truth or g_hypothesis contain negative values from the sparse tensor indices.
loc is computed via std::inner_product of the group vector (containing negative values) and output_strides. This yields a negative value.
The bounds check `loc < output_elements` passes because -1 < 2 is true. The check does not verify loc >= 0.
output_t(loc) is written with loc = -1, causing an out-of-bounds write before the start of the output buffer.

commit_message:
CVE-2022-21654

tls allows re-use when some cert validation settings have changed

Signed-off-by: Yan Avlasov <yavlasov@google.com>
cve_desc: Envoy is an open source edge and service proxy, designed for cloud-native applications. Envoy's tls allows re-use when some cert validation settings have changed from their default configuration. The only workaround for this issue is to ensure that default tls settings are used. Users are advised to upgrade.

commit_message:
Validate and require subkey binding signatures on PGP public keys

All subkeys must be followed by a binding signature by the primary key
as per the OpenPGP RFC, enforce the presence and validity in the parser.

The implementation is as kludgey as they come to work around our
simple-minded parser structure without touching API, to maximise
backportability. Store all the raw packets internally as we decode them
to be able to access previous elements at will, needed to validate ordering
and access the actual data. Add testcases for manipulated keys whose
import previously would succeed.

Depends on the two previous commits:
7b399fcb8f52566e6f3b4327197a85facd08db91 and
236b802a4aa48711823a191d1b7f753c82a89ec5

Fixes CVE-2021-3521.
cve_desc: There is a flaw in RPM's signature functionality. OpenPGP subkeys are associated with a primary key via a "binding signature." RPM does not check the binding signature of subkeys prior to importing them. If an attacker is able to add or socially engineer another party to add a malicious subkey to a legitimate public key, RPM could wrongly trust a malicious signature. The greatest impact of this flaw is to data integrity. To exploit this flaw, an attacker must either compromise an RPM repository or convince an administrator to install an untrusted RPM or public key. It is strongly recommended to only use RPMs and public keys from trusted sources.

commit_message: vm.c: check if target_class is NULL (when prepended).
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in mrb_obj_is_kind_of in in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. # Impact: Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &c, blk, 0);
vulnerabilities.description: In the OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler, the local variable `blk` is declared but never initialized before being used. It is passed to `prepare_missing()` when a method is not found, and later checked with `mrb_proc_p(blk)` after a C function call returns. Since `blk` contains stack garbage, this can lead to treating arbitrary memory as a valid mrb_value, potentially dereferencing invalid pointers.

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &c, blk, 0);

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution enters the OP_SENDB handler (or OP_SEND, OP_SSEND, OP_SSENDB which fall through to the same block). Local variables `recv` and `blk` are declared on the stack without initialization.
ARGUMENT_NORMALIZE is called, and `recv` is assigned from `regs[a]`. However `blk` remains uninitialized.
mrb_method_search_vm is called. If the method is not found (MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P(m) is true), `prepare_missing` is called with the uninitialized `blk` value as an argument.

If the method is a C function (MRB_METHOD_CFUNC_P(m)), after calling it and returning, the code checks `mrb_proc_p(blk)`. Since `blk` is uninitialized garbage, if the garbage type tag happens to match MRB_TT_PROC, the code will call `mrb_proc_ptr(blk)` and dereference the garbage pointer to access `p->flags`, `MRB_PROC_STRICT_P(p)`, and `MRB_PROC_ENV(p)`, leading to reads/writes on arbitrary memory.

state.value: uninitialized (stack garbage, may have type tag matching MRB_TT_PROC)
garbage pointer derived from uninitialized blk

commit_message: Fix for CVE-2015-2141. Thanks to Evgeny Sidorov for reporting. Squaring to satisfy Jacobi requirements suggested by JPM.
cve_desc: The InvertibleRWFunction::CalculateInverse function in rw.cpp in libcrypt++ 5.6.2 does not properly blind private key operations for the Rabin-Williams digital signature algorithm, which allows remote attackers to obtain private keys via a timing attack.

commit_message: add assert for addition overflow on queue creation (#225)
cve_desc: The kernel in Amazon Web Services FreeRTOS before 10.4.3 has an integer overflow in queue.c for queue creation.

code_snippets: pxNewQueue = ( Queue_t * ) pvPortMalloc( sizeof( Queue_t ) + xQueueSizeInBytes );
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the addition `sizeof(Queue_t) + xQueueSizeInBytes` when computing the allocation size for pvPortMalloc. The multiplication overflow is checked via configASSERT, but the subsequent addition of sizeof(Queue_t) is not checked. If xQueueSizeInBytes is large enough (close to SIZE_MAX), the addition wraps around to a small value, resulting in a small heap allocation. The queue then operates as if it has xQueueSizeInBytes bytes of storage, leading to a heap buffer overflow when items are enqueued.

code_snippets: pxNewQueue = ( Queue_t * ) pvPortMalloc( sizeof( Queue_t ) + xQueueSizeInBytes );

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with values that produce a large but non-overflowing product. For example on a 32-bit system: uxQueueLength=2, uxItemSize=0x7FFFFFF0.
xQueueSizeInBytes is computed as uxQueueLength * uxItemSize = 2 * 0x7FFFFFF0 = 0xFFFFFFE0. The multiplication does not overflow a 32-bit size_t.
The configASSERT multiplication overflow check passes because uxItemSize != 0 and xQueueSizeInBytes / uxItemSize == uxQueueLength (0xFFFFFFE0 / 0x7FFFFFF0 == 2).

pvPortMalloc is called with sizeof(Queue_t) + xQueueSizeInBytes. Assuming sizeof(Queue_t) is ~80 bytes, the addition is 80 + 0xFFFFFFE0 = 0x100000030, which wraps around to 0x30 (48 bytes) on a 32-bit system.

state.value: 0x30 (48 bytes) due to integer overflow wrap-around

step_by_step_execution.description: pvPortMalloc allocates only ~48 bytes. pucQueueStorage is set to point sizeof(Queue_t) bytes past the allocation, which is already out of bounds. prvInitialiseNewQueue is called with uxQueueLength=2 and uxItemSize=0x7FFFFFF0, configuring the queue to believe it has ~4GB of storage in a 48-byte buffer.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-p6g5-v97c-w5q4

* Prevent heap buffer overflow when parsing DNS packets

* Make sure packet parsing doesn't advance beyond max/end

* Update checks

* Remove  check

Co-authored-by: sauwming <ming@teluu.com>
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. A buffer overflow vulnerability in versions 2.12 and prior affects applications that use PJSIP DNS resolution. It doesn't affect PJSIP users who utilize an external resolver. This vulnerability is related to CVE-2023-27585. The difference is that this issue is in parsing the query record `parse_rr()`, while the issue in CVE-2023-27585 is in `parse_query()`. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. A workaround is to disable DNS resolution in PJSIP config (by setting `nameserver_count` to zero) or use an external resolver instead.

vulnerabilities.description: After parsing the DNS name, the code reads 4 bytes (2 for type, 2 for class) from pointer `p` without verifying that `p + 4 <= max`. If the packet is truncated such that it contains a valid DNS name but fewer than 4 bytes remaining afterwards, the `pj_memcpy` calls will read beyond the `max` boundary, causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a DNS packet where `max` points just past the end of valid data. The name portion is valid but is immediately followed by fewer than 4 bytes before `max`.

state.value: points to beginning of query section in packet
points to end of valid packet data, e.g. start + name_part_len + 1 (only 1 byte after name)

step_by_step_execution.description: `get_name_len` is called, which validates the name fits within [pkt, max). It returns successfully with `name_part_len` set to the number of bytes the name occupies in the packet.

state.value: length of encoded name in packet (valid, fits before max)

step_by_step_execution.description: Memory is allocated for the name and `get_name` successfully parses the name.
`p` is set to `start + name_part_len`, pointing just past the name in the packet.

state.value: start + name_part_len (e.g., max - 1, only 1 byte before max)

step_by_step_execution.description: `pj_memcpy(&q->type, p, 2)` reads 2 bytes from `p`. Since `p + 2 > max`, this reads beyond the valid buffer boundary, causing an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
Fix for bug #72790 and bug #72799

(cherry picked from commit a14fdb9746262549bbbb96abb87338bacd147e1b)

Conflicts:
	ext/wddx/wddx.c
cve_desc: ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid wddxPacket XML document that is mishandled in a wddx_deserialize call, as demonstrated by a stray element inside a boolean element, leading to incorrect pop processing.

vulnerabilities.description: If php_base64_decode returns NULL (e.g., due to invalid base64 input or allocation failure), the result is passed directly to ZVAL_STR without a NULL check. This causes a NULL pointer dereference when the zval is subsequently accessed (e.g., during the __wakeup call, or when merging into a parent element).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with an EL_BINARY closing element name. stack->top > 0.
ent1 is retrieved from the top of the stack. ent1->data is a string containing invalid or empty base64 data, so Z_TYPE(ent1->data) != IS_UNDEF.
The element name matches EL_BINARY, so php_base64_decode is called. It returns NULL because the input is invalid or causes a decode failure.
zval_ptr_dtor frees the old ent1->data string. Then ZVAL_STR sets ent1->data to the NULL zend_string pointer, creating a zval with a NULL string pointer.

state.value: zval with IS_STRING type but NULL zend_string pointer

step_by_step_execution.description: Subsequent code accesses ent1->data (e.g., the __wakeup check reads Z_TYPE, or the parent element merge dereferences the string), leading to a NULL pointer dereference or crash.

commit_message:
Fix for bug #72790 and bug #72799

(cherry picked from commit a14fdb9746262549bbbb96abb87338bacd147e1b)

Conflicts:
	ext/wddx/wddx.c
cve_desc: ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid wddxPacket XML document that is mishandled in a wddx_deserialize call, as demonstrated by a stray element inside a boolean element, leading to incorrect pop processing.

vulnerabilities.description: php_base64_decode can return NULL on failure. The return value is passed directly to ZVAL_STR without a NULL check, resulting in a NULL pointer dereference when the zval is subsequently accessed (e.g., during __wakeup call, hash insertion, or later destruction).

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with name matching EL_BINARY and stack->top > 0.
wddx_stack_top retrieves ent1 from the stack. ent1->data is a string (IS_STRING) containing invalid base64 data.
The Z_TYPE(ent1->data) == IS_UNDEF check passes (it's IS_STRING, not IS_UNDEF), so execution continues.
The name matches EL_BINARY, so php_base64_decode is called. With malformed input, it can return NULL.
zval_ptr_dtor frees the old string in ent1->data. Then ZVAL_STR sets ent1->data to hold the NULL zend_string pointer.

state.value: zval with IS_STRING type but NULL zend_string pointer

step_by_step_execution.description: Subsequent operations on ent1->data (e.g., the __wakeup check which calls Z_TYPE, or hash insertion which accesses the string value) will dereference the NULL pointer, causing a crash.

state.value: NULL zend_string pointer, any access is a NULL dereference

commit_message:
Fix potential divide by zero error when executing FractionalMaxPool, when pooling ratio is higher than input size for a particular dimension.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 412151722
Change-Id: I06e57cbb8eca43816eff79eac264fa7aae8f7163
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalMaxPool` can be made to crash a TensorFlow process via a division by 0. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
ConstEigenMatrixMap in_mat(tensor_in.flat<T>().data(), input_size[3],
                               input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]);

EigenMatrixMap out_mat(output_tensor->flat<T>().data(), output_size[3],
                           output_size[2] * output_size[1] * output_size[0]);
vulnerabilities.description: The number of columns for the Eigen matrix maps is computed by multiplying three `int` values together (`input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]` and similarly for output). These multiplications can overflow a 32-bit integer for large but valid tensor dimensions, resulting in an incorrect (potentially much smaller or negative) column count for the Eigen map. Subsequent `.col(offset)` accesses using correctly-computed `int64_t` offsets will then read/write out of the bounds indicated by the map, causing heap buffer over-read and over-write.

code_snippets:
ConstEigenMatrixMap in_mat(tensor_in.flat<T>().data(), input_size[3],
                               input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]);

step_by_step_execution.description: The input tensor has 4 dimensions. For example, shape [2, 32768, 32768, 1]. Each dimension passes the individual check `input_size[i] >= pooling_ratio_[i]`.
output_size is computed via floor(input_size[i] / pooling_ratio_[i]). With ratio 1.0, output_size equals input_size.

The Eigen matrix map for the input is constructed. The column count is `input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]` = 32768 * 32768 * 2. Since these are all `int` (32-bit), the multiplication 32768 * 32768 = 1073741824, then * 2 = 2147483648 which overflows signed int32 (max 2147483647), causing undefined behavior / wrap-around.

state.value: overflows int32, e.g. wraps to -2147483648 or other incorrect value

step_by_step_execution.description: The ConstEigenMatrixMap `in_mat` is constructed with an incorrect (overflowed) number of columns. The same issue applies to `out_mat`.

In the nested loop, `in_offset` and `out_offset` are computed as int64_t and can be large correct values. When `in_mat.col(in_offset)` or `out_mat.col(out_offset)` is accessed, the offset exceeds the (incorrectly small) column count of the Eigen map, leading to out-of-bounds heap memory read and write.

commit_message:
Secure tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2

Validate size and shape of min_range and max_range
Ensure axis is within input dims limits

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387232799
Change-Id: I36975281f7b5758e9e31a8dcc73fe610ef456318
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2`, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/quantize_op.cc#L59) has some validation but does not check that `min_range` and `max_range` both have the same non-zero number of elements. If `axis` is provided (i.e., not `-1`), then validation should check that it is a value in range for the rank of `input` tensor and then the lengths of `min_range` and `max_range` inputs match the `axis` dimension of the `input` tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 6da6620efad397c85493b8f8667b821403516708. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
avformat/adtsenc: return value check for init_get_bits in adts_decode_extradata

As the second argument for init_get_bits (buf) can be crafted, a return value check for this function call is necessary.
'buf' is  part of  'AVPacket pkt'.
replace init_get_bits with init_get_bits8.

Signed-off-by: Michael Niedermayer <michael@niedermayer.cc>
cve_desc: adts_decode_extradata in libavformat/adtsenc.c in FFmpeg 4.4 does not check the init_get_bits return value, which is a necessary step because the second argument to init_get_bits can be crafted.
