commit_message:
Prevent nullptr deref in SparseTensorSliceDataset

The arguments must determine a valid sparse tensor. This means that when indices are empty then the values must be empty too (and the reverse).

Also added test, by modifying existing test with empty sparse tensor to now run with an invalid sparse tensor input.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388562757
Change-Id: Id8b54cd7c2316025b4f9a77292c8fb5344d17609
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When a user does not supply arguments that determine a valid sparse tensor, `tf.raw_ops.SparseTensorSliceDataset` implementation can be made to dereference a null pointer. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L240-L251) has some argument validation but fails to consider the case when either `indices` or `values` are provided for an empty sparse tensor when the other is not. If `indices` is empty, then [code that performs validation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L260-L261) (i.e., checking that the indices are monotonically increasing) results in a null pointer dereference. If `indices` as provided by the user is empty, then `indices` in the C++ code above is backed by an empty `std::vector`, hence calling `indices->dim_size(0)` results in null pointer dereferencing (same as calling `std::vector::at()` on an empty vector). We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 02cc160e29d20631de3859c6653184e3f876b9d7. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when the `indices` tensor is a matrix with shape [N, 0] (N > 0, 0 columns). The NumElements check (num_indices == 0) passes along with (num_values == 0), but the loop iterates based on dim_size(0) which is N, causing an access to column 0 of a 0-column matrix.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `indices` input tensor is obtained. An attacker provides a tensor with shape [N, 0] where N > 0 (e.g. [5, 0]).
The `values` input tensor is obtained. An attacker provides a vector with shape [0].
The IsMatrix check on indices passes because it has 2 dimensions.
num_indices and num_values are computed. Since indices has shape [N, 0], num_indices = N*0 = 0. Values has shape [0], so num_values = 0.
The emptiness check passes because both num_indices and num_values are 0.
IsVector check on values passes. IsVector check on dense_shape passes.
The loop starts with i=0. The loop condition is i < indices->dim_size(0), which is i < N (e.g. i < 5), so the loop body executes.
Inside the loop, `indices->matrix<int64>()(i, 0)` accesses row i, column 0 of the matrix. However, the matrix has 0 columns, resulting in an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message: fixed #2163
cve_desc: GPAC 2.1-DEV-rev87-g053aae8-master. has a Null Pointer Dereference vulnerability in gf_isom_parse_movie_boxes_internal due to improper return value handling of GF_SKIP_BOX, which causes a Denial of Service. This vulnerability was fixed in commit 37592ad.

vulnerabilities.description: When processing a MOOV box outside of debug mode, the code iterates over tracks and dereferences trak->Media->information->sampleTable without any NULL checks. A malformed ISO file could contain a track box where Media, information, or sampleTable is NULL, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: gf_isom_parse_root_box parses a top-level box and returns a MOOV box with e >= 0. The MOOV box contains a track with incomplete sub-boxes (e.g., Media is NULL, or Media->information is NULL, or sampleTable is NULL).
The code enters the GF_ISOM_BOX_TYPE_MOOV case, sets mov->moov, passes the mvhd check, and reaches the else branch (non-debug mode).
The code iterates over mov->moov->trackList. For a track at index k, it retrieves a GF_TrackBox pointer.

state.value: a track box from the malformed MOOV, with trak->Media == NULL or trak->Media->information == NULL or trak->Media->information->sampleTable == NULL

step_by_step_execution.description: The code dereferences trak->Media->information->sampleTable->sampleGroups without any NULL checks, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

state.value: NULL (or any intermediate pointer is NULL)

vulnerabilities.description: After processing a MOOF box, the code checks mov->root_ssix but deletes mov->seg_ssix. This is a likely copy-paste bug: (1) mov->root_ssix is never actually freed, causing a memory leak, and (2) if root_ssix is non-NULL but seg_ssix is NULL, a NULL pointer is passed to gf_isom_box_del. Additionally, if seg_ssix was already freed earlier, this constitutes a use-after-free or double-free.

step_by_step_execution.description: A SIDX/SSIX box is parsed with signal_frag_bounds set. The code sets mov->seg_ssix to a box or it may remain NULL. Separately, mov->root_ssix could have been set.
A MOOF box is parsed and processed. After MOOF handling, the code checks mov->root_ssix, finds it non-NULL, and calls gf_isom_box_del(mov->seg_ssix).
gf_isom_box_del is called with mov->seg_ssix (which may be NULL), then mov->root_ssix is set to NULL without being freed, leaking the memory.

state.value: set to NULL without freeing the pointed-to memory

commit_message: Fix overflow of triggered topics
cve_desc: uWebSockets 18.11.0 and 18.12.0 has a stack-based buffer overflow in uWS::TopicTree::trimTree (called from uWS::TopicTree::unsubscribeAll). NOTE: the vendor's position is that this is "a minor issue or not even an issue at all" because the developer of an application (that uses uWebSockets) should not be allowing the large number of triggered topics to accumulate

commit_message: fixed #2163
cve_desc: GPAC 2.1-DEV-rev87-g053aae8-master. has a Null Pointer Dereference vulnerability in gf_isom_parse_movie_boxes_internal due to improper return value handling of GF_SKIP_BOX, which causes a Denial of Service. This vulnerability was fixed in commit 37592ad.

vulnerabilities.description: In the MOOF cleanup code, when `mov->root_ssix` is non-NULL, the code incorrectly deletes `mov->seg_ssix` instead of `mov->root_ssix`. This frees `mov->seg_ssix` without setting it to NULL, causing a use-after-free if `seg_ssix` is later accessed (e.g., in a subsequent SIDX/SSIX box handling). Additionally, `root_ssix` itself is leaked.

step_by_step_execution.description: A SIDX or SSIX box is parsed with signal_frag_bounds set, causing seg_ssix to be assigned.

state.value: pointer to a valid GF_Box (from SSIX parsing)

step_by_step_execution.description: A MOOF box is parsed. During the MOOF cleanup, the code checks if mov->root_ssix is non-NULL.

state.value: non-NULL pointer to a valid object
non-NULL pointer to a valid GF_Box

step_by_step_execution.description: The code calls gf_isom_box_del(mov->seg_ssix) instead of gf_isom_box_del(mov->root_ssix). This frees the seg_ssix object.
mov->root_ssix is set to NULL, but mov->seg_ssix remains a dangling pointer.

On a subsequent iteration parsing another SSIX box in the SIDX/SSIX case, the code checks `if (mov->seg_ssix) gf_isom_box_del(mov->seg_ssix);` which performs a double-free on the already freed seg_ssix pointer.

vulnerabilities.description: When processing the MOOV box outside of debug mode, the code dereferences a chain of pointers (trak->Media->information->sampleTable) without null checks. A malformed file could have a track with a NULL Media, information, or sampleTable pointer, leading to a null pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: A MOOV box is parsed from a crafted/malformed ISO file. The moov contains tracks in its trackList.
The code iterates over trackList. A track box (trak) is retrieved that has a NULL or malformed Media pointer (e.g., trak->Media is NULL).
The code dereferences trak->Media->information->sampleTable->sampleGroups without any null checks, causing a null pointer dereference / crash.

commit_message: fixed #1659
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in GPAC version 0.8.0 and 1.0.1. There is an invalid pointer dereference in the function SetupWriters() in isomedia/isom_store.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When allocation of writer->stsc or writer->stco fails, the function returns GF_OUT_OF_MEM directly instead of jumping to the 'exit' label. This leaks the current 'writer' object (and its already-allocated members like stsc), as well as all TrackWriter objects previously added to the 'writers' list, since CleanWriters is never called.

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop iterates over tracks. On some iteration i, a TrackWriter 'writer' is successfully allocated via GF_SAFEALLOC.
writer->stsc is allocated via gf_isom_box_new. Suppose this succeeds.
writer->stco is allocated via gf_isom_box_new. Suppose this returns NULL (allocation failure).

The code executes 'return GF_OUT_OF_MEM;' directly, bypassing the 'exit' label which would call CleanWriters(writers). The current 'writer' (and its allocated stsc) is leaked. All previously added TrackWriter objects in 'writers' list are also leaked.

state.value: list containing previously allocated TrackWriters that are never freed
leaked heap allocation with leaked writer->stsc

commit_message:
Fix a null pointer exception caused by branching on uninitialized data.

This is due to not checking that the params for the quantization exists. If there is no quantization, we should not access the `.params` field.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385163909
Change-Id: I2beb8d50649b6542db224c163033fbcbaa49314f
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions all TFLite operations that use quantization can be made to use unitialized values. [For example](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/depthwise_conv.cc#L198-L200). The issue stems from the fact that `quantization.params` is only valid if `quantization.type` is different that `kTfLiteNoQuantization`. However, these checks are missing in large parts of the code. We have patched the issue in GitHub commits 537bc7c723439b9194a358f64d871dd326c18887, 4a91f2069f7145aab6ba2d8cfe41be8a110c18a5 and 8933b8a21280696ab119b63263babdb54c298538. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fix segmentation fault in shape inference logic.

When running shape functions, some functions (such as `MutableHashTableShape`)
produce extra output information in the form of a `ShapeAndType` struct.  The
shapes embedded in this struct are owned by an inference context that is
cleaned up almost immediately; if the upstream code attempts to access this
shape information, it can trigger a segfault.

`ShapeRefiner` is mitigating this for normal output shapes by cloning them
(and thus putting the newly created shape under ownership of an inference
context that will not die), but we were not doing the same for shapes and
types.  This commit fixes that by doing similar logic on output shapes and
types.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 384761124
Change-Id: I07c0c42d29dfbb55bfa13ec1f09ef825fb0a1a1d
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions when running shape functions, some functions (such as `MutableHashTableShape`) produce extra output information in the form of a `ShapeAndType` struct. The shapes embedded in this struct are owned by an inference context that is cleaned up almost immediately; if the upstream code attempts to access this shape information, it can trigger a segfault. `ShapeRefiner` is mitigating this for normal output shapes by cloning them (and thus putting the newly created shape under ownership of an inference context that will not die), but we were not doing the same for shapes and types. This commit fixes that by doing similar logic on output shapes and types. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit ee119d4a498979525046fba1c3dd3f13a039fbb1. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4219: reading before the start of the line

Problem:    Reading before the start of the line.
Solution:   Check boundary before trying to read the character.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: The while-loop condition checks `VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + s - 1))` before checking `s > 0`. Due to C's left-to-right short-circuit evaluation of `&&`, when `s` reaches 0 (or starts at 0 when `bd->textlen` is 0), the code reads `*(bd->textstart - 1)`, which is an out-of-bounds read. The `s > 0` guard is on the wrong side of the `&&` operator.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `exclude_trailing_space` set to a non-zero (true) value.
`bd->endspaces` is set to 0 because `exclude_trailing_space` is true.
Memory is allocated and text is copied. Consider the case where `bd->textlen` is 0 (empty block selection).
The trailing-space removal block is entered. `s` is computed as `bd->textlen + bd->endspaces`.

The while-loop condition is evaluated left-to-right: `VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + s - 1))` is evaluated first, which dereferences `bd->textstart - 1`. This is an out-of-bounds read one byte before the text buffer. The `s > 0` check that would prevent this comes second and is never reached.

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.slice() with slow "this" argument.

Previously, when "this" argument was not a fast array, but the "deleted" array
was a fast array, the "deleted" array may be left in uninitialized state if
"this" argument had gaps.

This fix is to ensure that "deleted" is properly initialized.

This fixes #485 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_value_own_enumerate at src/njs_value.c.

commit_message: fixed #1782 (fuzz)
cve_desc: Memory leak in the afra_box_read function in MP4Box in GPAC 1.0.1 allows attackers to read memory via a crafted file.

commit_message: fixed #1782 (fuzz)

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.EditDistance vulnerability with negative indices.

Check that indices are non-negative. Fix several identical code sites.
Clean up grammar in error message.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445442017
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.EditDistance` has incomplete validation. Users can pass negative values to cause a segmentation fault based denial of service. In multiple places throughout the code, one may compute an index for a write operation. However, the existing validation only checks against the upper bound of the array. Hence, it is possible to write before the array by massaging the input to generate negative values for `loc`. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: When the input sparse tensors have exactly 1 dimension, `group_dims` has size 0, `output_shape` ends up with 0 dimensions, and `output_strides` is an empty vector. The line `output_strides[output_shape.dims() - 1]` then evaluates to `output_strides[-1]` (i.e., `output_strides[SIZE_MAX]` after unsigned conversion), causing an out-of-bounds memory write.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input sparse tensors are read and validated via ValidateShapes (which does not enforce a minimum number of dimensions > 1).

state.value: A 1-D tensor, e.g. [5], meaning the sparse tensor is 1-dimensional

step_by_step_execution.description: truth_st_shape is constructed from truth_shape, resulting in a shape with 1 dimension.
group_dims is created with size truth_st_shape.dims() - 1 = 0.
The loop building output_shape iterates 0 times (since group_dims is empty), leaving output_shape as a scalar shape with 0 dimensions.
output_strides is created as an empty vector with 0 elements.

The code attempts to write output_strides[output_shape.dims() - 1] = output_strides[0 - 1]. Since output_shape.dims() is 0, the index is -1 which, when cast to size_t, becomes SIZE_MAX, causing an out-of-bounds write on the empty vector.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.DeleteSessionTensor vulnerability with invalid `handle`.

Check that `handle` input is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445228994
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.DeleteSessionTensor` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message:

commit_message:
Add more validation to `RequantizationRangePerChannel`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387693946
Change-Id: Ife8dcbdb021bec4787eef6a4361dd08f17c14bd6
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable 'depth' is derived from input_max.NumElements(), but there is no check that this value matches the inner dimension of the 'input' tensor. If an attacker provides an input_max tensor with more elements than the inner dimension of the input tensor, the loop will iterate out of bounds when calling transposed_input.chip<0>(i), leading to an out-of-bounds read on the input tensor's data.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads the input tensors from the OpKernelContext.

state.value: A tensor with some shape, e.g., shape [2, 3] so inner dim = 3
A tensor with more elements than input's inner dim, e.g., NumElements() = 10
A tensor also with 10 elements (to pass the check)

step_by_step_execution.description: depth is set to input_max.NumElements().
The checks verify input_min.dim_size(0) == depth and input_max.dim_size(0) == depth. Both pass since both have 10 elements. No check relates depth to the input tensor's inner dimension.
input_matrix is created via input.flat_inner_dims<qint32>(), yielding a 2D view. transposed_input is its shuffle (transpose). The transposed shape's first dimension equals input's inner dim (3).
The loop iterates for i from 0 to depth-1 (0..9). When i >= 3, transposed_input.chip<0>(i) accesses beyond the valid range of the first dimension (size 3), resulting in an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: 3 to 9 (out of bounds, valid range is 0-2)

commit_message:
Reorganize and add more validation to MKL requantization

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387901341
Change-Id: I2515b9034c64e113db0bcec8337d30643ab0a0f1
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: If both input_requested_min_float and input_requested_max_float are 0.0f, then requested_min_max will be 0.0f, leading to a floating-point division by zero when computing scales[i]. There is no check that requested_min_max is non-zero before using it as a divisor.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads input_requested_min and input_requested_max tensors from the context.
requested_min_max is computed as max(abs(0.0f), abs(0.0f)) = 0.0f

The loop computes scales[i] which divides min_max_from_vec by requested_min_max (0.0f), resulting in a division by zero producing inf or NaN values. These are then passed to MKL-DNN reorder which may produce undefined behavior or crash.

state.value: inf or NaN due to division by zero

Fix FPE issue with `tf.raw_ops.Reverse`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 371176973
Change-Id: Ic6d483bfc95313ec2299c2d1c956cfe96c96626c
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. An attacker can cause a denial of service via a FPE runtime error in `tf.raw_ops.Reverse`. This is because the implementation(https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/36229ea9e9451dac14a8b1f4711c435a1d84a594/tensorflow/core/kernels/reverse_op.cc#L75-L76) performs a division based on the first dimension of the tensor argument. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.5.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.4.2, TensorFlow 2.3.3, TensorFlow 2.2.3 and TensorFlow 2.1.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
string_number_format: Correctly handles return value of snprintf

Summary: `snprintf` can return a value greater than the number of bytes copied. In case the first byte of the string is not a digit (could be '-'), size of `tmpstr` was being updated without checking `tmplen`. This resulted in either an assertion error or a heap overflow depending on whether the assertion is compiled or not.

Reviewed By: mofarrell, qianxuweiren

Differential Revision: D17327899

fbshipit-source-id: ee53875d21e02608c6d870388eecf1464de24ff1
cve_desc: Insufficient boundary checks when formatting numbers in number_format allows read/write access to out-of-bounds memory, potentially leading to remote code execution. This issue affects HHVM versions prior to 3.30.10, all versions between 4.0.0 and 4.8.5, all versions between 4.9.0 and 4.18.2, and versions 4.19.0, 4.19.1, 4.20.0, 4.20.1, 4.20.2, 4.21.0, 4.22.0, 4.23.0.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in buffer size calculation. The overflow checks compare mixed signed int and unsigned size_t values, so they only detect size_t overflow. However, the results are stored into int variables (integral, reslen). A sufficiently large thousand_sep or dec_point string can cause the sum to exceed INT_MAX without triggering the size_t-based overflow check, resulting in the int wrapping to a small or negative value. This leads to a too-small buffer allocation followed by out-of-bounds heap writes in the subsequent memcpy/copy loops.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a number d that has many integral digits (e.g., d = 1e308) and a very large thousand_sep string (e.g., size ~21 million bytes), dec=0.
After snprintf, tmplen is around 309, and integral is set to tmplen (since dec=0, dp is nullptr).

Overflow check is performed: integral + thousand_sep.size() * ((integral-1) / 3). The computation is done in size_t due to promotion. (309-1)/3 = 102, 102 * 21000000 = 2142000000. 309 + 2142000000 = 2142000309. This is > 309 (unsigned comparison), so the overflow check passes without raising an error.

state.value: ~2142000000 (fits in size_t, but exceeds INT_MAX ~2147483647 is close)

step_by_step_execution.description: The assignment integral += ((integral-1) / 3) * thousand_sep.size() truncates the size_t result to int. If the product is slightly above INT_MAX (e.g., thousand_sep.size()=21100000, product=102*21100000=2152200000 > 2147483647), this overflows the int, wrapping integral to a small or negative value.

state.value: Overflowed int, e.g. a small negative or small positive number

step_by_step_execution.description: A String buffer of size reslen is allocated. Because reslen overflowed to a small value, the buffer is tiny (or the allocation behavior is undefined for negative sizes).

state.value: Points to a very small heap buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: The copy loop writes thousand_sep data via memcpy into the buffer. Each separator write copies ~21MB. Since the allocated buffer is tiny, this results in a massive heap buffer overflow.

state.value: Pointer goes far before resbuf start

commit_message: Skip MNG CLIP chunk with out-of-range object IDs
cve_desc: In ImageMagick before 6.9.9-0 and 7.x before 7.0.6-1, the ReadOneMNGImage function in coders/png.c has an out-of-bounds read with the MNG CLIP chunk.

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_LOOP chunk handler, `loop_level` is read directly from `chunk[0]` (an unsigned char, range 0-255) without any bounds checking against the size of the `loop_active`, `loop_jump`, `loop_count`, and `loop_iteration` arrays in the MngInfo structure. If the array sizes are smaller than 256, an attacker can craft a MNG file with a LOOP chunk whose first byte causes an out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads a MNG file and enters the main do-while loop to process chunks.
A chunk of type mng_LOOP is read with length > 4.
loop_level is assigned from chunk[0], which is an attacker-controlled byte value in range 0-255.
mng_info->loop_active[loop_level] is written to with value 1, with no bounds check on loop_level. If loop_level exceeds the array bounds, this is an out-of-bounds write.
Similarly, mng_info->loop_jump[loop_level], loop_count[loop_level], and loop_iteration[loop_level] are written without bounds checks.

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_ENDL chunk handler, `loop_level` is also read from `chunk[0]` without bounds checking, leading to out-of-bounds read/write on `mng_info->loop_active[]`, `loop_count[]`, and `loop_iteration[]`.

step_by_step_execution.description: A chunk of type mng_ENDL is read with length > 0.
loop_level is assigned from chunk[0], an attacker-controlled byte (0-255), with no bounds check.
mng_info->loop_active[loop_level] is read and/or written without bounds checking, causing out-of-bounds access.

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_DEFI chunk handler, when object_id exceeds MNG_MAX_OBJECTS it is clamped to MNG_MAX_OBJECTS (not MNG_MAX_OBJECTS-1). Since arrays like mng_info->exists[] are typically sized MNG_MAX_OBJECTS, indexing with MNG_MAX_OBJECTS is an off-by-one out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: A DEFI chunk is read with length >= 2.
object_id is computed as (p[0] << 8) | p[1], potentially up to 65535.
If object_id > MNG_MAX_OBJECTS, it is clamped to MNG_MAX_OBJECTS (not MNG_MAX_OBJECTS-1).
mng_info->exists[object_id] is set, but since arrays are sized MNG_MAX_OBJECTS (valid indices 0..MNG_MAX_OBJECTS-1), index MNG_MAX_OBJECTS is one past the end.

commit_message:
Fix NPE in restoring code.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388303253
Change-Id: Ia8c68568cb854bca538909a182b31a618d68ce55
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When restoring tensors via raw APIs, if the tensor name is not provided, TensorFlow can be tricked into dereferencing a null pointer. Alternatively, attackers can read memory outside the bounds of heap allocated data by providing some tensor names but not enough for a successful restoration. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/47a06f40411a69c99f381495f490536972152ac0/tensorflow/core/kernels/save_restore_tensor.cc#L158-L159) retrieves the tensor list corresponding to the `tensor_name` user controlled input and immediately retrieves the tensor at the restoration index (controlled via `preferred_shard` argument). This occurs without validating that the provided list has enough values. If the list is empty this results in dereferencing a null pointer (undefined behavior). If, however, the list has some elements, if the restoration index is outside the bounds this results in heap OOB read. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e82dce6e6bd1f36a57e08fa85af213e2b2f2622. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
crypto: ccp - fix resource leaks in ccp_run_aes_gcm_cmd()

There are three bugs in this code:

1) If we ccp_init_data() fails for &src then we need to free aad.
   Use goto e_aad instead of goto e_ctx.
2) The label to free the &final_wa was named incorrectly as "e_tag" but
   it should have been "e_final_wa".  One error path leaked &final_wa.
3) The &tag was leaked on one error path.  In that case, I added a free
   before the goto because the resource was local to that block.

Fixes: 36cf515b9bbe ("crypto: ccp - Enable support for AES GCM on v5 CCPs")
Reported-by: "minihanshen(沈明航)" <minihanshen@tencent.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: John Allen <john.allen@amd.com>
Tested-by: John Allen <john.allen@amd.com>
Signed-off-by: Herbert Xu <herbert@gondor.apana.org.au>
cve_desc: A memory leak flaw was found in the Linux kernel in the ccp_run_aes_gcm_cmd() function in drivers/crypto/ccp/ccp-ops.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption). This vulnerability is similar with the older CVE-2019-18808.

vulnerabilities.description: When the AES action is decrypt, `ilen` is computed as `aes->src_len - authsize` with both being unsigned integers. If `aes->src_len` is less than `authsize`, this subtraction wraps around to a very large value (close to UINT_MAX). This corrupted `ilen` is subsequently used in `scatterwalk_ffwd`, `ccp_init_data`, and other memory operations, leading to out-of-bounds memory access.

code_snippets: ret = ccp_init_data(&src, cmd_q, p_inp, ilen, AES_BLOCK_SIZE, in_place ? DMA_BIDIRECTIONAL : DMA_TO_DEVICE);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function validates key length and IV presence but does not validate that aes->src_len >= authsize.

state.value: e.g., 0 (defaults to 16) or any valid authsize like 16

step_by_step_execution.description: authsize is computed. If aes->authsize is 0, it defaults to AES_BLOCK_SIZE (16). Otherwise it is validated to be one of {4, 8, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16}.
Since aes->action == CCP_AES_ACTION_DECRYPT (attacker-controlled path), ilen is computed as aes->src_len - authsize. With aes->src_len = 0 and authsize = 16, this unsigned subtraction wraps around.
scatterwalk_ffwd is called with the wrapped ilen value to compute p_tag, potentially walking past the end of the scatterlist buffer.
Later, since ilen > 0 evaluates to true (huge unsigned value), ccp_init_data is called with this enormous length, causing out-of-bounds DMA setup or memory access.

state.value: initialized with corrupted length, leading to out-of-bounds access

commit_message:
Validate and require subkey binding signatures on PGP public keys

All subkeys must be followed by a binding signature by the primary key
as per the OpenPGP RFC, enforce the presence and validity in the parser.

The implementation is as kludgey as they come to work around our
simple-minded parser structure without touching API, to maximise
backportability. Store all the raw packets internally as we decode them
to be able to access previous elements at will, needed to validate ordering
and access the actual data. Add testcases for manipulated keys whose
import previously would succeed.

Depends on the two previous commits:
7b399fcb8f52566e6f3b4327197a85facd08db91 and
236b802a4aa48711823a191d1b7f753c82a89ec5

Fixes CVE-2021-3521.
cve_desc: There is a flaw in RPM's signature functionality. OpenPGP subkeys are associated with a primary key via a "binding signature." RPM does not check the binding signature of subkeys prior to importing them. If an attacker is able to add or socially engineer another party to add a malicious subkey to a legitimate public key, RPM could wrongly trust a malicious signature. The greatest impact of this flaw is to data integrity. To exploit this flaw, an attacker must either compromise an RPM repository or convince an administrator to install an untrusted RPM or public key. It is strongly recommended to only use RPMs and public keys from trusted sources.

commit_message:
Fixed typo while calculating module path length.

The issue was introduced in 77c398f26d7e (not released yet).
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.3 was discovered to contain a stack overflow in the function njs_default_module_loader at /src/njs/src/njs_module.c. NOTE: multiple third parties dispute this report, e.g., the behavior is only found in unreleased development code that was not part of the 0.7.2, 0.7.3, or 0.7.4 release

vulnerabilities.description: When `dir` is not NULL, `length` is set to `dir->length` (plus an optional trailing slash), but `info->name.length` is not added to `length` before the bounds check against NJS_MAX_PATH. The subsequent copies write `dir->length + trail + info->name.length` bytes into `src`, which is only `NJS_MAX_PATH + 1` bytes. This allows a stack buffer overflow if `dir->length + info->name.length` exceeds NJS_MAX_PATH.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a non-NULL `dir` argument. `trail` is initialized to 0 and `length` is set to `info->name.length`.
Since `dir != NULL`, `length` is reassigned to `dir->length`. Suppose `dir->length` is 100 and the last character is not '/', so `trail` becomes 1 and `length` becomes 101.
The bounds check `length > NJS_MAX_PATH` is evaluated. With NJS_MAX_PATH typically 1024, length (101) passes the check. However, `info->name.length` (e.g. 1000) is not included in this check.
dir content (100 bytes) and trailing slash are copied into `src`, advancing `p` by 101 bytes.

`njs_cpymem` copies `info->name.length` (1000) bytes starting at `src[101]`, writing up to `src[1100]`. Since `src` is only `NJS_MAX_PATH + 1` (1025) bytes, this overflows the stack buffer by 76 bytes.

commit_message: fixed #2212
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository gpac/gpac prior to 2.1-DEV.

commit_message:
Fix potential divide by zero error when executing FractionalMaxPool, when pooling ratio is higher than input size for a particular dimension.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 412151722
Change-Id: I06e57cbb8eca43816eff79eac264fa7aae8f7163
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalMaxPool` can be made to crash a TensorFlow process via a division by 0. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
ConstEigenMatrixMap in_mat(tensor_in.flat<T>().data(), input_size[3],
                               input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]);

EigenMatrixMap out_mat(output_tensor->flat<T>().data(), output_size[3],
                           output_size[2] * output_size[1] * output_size[0]);
vulnerabilities.description: The Eigen matrix maps are constructed with column counts computed as a product of three `int` values. When input dimensions are large (e.g., batch=2, height=40000, width=40000), the multiplication `input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]` overflows `int`, producing a small or negative wrapped value. The later loop computes `in_offset` using `int64_t` arithmetic which yields the correct large offset. Accessing `in_mat.col(in_offset)` with an offset exceeding the overflowed column count results in a heap-based out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: tensor_in is read with 4 dimensions. Suppose its shape is [2, 40000, 40000, 3].
output_size is computed by dividing each input dimension by pooling_ratio. With typical pooling ratios (e.g., 1.0 for batch/depth, ~1.5 for spatial), output sizes remain large.
DCHECK_GT(output_size[i], 0) is a debug-only assertion, so no runtime check prevents proceeding.

The column count for in_mat is computed as input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0] using int arithmetic: 40000 * 40000 * 2 = 3,200,000,000 which overflows int32 (max ~2.1 billion), wrapping to a small or negative value.
ConstEigenMatrixMap in_mat is created with the overflowed column count, creating a map over a much smaller (or invalid) region of memory than the actual tensor data.
In the nested loop, in_offset is computed with int64_t arithmetic: (b * input_size[1] + h) * input_size[2] + w, which correctly yields large values (e.g., up to ~3.2 billion).
in_mat.col(in_offset) is called with in_offset exceeding the Eigen map's (overflowed) column count, causing an out-of-bounds memory access on the heap.

vulnerabilities.description: DCHECK_GT is a debug-only check. In release/production builds, if pooling_ratio_[i] is sufficiently large (e.g., greater than input_size[i]), output_size[i] becomes 0. This leads to allocation of a zero-size output tensor, while loop bounds derived from `height_cum_seq.size() - 1` (unsigned subtraction) could wrap around, causing massive out-of-bounds memory access.

step_by_step_execution.description: An attacker provides a tensor_in with small spatial dimensions (e.g., shape [1,1,1,1]) and pooling_ratio values > 1 for those dimensions (e.g., pooling_ratio_[1] = 2.0).
output_size[1] = floor(1 / 2.0) = 0. DCHECK_GT does not fire in release builds.
GeneratePoolingSequence is called with output_size=0. If it returns an empty vector, then height_cum_seq.size() = 0.
The loop condition `hs < height_cum_seq.size() - 1` computes 0 - 1 on an unsigned type (size_t), wrapping to SIZE_MAX (~18 quintillion). The loop iterates massively out of bounds.
Accessing height_cum_seq[hs] and height_cum_seq[hs+1] with hs values far exceeding vector size causes out-of-bounds heap reads. Computed offsets for out_mat lead to out-of-bounds heap writes.

commit_message: fixed #1659
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in GPAC version 0.8.0 and 1.0.1. There is an invalid pointer dereference in the function SetupWriters() in isomedia/isom_store.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When allocation of writer->stsc or writer->stco fails, the function returns GF_OUT_OF_MEM directly without freeing the already-allocated 'writer' object (and writer->stsc if it succeeded). Additionally, any TrackWriter objects already added to the 'writers' list in previous loop iterations are not cleaned up (CleanWriters is not called). This contrasts with the GF_SAFEALLOC failure path which correctly jumps to 'exit' to call CleanWriters.

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop begins iterating over tracks. On the first iteration (i=0), a TrackWriter is successfully allocated and added to the 'writers' list.
On the second iteration (i=1), GF_SAFEALLOC successfully allocates a new TrackWriter for 'writer'.
writer->stsc is allocated via gf_isom_box_new(GF_ISOM_BOX_TYPE_STSC) and succeeds.
gf_isom_box_new for writer->stco fails and returns NULL (e.g., under memory pressure).

The check '!writer->stco' is true, so the function executes 'return GF_OUT_OF_MEM' directly, bypassing the 'exit' label. The current 'writer' (and its writer->stsc) is leaked, and the previously added TrackWriter in the 'writers' list is also not cleaned up via CleanWriters.

state.value: contains 1 TrackWriter from previous iteration, also leaked

commit_message: Skip MNG CLIP chunk with out-of-range object IDs
cve_desc: In ImageMagick before 6.9.9-0 and 7.x before 7.0.6-1, the ReadOneMNGImage function in coders/png.c has an out-of-bounds read with the MNG CLIP chunk.

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_LOOP chunk handler, `loop_level` is read directly from user-controlled data (`chunk[0]`, a byte value 0-255) without any bounds checking. This value is then used as an index into `mng_info->loop_active`, `mng_info->loop_jump`, `mng_info->loop_count`, and `mng_info->loop_iteration` arrays. If these arrays are smaller than 256 elements, an out-of-bounds write occurs. The same issue exists in the mng_ENDL handler.

step_by_step_execution.description: The image format is MNG, so the MNG chunk-reading loop is entered.
A chunk with type mng_LOOP is read. The length is checked to be > 4.
loop_level is set from chunk[0], which is a raw byte from user input with no bounds check.
mng_info->loop_active[loop_level] is written to with value 1, causing an out-of-bounds array write if loop_level exceeds the array size.
Similarly, mng_info->loop_jump[loop_level], loop_count[loop_level], and loop_iteration[loop_level] are written with attacker-influenced values.

state.value: 200 (out of bounds for all four arrays)

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_CLIP chunk handler, `first_object` and `last_object` are read from user-controlled chunk data and can be up to 65535. The loop iterates from `first_object` to `last_object` and accesses `mng_info->exists[i]` and `mng_info->frozen[i]` without checking whether `i` is within the bounds of these arrays (sized MNG_MAX_OBJECTS). This leads to out-of-bounds reads and potentially out-of-bounds writes on `mng_info->object_clip[i]`.

step_by_step_execution.description: A mng_CLIP chunk is read with length > 3.
first_object and last_object are read from user-controlled data p[0..3].
The loop iterates from first_object to last_object, accessing mng_info->exists[i] for i up to 65535, which exceeds MNG_MAX_OBJECTS array bounds.
Out-of-bounds read on mng_info->exists[i] and potentially out-of-bounds write on mng_info->object_clip[i].

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_DEFI chunk handler, when object_id exceeds MNG_MAX_OBJECTS, it is clamped to MNG_MAX_OBJECTS (not MNG_MAX_OBJECTS-1). Since arrays like mng_info->exists[] are indexed from 0 to MNG_MAX_OBJECTS-1, accessing index MNG_MAX_OBJECTS is an off-by-one out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: A mng_DEFI chunk is read with length >= 2.
object_id is read from user data: (p[0] << 8) | p[1], which can be up to 65535.
The bounds check uses > instead of >=, so object_id is clamped to MNG_MAX_OBJECTS exactly.
mng_info->exists[MNG_MAX_OBJECTS] is written, which is one past the last valid index (0 to MNG_MAX_OBJECTS-1), causing an off-by-one out-of-bounds write.

commit_message:
Reorganize and add more validation to MKL requantization

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387901341
Change-Id: I2515b9034c64e113db0bcec8337d30643ab0a0f1
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The validation for `input_requested_max` accidentally checks `input_requested_min.NumElements()` instead of `input_requested_max.NumElements()`. This means `input_requested_max` can be an empty tensor (0 elements), and the subsequent access `input_requested_max.flat<float>()(0)` performs an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The input tensor is validated to be 4D, and input_min_vec and input_max_vec are read and validated.
input_requested_min is read and correctly validated to have exactly 1 element.

input_requested_max is read from the context. However, the guard checks `input_requested_min.NumElements() == 1` instead of `input_requested_max.NumElements() == 1`. An attacker supplies an empty tensor for input_requested_max (0 elements). The check passes because input_requested_min already has 1 element.
The code accesses `input_requested_max.flat<float>()(0)` on a tensor with 0 elements, causing an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
Fixed frame allocation from an awaited frame.

njs_function_frame_save() is used to save the awaited frame when "await"
instruction is encountered. The saving was done as a memcpy() of
existing runtime frame.

njs_function_frame_alloc() is used to alloc a new function frame, this
function tries to use a spare preallocated memory from the previous
frame first.  Previously, this function might result in "use-after-free"
when invoked from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().
Because njs_function_frame_save() left pointers to the spare memory of
the original frame which may be already free when saved frame is
restored.

The fix is to erase fields for the spare memory from the saved frame.

This closes #469 issue on Github.
cve_desc: nginx njs 0.7.2 is affected suffers from Use-after-free in njs_function_frame_alloc() when it try to invoke from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().

commit_message:
Secure tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2

Validate size and shape of min_range and max_range
Ensure axis is within input dims limits

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387232799
Change-Id: I36975281f7b5758e9e31a8dcc73fe610ef456318
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2`, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/quantize_op.cc#L59) has some validation but does not check that `min_range` and `max_range` both have the same non-zero number of elements. If `axis` is provided (i.e., not `-1`), then validation should check that it is a value in range for the rank of `input` tensor and then the lengths of `min_range` and `max_range` inputs match the `axis` dimension of the `input` tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 6da6620efad397c85493b8f8667b821403516708. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The number of elements in input_min_range and input_max_range tensors is never validated against num_slices (derived from input.dim_size(axis_)). An attacker can supply min/max range tensors with fewer elements than num_slices, causing out-of-bounds reads on min_ranges(i)/max_ranges(i) and out-of-bounds writes on output_min_tensor/output_max_tensor (which are allocated with the same shape as the min/max input).

step_by_step_execution.description: axis_ is set to a valid axis (e.g., 0), and the input tensor has a large dimension along that axis.
num_slices is computed from input.dim_size(axis_), yielding a large value.
input_min_range and input_max_range are attacker-controlled tensors with only 1 element each.
Since num_slices != 1, execution enters the multi-slice branch. minmax_shape is {1}, so output_min_tensor and output_max_tensor are allocated with shape {1} (1 element each).
min_ranges and max_ranges are created as vec<float> views over the 1-element input tensors. The loop iterates i from 0 to 99.

On iteration i=1 (and beyond), min_ranges(i), max_ranges(i), output_min_tensor->flat<float>()(i), and output_max_tensor->flat<float>()(i) all access memory out of bounds, resulting in OOB read and OOB write.

commit_message: Fix wolfmqtt-fuzzer: Null-dereference WRITE in MqttProps_Free
cve_desc: wolfSSL wolfMQTT 1.9 has a heap-based buffer overflow in MqttDecode_Disconnect (called from MqttClient_DecodePacket and MqttClient_WaitType).

commit_message:
Fixed frame allocation from an awaited frame.

njs_function_frame_save() is used to save the awaited frame when "await"
instruction is encountered. The saving was done as a memcpy() of
existing runtime frame.

njs_function_frame_alloc() is used to alloc a new function frame, this
function tries to use a spare preallocated memory from the previous
frame first.  Previously, this function might result in "use-after-free"
when invoked from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().
Because njs_function_frame_save() left pointers to the spare memory of
the original frame which may be already free when saved frame is
restored.

The fix is to erase fields for the spare memory from the saved frame.

This closes #469 issue on Github.
cve_desc: nginx njs 0.7.2 is affected suffers from Use-after-free in njs_function_frame_alloc() when it try to invoke from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().

commit_message:
Ensure non-empty rt_nested_splits in tf.raw_ops.RaggedTensorToVariant

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387664237
Change-Id: Ia1700c34b5610873d63561abc86e23b46ead93b3
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can cause undefined behavior via binding a reference to null pointer in `tf.raw_ops.RaggedTensorToVariant`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/ragged_tensor_to_variant_op.cc#L129) has an incomplete validation of the splits values, missing the case when the argument would be empty. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit be7a4de6adfbd303ce08be4332554dff70362612. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fixed typo while calculating module path length.

The issue was introduced in 77c398f26d7e (not released yet).
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.3 was discovered to contain a stack overflow in the function njs_default_module_loader at /src/njs/src/njs_module.c. NOTE: multiple third parties dispute this report, e.g., the behavior is only found in unreleased development code that was not part of the 0.7.2, 0.7.3, or 0.7.4 release

vulnerabilities.description: When `dir` is not NULL and `dir->length` is 0, but `info->name.length` is non-zero, the `length == 0` check is not triggered. The code then evaluates `dir->start[dir->length - 1]`. Since `dir->length` is a `size_t` (unsigned), `dir->length - 1` wraps around to `SIZE_MAX`, causing a massive out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a non-NULL `dir` where `dir->length == 0`, and `info->name.length > 0` (e.g., 5).
`trail` is initialized to 0 and `length` is set to `info->name.length`.
Since `dir != NULL`, we enter the if block. `length += dir->length` keeps length at 5.
The check `if (length == 0)` is false since length is 5, so we do not return early.
`dir->length - 1` is evaluated. Since `dir->length` is `size_t` (unsigned) with value 0, `0 - 1` wraps to `SIZE_MAX`. The code reads `dir->start[SIZE_MAX]`, which is a massive out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
The code now checks for consistency of the MCU sizes across
hierarchical levels, and fails in case they are different.
cve_desc: libjpeg 1.63 has a heap-based buffer over-read in HierarchicalBitmapRequester::FetchRegion in hierarchicalbitmaprequester.cpp because the MCU size can be different between allocation and use.

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB read in TFLite's `gather_nd.cc`.

Passing negative indices is illegal but there was a missing check so that resulted in OOB accesses.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387208551
Change-Id: I6b7a8a62d3e7c13a16d81619e5bc23ae2cdbc7fd
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TFLite's [`GatherNd` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather_nd.cc#L124) does not support negative indices but there are no checks for this situation. Hence, an attacker can read arbitrary data from the heap by carefully crafting a model with negative values in `indices`. Similar issue exists in [`Gather` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather.cc). We have patched the issue in GitHub commits bb6a0383ed553c286f87ca88c207f6774d5c4a8f and eb921122119a6b6e470ee98b89e65d721663179d. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

Passing negative indices is illegal but there was a missing check so that resulted in OOB accesses.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387208551

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TFLite's [`GatherNd` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather_nd.cc#L124) does not support negative indices but there are no checks for this situation. Hence, an attacker can read arbitrary data from the heap by carefully crafting a model with negative values in `indices`. Similar issue exists in [`Gather` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather.cc). We have patched the issue in GitHub commits bb6a0383ed553c286f87ca88c207f6774d5c4a8f and eb921122119a6b6e470ee98b89e65d721663179d. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent unitialized variable use in grappler.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 399702928
Change-Id: Id7e75451fbff297692dfb687f60ea04b25c96b24
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer has a use of unitialized variable. If the `train_nodes` vector (obtained from the saved model that gets optimized) does not contain a `Dequeue` node, then `dequeue_node` is left unitialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The pointer `dequeue_node` is declared without initialization. If no node in `train_nodes` satisfies `IsDequeueOp`, the variable remains uninitialized. The subsequent `if (dequeue_node)` check reads an indeterminate pointer value, which is undefined behavior. If the indeterminate value happens to be non-null, the code dereferences it (`dequeue_node->name()`), leading to a potential crash or information leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: The pointer `dequeue_node` is declared on the stack without being initialized.
The for-loop iterates over `train_nodes` looking for an `IsDequeueOp`. If none of the nodes is a dequeue op, the loop completes without ever assigning `dequeue_node`.
The condition `if (dequeue_node)` is evaluated, reading the uninitialized pointer. This is undefined behavior. If the garbage value is non-zero, the branch is taken.
Inside the branch, `dequeue_node->name()` dereferences the indeterminate pointer, causing a read from an arbitrary memory address (use of uninitialized pointer).

commit_message:
Make Transpose's shape inference function validate that negative `perm` values are within the tensor's rank.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 403252853
Change-Id: Ia6b31b45b237312668bb31c2c3b3c7bbce2d2610
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the shape inference function for `Transpose` is vulnerable to a heap buffer overflow. This occurs whenever `perm` contains negative elements. The shape inference function does not validate that the indices in `perm` are all valid. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Disallow division by zero FPE in `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv`

Had to update a test that was broken.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388516976
Change-Id: Ic358e6bf0559e011539974d453fc7aa18b427e9c
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv` is vulnerable to a division by 0 error. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/resource_variable_ops.cc#L865) uses a common class for all binary operations but fails to treat the division by 0 case separately. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4aacb30888638da75023e6601149415b39763d76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The code validates that the rank of updates matches indices.dims() + params.dims() - 1 and that updates.shape starts with indices.shape, but it never validates that the trailing dimensions of updates match params.shape[1:]. An attacker can craft updates with correct rank and prefix but mismatched trailing dimensions (e.g., params=[5,3], indices=[2], updates=[2,100]), causing the scatter functor to write num_updates/N elements per index into params rows that only have product(params.shape[1:]) elements, resulting in an out-of-bounds write into the params buffer.

code_snippets:
const Index bad_i = functor(c, c->template eigen_device<Device>(),
                                    params_flat, updates_flat, indices_flat);

step_by_step_execution.description: params is looked up as a resource variable. Suppose params has shape [5, 3] (15 elements total).
indices input is provided with shape [2], e.g. values [0, 1]. N_big = 2.
updates input is provided with shape [2, 100] (200 elements total).
Rank check passes: updates.dims()==2 == indices.dims()+params->dims()-1 == 1+2-1 == 2.
N is set to 2 (indices.NumElements()), which is > 0, so the if-block is entered.
updates is not scalar, so the else branch is taken. num_updates = 200.
StartsWith check passes because updates.shape [2, 100] starts with indices.shape [2].

updates_flat is reshaped to {N, num_updates/N} = {2, 100}. params_flat via flat_outer_dims<T>() has shape {5, 3}. The scatter functor writes 100 elements per row into params rows that only have 3 elements, causing an out-of-bounds write of 97 elements per indexed row.

commit_message:
Validate that matrix dimension sizes in SparseMatMul are positive.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 401149683
Change-Id: Ib33eafc561a39c8741ece80b2edce6d4aae9a57d
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the code for sparse matrix multiplication is vulnerable to undefined behavior via binding a reference to `nullptr`. This occurs whenever the dimensions of `a` or `b` are 0 or less. In the case on one of these is 0, an empty output tensor should be allocated (to conserve the invariant that output tensors are always allocated when the operation is successful) but nothing should be written to it (that is, we should return early from the kernel implementation). Otherwise, attempts to write to this empty tensor would result in heap OOB access. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets: const int m = transpose_a_ ? a.dim_size(1) : a.dim_size(0);
const int k = transpose_a_ ? a.dim_size(0) : a.dim_size(1);
const int n = transpose_b_ ? b.dim_size(0) : b.dim_size(1);
const int k2 = transpose_b_ ? b.dim_size(1) : b.dim_size(0);
vulnerabilities.description: The tensor dimension sizes (int64) are narrowed to `int`, which can overflow or wrap around. If a dimension exceeds INT_MAX, the truncated `m` or `n` values become small or negative, causing the output tensor to be allocated with an incorrect (smaller) shape. Subsequent matrix operations then write beyond the allocated buffer.

code_snippets: const int m = transpose_a_ ? a.dim_size(1) : a.dim_size(0);
const int n = transpose_b_ ? b.dim_size(0) : b.dim_size(1);

step_by_step_execution.description: Inputs a and b are obtained from the context. Both pass the IsMatrix check so they are 2-D tensors, but their dimensions can be very large int64 values.
The dimension is assigned to an int variable `m`, causing truncation from int64 to int. A value of 2^31 wraps to a negative number or zero depending on platform.

state.value: negative or small due to int overflow, e.g. -2147483648

step_by_step_execution.description: The k == k2 check passes because the inner dimensions match (both 1).

Output tensor is allocated with TensorShape({m, n}). With a negative or wrapped `m`, the TensorShape constructor may interpret this as a small or different size, allocating a buffer that is much smaller than what the matrix operation expects.

state.value: incorrectly sized due to truncated m

step_by_step_execution.description: The matrix multiplication writes results assuming the original large dimensions, causing an out-of-bounds write into the undersized output buffer.

state.value: matrix view over undersized buffer, writes go out of bounds

commit_message:
Avoid buffer overflow when loading tensors with insufficient data from checkpoints.

`CopyDataFromTensorSliceToTensorSlice` does not (and cannot conveniently)
provide any bounds checking on its own, so the size is instead checked prior
to passing unvalidated data to that function.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 392971286
Change-Id: If2073b36d4d5eedd386329f56729395fd7effee1
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can trigger undefined behavior, integer overflows, segfaults and `CHECK`-fail crashes if they can change saved checkpoints from outside of TensorFlow. This is because the checkpoints loading infrastructure is missing validation for invalid file formats. The fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Don't constant-fold DT_RESOURCE constants.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 391803952
Change-Id: I0ea3ec31d3e7dfda0f03b4027a237f08d00a3091
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions during TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer phase, constant folding might attempt to deep copy a resource tensor. This results in a segfault, as these tensors are supposed to not change. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Add more validation to `RequantizationRangePerChannel`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387693946
Change-Id: Ife8dcbdb021bec4787eef6a4361dd08f17c14bd6
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
#ifdef _MSC_VER
#pragma omp parallel for
#else
#pragma omp parallel for reduction(max : out_min_max)
#endif
vulnerabilities.description: On MSVC with ENABLE_ONEDNN_OPENMP defined, the OpenMP parallel for loop is missing the `reduction(max : out_min_max)` clause. Multiple threads concurrently read and write `out_min_max` without synchronization, resulting in a data race and undefined behavior. This can cause the final `out_min_max` value to be incorrect, potentially leading to wrong output min/max values which could propagate incorrect quantization parameters.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives valid inputs with depth > 1 and the code is compiled on MSVC with ENABLE_ONEDNN_OPENMP defined.
The MSVC preprocessor branch is taken, emitting `#pragma omp parallel for` without a reduction clause for `out_min_max`.
Multiple OpenMP threads execute the loop body concurrently. Each thread reads `out_min_max`, computes `std::max(out_min_max, ranges[i])`, and writes back to `out_min_max` without any synchronization.

state.value: indeterminate due to data race — concurrent unsynchronized reads and writes

step_by_step_execution.description: The final value of `out_min_max` may be incorrect (e.g., a thread's update is lost because another thread read the old value before the write). This incorrect value is written to the output tensors.

vulnerabilities.description: Under OpenMP (both MSVC and non-MSVC paths), `is_non_negative` is a shared variable written to by multiple threads without synchronization or a reduction clause. This is a data race and undefined behavior. Although in practice it transitions only from true to false, it is formally UB and on some architectures or with compiler optimizations the final value could be incorrect, leading to a wrong output_min value (0.0 instead of -out_min_max or vice-versa).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives valid inputs with depth > 1 and ENABLE_ONEDNN_OPENMP is defined.

Multiple OpenMP threads execute the loop. Some channels have min_per_channel < 0, so those threads write `is_non_negative = false`. Other threads may read `is_non_negative` concurrently or also write to it.
After the loop, `is_non_negative` is used to decide `output_min`. If the race causes the wrong value, `output_min` is incorrect.

state.value: could be 0.0f when it should be -out_min_max, or vice-versa

vulnerabilities.description: If `min_per_channel` is INT32_MIN (-2147483648), calling `std::abs` on it is undefined behavior because the positive value 2147483648 cannot be represented as int32_t. This can result in an incorrect (e.g., negative) `abs_max` value, leading to wrong range calculations.

step_by_step_execution.description: An input tensor contains a qint32 element with value INT32_MIN (-2147483648) for some channel i.
std::abs(min_per_channel) is called. Since abs(INT32_MIN) = 2147483648 which overflows int32_t, this is signed integer overflow / undefined behavior.
The resulting abs_max may be negative or otherwise incorrect, leading to a wrong ranges[i] value and potentially incorrect output min/max.

commit_message:
Don't constant-fold DT_RESOURCE constants.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 391803952
Change-Id: I0ea3ec31d3e7dfda0f03b4027a237f08d00a3091
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions during TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer phase, constant folding might attempt to deep copy a resource tensor. This results in a segfault, as these tensors are supposed to not change. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB read in TFLite's `gather.cc`.

Passing negative indices is illegal but there was a missing check so that resulted in OOB accesses.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387231300
Change-Id: I3111b54b2f232638d795be17efc46abe4ede6bf8
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TFLite's [`GatherNd` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather_nd.cc#L124) does not support negative indices but there are no checks for this situation. Hence, an attacker can read arbitrary data from the heap by carefully crafting a model with negative values in `indices`. Similar issue exists in [`Gather` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather.cc). We have patched the issue in GitHub commits bb6a0383ed553c286f87ca88c207f6774d5c4a8f and eb921122119a6b6e470ee98b89e65d721663179d. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fix NPE in restoring code.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388303253
Change-Id: Ia8c68568cb854bca538909a182b31a618d68ce55
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When restoring tensors via raw APIs, if the tensor name is not provided, TensorFlow can be tricked into dereferencing a null pointer. Alternatively, attackers can read memory outside the bounds of heap allocated data by providing some tensor names but not enough for a successful restoration. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/47a06f40411a69c99f381495f490536972152ac0/tensorflow/core/kernels/save_restore_tensor.cc#L158-L159) retrieves the tensor list corresponding to the `tensor_name` user controlled input and immediately retrieves the tensor at the restoration index (controlled via `preferred_shard` argument). This occurs without validating that the provided list has enough values. If the list is empty this results in dereferencing a null pointer (undefined behavior). If, however, the list has some elements, if the restoration index is outside the bounds this results in heap OOB read. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e82dce6e6bd1f36a57e08fa85af213e2b2f2622. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: vm.c: check if target_class is NULL (when prepended).
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in mrb_obj_is_kind_of in in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. # Impact: Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

code_snippets:
if (MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P(m)) {
        m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &c, blk, 0);
vulnerabilities.description: In the OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler, the local variable `blk` is declared but never initialized before being used. It is passed to `prepare_missing()` and later checked with `mrb_proc_p(blk)`. If `mrb_proc_p` evaluates to true on the garbage value, `mrb_proc_ptr(blk)` will dereference it, leading to a read from an unpredictable memory location. The same issue exists in the OP_SUPER handler.

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &c, blk, 0);

step_by_step_execution.description: The OP_SEND or OP_SENDB opcode is dispatched. The variable `blk` is declared on the stack without initialization.

state.value: uninitialized (contains whatever was on the C stack)

step_by_step_execution.description: ARGUMENT_NORMALIZE is called, then `recv` is set. `blk` remains uninitialized throughout.
Method lookup via mrb_method_search_vm fails (MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P is true), and prepare_missing is called with the uninitialized `blk` value.

After the C function method call returns (MRB_METHOD_CFUNC_P path), the code checks `mrb_proc_p(blk)`. Since blk was never assigned, the type field contains garbage. If the garbage happens to match MRB_TT_PROC, the code dereferences it via mrb_proc_ptr, reading from an arbitrary/invalid memory address.

state.value: uninitialized, checked as if it were a valid mrb_value
pointer derived from garbage blk value — potential arbitrary memory read/write

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.LoadAndRemapMatrix vulnerability with invalid `row_remapping`.

Check that `row_remapping` has the correct dims().

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445522800
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.LoadAndRemapMatrix does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `initializing_values` is a vector but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB in sparse reduction ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387934524

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of sparse reduction operations in TensorFlow can trigger accesses outside of bounds of heap allocated data.
