commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad vulnerability with invalid input_min or input_max.

Check that argument is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445198280
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: When axis_ is 0 (which passes the validation checks since 0 >= -1 and 0 < input.shape().dims() for any non-scalar tensor), the else branch is taken and `flat_inner_outer_dims<T, 3>(axis_ - 1)` is called with the argument -1. This negative index is not a valid argument for flat_inner_outer_dims and can lead to undefined behavior or an out-of-bounds memory access.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads axis_ which was set at construction time. axis_ can be 0.
The check `axis_ >= -1` passes since 0 >= -1.
The check `axis_ == -1 || axis_ < input.shape().dims()` passes for any input tensor with dims() >= 1, since 0 < dims().
depth is computed as input.dim_size(0) since axis_ != -1.
Since axis_ != -1, the else branch is taken. flat_inner_outer_dims<T, 3>(axis_ - 1) is called with argument -1, which is an invalid (negative) begin index.

commit_message:
Fix EinsumHelper::ParseEquation to avoid uninitialized accesses.

EinsumHelper::ParseEquation is supposed to return true or false in
input_has_ellipsis and output_has_ellipsis to indicate whether there is
ellipsis in the inputs and output. Previously, when there is no ellipsis in the
inputs or output, the routine doesn't assign false to the variables. This
change initializes the two variables with false to fix the problem.
PiperOrigin-RevId: 391772004
Change-Id: I17b6c88aadef4131470378e48cced054bf252e86
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affeced versions during execution, `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` is supposed to set the flags in `input_has_ellipsis` vector and `*output_has_ellipsis` boolean to indicate whether there is ellipsis in the corresponding inputs and output. However, the code only changes these flags to `true` and never assigns `false`. This results in unitialized variable access if callers assume that `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` always sets these flags. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: When num_inputs is 0 (no inputs parsed from the equation), the code reaches the label_types loop where it accesses (*input_label_counts)[0] and (*input_label_counts)[1] out of bounds, since input_label_counts was resized to 0. The short-circuit evaluation only guards against num_inputs==1 (skipping index 1), but does not guard against num_inputs==0 (which would make index 0 also out of bounds).

step_by_step_execution.description: ParseEinsumEquation parses the equation and returns an input_str vector. If the equation has no inputs (e.g., '->abc'), input_str is empty.
num_inputs is set to input_str.size(), which is 0.
input_label_counts is resized to num_inputs (0), so it is empty.
MapToLabels is called for output_str, populating label_mapping with labels from the output. Assume output has at least one label.

The loop 'for (int label = 0; label < num_labels; ++label)' executes. For a non-ellipsis label, it evaluates 'num_inputs == 1' which is false (num_inputs is 0), so it proceeds to evaluate '(*input_label_counts)[0][label]'.
Accessing (*input_label_counts)[0] is an out-of-bounds read on a vector of size 0, leading to undefined behavior.

commit_message:
Fix a null pointer exception in SVDF

This is due to not checking that `GetVariableInput` returns non-null tensor.

Also fix a potential null pointer exception in `GetVariableInput`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385160147
Change-Id: Iadf3f0705b036a9014d27caa5a8bbd91f4c4c401
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of SVDF in TFLite is [vulnerable to a null pointer error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/svdf.cc#L300-L313). The [`GetVariableInput` function](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L115-L119) can return a null pointer but `GetTensorData` assumes that the argument is always a valid tensor. Furthermore, because `GetVariableInput` calls [`GetMutableInput`](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L82-L90) which might return `nullptr`, the `tensor->is_variable` expression can also trigger a null pointer exception. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5b048e87e4e55990dae6b547add4dae59f4e1c76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.slice() with slow "this" argument.

Previously, when "this" argument was not a fast array, but the "deleted" array
was a fast array, the "deleted" array may be left in uninitialized state if
"this" argument had gaps.

This fix is to ensure that "deleted" is properly initialized.

This fixes #485 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_value_own_enumerate at src/njs_value.c.

vulnerabilities.description: Time-of-check to time-of-use (TOCTOU) vulnerability: `length` is obtained from `njs_object_length` early on, then `njs_value_to_integer` is called on user-supplied arguments (args[1] for `start`, args[2] for `delete`). If these arguments are objects with custom `valueOf`/`toString` methods, arbitrary JavaScript can execute during these conversions, potentially shrinking or modifying the array. However, `start` and `delete` were computed using the stale `length`. In the fast_array path, `array->start[n]` is then accessed where `n` can range up to `start + delete - 1`, which may exceed the array's actual (now-reduced) allocated size, causing an out-of-bounds read. The same issue applies to the second fast_array block where `array->length` may no longer reflect the true allocated capacity after side effects from the conversion calls.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads the length of the target array/object.

njs_value_to_integer is called on args[1] to compute `start`. If args[1] is an object with a valueOf method, arbitrary JS executes. That JS code could shrink the array (e.g., set array.length = 0 or pop all elements), deallocating or reducing the backing store.

state.value: computed based on stale length, e.g. 0

step_by_step_execution.description: njs_value_to_integer is called on args[2] to compute `delete`. Another side effect could occur but the array may already be shrunk.

state.value: computed as njs_min(njs_max(val, 0), length - start), e.g. 1000 based on stale length

step_by_step_execution.description: njs_array_alloc allocates the `deleted` array with size `delete` (e.g., 1000).

The fast_array path is entered. The loop copies `delete` (1000) elements from array->start[start..start+delete-1], but the actual array backing store may have been shrunk to 0 elements, causing out-of-bounds reads from array->start.

commit_message:
password: Use crypto_memneq() to compare hashes

Cryptographic verifications should be time-constant so that an attacker
cannot get information about the secrets used by observing the system,
so use crypto_memneq() rather than memcmp() to compare password hashes.

Signed-off-by: Sascha Hauer <s.hauer@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: common/password.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because strncmp is used during hash comparison.

commit_message: Fix fido2 signature validation bug
cve_desc: scheme/webauthn.c in Glewlwyd SSO server through 2.5.3 has a buffer overflow during FIDO2 signature validation in webauthn registration.

code_snippets: unsigned char data_signed[200], client_data_hash[32], cert_export[32], cert_export_b64[64];
vulnerabilities.description: The fixed-size stack buffer `data_signed[200]` is filled by concatenating multiple caller-supplied buffers (rpid_hash, client_data_hash, credential_id, cert_x, cert_y) without any bounds checking. The total written is 1 + rpid_hash_len + 32 + credential_id_len + 1 + cert_x_len + cert_y_len. Since credential_id_len can be large (WebAuthn allows up to 1023 bytes), the combined size easily exceeds 200 bytes, causing a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with attacker-controlled credential_id of, say, 256 bytes (credential_id_len=256). rpid_hash_len=32, cert_x_len=32, cert_y_len=32.
data_signed[200] is allocated on the stack. The first byte is set to 0x0, data_signed_offset becomes 1.
rpid_hash (32 bytes) is copied into data_signed at offset 1. Offset becomes 33.
client_data_hash (32 bytes) is copied into data_signed at offset 33. Offset becomes 65.
credential_id (256 bytes) is memcpy'd into data_signed at offset 65. This writes to data_signed[65..320], which overflows past the 200-byte buffer boundary at byte index 200.

commit_message:
Make Transpose's shape inference function validate that negative `perm` values are within the tensor's rank.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 403252853
Change-Id: Ia6b31b45b237312668bb31c2c3b3c7bbce2d2610
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the shape inference function for `Transpose` is vulnerable to a heap buffer overflow. This occurs whenever `perm` contains negative elements. The shape inference function does not validate that the indices in `perm` are all valid. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The validation of `in_idx` only checks if it is >= rank, but does not check for negative values. Since `in_idx` is an `int64_t` derived from user-controlled tensor data, a negative value would pass the bounds check and be used as an index in `c->Dim(input, in_idx)`, potentially causing an out-of-bounds read or other undefined behavior.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function retrieves the permutation tensor `perm` from input 1.

state.value: non-null pointer to a user-supplied Tensor

step_by_step_execution.description: The rank is determined (e.g., from known input rank or perm elements). Suppose rank is 3.
The perm tensor data is read into `data` via AsInt64. Suppose the user provides perm = [-1, 0, 1].
In the loop iteration i=0, `in_idx = data[0]` which is -1. The check `in_idx >= rank` evaluates to `-1 >= 3` which is false, so the check passes.
`c->Dim(input, in_idx)` is called with in_idx = -1, causing an out-of-bounds or negative index access on the shape dimensions.

commit_message: Fix handling of APNG with 0 delay_den (#313)
cve_desc: libjxl v0.3.7 is affected by a Divide By Zero in issue in lib/extras/codec_apng.cc jxl::DecodeImageAPNG(). When encoding a malicous APNG file using cjxl, an attacker can trigger a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `rowbytes` (w * 4) and `imagesize` (h * rowbytes). Both are `unsigned int`, and their multiplication can wrap around, causing a much smaller buffer to be allocated than expected. Subsequent writes through `frameRaw.rows` (which are calculated assuming the non-overflowed sizes) will write far beyond the allocated heap buffer.

code_snippets: for (j = 0; j < h; j++) frameRaw.rows[j] = frameRaw.p + j * rowbytes;

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads the IHDR chunk and extracts w and h dimensions from the PNG data.
The dimension check `w > cMaxPNGSize || h > cMaxPNGSize` passes because each dimension individually is within bounds.

`rowbytes = w * 4` is computed as unsigned int. 65536 * 4 = 262144, which fits. Then `imagesize = h * rowbytes` = 65536 * 262144 = 17179869184, which overflows unsigned int (max ~4.29 billion) and wraps to a small value.

state.value: 17179869184 mod 2^32 = some small value (e.g., 0 if w=1048576, h=1024, rowbytes=4194304, imagesize=4294967296 wraps to 0)

step_by_step_execution.description: With w=1048576 and h=1024 (both plausibly within cMaxPNGSize): rowbytes = 1048576*4 = 4194304, imagesize = 1024 * 4194304 = 4294967296 which wraps to 0 in unsigned int. `new unsigned char[0]` allocates a minimal buffer.
`frameRaw.p` is allocated with the overflowed (tiny) size. Then the loop sets `frameRaw.rows[j] = frameRaw.p + j * rowbytes`, pointing far past the allocated buffer.
When PNG decoding writes decompressed pixel data through `frameRaw.rows`, it writes to out-of-bounds heap memory, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: pointers far beyond allocated heap buffer

vulnerabilities.description: When an fcTL chunk is encountered, multiple fields are read from `chunk.p` at offsets up to 33 without verifying that the chunk size is large enough to contain all these fields. A malformed fcTL chunk with a small `chunk.size` could lead to out-of-bounds reads on heap memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: The main loop reads a chunk via `read_chunk(&r, &chunk)` which returns its id and populates chunk.p and chunk.size.

state.value: attacker-controlled, could be small (e.g., 12 bytes - just the header)

step_by_step_execution.description: The code enters the branch for `id == kId_fcTL` and reaches the section that reads frame control parameters from chunk.p at offsets 12 through 33.
Accesses like `chunk.p[32]` and `chunk.p[33]` read beyond the allocated chunk buffer, causing a heap out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad vulnerability with invalid input_min or input_max.

Check that argument is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445198280
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: When axis_ is 0, the expression `axis_ - 1` evaluates to -1, which is passed to `flat_inner_outer_dims<T, 3>()`. This method expects a non-negative dimension index. Passing -1 leads to an out-of-bounds read/write or undefined behavior when computing the tensor reshape, as it will access dimension metadata at an invalid index.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads axis_ which is a member variable. An attacker provides axis_ = 0.
The check `axis_ >= -1` passes since 0 >= -1.
The check `axis_ == -1 || axis_ < input.shape().dims()` passes since 0 < dims (assuming dims >= 1).
Since axis_ != -1, execution enters the else branch.

The expression `axis_ - 1` is computed, yielding -1, which is passed to `flat_inner_outer_dims<T, 3>()`. This function expects a valid dimension index (0 to dims-1). Passing -1 causes it to read/compute with an invalid dimension boundary, leading to undefined behavior such as out-of-bounds memory access.

commit_message:
Fix EinsumHelper::ParseEquation to avoid uninitialized accesses.

EinsumHelper::ParseEquation is supposed to return true or false in
input_has_ellipsis and output_has_ellipsis to indicate whether there is
ellipsis in the inputs and output. Previously, when there is no ellipsis in the
inputs or output, the routine doesn't assign false to the variables. This
change initializes the two variables with false to fix the problem.
PiperOrigin-RevId: 391772004
Change-Id: I17b6c88aadef4131470378e48cced054bf252e86
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affeced versions during execution, `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` is supposed to set the flags in `input_has_ellipsis` vector and `*output_has_ellipsis` boolean to indicate whether there is ellipsis in the corresponding inputs and output. However, the code only changes these flags to `true` and never assigns `false`. This results in unitialized variable access if callers assume that `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` always sets these flags. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: When num_inputs is 0, the code accesses (*input_label_counts)[0] and (*input_label_counts)[1] out of bounds, since input_label_counts was resized to num_inputs (0). The short-circuit evaluation of `num_inputs == 1` is false when num_inputs is 0, so both out-of-bounds accesses are evaluated.

step_by_step_execution.description: ParseEinsumEquation parses the equation. If the equation has no inputs (e.g., an output-only equation or a malformed equation that ParseEinsumEquation accepts with 0 inputs), input_str will be empty.
num_inputs is set to input_str.size().
input_label_counts is resized to num_inputs (0). The loop to populate counts is skipped because num_inputs == 0.
MapToLabels is called for output_str, populating output_labels and label_mapping. If output_str has characters, num_labels > 0.
output_label_counts is resized to num_labels and populated. The final loop iterates for label from 0 to num_labels-1.

For a non-ellipsis label, `removed` is computed. Then `unique` is evaluated: `num_inputs == 1` is false (num_inputs is 0), so (*input_label_counts)[0] is accessed, which is out-of-bounds on an empty vector.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-vhxv-phmx-g52q

* Prevent OOB read/write when parsing RTCP FB RPSI

* Add log information

* Modification based on comments.
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. PJSIP versions 2.12 and prior do not parse incoming RTCP feedback RPSI (Reference Picture Selection Indication) packet, but any app that directly uses pjmedia_rtcp_fb_parse_rpsi() will be affected. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The attacker-controlled `padlen` value read from the packet is not validated against `rpsi_len`. If `padlen` is larger than `rpsi_len*8 - 16`, the unsigned subtraction `rpsi_len*8 - 16 - padlen` wraps around to a very large value. This huge value is then used to compute the string length passed to `pj_strset`, resulting in the `rpsi->rpsi` string referencing memory far beyond the actual buffer, enabling an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a valid buffer. Assertions pass: buf and rpsi are non-null, length >= sizeof(pjmedia_rtcp_common).
Header check passes: hdr->pt == RTCP_PSFB and hdr->count == 3.
rpsi_len is computed from hdr->length. For example, hdr->length (after ntohs) = 3, so rpsi_len = (3-2)*4 = 4.
Length check: length < rpsi_len + 12 => 24 < 16 is false, so check passes.
p is set to point just past the common header. padlen is read from the first byte of the payload. An attacker sets this byte to 255.

rpsi->rpsi_bit_len = rpsi_len*8 - 16 - padlen = 4*8 - 16 - 255 = 32 - 16 - 255 = -239. Since rpsi_bit_len is pj_size_t (unsigned), this wraps to a very large value (e.g., 2^64 - 239 on 64-bit or 2^32 - 239 on 32-bit).
pj_strset is called with length (rpsi->rpsi_bit_len + 7)/8, which is an extremely large value, setting the rpsi string to reference far beyond the actual packet buffer.

commit_message:
Fix heap OOB in `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388355464
Change-Id: If14d96231d1cd7aad7c4d1c22c1bab1576b75717
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions if the arguments to `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather` don't determine a valid ragged tensor code can trigger a read from outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/ragged_gather_op.cc#L70) directly reads the first dimension of a tensor shape before checking that said tensor has rank of at least 1 (i.e., it is not a scalar). Furthermore, the implementation does not check that the list given by `params_nested_splits` is not an empty list of tensors. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit a2b743f6017d7b97af1fe49087ae15f0ac634373. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent unitialized variable use in grappler.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 399702928
Change-Id: Id7e75451fbff297692dfb687f60ea04b25c96b24
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer has a use of unitialized variable. If the `train_nodes` vector (obtained from the saved model that gets optimized) does not contain a `Dequeue` node, then `dequeue_node` is left unitialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: If a graph node has an op name matching one of the apply_gradients_ops (e.g., "ApplyAdam") but has fewer inputs than expected by gradient_pos (e.g., ApplyAdam expects index 9, requiring at least 10 inputs), then `input(gradient_pos[...])` and `mutable_input(gradient_pos[...])` will perform out-of-bounds access on the protobuf repeated field. There is no validation that the node's input_size() is greater than the index being accessed.

step_by_step_execution.description: The graph is loaded from the GrapplerItem. A malicious or malformed graph contains a node with op "ApplyAdam" but with fewer than 10 inputs (e.g., only 3 inputs).
The node's op matches an entry in apply_gradients_ops, so its name is inserted into apply_gradients_nodes_.
In the loop over apply_gradients_nodes_, gradient_pos is consulted for "ApplyAdam", which returns 9.
apply_gradients_node->input(9) is called, but the node only has 3 inputs. This is an out-of-bounds read on the protobuf repeated field.
Similarly, mutable_input(9) is called to write the div_node's name, causing an out-of-bounds write.

Fix heap OOB in `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388355464

Furthermore, the implementation does not check that the list given by `params_nested_splits` is not an empty list of tensors. commit_message: Fix handling of APNG with 0 delay_den (#313)
cve_desc: libjxl v0.3.7 is affected by a Divide By Zero in issue in lib/extras/codec_apng.cc jxl::DecodeImageAPNG(). When encoding a malicous APNG file using cjxl, an attacker can trigger a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `rowbytes` (w * 4) and `imagesize` (h * rowbytes). Both are `unsigned int` and can wrap around to a small value when w and h are large (but still within cMaxPNGSize). This causes a small heap allocation for `frameRaw.p`, followed by out-of-bounds writes when PNG image data is decoded into the undersized buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Width and height are read from the IHDR chunk and checked against cMaxPNGSize.
rowbytes is computed as w * 4. For w=50000, rowbytes = 200000, which fits in unsigned int.
imagesize is computed as h * rowbytes = 50000 * 200000 = 10,000,000,000 which overflows unsigned int (max ~4.29 billion), wrapping to a small value.
A heap buffer of the (incorrect, smaller) imagesize is allocated for frameRaw.p.
Row pointers are set up and PNG decoding writes pixel data for 50000 rows of 200000 bytes each into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: pointers past the end of the allocated buffer for large j

vulnerabilities.description: Division by zero when `delay_den` is read as 0 from a crafted fcTL chunk. The APNG specification states delay_den=0 should be treated as 100, but this code does not handle that case.

step_by_step_execution.description: An fcTL chunk is encountered. delay_den is read from chunk.p + 30.
On the next fcTL or IEND chunk, processing_finish succeeds and the code computes bundle.duration.
Division by zero occurs: delay_num * 1000 / delay_den = 1000 / 0.

commit_message:
fix possible pattern crash

issue #325
cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in tildearrow Furnace. It has been classified as problematic. This is due to an incomplete fix of CVE-2022-1211. It is possible to initiate the attack remotely but it requires user interaction. The issue got fixed with the patch 0eb02422d5161767e9983bdaa5c429762d3477ce.

vulnerabilities.description: The static buffer `id` is only 32 bytes, and `sprintf` is used without length limits. The format string "%s##PN_%d_%d" includes the return value of `noteName()` (unbounded string) plus two integer format specifiers. If `noteName()` returns a longer-than-expected string, or if the combination of the string and the two integer parameters exceeds 31 characters (plus null terminator), a stack/static buffer overflow occurs. Similarly, other `sprintf` calls into `id` with three `%d` parameters (e.g., the effect rows) could overflow if integer values are large.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is entered with valid parameters. `id` is a static char buffer of size 32.
The row visibility and range checks pass, so execution continues to the main loop over channels.

In the channel loop, the note name is formatted into `id` via sprintf. `noteName()` returns a string whose length is not bounded by the caller. Combined with the `##PN_` separator and two decimal integer parameters, the total output can exceed 31 characters.

state.value: potentially long string, e.g., if returning an error or unexpected value

step_by_step_execution.description: sprintf writes beyond the 32-byte `id` buffer, causing a buffer overflow that corrupts adjacent memory (since `id` is static, this corrupts other static/global data).

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.lastIndexOf() with unicode string as "this".

Previously, when lastIndexOf() was called with unicode string as "this"
argument and a negative "fromIndex" argument null-pointer dererence
might occur because njs_string_offset() was called with invalid index
value whereas njs_string_offset() should always be called with valid
index argument.

The fix is to verify that from index is valid.

This closes #482 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_string_offset at src/njs_string.c.

vulnerabilities.description: In the ASCII/byte string branch, `from` (from `args->from`) is used directly as an offset into the string buffer without any bounds check against the string length. If `from` is greater than or equal to `string_prop.size`, the pointer `p` will point beyond the allocated string buffer, leading to an out-of-bounds read when `njs_string_new` copies 1 byte from `p`. The array code path has a guard (`from < array->length`) before direct access, but the string code path has no equivalent check.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads `from` and `to` from the caller-supplied `args` structure.
The value is determined to be a string (njs_is_string(value) is true), so the string handling branch is entered.
njs_string_prop is populated from value. Suppose the string is a short ASCII string of length 5.
The condition `(size_t) length == string_prop.size` is true, so the ASCII string branch is taken.
Pointer `p` is computed as `string_prop.start + from`, which points 100 bytes into a 5-byte buffer, well past its end.
The loop condition `i-- > to` is satisfied (i starts at 101, to is 0). `njs_string_new(vm, &character, p, 1, 1)` reads 1 byte from the out-of-bounds pointer `p`.

commit_message:
Fix a null pointer exception in SVDF

This is due to not checking that `GetVariableInput` returns non-null tensor.

Also fix a potential null pointer exception in `GetVariableInput`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385160147
Change-Id: Iadf3f0705b036a9014d27caa5a8bbd91f4c4c401
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of SVDF in TFLite is [vulnerable to a null pointer error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/svdf.cc#L300-L313). The [`GetVariableInput` function](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L115-L119) can return a null pointer but `GetTensorData` assumes that the argument is always a valid tensor. Furthermore, because `GetVariableInput` calls [`GetMutableInput`](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L82-L90) which might return `nullptr`, the `tensor->is_variable` expression can also trigger a null pointer exception. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5b048e87e4e55990dae6b547add4dae59f4e1c76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
TfLiteTensor* tensor = GetMutableInput(context, node, index);
  return tensor->is_variable ? tensor : nullptr;
vulnerabilities.description: If GetMutableInput returns nullptr (e.g., due to an invalid index or missing input), the subsequent dereference `tensor->is_variable` will cause a null pointer dereference, leading to a crash or potentially exploitable undefined behavior.

step_by_step_execution.description: GetMutableInput is called with the provided context, node, and index.

state.value: any integer, possibly out of range or referencing a missing input

step_by_step_execution.description: GetMutableInput returns nullptr because the index is invalid or the input tensor cannot be resolved.
The code dereferences tensor via `tensor->is_variable` without a null check, causing a null pointer dereference.

commit_message:
Improve to cover scale value greater than one

PiperOrigin-RevId: 433050921
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, certain TFLite models that were created using TFLite model converter would crash when loaded in the TFLite interpreter. The culprit is that during quantization the scale of values could be greater than 1 but code was always assuming sub-unit scaling. Thus, since code was calling `QuantizeMultiplierSmallerThanOneExp`, the `TFLITE_CHECK_LT` assertion would trigger and abort the process. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
Fixed recursive async function calls.

Previously, PromiseCapability record was stored (function->context)
directly in function object during a function invocation.  This is
not correct, because PromiseCapability record should be linked to
current execution context.  As a result, function->context is
overwritten with consecutive recursive calls which results in
use-after-free.

This closes #451 issue on Github.
cve_desc: njs through 0.7.0, used in NGINX, was discovered to contain a heap use-after-free in njs_await_fulfilled.

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB in sparse reduction ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387934524
Change-Id: I894aa30f1e454f09b471d565b4a325da49322c1a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of sparse reduction operations in TensorFlow can trigger accesses outside of bounds of heap allocated data. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a1bc56203f21a5a4995311825ffaba7a670d7747/tensorflow/core/kernels/sparse_reduce_op.cc#L217-L228) fails to validate that each reduction group does not overflow and that each corresponding index does not point to outside the bounds of the input tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 87158f43f05f2720a374f3e6d22a7aaa3a33f750. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The flat index computed by CoordinatesToFlatIndex is used to write into out_flat without any bounds check. If a sparse tensor's indices contain out-of-range values (negative or exceeding the declared shape), the computed idx can be negative or exceed the size of the allocated output tensor, leading to an out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides input_indices, input_values, input_shape, and reduction_axes tensors. The input_indices may contain coordinate values that are negative or exceed the bounds declared in input_shape.

state.value: User-controlled sparse indices, potentially containing out-of-range values

step_by_step_execution.description: A SparseTensor is created from deep copies of indices and values. SparseTensor::Create does not necessarily validate that all index values are within the declared shape bounds.

state.value: SparseTensor with potentially out-of-range index coordinates

step_by_step_execution.description: output_strides are computed based on shape_vec at reduction.group_by_dims positions. The output tensor out_values is allocated with reduction.reduced_shape.

state.value: Flat view of output tensor with size = product of reduced_shape dimensions

step_by_step_execution.description: sp.Reorder and sp.group iterate over the sparse tensor. g.group() returns coordinates derived from the sparse tensor's index values. If indices are out-of-range, these coordinates can be arbitrary.

state.value: Coordinates from sparse tensor indices — could be negative or exceed shape bounds

step_by_step_execution.description: CoordinatesToFlatIndex computes idx = sum(coords[i] * strides[i]). With out-of-range coordinates, idx can be negative or larger than the output tensor size.

state.value: Potentially negative or exceeding out_flat size

step_by_step_execution.description: out_flat(idx) = reduced_val() writes to the computed index without any bounds check, causing an out-of-bounds memory write.

commit_message:
Fixed recursive async function calls.

Previously, PromiseCapability record was stored (function->context)
directly in function object during a function invocation.  This is
not correct, because PromiseCapability record should be linked to
current execution context.  As a result, function->context is
overwritten with consecutive recursive calls which results in
use-after-free.

This closes #451 issue on Github.
cve_desc: njs through 0.7.0, used in NGINX, was discovered to contain a heap use-after-free in njs_await_fulfilled.

commit_message: fixed #2194
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository gpac/gpac prior to v2.1.0-DEV.

vulnerabilities.description: The function calls gf_node_unregister (which decrements reference count and potentially frees the node) twice on 'node'. The comment says 'register TWICE' but the code calls 'unregister' instead of 'register'. If the node's reference count reaches zero after the first or second unregister call, the node is freed. The pointer is then stored in inf->new_node and was already stored in codec->ActiveQP and codec->scenegraph->global_qp, leading to use-after-free when those pointers are later accessed.

step_by_step_execution.description: A node is decoded from the bitstream via gf_bifs_dec_node. The returned node has some initial reference count (typically 1).

state.value: valid pointer to a newly decoded GF_Node

step_by_step_execution.description: The old global_qp is cleaned up and codec->ActiveQP and codec->scenegraph->global_qp are set to NULL.
The node passes the tag check (TAG_MPEG4_QuantizationParameter), so the function continues to the registration section.
codec->ActiveQP and codec->scenegraph->global_qp are set to point to node.
First gf_node_unregister(node, NULL) is called, decrementing the reference count. If the count was 1, it becomes 0 and the node is freed.
Second gf_node_unregister(node, NULL) is called on the potentially freed node, causing a use-after-free or double-free.
The (potentially freed) node pointer is stored in inf->new_node and used for the command field, propagating the dangling pointer.

commit_message:
[misc] Fix use-after-free in net_bind()

Thanks to Ba Jinsheng for reporting this bug
cve_desc: OwnTone (aka owntone-server) through 28.1 has a use-after-free in net_bind() in misc.c.

vulnerabilities.description: After freeaddrinfo(servinfo) is called, the pointer `ptr` (which points into the freed `servinfo` linked list) is dereferenced to access `ptr->ai_addr` and `ptr->ai_addrlen`. This is a use-after-free vulnerability.

step_by_step_execution.description: getaddrinfo is called, populating `servinfo` with a linked list of addrinfo structures.

state.value: valid pointer to addrinfo linked list

step_by_step_execution.description: The for loop iterates over the linked list. When bind() succeeds, the loop breaks with `ptr` pointing to the successful addrinfo node.

state.value: non-NULL, points to a node within servinfo linked list

step_by_step_execution.description: freeaddrinfo(servinfo) is called, freeing all addrinfo nodes including the one `ptr` points to.
The check `if (!ptr)` passes (ptr is non-NULL, though dangling), so execution continues.
getsockname(fd, ptr->ai_addr, &ptr->ai_addrlen) dereferences the dangling pointer `ptr`, reading freed memory for ai_addr and ai_addrlen. It also writes to the freed memory via &ptr->ai_addrlen.
net_port_get and net_address_get further dereference ptr->ai_addr from freed memory.

commit_message:
i2c: fix stack buffer overflow vulnerability in i2c md command

When running "i2c md 0 0 80000100", the function do_i2c_md parses the
length into an unsigned int variable named length. The value is then
moved to a signed variable:

    int nbytes = length;
    #define DISP_LINE_LEN 16
    int linebytes = (nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN) ? DISP_LINE_LEN : nbytes;
    ret = dm_i2c_read(dev, addr, linebuf, linebytes);

On systems where integers are 32 bits wide, 0x80000100 is a negative
value to "nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN" is false and linebytes gets assigned
0x80000100 instead of 16.

The consequence is that the function which reads from the i2c device
(dm_i2c_read or i2c_read) is called with a 16-byte stack buffer to fill
but with a size parameter which is too large. In some cases, this could
trigger a crash. But with some i2c drivers, such as drivers/i2c/nx_i2c.c
(used with "nexell,s5pxx18-i2c" bus), the size is actually truncated to
a 16-bit integer. This is because function i2c_transfer expects an
unsigned short length. In such a case, an attacker who can control the
response of an i2c device can overwrite the return address of a function
and execute arbitrary code through Return-Oriented Programming.

Fix this issue by using unsigned integers types in do_i2c_md. While at
it, make also alen unsigned, as signed sizes can cause vulnerabilities
when people forgot to check that they can be negative.

Signed-off-by: Nicolas Iooss <nicolas.iooss+uboot@ledger.fr>
Reviewed-by: Heiko Schocher <hs@denx.de>
cve_desc: In Das U-Boot through 2022.07-rc5, an integer signedness error and resultant stack-based buffer overflow in the "i2c md" command enables the corruption of the return address pointer of the do_i2c_md function.

commit_message:
i2c: fix stack buffer overflow vulnerability in i2c md command

When running "i2c md 0 0 80000100", the function do_i2c_md parses the
length into an unsigned int variable named length. The value is then
moved to a signed variable:

    int nbytes = length;
    #define DISP_LINE_LEN 16
    int linebytes = (nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN) ? DISP_LINE_LEN : nbytes;
    ret = dm_i2c_read(dev, addr, linebuf, linebytes);

On systems where integers are 32 bits wide, 0x80000100 is a negative
value to "nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN" is false and linebytes gets assigned
0x80000100 instead of 16.

The consequence is that the function which reads from the i2c device
(dm_i2c_read or i2c_read) is called with a 16-byte stack buffer to fill
but with a size parameter which is too large. In some cases, this could
trigger a crash. But with some i2c drivers, such as drivers/i2c/nx_i2c.c
(used with "nexell,s5pxx18-i2c" bus), the size is actually truncated to
a 16-bit integer. This is because function i2c_transfer expects an
unsigned short length. In such a case, an attacker who can control the
response of an i2c device can overwrite the return address of a function
and execute arbitrary code through Return-Oriented Programming.

Fix this issue by using unsigned integers types in do_i2c_md. While at
it, make also alen unsigned, as signed sizes can cause vulnerabilities
when people forgot to check that they can be negative.

Signed-off-by: Nicolas Iooss <nicolas.iooss+uboot@ledger.fr>
Reviewed-by: Heiko Schocher <hs@denx.de>
cve_desc: In Das U-Boot through 2022.07-rc5, an integer signedness error and resultant stack-based buffer overflow in the "i2c md" command enables the corruption of the return address pointer of the do_i2c_md function.

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.lastIndexOf() with unicode string as "this".

Previously, when lastIndexOf() was called with unicode string as "this"
argument and a negative "fromIndex" argument null-pointer dererence
might occur because njs_string_offset() was called with invalid index
value whereas njs_string_offset() should always be called with valid
index argument.

The fix is to verify that from index is valid.

This closes #482 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_string_offset at src/njs_string.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When processing a byte/ASCII string, the pointer `p` is computed as `string_prop.start + from` without verifying that `from` is within the string's bounds (i.e., `from < length`). If `args->from` exceeds or equals the string length, `p` will point past the end of the string buffer, and the subsequent `njs_string_new` call reads 1 byte from that out-of-bounds location. The array code path checks `from < array->length` before access, but the string code path has no equivalent guard.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function receives args where value is an ASCII string of, say, length 5 (size 5), and args->from = 10, args->to = 0.
The code enters the string branch and determines it is a byte/ASCII string because (size_t)length == string_prop.size.
Pointer p is computed as string_prop.start + from, which is string_prop.start + 10, pointing 5 bytes past the end of the 5-byte string buffer.
Loop variable i is set to from + 1 = 11, then loop iterates with i-- > to (0). On the first iteration i=10, and njs_string_new is called reading 1 byte from the out-of-bounds pointer p.

code_snippets:
p = njs_string_offset(string_prop.start, end, from);
            p = njs_utf8_next(p, end);
vulnerabilities.description: In the UTF-8 string branch, if `from` is greater than or equal to the character length of the string, `njs_string_offset` may return a pointer at or past `end`. Then `njs_utf8_next(p, end)` on such a pointer, and later `njs_utf8_prev(p)`, can produce out-of-bounds pointers, leading to out-of-bounds reads in `njs_string_new`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function receives args where value is a UTF-8 string with character length 3 but byte size > 3 (e.g., 6 bytes), and args->from = 10, args->to = 0.
The code enters the UTF-8 branch because (size_t)length != string_prop.size.
njs_string_offset is called with from=10, which exceeds the character count (3). It returns a pointer at or beyond end.
njs_utf8_next(p, end) is called with p >= end, potentially producing an invalid pointer. The loop then calls njs_utf8_prev and njs_string_new reading from out-of-bounds memory.

state.value: result of njs_utf8_prev on out-of-bounds p

commit_message:
Prevent use after free.

A very old version of the code used `result` as a simple pointer to a resource. Two years later, the pointer got changed to a `unique_ptr` but author forgot to remove the call to `Unref`. Three years after that, we finally uncover the UAF.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387924872
Change-Id: I70fb6f199164de49fac20c168132a07b84903f9b
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateEnsemble` can result in a use after free error if an attacker supplies specially crafted arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/resource_ops.cc#L55) uses a reference counted resource and decrements the refcount if the initialization fails, as it should. However, when the code was written, the resource was represented as a naked pointer but later refactoring has changed it to be a smart pointer. Thus, when the pointer leaves the scope, a subsequent `free`-ing of the resource occurs, but this fails to take into account that the refcount has already reached 0, thus the resource has been already freed. During this double-free process, members of the resource object are accessed for cleanup but they are invalid as the entire resource has been freed. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5ecec9c6fbdbc6be03295685190a45e7eee726ab. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
FTP: Fix buffer overflow in PORT parser, reported by Aaron Esau

Signed-off-by: Joachim Nilsson <troglobit@gmail.com>
cve_desc: An unauthenticated stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in common.c's handle_PORT in uftpd FTP server versions 2.10 and earlier can be abused to cause a crash and could potentially lead to remote code execution.

vulnerabilities.description: The values a, b, c, d are read from user input via sscanf as integers with no range validation. Each %d can produce up to 11 characters (e.g. -2147483648). sprintf into addr (INET_ADDRSTRLEN = 16 bytes) can overflow when these integers are outside 0-255, since the formatted string could be up to ~47 bytes.

step_by_step_execution.description: User-controlled string `str` is passed to sscanf, which parses six comma-separated integers.
sscanf stores parsed integers into a, b, c, d, e, f with no bounds checking.

sprintf formats these integers into addr buffer of size INET_ADDRSTRLEN (16 bytes). The resulting string is "-2147483648.-2147483648.-2147483648.-2147483648" which is 47 bytes plus null terminator, far exceeding the 16-byte buffer.
Stack buffer overflow occurs, potentially overwriting adjacent stack variables including sin, return address, etc.

commit_message:
exec: --cap do not set inheritable capabilities

Closes: CVE-2022-27650

Signed-off-by: Giuseppe Scrivano <gscrivan@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in crun where containers were incorrectly started with non-empty default permissions. A vulnerability was found in Moby (Docker Engine) where containers were started incorrectly with non-empty inheritable Linux process capabilities. This flaw allows an attacker with access to programs with inheritable file capabilities to elevate those capabilities to the permitted set when execve(2) runs.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one error in the loop populating process->args. The loop iterates `argc - first_arg` times with index accessing `argv[first_arg + i + 1]`. On the last iteration (i = argc - first_arg - 1), it accesses `argv[first_arg + (argc - first_arg - 1) + 1]` = `argv[argc]`, which is NULL by the C standard. Passing NULL to xstrdup (which likely wraps strdup) causes undefined behavior / NULL pointer dereference. Additionally, `process->args_len` is set to `argc` (total argument count) instead of the actual number of copied arguments, which misrepresents the array length to downstream consumers.

step_by_step_execution.description: argp_parse processes options and sets first_arg to the index of the first non-option argument.
crun_assert_n_args checks argc - first_arg >= 2 (since exec_options.process is NULL). With argc=4, first_arg=1, argc - first_arg = 3 >= 2, so the check passes.
process->args is allocated with (argc + 1) entries and process->args_len is set to argc.

state.value: 4 (argc, but only 2 args will actually be copied)

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop runs for i = 0 to argc - first_arg - 1 = 2. On i=0: argv[2] (valid). On i=1: argv[3] (valid). On i=2: argv[first_arg + 2 + 1] = argv[4] = argv[argc].
argv[argc] is NULL per C standard. xstrdup(NULL) is called, resulting in a NULL pointer dereference inside strdup.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-26j7-ww69-c4qj
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions prior to and including 2.12.1 a stack buffer overflow vulnerability affects PJSIP users that use STUN in their applications, either by: setting a STUN server in their account/media config in PJSUA/PJSUA2 level, or directly using `pjlib-util/stun_simple` API. A patch is available in commit 450baca which should be included in the next release. There are no known workarounds for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The while loop parsing attributes increments msg->attr_count without ever checking whether it exceeds the bounds of the msg->attr array. A crafted STUN message with many small attributes can cause msg->attr_count to grow beyond the fixed-size array in pjstun_msg, resulting in an out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a buffer and casts its header. msg_len is read from the header and validated against buf_len.

state.value: Value from header, e.g. a large value like 1000 bytes of attribute data

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop begins iterating over attributes. Each iteration computes the attribute pointer as &msg->attr[msg->attr_count] and writes to it.
An attacker crafts a message with many small attributes (minimum attribute size is 4 bytes for the header, padded to 4). With msg_len=1000, this yields up to 250 attributes.

If the msg->attr array has a fixed size (e.g., PJSTUN_MAX_ATTR which is typically 16), once msg->attr_count exceeds that size, the write to msg->attr[msg->attr_count] is out of bounds, corrupting adjacent memory in the pjstun_msg structure.

state.value: exceeds array bounds, e.g. 17, 18, ... up to 250

commit_message:
password: Use crypto_memneq() to compare hashes

Cryptographic verifications should be time-constant so that an attacker
cannot get information about the secrets used by observing the system,
so use crypto_memneq() rather than memcmp() to compare password hashes.

Signed-off-by: Sascha Hauer <s.hauer@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: common/password.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because strncmp is used during hash comparison.

vulnerabilities.description: strncmp is used to compare binary hash digests. strncmp stops comparing at the first null byte ('\0'). If both the computed hash and the stored hash happen to share a null byte at the same early position, strncmp will return 0 (equal) without comparing the remaining bytes. An attacker can brute-force passwords whose derived hash starts with a null byte, dramatically reducing the search space and bypassing authentication. The correct function to use is memcmp, which compares all bytes regardless of null bytes.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a user-supplied passwd and length.
passwd1_sum and passwd2_sum buffers are allocated. passwd2_sum is loaded with the stored password hash (from environment or default).

state.value: stored password hash (binary data, may contain null bytes)

step_by_step_execution.description: In the non-PBKDF2 path, digest_digest computes the hash of the attacker-supplied password into passwd1_sum. The attacker chooses a password whose hash has a '\0' as the first byte.

If the stored hash passwd2_sum also starts with 0x00 (probability ~1/256, or attacker targets this), strncmp(passwd1_sum, passwd2_sum, hash_len) is called. strncmp treats its arguments as C strings and stops at the first null byte.

state.value: 0 (equal), because comparison stops after seeing '\0' in both strings at index 0

step_by_step_execution.description: Since strncmp returns 0, ret is set to 1, indicating successful authentication, even though the remaining hash_len-1 bytes may differ entirely.

commit_message:
xfrm: policy: check policy direction value

The 'dir' parameter in xfrm_migrate() is a user-controlled byte which is used
as an array index. This can lead to an out-of-bound access, kernel lockup and
DoS. Add a check for the 'dir' value.

This fixes CVE-2017-11600.

References: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1474928
Fixes: 80c9abaabf42 ("[XFRM]: Extension for dynamic update of endpoint address(es)")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # v2.6.21-rc1
Reported-by: "bo Zhang" <zhangbo5891001@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Vladis Dronov <vdronov@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c in the Linux kernel through 4.12.3, when CONFIG_XFRM_MIGRATE is enabled, does not ensure that the dir value of xfrm_userpolicy_id is XFRM_POLICY_MAX or less, which allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds access) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an XFRM_MSG_MIGRATE xfrm Netlink message.

commit_message:
ui/vnc: limit client_cut_text msg payload size

currently a malicious client could define a payload
size of 2^32 - 1 bytes and send up to that size of
data to the vnc server. The server would allocated
that amount of memory which could easily create an
out of memory condition.

This patch limits the payload size to 1MB max.

Please note that client_cut_text messages are currently
silently ignored.

Signed-off-by: Peter Lieven <pl@kamp.de>
Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the VNC display driver in QEMU before 2.1.0 allows attachers to cause a denial of service (process crash) via a CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message, which triggers an infinite loop.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the VNC_MSG_CLIENT_CUT_TEXT handler. The expression `8 + dlen` where dlen is a uint32_t can wrap around. For example, if dlen is 0xFFFFFFF8, then `8 + dlen` equals 0, which is returned as int 0 (meaning 'message fully processed'). This causes the code to fall through and call `client_cut_text` with a massive length but a buffer that only contains 8 bytes of actual data, leading to a heap buffer over-read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with len=1, data[0]=VNC_MSG_CLIENT_CUT_TEXT. The check `if (len == 1) return 8;` causes the framework to call back with 8 bytes.
Function is called again with len=8. The attacker crafts data so that read_u32(data, 4) = 0xFFFFFFF8.
The condition `dlen > 0` is true, so the code evaluates `return 8 + dlen`. Since dlen is uint32_t, `8 + 0xFFFFFFF8` wraps around to 0. The return value (int) is 0.

Returning 0 signals the framework that the message is fully consumed. On the next invocation (or continued execution in some frameworks), the code falls through to `client_cut_text(vs, read_u32(data, 4), data + 8)` with the length parameter being 0xFFFFFFF8 but the actual data buffer being far smaller, causing a massive heap buffer over-read.

commit_message: fixed #1885
cve_desc: Segmentation fault vulnerability exists in Gpac through 1.0.1 via the gf_odf_size_descriptor function in desc_private.c when using mp4box, which causes a denial of service.

code_snippets:
if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;
		else if (esd->OCRESID == esd->ESID) esd->OCRESID = 0;
vulnerabilities.description: If gf_isom_get_esd(file, sceneT, 1) returns NULL, the code unconditionally dereferences esd when checking/setting OCRESID after the if block. This occurs whether or not the sample count is 1, since the OCRESID access is outside that if block. Similarly, if esd is NULL and sample count is 1 but the sample cannot be embedded, the code still dereferences esd.

code_snippets:
if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;
		else if (esd->OCRESID == esd->ESID) esd->OCRESID = 0;

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with IOD_Profile == GF_SDP_IOD_ISMA (or GF_SDP_IOD_ISMA_STRICT which becomes GF_SDP_IOD_ISMA).
sceneT is set to a non-zero track index during the loop (required, or function returns GF_BAD_PARAM earlier).
IOD is obtained and is non-NULL; code enters the ISMA branch.
gf_isom_get_esd(file, sceneT, 1) is called for the scene track and returns NULL (e.g., because the track has no valid ESD).
Regardless of the sample count check outcome, execution reaches 'if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;' which dereferences the NULL esd pointer.

code_snippets:
if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;
			else if (esd->OCRESID == esd->ESID) esd->OCRESID = 0;
vulnerabilities.description: If odT is non-zero and gf_isom_get_esd(file, odT, 1) returns NULL, the code dereferences esd when accessing OCRESID outside the sample count check block.

code_snippets:
if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;
			else if (esd->OCRESID == esd->ESID) esd->OCRESID = 0;

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with IOD_Profile == GF_SDP_IOD_ISMA and a file that has an OD track.
gf_isom_get_esd(file, odT, 1) returns NULL.
If gf_isom_get_sample_count(file, odT) != 1, the if block is skipped entirely. Execution reaches 'if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;' which dereferences NULL.

vulnerabilities.description: The base64 encoding output buffer limit is 2000 bytes, but the result is then sprintf'd into sdpLine (5100 bytes) and buf64 (5000 bytes). If gf_base64_encode returns a value larger than the output buffer capacity (e.g., it returns the required size rather than the written size), buf64[size64]=0 writes out of bounds of the 5000-byte stack buffer. Even within bounds, the sprintf into sdpLine with a full 5000-byte buf64 string plus a prefix could overflow sdpLine's 5100-byte buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The IOD descriptor is serialized via gf_odf_desc_write, producing a buffer of arbitrary size.
gf_base64_encode(buffer, size, buf64, 2000) is called. If the function returns the number of bytes that would be needed (not clamped to 2000), size64 could exceed 5000.
buf64[size64] = 0 writes a null terminator at an offset beyond the 5000-byte stack buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.

commit_message:
string_number_format: Correctly handles return value of snprintf

Summary: `snprintf` can return a value greater than the number of bytes copied. In case the first byte of the string is not a digit (could be '-'), size of `tmpstr` was being updated without checking `tmplen`. This resulted in either an assertion error or a heap overflow depending on whether the assertion is compiled or not.

Reviewed By: mofarrell, qianxuweiren

Differential Revision: D17327899

fbshipit-source-id: ee53875d21e02608c6d870388eecf1464de24ff1
cve_desc: Insufficient boundary checks when formatting numbers in number_format allows read/write access to out-of-bounds memory, potentially leading to remote code execution. This issue affects HHVM versions prior to 3.30.10, all versions between 4.0.0 and 4.8.5, all versions between 4.9.0 and 4.18.2, and versions 4.19.0, 4.19.1, 4.20.0, 4.20.1, 4.20.2, 4.21.0, 4.22.0, 4.23.0.

vulnerabilities.description: The overflow check for the thousand separator size uses mixed signed/unsigned arithmetic. `integral` is an `int` while `thousand_sep.size()` returns `size_t` (unsigned). The comparison `integral + thousand_sep.size() * ((integral-1) / 3) < integral` is performed in `size_t` arithmetic (due to implicit promotion), which will not detect an overflow that occurs when the result is truncated back to `int` in the assignment `integral += ((integral-1) / 3) * thousand_sep.size()`. A sufficiently large `thousand_sep` string causes `integral` to wrap to a small or negative value, leading to an undersized buffer allocation and subsequent heap buffer overflow when writing the result.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with d=1234567.0, dec=0, and a thousand_sep string of ~25 million bytes. dec_point can be any value.
d is positive, so is_negative=0. dec=0 remains. d is rounded (no change). snprintf formats d as '1234567', tmplen=7.
Since dec=0, dp is set to nullptr. integral is set to tmplen=7.

thousand_sep is not empty, so the overflow check is evaluated: integral + thousand_sep.size() * ((integral-1) / 3) is computed. (7-1)/3 = 2. 2 * 25000000 = 50000000. 7 + 50000000 = 50000007. This is computed as size_t, and 50000007 is NOT < 7 (size_t comparison), so the overflow check passes (no error raised).

integral += ((integral-1) / 3) * thousand_sep.size(). The right side evaluates to 50000000 as size_t, which is then added to integral (int). With thousand_sep.size()=25000000, result is 50000007 which fits in int. However, if thousand_sep.size() is chosen such that the product exceeds INT_MAX (e.g., thousand_sep.size()=1100000000, product=2*1100000000=2200000000 > INT_MAX=2147483647), then integral overflows to a negative value.
reslen = integral (negative). Since dec=0, no further additions. is_negative=0, so reslen stays negative.
String resstr(reslen, ReserveString) is called with a negative (or very small when interpreted) size, allocating a very small buffer.

state.value: very small or undefined behavior from negative size

step_by_step_execution.description: t = resbuf + reslen - 1 points before the buffer. The while loop copies digits from tmpbuf and writes thousand_sep via memcpy, writing far outside the allocated buffer bounds, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: out-of-bounds heap writes via *t-- and memcpy
