commit_message:
misc: fastrpc: prevent memory leak in fastrpc_dma_buf_attach

In fastrpc_dma_buf_attach if dma_get_sgtable fails the allocated memory
for a should be released.

Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20190925152742.16258-1-navid.emamdoost@gmail.com
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the fastrpc_dma_buf_attach() function in drivers/misc/fastrpc.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.9 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering dma_get_sgtable() failures, aka CID-fc739a058d99.

vulnerabilities.description: When dma_get_sgtable fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the previously allocated memory 'a', causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: Memory is allocated for 'a' via kzalloc
dma_get_sgtable is called and returns a negative error code
The error branch is taken, returning -EINVAL without calling kfree(a)

state.value: still allocated, pointer lost on return — memory leak

commit_message:
Check configure file permissions even for non-default files if still privileged

(Bug 1044, CVE-2010-4345)
cve_desc: Exim 4.72 and earlier allows local users to gain privileges by leveraging the ability of the exim user account to specify an alternate configuration file with a directive that contains arbitrary commands, as demonstrated by the spool_directory directive.

code_snippets:
uschar *s = expand_string(host_number_string);
  long int n = Ustrtol(s, &end, 0);
vulnerabilities.description: If expand_string(host_number_string) returns NULL, the result is passed directly to Ustrtol without a NULL check, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The code checks if host_number_string is not NULL and enters the block.
expand_string is called on host_number_string. If expansion fails, it returns NULL.
Ustrtol(s, &end, 0) is called with s == NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

vulnerabilities.description: When CONFIGURE_FILE_USE_NODE or CONFIGURE_FILE_USE_EUID is defined, 'suffix' points to the end of 'filename' within 'big_buffer'. The sprintf calls append up to ~280 additional bytes beyond the end of the filename. If the filename already nearly fills big_buffer, this writes past the end of big_buffer, causing a buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: string_nextinlist reads a filename into big_buffer, which has size big_buffer_size. filename points to big_buffer.

state.value: points to big_buffer, could be up to big_buffer_size - 1 chars long

step_by_step_execution.description: suffix is set to filename + Ustrlen(filename), pointing to the null terminator at the end of the filename within big_buffer.

state.value: points to end of filename string, near end of big_buffer if filename is long

step_by_step_execution.description: sprintf appends a string like '.%ld.%.256s' which can be up to ~280 bytes (1 dot + up to 20 digits for long + 1 dot + up to 256 chars for nodename). If filename already consumed most of big_buffer, this writes past the buffer boundary.

commit_message:
selinux,smack: fix subjective/objective credential use mixups

Jann Horn reported a problem with commit eb1231f73c4d ("selinux:
clarify task subjective and objective credentials") where some LSM
hooks were attempting to access the subjective credentials of a task
other than the current task.  Generally speaking, it is not safe to
access another task's subjective credentials and doing so can cause
a number of problems.

Further, while looking into the problem, I realized that Smack was
suffering from a similar problem brought about by a similar commit
1fb057dcde11 ("smack: differentiate between subjective and objective
task credentials").

This patch addresses this problem by restoring the use of the task's
objective credentials in those cases where the task is other than the
current executing task.  Not only does this resolve the problem
reported by Jann, it is arguably the correct thing to do in these
cases.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: eb1231f73c4d ("selinux: clarify task subjective and objective credentials")
Fixes: 1fb057dcde11 ("smack: differentiate between subjective and objective task credentials")
Reported-by: Jann Horn <jannh@google.com>
Acked-by: Eric W. Biederman <ebiederm@xmission.com>
Acked-by: Casey Schaufler <casey@schaufler-ca.com>
Signed-off-by: Paul Moore <paul@paul-moore.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel before 5.14.8. A use-after-free in selinux_ptrace_traceme (aka the SELinux handler for PTRACE_TRACEME) could be used by local attackers to cause memory corruption and escalate privileges, aka CID-a3727a8bac0a. This occurs because of an attempt to access the subjective credentials of another task.

commit_message:
rsi: fix a double free bug in rsi_91x_deinit()

`dev` (struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) field of adapter
(struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) is allocated  and initialized in
`rsi_init_usb_interface`. If any error is detected in information
read from the device side,  `rsi_init_usb_interface` will be
freed. However, in the higher level error handling code in
`rsi_probe`, if error is detected, `rsi_91x_deinit` is called
again, in which `dev` will be freed again, resulting double free.

This patch fixes the double free by removing the free operation on
`dev` in `rsi_init_usb_interface`, because `rsi_91x_deinit` is also
used in `rsi_disconnect`, in that code path, the `dev` field is not
 (and thus needs to be) freed.

This bug was found in v4.19, but is also present in the latest version
of kernel. Fixes CVE-2019-15504.

Reported-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Mathias Payer <mathias.payer@nebelwelt.net>
Signed-off-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Guenter Roeck <linux@roeck-us.net>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.2.9 has a Double Free via crafted USB device traffic (which may be remote via usbip or usbredir).

commit_message:
ALSA: pcm: oss: Fix race at SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC

There is a small race window at snd_pcm_oss_sync() that is called from
OSS PCM SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl; namely the function calls
snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() at first, then takes the params_lock mutex
for the rest.  When the stream is set up again by another thread
between them, it leads to inconsistency, and may result in unexpected
results such as NULL dereference of OSS buffer as a fuzzer spotted
recently.

The fix is simply to cover snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() call into the same
params_lock mutex with snd_pcm_oss_make_ready_locked() variant.

Reported-and-tested-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Jaroslav Kysela <perex@perex.cz>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/CAFcO6XN7JDM4xSXGhtusQfS2mSBcx50VJKwQpCq=WeLt57aaZA@mail.gmail.com
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220905060714.22549-1-tiwai@suse.de
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: A race condition flaw was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem due to improper locking. It could lead to a NULL pointer dereference while handling the SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl. A privileged local user (root or member of the audio group) could use this flaw to crash the system, resulting in a denial of service condition

commit_message:
Add a check for pad width to be a positive value.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 413275853
Change-Id: I261a8db9dabf5ce48a806a9e58129080c9fac619
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `StringNGrams` can be used to trigger a denial of service attack by causing an out of memory condition after an integer overflow. We are missing a validation on `pad_witdh` and that result in computing a negative value for `ngram_width` which is later used to allocate parts of the output. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer truncation from 64-bit size_t/Eigen::Index to 32-bit int when computing `splits_vec_size` and `input_data_size`. If `splits_vec.size()` equals exactly 2^31, the truncation to int produces INT_MIN (negative), causing the validation block `if (splits_vec_size > 0)` to be skipped entirely. Meanwhile, `num_batch_items = (int)(2^31 - 1) = INT_MAX`, so the main computation loops execute with unvalidated split values. This allows arbitrary out-of-bounds reads from input_data and potential out-of-bounds writes to ngrams_data.

step_by_step_execution.description: Attacker provides a `splits` tensor with exactly 2^31 (2147483648) elements, containing arbitrary SPLITS_TYPE values (e.g., negative indices or values exceeding data size). `data` tensor has a small number of strings.
input_data_size is computed by truncating data size to int. This is fine for small data. splits_vec_size is computed by truncating splits_vec.size() (2^31) to int, causing signed integer overflow.

state.value: -2147483648 (INT_MIN, due to truncation of 2^31 to int)

step_by_step_execution.description: The validation block checks `if (splits_vec_size > 0)`. Since splits_vec_size is INT_MIN (negative), the condition is false and ALL split validation is skipped. No bounds checks are performed on any split values.
num_batch_items is computed from `splits_vec.size() - 1`, which is 2^31 - 1. Truncating to int gives INT_MAX (2147483647).
The early return check `if (data->flat<tstring>().size() == 0 || splits_vec.size() == 0)` is false (data size is 5, splits size is 2^31), so execution continues past it.

The main computation loop iterates with `for (int i = 0; i < num_batch_items; ++i)`. Inside the loop, `splits_vec(i)` is used as an index into input_data WITHOUT any prior validation. Since validation was skipped, splits_vec(i) can be any value (negative, or exceeding input_data_size), causing out-of-bounds memory access.

state.value: attacker-controlled, unvalidated, can be any value

commit_message:
rsi: fix a double free bug in rsi_91x_deinit()

`dev` (struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) field of adapter
(struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) is allocated  and initialized in
`rsi_init_usb_interface`. If any error is detected in information
read from the device side,  `rsi_init_usb_interface` will be
freed. However, in the higher level error handling code in
`rsi_probe`, if error is detected, `rsi_91x_deinit` is called
again, in which `dev` will be freed again, resulting double free.

This patch fixes the double free by removing the free operation on
`dev` in `rsi_init_usb_interface`, because `rsi_91x_deinit` is also
used in `rsi_disconnect`, in that code path, the `dev` field is not
 (and thus needs to be) freed.

This bug was found in v4.19, but is also present in the latest version
of kernel. Fixes CVE-2019-15504.

Reported-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Mathias Payer <mathias.payer@nebelwelt.net>
Signed-off-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Guenter Roeck <linux@roeck-us.net>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.2.9 has a Double Free via crafted USB device traffic (which may be remote via usbip or usbredir).

vulnerabilities.description: On the fail_eps error path, the function returns without freeing 'rsi_dev', which was allocated with kzalloc at the start of the function. This leads to a memory leak whenever rsi_find_bulk_in_and_out_endpoints fails or the tx_buffer allocation fails.

step_by_step_execution.description: rsi_dev is allocated via kzalloc
adapter->rsi_dev is set to rsi_dev
rsi_find_bulk_in_and_out_endpoints returns a non-zero (failure) value
Execution jumps to fail_eps label, which does not call kfree(rsi_dev) before returning

state.value: still allocated, never freed — memory leak

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/131
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in the CalcMinMax function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick before 6.9.4-0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read and application crash) via a crafted mat file.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in ldblk calculation. MATLAB_HDR.SizeX is an unsigned 32-bit integer read from the file. The multiplications (2*, 4*, 8*) are performed as 32-bit unsigned arithmetic before casting to ssize_t, so they can wrap around to a small value. This causes a small buffer to be allocated for BImgBuff, but image->columns is set to the original large SizeX. ImportQuantumPixels then reads past the undersized BImgBuff based on image->columns, causing a heap buffer over-read/overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: MATLAB_HDR.SizeX is read from the attacker-controlled file via ReadBlobXXXLong. Attacker sets SizeX to a large value, e.g. 0x20000001.

CellType is miUINT32 (32-bit cell). ldblk is calculated as (ssize_t)(4 * MATLAB_HDR.SizeX). The multiplication 4 * 0x20000001 = 0x80000004, which wraps to 0x80000004 as unsigned 32-bit. On a system where unsigned int is 32-bit, this may be cast to ssize_t as a large or negative value depending on platform. With SizeX=0x40000000 and 8x multiplier: 8*0x40000000=0x200000000 wraps to 0x00000000 as 32-bit.
image->columns is set to the original MATLAB_HDR.SizeX value (0x40000000), and image->rows is set to MATLAB_HDR.SizeY (attacker can set to 1 for a small image).
BImgBuff is allocated with AcquireQuantumMemory((size_t)ldblk, sizeof(double)) = AcquireQuantumMemory(0, 8). This returns either NULL (caught) or a minimal/zero-size allocation.
ReadBlob(image2, ldblk=0, BImgBuff) reads 0 bytes, returns 0, which equals (ssize_t)ldblk=0, so the error check passes.

ImportQuantumPixels is called with the image (columns=0x40000000) and BImgBuff (tiny/empty buffer). It processes image->columns pixels worth of data from BImgBuff, reading far past the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer over-read.

commit_message:
Fix oobread and unaligned casts in the NE entrypoint logic ##crash

* Reported by @hmsec via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: nepocaligns
* BountyID: ec538fa4-06c6-4050-a141-f60153ddeaac
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability may allow attackers to read sensitive information or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on `bin->entry_table`. The bounds check in the inner for-loop (`tableat + off + 4 >= r_buf_size(bin->buf)`) validates against the total file buffer size, not against the actual size of the `entry_table` buffer (`EntryTableLength`). For moveable entries (bundle_type == 0xff), the code consumes 6 bytes (1 flag + 2 skip + 1 segnum + 2 segoff), but only 4 bytes of headroom are ensured. Furthermore, reads of `bundle_length` and `bundle_type` at the top of the while loop only check `tableat + off >= r_buf_size(bin->buf)` (one byte), but two bytes are read. This allows reading past the end of the `entry_table` allocation.

step_by_step_execution.description: entry_table is allocated with a size of EntryTableLength bytes. Suppose EntryTableLength = 5, and r_buf_size(bin->buf) is large.
While loop begins. off=0 < 5, check tableat+0 < buf_size passes. Reads bundle_length from entry_table[0], e.g. value 1.
off incremented to 1. Reads bundle_type from entry_table[1], e.g. value 0xff (moveable).
off incremented to 2. Inner for-loop i=0: checks tableat + 2 + 4 >= r_buf_size(buf). Since buf is large, this check passes.
off++ for flags byte → off=3. Then off += 2 (skip two bytes) → off=5.
Code reads segnum = *(bin->entry_table + 5). Since entry_table has only 5 bytes (indices 0-4), this is an out-of-bounds read.
If execution continues, off becomes 6, and r_read_le16(entry_table + 6) is another OOB read.

vulnerabilities.description: Memory leak: when breaking from the inner loop in the moveable or fixed entry cases, the already-allocated `entry` (via R_NEW0) is not freed before the break, causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: An RBinAddr *entry is allocated with R_NEW0 at the start of the for-loop iteration.
In the moveable branch (bundle_type == 0xff), after advancing off, the check `off > EntryTableLength` triggers a break.
The break exits the for-loop without freeing `entry`, leaking the allocation.

state.value: leaked, never freed or appended to list

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5050: using freed memory when searching for pattern in path

Problem:    Using freed memory when searching for pattern in path.
Solution:   Make a copy of the line.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow when the reallocation of the `files` array fails (bigger == NULL) and no previously visited files have been closed yet (old_files == max_path_depth). In this case, `files[depth + 1]` accesses `files[old_files]` which equals `files[max_path_depth]`, one element past the end of the allocated array. Subsequent writes to `files[depth].name`, `.lnum`, and `.matched` are also out-of-bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The files array is allocated with max_path_depth elements (initially 50) and old_files is set to max_path_depth.

Through 50 iterations of the main loop, each line in the current buffer and each opened include file contains an include directive pointing to a new file. Each iteration increments depth by 1 (from -1 to 0, 1, 2, ..., 49). No files reach EOF so old_files remains 50.

On the 51st include, the condition `depth + 1 == old_files` is true (50 == 50). ALLOC_MULT is called to resize the array. Under memory pressure, this allocation fails and bigger is NULL. The code falls through without resizing.
The code accesses files[depth + 1].fp = mch_fopen(...), which is files[50].fp — one past the end of the 50-element array. This is a heap out-of-bounds write.

If mch_fopen succeeds, ++depth makes depth == 50 == old_files. The code then writes to files[old_files].name (OOB read/free), increments old_files, then writes files[depth].name, files[depth].lnum, files[depth].matched — all at index 50, which is out-of-bounds.

commit_message:
Never follow symlinks when setting file flags on Linux

When opening a file descriptor to set file flags on linux, ensure
no symbolic links are followed. This fixes the case when an archive
contains a directory entry followed by a symlink entry with the same
path. The fixup code would modify file flags of the symlink target.
cve_desc: An improper link resolution flaw can occur while extracting an archive leading to changing modes, times, access control lists, and flags of a file outside of the archive. An attacker may provide a malicious archive to a victim user, who would trigger this flaw when trying to extract the archive. A local attacker may use this flaw to gain more privileges in a system.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx

* Fix HTTP request smuggling vulnerability

See GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx or CVE-2021-41136 for more info.

* 4.3.9 release note

* 5.5.1 release note

* 5.5.1
cve_desc: Puma is a HTTP 1.1 server for Ruby/Rack applications. Prior to versions 5.5.1 and 4.3.9, using `puma` with a proxy which forwards HTTP header values which contain the LF character could allow HTTP request smugggling. A client could smuggle a request through a proxy, causing the proxy to send a response back to another unknown client. The only proxy which has this behavior, as far as the Puma team is aware of, is Apache Traffic Server. If the proxy uses persistent connections and the client adds another request in via HTTP pipelining, the proxy may mistake it as the first request's body. Puma, however, would see it as two requests, and when processing the second request, send back a response that the proxy does not expect. If the proxy has reused the persistent connection to Puma to send another request for a different client, the second response from the first client will be sent to the second client. This vulnerability was patched in Puma 5.5.1 and 4.3.9. As a workaround, do not use Apache Traffic Server with `puma`.

code_snippets: size_t puma_parser_execute(puma_parser *parser, const char *buffer, size_t len, size_t off)
vulnerabilities.description: The function declares `buffer` as `const char *`, but the parser modifies the buffer in-place by casting away const and calling `snake_upcase_char((char *)p)` on HTTP header field name characters. If the caller passes a genuinely read-only memory region (e.g., memory-mapped file, string literal, or other read-only buffer), this results in a write to read-only memory, causing undefined behavior or a crash.

step_by_step_execution.description:
Function is called with a const char* buffer containing a valid HTTP request, e.g., "GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com

". The buffer may reside in read-only memory.

state.value: pointer to const/read-only memory containing HTTP request

step_by_step_execution.description: The parser processes the HTTP method 'GET', the space, the URI '/', the space, and 'HTTP/1.1', then the \r\n. It reaches state 15 (start of header line parsing).
At state 15, the character 'H' (ASCII 72) matches the token character range, so the parser takes transition tr21.

Transition tr21 executes MARK(field_start, p) to record the start of the header field, then calls snake_upcase_char((char *)p), which casts away the const qualifier and writes to the buffer location pointed to by p. If the buffer is in read-only memory, this is a write to read-only memory (undefined behavior / segfault).

state.value: const cast removed, write attempted to read-only memory

step_by_step_execution.description: For subsequent characters like 'o', 's', 't' in 'Host', transition tr23 is taken which also calls snake_upcase_char((char *)p), performing additional writes to the const buffer for each header field name character.

state.value: advancing through header field name characters
snake_upcase_char writes to each character position

Never follow symlinks when setting file flags on Linux

When opening a file descriptor to set file flags on linux, ensure
no symbolic links are followed. This fixes the case when an archive
contains a directory entry followed by a symlink entry with the same
path. The fixup code would modify file flags of the symlink target.
cve_desc: An improper link resolution flaw can occur while extracting an archive leading to changing modes, times, access control lists, and flags of a file outside of the archive. An attacker may provide a malicious archive to a victim user, who would trigger this flaw when trying to extract the archive. A local attacker may use this flaw to gain more privileges in a system.

commit_message:
FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA: Don't return 4 extra bytes at end

labels_data_count already accounts for the unicode null character at the
end of the array. There is no need in adding space for it again.

Signed-off-by: Christof Schmitt <christof.schmitt@us.ibm.com>
Reviewed-by: Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Reviewed-by: Simo Sorce <idra@samba.org>

Autobuild-User(master): Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Autobuild-Date(master): Tue Aug  6 04:03:17 CEST 2013 on sn-devel-104
cve_desc: Samba 3.6.6 through 3.6.23, 4.0.x before 4.0.18, and 4.1.x before 4.1.8, when a certain vfs shadow copy configuration is enabled, does not properly initialize the SRV_SNAPSHOT_ARRAY response field, which allows remote authenticated users to obtain potentially sensitive information from process memory via a (1) FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA or (2) FSCTL_SRV_ENUMERATE_SNAPSHOTS request.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA when computing labels_data_count. If shadow_data->num_volumes (returned by the VFS layer) is sufficiently large, the multiplication `num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)` can overflow a uint32, resulting in a small labels_data_count. This causes a small buffer to be allocated via talloc_zero_array, but the subsequent loop writes `num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)` bytes into it, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA case with max_out_len > 16, so labels is set to True.
SMB_VFS_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA returns successfully with a very large num_volumes value from the VFS layer.
labels_data_count is computed. With num_volumes=85899346 and sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)=25, the expression num_volumes * 2 * 25 = 4294967300 which overflows uint32 to a small value (e.g., 4).
*out_len is computed as 12 + labels_data_count + 4 = 20, a very small value.
The check max_out_len < *out_len passes because max_out_len (0xFFFFFFFF) >= 20.
talloc_zero_array allocates a buffer of only 20 bytes.
The loop iterates num_volumes (85899346) times, each iteration writing 2*sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)=50 bytes and advancing cur_pdata by 50 bytes, writing far beyond the 20-byte allocation.

commit_message:
Fix oobread in java parser ##crash

* Reported by @bet4it via @huntrdev
* BountyID c8f4c2de-7d96-4ad4-857a-c099effca2d6
* Reproducer: bootstrap.class
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_java_bootstrap_methods_attr_new function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0. The bug causes the program reads data past the end 2f the intented buffer. Typically, this can allow attackers to read sensitive information from other memory locations or cause a crash. More details see [CWE-125: Out-of-bounds read](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/125.html).

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4253: using freed memory when substitute with function call

Problem:    Using freed memory when substitute uses a recursive function call.
Solution:   Make a copy of the substitute text.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in `needed_len` calculation can cause a heap buffer overflow. `needed_len` is declared as `int`. When accumulating the length of already-substituted text (`len` from `STRLEN(new_start)`) plus the new copy/substitution lengths, the sum can overflow the signed 32-bit `int`, wrapping to a negative value. When negative, the check `needed_len > (int)new_start_len` evaluates to false, so the buffer `new_start` is not reallocated to a sufficient size. Subsequent `mch_memmove` and `vim_regsub_multi` calls then write beyond the allocated heap buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: A substitute command is executed on a very long line (or a line that grows through repeated substitutions with the 'g' flag), e.g. `:s/x/AAAAAA.../g` on a line with many 'x' characters.
On the first match, `new_start` is allocated. `needed_len` is computed as `copy_len + remaining_len + sublen + 1`, and `new_start_len = needed_len + 50`.
On subsequent matches (inner for(;;) loop with do_all), `len = (unsigned)STRLEN(new_start)` grows with each substitution as text is appended to new_start.

state.value: grows with each substitution, cast to int from unsigned STRLEN result

step_by_step_execution.description: `needed_len` is first computed for the new match's copy_len + p1 remainder + sublen + 1 (as int), then `needed_len += len` is executed. When `len` is large enough (e.g., approaching or exceeding INT_MAX/2), this signed addition overflows, producing a negative value.

state.value: negative (wrapped from signed int overflow, e.g. around -2 billion)

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `if (needed_len > (int)new_start_len)` compares the negative `needed_len` against the (positive) cast of `new_start_len`. The condition is false, so the buffer is NOT reallocated.

`new_end = new_start + len` points near or past the end of the allocated buffer. `mch_memmove(new_end, sub_firstline + copycol, (size_t)copy_len)` and `vim_regsub_multi(... new_end ...)` write beyond the allocated heap buffer.

state.value: new_start + len, potentially beyond allocated region
copy_len + sublen bytes written past allocation

commit_message:
handshake: check inappropriate fallback against the configured max version

That allows to operate on a server which is explicitly configured to
utilize earlier than TLS 1.2 versions.
cve_desc: The SSL protocol 3.0, as used in OpenSSL through 1.0.1i and other products, uses nondeterministic CBC padding, which makes it easier for man-in-the-middle attackers to obtain cleartext data via a padding-oracle attack, aka the "POODLE" issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The first loop accesses data[i] and data[i+1] for odd values of datalen before the datalen % 2 check is performed. When datalen is odd, the last iteration will have i = datalen-1, and data[i+1] reads one byte beyond the valid buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with an odd datalen, e.g. datalen = 5, and data points to a buffer of exactly 5 bytes.
The first for-loop begins iterating: i=0, accesses data[0] and data[1] — both valid.
Loop continues: i=2, accesses data[2] and data[3] — both valid.
Loop continues: i=4, which is less than datalen(5). data[4] is valid, but data[4+1] = data[5] is one byte past the end of the buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read.
The datalen % 2 != 0 check that would reject odd-length input is only performed later in the function, after the out-of-bounds read has already occurred.

commit_message:
FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA: Don't return 4 extra bytes at end

labels_data_count already accounts for the unicode null character at the
end of the array. There is no need in adding space for it again.

Signed-off-by: Christof Schmitt <christof.schmitt@us.ibm.com>
Reviewed-by: Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Reviewed-by: Simo Sorce <idra@samba.org>

Autobuild-User(master): Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Autobuild-Date(master): Tue Aug  6 04:03:17 CEST 2013 on sn-devel-104
cve_desc: Samba 3.6.6 through 3.6.23, 4.0.x before 4.0.18, and 4.1.x before 4.1.8, when a certain vfs shadow copy configuration is enabled, does not properly initialize the SRV_SNAPSHOT_ARRAY response field, which allows remote authenticated users to obtain potentially sensitive information from process memory via a (1) FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA or (2) FSCTL_SRV_ENUMERATE_SNAPSHOTS request.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in labels_data_count calculation in FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA. If shadow_data->num_volumes (returned by VFS) is sufficiently large, the multiplication `num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)` overflows a uint32, producing a small labels_data_count. This leads to a small buffer allocation via talloc_zero_array, but the subsequent loop writes num_volumes entries into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: SMB_VFS_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA is called and populates shadow_data with a large num_volumes value (e.g., a malicious or compromised VFS backend).

labels_data_count is computed. With sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL) = 25 and num_volumes = 0x01000001: 0x01000001 * 2 * 25 = 0x32000032, but if num_volumes is chosen such that the product wraps uint32, e.g. num_volumes ~ 85899346 (0x51EB852), then 85899346 * 50 = 4294967300 which overflows uint32 to a small value like 4.
*out_len is computed as 12 + labels_data_count = 18, a small value.
The check max_out_len < *out_len passes because max_out_len is likely large enough to be >= 18.
talloc_zero_array allocates a buffer of only 18 bytes for cur_pdata.
cur_pdata is advanced by 12 bytes for the header, leaving 6 bytes remaining in the buffer.

The loop iterates num_volumes (85899346) times, each iteration writing 2*sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL) = 50 bytes and advancing cur_pdata by 50 bytes. This massively overflows the 6 remaining bytes in the heap buffer.

state.value: ~4.29 GB total, far exceeding the 18-byte allocation

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-f5qg-pqcg-765m
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a stack buffer overflow vulnerability that affects PJSUA2 users or users that call the API `pjmedia_sdp_print(), pjmedia_sdp_media_print()`. Applications that do not use PJSUA2 and do not directly call `pjmedia_sdp_print()` or `pjmedia_sdp_media_print()` should not be affected. A patch is available on the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The initial buffer length check does not account for the total size of all format (fmt) entries. The check only validates space for media, transport, and a fixed overhead of 12+24 bytes, but the subsequent loop writes an arbitrary number of fmt strings into the buffer without any bounds checking, leading to a heap/stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a buffer `buf` of size `len` and an SDP media descriptor `m`.
The initial length check compares len against m->desc.media.slen + m->desc.transport.slen + 12 + 24. With small media/transport strings this check passes.

state.value: e.g. media.slen(5) + transport.slen(7) + 36 = 48, which is < 128, so check passes

step_by_step_execution.description: The function writes the 'm=' prefix, media name, port, transport into the buffer, consuming some bytes. p advances accordingly.
The loop iterates over fmt_count entries, writing a space and the fmt string each time without checking if p remains within bounds of buf+len.
After several iterations, p exceeds end (buf+len), and pj_memcpy writes past the buffer boundary, causing an out-of-bounds write.

commit_message:
handshake: check inappropriate fallback against the configured max version

That allows to operate on a server which is explicitly configured to
utilize earlier than TLS 1.2 versions.
cve_desc: The SSL protocol 3.0, as used in OpenSSL through 1.0.1i and other products, uses nondeterministic CBC padding, which makes it easier for man-in-the-middle attackers to obtain cleartext data via a padding-oracle attack, aka the "POODLE" issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The check for odd datalen occurs after the first loop that iterates over data in 2-byte steps. If datalen is odd, the last iteration of the loop accesses data[datalen-1] (valid) and data[datalen] (one byte out of bounds read), since the loop condition `i < datalen` allows `i = datalen - 1` when datalen is odd, and then `data[i + 1]` reads past the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with an odd datalen, e.g. datalen = 5. The data buffer has exactly 5 valid bytes (indices 0-4).
First loop iteration: i=0, accesses data[0] and data[1] — both valid.
Second loop iteration: i=2, accesses data[2] and data[3] — both valid.
Third loop iteration: i=4, loop condition 4 < 5 is true. Accesses data[4] (valid) and data[4+1] = data[5] which is one byte past the end of the buffer — out-of-bounds read.
The datalen % 2 != 0 check that would reject this input is located after this loop, so it never prevents the out-of-bounds access.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx

* Fix HTTP request smuggling vulnerability

See GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx or CVE-2021-41136 for more info.

* 4.3.9 release note

* 5.5.1 release note

* 5.5.1
cve_desc: Puma is a HTTP 1.1 server for Ruby/Rack applications. Prior to versions 5.5.1 and 4.3.9, using `puma` with a proxy which forwards HTTP header values which contain the LF character could allow HTTP request smugggling. A client could smuggle a request through a proxy, causing the proxy to send a response back to another unknown client. The only proxy which has this behavior, as far as the Puma team is aware of, is Apache Traffic Server. If the proxy uses persistent connections and the client adds another request in via HTTP pipelining, the proxy may mistake it as the first request's body. Puma, however, would see it as two requests, and when processing the second request, send back a response that the proxy does not expect. If the proxy has reused the persistent connection to Puma to send another request for a different client, the second response from the first client will be sent to the second client. This vulnerability was patched in Puma 5.5.1 and 4.3.9. As a workaround, do not use Apache Traffic Server with `puma`.

code_snippets: size_t puma_parser_execute(puma_parser *parser, const char *buffer, size_t len, size_t off)
vulnerabilities.description: The parser modifies the input buffer in-place by casting away the `const` qualifier from `buffer`. In states tr21 and tr23, `snake_upcase_char((char *)p)` writes through a pointer derived from the `const char *buffer` parameter. If the caller provides a buffer residing in read-only memory (e.g., memory-mapped read-only page, string literal), this results in undefined behavior and likely a crash (denial of service). Even with writable memory, the caller's data is unexpectedly mutated through a const pointer.

step_by_step_execution.description:
Function is called with a const char *buffer containing a valid HTTP request, e.g. 'GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com

', with off=0.

state.value: const char* pointing to HTTP request data (possibly in read-only memory)

step_by_step_execution.description: Parser processes 'GET' (states 1->2->27->28), then space (tr2 fires request_method callback), then '/' (tr6), then space (tr37), then 'HTTP/1.1' (states 6-13), then CR (tr18), then LF (state 15).

state.value: points to first character after the LF following 'HTTP/1.1\r\n'

step_by_step_execution.description: At state 15, the parser encounters 'H' from 'Host' header field name. Character matches the token range so transition tr21 is taken.

state.value: 'H' (0x48), a valid header field start character

step_by_step_execution.description: Transition tr21 executes MARK(field_start, p) and then snake_upcase_char((char *)p), which casts away const and writes to the buffer. This modifies the byte at *p in the caller's supposedly-const buffer.

state.value: points into the const buffer at the header field name character
non-const pointer used to write to read-only memory

step_by_step_execution.description: For each subsequent character of the header field name (e.g., 'o','s','t'), transition tr23 fires snake_upcase_char((char *)p) again, performing additional writes to the const buffer.

state.value: advances through each character of the header field name
being mutated in-place despite being declared const

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-f5qg-pqcg-765m
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a stack buffer overflow vulnerability that affects PJSUA2 users or users that call the API `pjmedia_sdp_print(), pjmedia_sdp_media_print()`. Applications that do not use PJSUA2 and do not directly call `pjmedia_sdp_print()` or `pjmedia_sdp_media_print()` should not be affected. A patch is available on the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

commit_message:
Fix oobread and unaligned casts in the NE entrypoint logic ##crash

* Reported by @hmsec via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: nepocaligns
* BountyID: ec538fa4-06c6-4050-a141-f60153ddeaac
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability may allow attackers to read sensitive information or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.description: In the moveable bundle case (bundle_type == 0xff), 'segnum' is read from attacker-controlled entry table data and only checked for '> 0'. There is no upper-bounds check against the actual number of segment entries. An attacker can craft a NE binary with a large segnum value, causing an out-of-bounds read on bin->segment_entries.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a crafted NE binary. bin->entry_table is non-NULL, entries and segments lists are created.
The while loop iterates over the entry table. bundle_length is read as non-zero, bundle_type is read as 0xff (moveable).
Inside the for loop, the bounds check 'tableat + off + 4 >= r_buf_size(bin->buf)' passes. off is incremented (flags byte). Then off += 2, and segnum is read from the entry table.
The check 'segnum > 0' passes (255 > 0), but there is no check that segnum <= number of segment entries. bin->segment_entries[segnum - 1] (index 254) is accessed out of bounds.

vulnerabilities.description: In the moveable bundle case, the bounds check ensures only off+4 is within buffer range, but the moveable path reads a ut16 at off+5 (relative to the check point). Specifically, the flags byte is at off+0, INT 3F word at off+1..off+2, segnum at off+3, and segoff (ut16) at off+4..off+5. Since the check only validates up to off+4, the byte at off+5 can be an out-of-bounds read from bin->entry_table.

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop reads bundle_length (non-zero) and bundle_type (0xff). off is advanced by 2.

state.value: some value O after reading bundle header

step_by_step_execution.description: The for-loop bounds check passes: tableat + O + 4 < r_buf_size(bin->buf). This means offsets O through O+4 are considered valid.
off is incremented by 1 (flags byte, at O+0), then off += 2 (INT 3F, at O+1..O+2), segnum read at O+3, off++, then ut16 segoff is read at O+4..O+5.
Byte at O+5 was not covered by the bounds check (which only ensured up to O+4). This reads one byte past the validated range from bin->entry_table.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4956: reading past end of line with "gf" in Visual block mode

Problem:    Reading past end of line with "gf" in Visual block mode.
Solution:   Do not include the NUL in the length.
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in function grab_file_name in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4956. This vulnerability is capable of crashing the software, memory modification, and possible remote execution.

vulnerabilities.description: If VIsual.col or curwin->w_cursor.col extends past the actual NUL-terminated string content (e.g., in virtual-edit mode or through stale cursor positions), *lenp can be computed larger than the actual byte length of the string starting at *pp. The subsequent access *pp + (*lenp - 1) would then read past the NUL terminator, causing an out-of-bounds read when passed to mb_ptr2len.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the non-'V' visual mode branch. Assume VIsual.lnum == curwin->w_cursor.lnum so the FAIL check is passed.
Assume curwin->w_cursor is before VIsual (LT_POS is true). *pp is set to ml_get_pos(&curwin->w_cursor) which points into a line buffer. *lenp is computed as VIsual.col - curwin->w_cursor.col + 1.

state.value: 20 (but the actual line string is only 5 bytes + NUL, e.g. via virtual edit or stale column)
pointer to start of the 5-byte line string

step_by_step_execution.description: **pp is checked against NUL. Since the line has content, **pp != NUL, so *lenp remains 21.

*lenp > 0 is true and has_mbyte is true, so the code accesses *pp + (*lenp - 1) = *pp + 20, which is 15 bytes past the NUL terminator of the 5-byte string. This is an out-of-bounds read passed to mb_ptr2len.

state.value: *pp + 20, well past the 6-byte buffer (5 chars + NUL)

commit_message:
Fix possible stack corruption with XM instrument headers claiming a size of less than 4

Closes #275
cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer over-read in pattern data parsing. The variable 'pc' is used to index into 'buffer' (allocated with size phead[y].patdata) without any bounds checking. A malformed XM file can specify rows*channum that require more data than phead[y].patdata bytes, causing reads past the end of the heap-allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: A pattern header is read from the file, including phead[y].rows and phead[y].patdata.
A buffer is allocated with size phead[y].patdata and filled from the file.
The nested loop iterates over rows * channum slots (256 * 32 = 8192 iterations), each consuming at least 1 byte from buffer via pc.

state.value: incremented each iteration, will exceed 10 quickly

step_by_step_execution.description: On iteration where pc >= phead[y].patdata (e.g., pc >= 10), buffer[pc] reads beyond the allocated heap buffer.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on smpReloc array via attacker-controlled nbu values. The nbu array is filled with bytes from the file (values 0-255), and these values are used as indices into smpReloc[MP_MAXINSSAMPS]. If any nbu byte value is >= MP_MAXINSSAMPS, the access smpReloc[nbu[sc]] reads out of bounds of the stack-allocated array.

step_by_step_execution.description: The nbu array is populated from insData, which is read from the file. The values are mp_ubyte (0-255).

state.value: array of MP_MAXINSSAMPS bytes from file, values 0-255

step_by_step_execution.description: smpReloc is a stack-allocated array of size MP_MAXINSSAMPS. Only indices 0..instr[y].samp-1 are meaningfully set.

The loop iterates over all MP_MAXINSSAMPS entries using nbu[sc] as an index into smpReloc. If nbu[sc] contains a value >= MP_MAXINSSAMPS (e.g., nbu[sc] = 200 when MP_MAXINSSAMPS = 96), this is an out-of-bounds stack read.

patch 8.2.4901: NULL pointer access when using invalid pattern

Problem:    NULL pointer access when using invalid pattern.
Solution:   Check for failed regexp program.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in function vim_regexec_string at regexp.c:2729 in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4901. NULL Pointer Dereference in function vim_regexec_string at regexp.c:2729 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) via a crafted input.

commit_message: Fix mask usage in image-compositor
cve_desc: A flaw was found in cairo's image-compositor.c in all versions prior to 1.17.4. This flaw allows an attacker who can provide a crafted input file to cairo's image-compositor (for example, by convincing a user to open a file in an application using cairo, or if an application uses cairo on untrusted input) to cause a stack buffer overflow -> out-of-bounds WRITE. The highest impact from this vulnerability is to confidentiality, integrity, as well as system availability.

vulnerabilities.description: In the else branch (when coverage is neither 0xff with sufficient run_length, nor 0x0), span coverage bytes are written into the buffer r->_buf via pointer m without any bounds checking. If the total length of consecutive non-special spans exceeds the size of r->_buf, a heap buffer overflow occurs.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with spans array. num_spans > 0, so we proceed past the early return.
m is initialized to r->_buf, a fixed-size heap buffer.
The loop iterates over spans. For a span where coverage is not 0xff (or len < run_length) and not 0x0, the else branch is taken.
*m++ writes one byte, then memset writes (len-1) bytes into the buffer. len is derived from span coordinates and is not bounded by the buffer size.

state.value: arbitrary value derived from span coordinates

step_by_step_execution.description: If multiple consecutive spans hit the else branch, or a single span has a large len, the total bytes written to r->_buf can exceed its allocated size, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Fix oobread in java parser ##crash

* Reported by @bet4it via @huntrdev
* BountyID c8f4c2de-7d96-4ad4-857a-c099effca2d6
* Reproducer: bootstrap.class
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_java_bootstrap_methods_attr_new function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0. The bug causes the program reads data past the end 2f the intented buffer. Typically, this can allow attackers to read sensitive information from other memory locations or cause a crash. More details see [CWE-125: Out-of-bounds read](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/125.html).

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when `sz` is smaller than 8 bytes. The code sets `offset = 6` and then reads a 2-byte unsigned short from `buffer` at that offset without first verifying that `sz >= offset + 2`. If the buffer is shorter than 8 bytes, this reads past the end of the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a small buffer, e.g. sz = 7 (or any value less than 8). attr is successfully allocated by r_bin_java_default_attr_new.
offset is incremented by 6.
attr is non-NULL, so we enter the if block. R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT(buffer, offset) reads 2 bytes from buffer at offset 6, i.e., buffer[6] and buffer[7]. Since sz is 7, buffer[7] is out of bounds.

state.value: buffer[6] and buffer[7], but buffer only has indices 0..6

commit_message: Add test to demonstrate buffer overrun
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds write vulnerability exists in the drill format T-code tool number functionality of Gerbv 2.7.0, dev (commit b5f1eacd), and the forked version of Gerbv (commit 71493260). A specially-crafted drill file can lead to code execution. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.description: The post-increment operator `fd++` advances the `gerb_file_t *fd` pointer after passing it to `get_line`. After this line, `fd` points one `gerb_file_t`-sized block past the original structure. All subsequent accesses to `fd` (e.g., `fd->filename`, `gerb_fgetc(fd)`, `gerb_ungetc(fd)`) operate on out-of-bounds memory. This leads to out-of-bounds reads and writes for the remainder of the function's execution when the `TCS` `T` branch is taken but the third character is not `T`, or in the error message right after `get_line`.

code_snippets: gerbv_stats_printf(stats->error_list, GERBV_MESSAGE_NOTE, -1, _("Tool change stop switch found \"%s\" at line %ld in file \"%s\""), tmps, file_line, fd->filename);

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is entered; `temp` is read via `gerb_fgetc(fd)` after the 'T' character.
The condition `(temp == 'C') && ((fd->ptr + 2) < fd->datalen)` is true, so we enter the outer if block.
Next character is read via `gerb_fgetc(fd)` and equals 'S', so we enter the inner if block.
Next character is read via `gerb_fgetc(fd)` and equals 'T', so we enter the innermost if block.
`fd->ptr` is decremented by 4 via `fd->ptr -= 4`.
`get_line(fd++)` is called. `get_line` receives the original `fd` pointer, but afterward `fd` is incremented by `sizeof(gerb_file_t)`, now pointing to out-of-bounds memory.

state.value: original_fd + 1 (points past the gerb_file_t structure, out-of-bounds)

step_by_step_execution.description: `fd->filename` is accessed in the subsequent `gerbv_stats_printf` call, reading from out-of-bounds memory relative to the original structure.

state.value: reads from out-of-bounds memory (original_fd + sizeof(gerb_file_t) + offsetof(filename))

commit_message:
media: vidtv: Check for null return of vzalloc

As the possible failure of the vzalloc(), e->encoder_buf might be NULL.
Therefore, it should be better to check it in order
to guarantee the success of the initialization.
If fails, we need to free not only 'e' but also 'e->name'.
Also, if the allocation for ctx fails, we need to free 'e->encoder_buf'
else.

Fixes: f90cf6079bf6 ("media: vidtv: add a bridge driver")
Signed-off-by: Jiasheng Jiang <jiasheng@iscas.ac.cn>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
Signed-off-by: Mauro Carvalho Chehab <mchehab@kernel.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.16-rc6. There is a lack of check after calling vzalloc() and lack of free after allocation in drivers/media/test-drivers/vidtv/vidtv_s302m.c.

vulnerabilities.description: vzalloc can return NULL on memory allocation failure, but the return value is never checked. The function continues and returns the encoder object with a NULL encoder_buf, leading to a NULL pointer dereference when the buffer is later accessed (e.g., in vidtv_s302m_encode).

step_by_step_execution.description: kzalloc succeeds for the encoder struct 'e'.
vzalloc(VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ) is called and fails, returning NULL.
The code does not check the return value and sets encoder_buf_sz to VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ, recording a non-zero size for a NULL buffer.
The function continues, eventually returning 'e' with a NULL encoder_buf. Any subsequent write or read to e->encoder_buf will dereference NULL.

state.value: returned to caller with e->encoder_buf == NULL

vulnerabilities.description: When the ctx allocation fails, only kfree(e) is called. The previously allocated e->encoder_buf (via vzalloc) and e->name (via kstrdup) are not freed, resulting in memory leaks.

step_by_step_execution.description: kzalloc succeeds for 'e'.
args.name is non-NULL, so kstrdup allocates memory for e->name.
vzalloc succeeds, allocating e->encoder_buf.
kzalloc for ctx fails, returning NULL.
Error path frees only 'e' with kfree(e), but neither e->name nor e->encoder_buf are freed, leaking both allocations.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4359: crash when repeatedly using :retab

Problem:    crash when repeatedly using :retab.
Solution:   Bail out when the line is getting too long.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

commit_message: improve fix #72558, while (u>=0) with unsigned int will always be true
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the _gdContributionsAlloc function in gd_interpolation.c in GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) before 2.2.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds memory write or memory consumption) via unspecified vectors.

vulnerabilities.description: When the allocation failure or overflow occurs on the very first iteration (u == 0), decrementing the unsigned integer `u` causes it to wrap around to UINT_MAX (4294967295). The subsequent cleanup loop `for (i=0; i<=u; i++)` then iterates up to UINT_MAX times, causing massive out-of-bounds reads and calls to gdFree on uninitialized/invalid pointers.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with valid line_length (e.g., 10) and a windows_size that causes overflow2 to return true (or a windows_size that causes gdMalloc to fail).

state.value: large value triggering overflow or allocation failure

step_by_step_execution.description: res is allocated and res->ContribRow is allocated successfully for line_length entries.

state.value: valid pointer to array of 10 ContributionType

step_by_step_execution.description: The for loop starts with u = 0. On the first iteration, overflow2(windows_size, sizeof(double)) returns 1, setting overflow_error = 1.
The error handling branch is entered. u-- is executed on an unsigned int with value 0, causing it to wrap to UINT_MAX.

The cleanup loop `for (i=0; i<=u; i++)` now iterates up to UINT_MAX times, accessing res->ContribRow[i].Weights far beyond the allocated array of 10 elements, reading uninitialized memory and passing invalid pointers to gdFree.

commit_message: Fix mask usage in image-compositor
cve_desc: A flaw was found in cairo's image-compositor.c in all versions prior to 1.17.4. This flaw allows an attacker who can provide a crafted input file to cairo's image-compositor (for example, by convincing a user to open a file in an application using cairo, or if an application uses cairo on untrusted input) to cause a stack buffer overflow -> out-of-bounds WRITE. The highest impact from this vulnerability is to confidentiality, integrity, as well as system availability.

vulnerabilities.description: When spans have coverage values that are neither 0x00 nor (0xff with sufficient run length), the code writes coverage bytes into the mask buffer pointed to by 'm' without any bounds checking against the actual allocated size of the mask image data. A sequence of such spans with large cumulative lengths can cause 'm' to advance well past the end of the heap-allocated mask buffer, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with spans array where multiple spans have coverage values other than 0x00 and 0xFF (e.g., 0x80), and span widths (spans[1].x - spans[0].x) that are large but below r->u.composite.run_length.
base and m are initialized from pixman_image_get_data(r->mask), which has a fixed allocated size.

state.value: pointer to mask pixel buffer of limited size

step_by_step_execution.description: First iteration: spans[0].coverage is 0x80 (not 0xff or 0x00), so the else branch executes. len = spans[1].x - spans[0].x. The code writes len bytes starting at m without checking if m + len exceeds the mask buffer bounds.
Subsequent iterations continue to take the else branch for non-0x00/0xff coverage spans, advancing m further each time. m is never reset to base in this branch.

state.value: base + cumulative span widths, potentially exceeding mask buffer allocation

step_by_step_execution.description: The memset and pointer increment write past the end of the heap-allocated mask buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: past end of allocated mask buffer

commit_message:
media: vidtv: Check for null return of vzalloc

As the possible failure of the vzalloc(), e->encoder_buf might be NULL.
Therefore, it should be better to check it in order
to guarantee the success of the initialization.
If fails, we need to free not only 'e' but also 'e->name'.
Also, if the allocation for ctx fails, we need to free 'e->encoder_buf'
else.

Fixes: f90cf6079bf6 ("media: vidtv: add a bridge driver")
Signed-off-by: Jiasheng Jiang <jiasheng@iscas.ac.cn>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
Signed-off-by: Mauro Carvalho Chehab <mchehab@kernel.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.16-rc6. There is a lack of check after calling vzalloc() and lack of free after allocation in drivers/media/test-drivers/vidtv/vidtv_s302m.c.

vulnerabilities.description: The encoder buffer is allocated with vzalloc() but freed with kfree() in the error path. Using kfree() on memory allocated by vzalloc() is undefined behavior and can lead to heap corruption, as vmalloc and kmalloc use different memory allocators. The correct function to use is vfree().

step_by_step_execution.description: The function allocates e with kzalloc successfully.
e->encoder_buf is allocated using vzalloc(VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ).
The context allocation ctx = kzalloc(priv_sz, GFP_KERNEL) fails, returning NULL.
Execution jumps to out_kfree_buf, which calls kfree(e->encoder_buf) on a vzalloc'd pointer, causing mismatched memory management and potential heap corruption.

state.value: vzalloc'd pointer being passed to kfree

commit_message: Add test to demonstrate buffer overrun
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds write vulnerability exists in the drill format T-code tool number functionality of Gerbv 2.7.0, dev (commit b5f1eacd), and the forked version of Gerbv (commit 71493260). A specially-crafted drill file can lead to code execution. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.description: When tool_num is outside the valid range (tool_num < TOOL_MIN or tool_num >= TOOL_MAX), the code logs an error but does NOT return. Execution continues and tool_num is used to index into image->aperture[tool_num], causing an out-of-bounds array read and potentially write.

step_by_step_execution.description: A character is read after 'T' and is determined to be a digit, so execution continues to parse a tool number.
gerb_fgetint reads the tool number from the file. An attacker-controlled file provides a large number, e.g. 9999.
tool_num is not 0, so the early return is skipped.
The bounds check fires (tool_num >= TOOL_MAX), an error is logged, but there is no return statement — execution falls through.
state->current_tool is set to the out-of-bounds tool_num, and image->aperture[tool_num] is accessed, reading out of bounds of the aperture array.
Later, if apert is NULL, the code writes to image->aperture[tool_num], performing an out-of-bounds write.

state.value: written with a newly allocated gerbv_aperture_t pointer at an out-of-bounds index

code_snippets:
gerbv_stats_printf(stats->error_list, GERBV_MESSAGE_NOTE, -1,
			_("Tool change stop switch found \"%s\" "
			    "at line %ld in file \"%s\""),
			tmps, file_line, fd->filename);
vulnerabilities.description: The expression 'fd++' performs a post-increment on the fd pointer. get_line receives the original fd, but after the call, fd points past the original gerb_file_t structure. The subsequent access to fd->filename reads from invalid memory (one struct past the original).

step_by_step_execution.description: The code reads a character after 'T' and finds 'C', then checks the next two characters are 'S' and 'T', entering the TCS/T branch.

state.value: pointer to the original gerb_file_t structure

step_by_step_execution.description: fd->ptr is decremented by 4, then get_line(fd++) is called. Post-increment means get_line gets the original fd, but fd is then incremented to point one gerb_file_t past the original.

state.value: original_fd + 1 (points to memory past the gerb_file_t struct)

step_by_step_execution.description: gerbv_stats_printf accesses fd->filename, but fd now points to invalid memory, causing an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: read from invalid memory (one struct offset past the original)

commit_message:
esp: Fix possible buffer overflow in ESP transformation

The maximum message size that can be send is bigger than
the  maximum site that skb_page_frag_refill can allocate.
So it is possible to write beyond the allocated buffer.

Fix this by doing a fallback to COW in that case.

v2:

Avoid get get_order() costs as suggested by Linus Torvalds.

Fixes: cac2661c53f3 ("esp4: Avoid skb_cow_data whenever possible")
Fixes: 03e2a30f6a27 ("esp6: Avoid skb_cow_data whenever possible")
Reported-by: valis <sec@valis.email>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow flaw was found in IPsec ESP transformation code in net/ipv4/esp4.c and net/ipv6/esp6.c. This flaw allows a local attacker with a normal user privilege to overwrite kernel heap objects and may cause a local privilege escalation threat.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1221
cve_desc: The ReadMATImageV4 function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick 7.0.8-7 uses an uninitialized variable, leading to memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.description: When HDR.imagf is 1 (complex data) and HDR.Type[1] is 2, 3, or 4 (integer types with depth 8 or 16), the code reads depth/8*columns bytes into the pixels buffer, but then passes the buffer to InsertComplexFloatRow which interprets it as an array of floats (4 bytes each). This means InsertComplexFloatRow reads beyond the data that was actually read and potentially beyond the allocated quantum pixel buffer, causing a heap buffer over-read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Read MAT4 header where HDR.Type[1] is set to 4 (unsigned 8-bit integer format) and HDR.imagf is set to 1 (complex data present).
Based on HDR.Type[1]==4, the switch statement sets depth=8 and format_type=UnsignedQuantumFormat.
SetQuantumDepth is called with depth=8, which sizes the quantum pixel buffer for 1 byte per pixel per column (approximately image->columns bytes).
The first loop reads real pixel data: ReadBlob reads depth/8*image->columns = 1*image->columns bytes into the pixels buffer. This fits within the allocated buffer.
Since HDR.imagf==1, the complex pixel reading loop starts. ReadBlob again reads depth/8*image->columns = image->columns bytes into the pixels buffer.

Since HDR.Type[1]==4 (not 0), the else branch executes InsertComplexFloatRow which casts pixels to (float*) and accesses it as an array of floats. Each float is 4 bytes, so accessing image->columns floats requires 4*image->columns bytes, but the buffer only contains image->columns bytes of valid data.

commit_message:
ip6_gre: fix ip6gre_err() invalid reads

Andrey Konovalov reported out of bound accesses in ip6gre_err()

If GRE flags contains GRE_KEY, the following expression
*(((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1)

accesses data ~40 bytes after the expected point, since
grehlen includes the size of IPv6 headers.

Let's use a "struct gre_base_hdr *greh" pointer to make this
code more readable.

p[1] becomes greh->protocol.
grhlen is the GRE header length.

Fixes: c12b395a4664 ("gre: Support GRE over IPv6")
Signed-off-by: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Reported-by: Andrey Konovalov <andreyknvl@google.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: The ip6gre_err function in net/ipv6/ip6_gre.c in the Linux kernel allows remote attackers to have unspecified impact via vectors involving GRE flags in an IPv6 packet, which trigger an out-of-bounds access.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow when computing MTU in the ICMPV6_PKT_TOOBIG case. If be32_to_cpu(info) is smaller than offset, the subtraction wraps around because mtu is __u32 (unsigned), resulting in a very large value. This huge value passes the IPV6_MIN_MTU check and is assigned directly to t->dev->mtu, setting the device MTU to an incorrect, excessively large value. An attacker can craft an ICMPv6 Packet Too Big message with a small MTU field to trigger this.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives an ICMPv6 error packet. The offset parameter indicates the offset to the GRE header within skb->data. For example, offset could be 40 (size of IPv6 header) or larger.
The GRE header is parsed, flags are checked, and grehlen is adjusted. The function passes both pskb_may_pull checks and proceeds to the tunnel lookup, which succeeds.

The switch statement enters the ICMPV6_PKT_TOOBIG case. The attacker controls the info field of the ICMPv6 message (the MTU field). Suppose info encodes a small value, e.g. be32_to_cpu(info) = 0 or any value less than offset.
mtu is computed as be32_to_cpu(info) - offset. Since mtu is __u32 (unsigned 32-bit), 0 - 40 wraps to 0xFFFFFFD8 (4294967256).
The check mtu < IPV6_MIN_MTU (1280) fails because 4294967256 > 1280.
t->dev->mtu is set to the wrapped-around huge value, corrupting the device MTU and potentially causing memory corruption or denial of service when oversized packets are processed.

commit_message:
Save stack space while handling errors

Because error handling (luaG_errormsg) uses slots from EXTRA_STACK,
and some errors can recur (e.g., string overflow while creating an
error message in 'luaG_runerror', or a C-stack overflow before calling
the message handler), the code should use stack slots with parsimony.

This commit fixes the bug "Lua-stack overflow when C stack overflows
while handling an error".
cve_desc: An issue in the component luaG_runerror of Lua v5.4.4 and below leads to a heap-buffer overflow when a recursive error occurs.

commit_message:
esp: Fix possible buffer overflow in ESP transformation

The maximum message size that can be send is bigger than
the  maximum site that skb_page_frag_refill can allocate.
So it is possible to write beyond the allocated buffer.

Fix this by doing a fallback to COW in that case.

v2:

Avoid get get_order() costs as suggested by Linus Torvalds.

Fixes: cac2661c53f3 ("esp4: Avoid skb_cow_data whenever possible")
Fixes: 03e2a30f6a27 ("esp6: Avoid skb_cow_data whenever possible")
Reported-by: valis <sec@valis.email>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow flaw was found in IPsec ESP transformation code in net/ipv4/esp4.c and net/ipv6/esp6.c. This flaw allows a local attacker with a normal user privilege to overwrite kernel heap objects and may cause a local privilege escalation threat.

commit_message:
parallels: Sanity check for s->tracks (CVE-2014-0142)

This avoids a possible division by zero.

Convert s->tracks to unsigned as well because it feels better than
surviving just because the results of calculations with s->tracks are
converted to unsigned anyway.

Signed-off-by: Kevin Wolf <kwolf@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Max Reitz <mreitz@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Stefan Hajnoczi <stefanha@redhat.com>
cve_desc: QEMU, possibly before 2.0.0, allows local users to cause a denial of service (divide-by-zero error and crash) via a zero value in the (1) tracks field to the seek_to_sector function in block/parallels.c or (2) extent_size field in the bochs function in block/bochs.c.

commit_message: improve fix #72558, while (u>=0) with unsigned int will always be true
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the _gdContributionsAlloc function in gd_interpolation.c in GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) before 2.2.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds memory write or memory consumption) via unspecified vectors.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable `u` is an `unsigned int`, so the condition `u >= 0` is always true. In the error cleanup loop, `u` will underflow past 0 to UINT_MAX, causing an infinite loop that performs out-of-bounds reads from `res->ContribRow` and calls `gdFree` on arbitrary memory. This happens on every error path in the allocation loop, including when the very first allocation (u=0) fails.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with valid `line_length` (e.g., 2) and `windows_size` such that the gdMalloc for Weights fails (e.g., very large but not triggering overflow2).
The main structures are allocated successfully. The loop begins with u=0.
First iteration (u=0): gdMalloc for Weights succeeds.
Second iteration (u=1): gdMalloc for Weights returns NULL.
Error path entered. `u--` decrements u to 0.
Cleanup loop: `while (u >= 0)` is checked — always true for unsigned int. gdFree(res->ContribRow[0].Weights) is called (valid).
`u--` decrements unsigned 0 to UINT_MAX (4294967295) due to unsigned integer underflow.
Loop condition `u >= 0` is still true. Code accesses `res->ContribRow[4294967295].Weights`, which is a massive out-of-bounds read on a 2-element array, leading to a crash or arbitrary gdFree call.

commit_message:
Bug 701785: fixed sanitizer heap-buffer-overflow in lprn_is_black().

In contrib/lips4/gdevlprn.c:lprn_is_black(), it seems that bpl is not
necessarily a multiple of lprn->nBw, so we need to explicitly avoid straying
into the next line's data.

This also avoids accessing beyond our buffer if we are already on the last
line, and so fixes the sanitizer error.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=lips2p ../bug-701785.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in lprn_is_black() in contrib/lips4/gdevlprn.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

vulnerabilities.description: If `r + h - bh` is negative (i.e., the sum of `r` and `h` is less than `bh`), the C `%` operator preserves the sign of the dividend, making `y0` negative. This negative `y0` is then used to compute an index into `lprn->ImageBuf`, resulting in an out-of-bounds (negative) memory read when `p[x]` is accessed.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with parameters where `r + h < bh`. For example, r=0, h=1, and lprn->nBh (bh) = 8.
maxY is computed as (BlockLine / nBh) * nBh. Assume BlockLine=64, nBh=8, so maxY=64.
y0 is computed as (r + h - bh) % maxY = (0 + 1 - 8) % 64 = (-7) % 64. In C, this yields -7.
The for loop starts with y=0. The index into ImageBuf is (y0 + y) * bpl + bx * nBw = (-7) * bpl + bx * nBw, which is a negative offset.
The pointer p is set to an address before the start of ImageBuf. Accessing p[x] reads memory out of bounds before the buffer.

commit_message:
sctp: account stream padding length for reconf chunk

sctp_make_strreset_req() makes repeated calls to sctp_addto_chunk()
which will automatically account for padding on each call. inreq and
outreq are already 4 bytes aligned, but the payload is not and doing
SCTP_PAD4(a + b) (which _sctp_make_chunk() did implicitly here) is
different from SCTP_PAD4(a) + SCTP_PAD4(b) and not enough. It led to
possible attempt to use more buffer than it was allocated and triggered
a BUG_ON.

Cc: Vlad Yasevich <vyasevich@gmail.com>
Cc: Neil Horman <nhorman@tuxdriver.com>
Cc: Greg KH <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
Fixes: cc16f00f6529 ("sctp: add support for generating stream reconf ssn reset request chunk")
Reported-by: Eiichi Tsukata <eiichi.tsukata@nutanix.com>
Signed-off-by: Eiichi Tsukata <eiichi.tsukata@nutanix.com>
Signed-off-by: Marcelo Ricardo Leitner <marcelo.leitner@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Marcelo Ricardo Leitner <mleitner@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Xin Long <lucien.xin@gmail.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/b97c1f8b0c7ff79ac4ed206fc2c49d3612e0850c.1634156849.git.mleitner@redhat.com
Signed-off-by: Jakub Kicinski <kuba@kernel.org>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the sctp_make_strreset_req function in net/sctp/sm_make_chunk.c in the SCTP network protocol in the Linux kernel with a local user privilege access. In this flaw, an attempt to use more buffer than is allocated triggers a BUG_ON issue, leading to a denial of service (DOS).

vulnerabilities.description: The computation of stream_len uses __u16 arithmetic, so stream_num * sizeof(__u16) can overflow a 16-bit value when stream_num is large (e.g., stream_num > 32767). This results in a small stream_len, causing a smaller buffer allocation via sctp_make_reconf, but the actual sctp_addto_chunk calls may write more data than expected, or the outlen/inlen calculations themselves overflow __u16, leading to a buffer overflow when data is written to the undersized chunk.

step_by_step_execution.description: stream_num is a __u16 parameter. stream_len = stream_num * sizeof(__u16) is computed in __u16 arithmetic.
outlen is computed with the truncated stream_len, resulting in a small value.
sctp_make_reconf allocates a chunk based on the small outlen + inlen value.

sctp_addto_chunk writes sizeof(outreq) bytes for the header, then stream_len (64) bytes from stream_list. However, the caller may have provided stream_num=32800 entries expecting all to be written. The param_hdr.length is set to the truncated outlen, causing protocol-level inconsistency. Additionally, if outlen + inlen itself overflows __u16, the allocation is even smaller while subsequent writes use the pre-overflow sizes.
