commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

commit_message:
Bluetooth: virtio_bt: fix memory leak in virtbt_rx_handle()

On the reception of packets with an invalid packet type, the memory of
the allocated socket buffers is never freed. Add a default case that frees
these to avoid a memory leak.

Fixes: afd2daa26c7a ("Bluetooth: Add support for virtio transport driver")
Signed-off-by: Soenke Huster <soenke.huster@eknoes.de>
Signed-off-by: Marcel Holtmann <marcel@holtmann.org>
cve_desc: drivers/bluetooth/virtio_bt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.3 has a memory leak (socket buffers have memory allocated but not freed).

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1221
cve_desc: The ReadMATImageV4 function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick 7.0.8-7 uses an uninitialized variable, leading to memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.description: The return values of SetQuantumFormat, SetQuantumDepth, and SetQuantumEndian are each assigned to `status` but immediately overwritten by the next call, so failures are never checked. If SetQuantumDepth fails (e.g., internal reallocation fails), the quantum pixel buffer remains sized for the original/default depth. The subsequent ReadBlob then writes `depth/8 * image->columns` bytes into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The MAT4 header is read. HDR.Type[1] is parsed, and based on its value, `depth` is set (e.g., depth=64 for Type[1]==0).
image->columns is set from HDR.nRows and image->rows from HDR.nCols. SetImageExtent validates and allocates image pixel cache.
AcquireQuantumInfo is called, which allocates a quantum pixel buffer based on the image's current depth (default, likely 8-bit at this point).

state.value: allocated with pixel buffer sized for default depth (8 bits) * image->columns

step_by_step_execution.description: SetQuantumDepth(image, quantum_info, 64) is called to resize the buffer for 64-bit depth. If this internal reallocation fails, it returns MagickFalse and the buffer remains at its original smaller size.
The status from SetQuantumDepth is immediately overwritten by SetQuantumEndian, so the failure is silently ignored.

state.value: overwritten by SetQuantumEndian return value, depth failure lost

step_by_step_execution.description: ReadBlob is called with size depth/8*image->columns = 8*100000 = 800000 bytes, writing into the pixels buffer that is only ~100000 bytes. This causes a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
sctp: account stream padding length for reconf chunk

sctp_make_strreset_req() makes repeated calls to sctp_addto_chunk()
which will automatically account for padding on each call. inreq and
outreq are already 4 bytes aligned, but the payload is not and doing
SCTP_PAD4(a + b) (which _sctp_make_chunk() did implicitly here) is
different from SCTP_PAD4(a) + SCTP_PAD4(b) and not enough. It led to
possible attempt to use more buffer than it was allocated and triggered
a BUG_ON.

Cc: Vlad Yasevich <vyasevich@gmail.com>
Cc: Neil Horman <nhorman@tuxdriver.com>
Cc: Greg KH <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
Fixes: cc16f00f6529 ("sctp: add support for generating stream reconf ssn reset request chunk")
Reported-by: Eiichi Tsukata <eiichi.tsukata@nutanix.com>
Signed-off-by: Eiichi Tsukata <eiichi.tsukata@nutanix.com>
Signed-off-by: Marcelo Ricardo Leitner <marcelo.leitner@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Marcelo Ricardo Leitner <mleitner@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Xin Long <lucien.xin@gmail.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/b97c1f8b0c7ff79ac4ed206fc2c49d3612e0850c.1634156849.git.mleitner@redhat.com
Signed-off-by: Jakub Kicinski <kuba@kernel.org>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the sctp_make_strreset_req function in net/sctp/sm_make_chunk.c in the SCTP network protocol in the Linux kernel with a local user privilege access. In this flaw, an attempt to use more buffer than is allocated triggers a BUG_ON issue, leading to a denial of service (DOS).

vulnerabilities.description: The computation of stream_len as `stream_num * sizeof(__u16)` is done in a __u16 variable, which can overflow/wrap around for large stream_num values (e.g., stream_num > 32767). This results in a small stream_len, causing a small buffer to be allocated via sctp_make_reconf, but then the actual sctp_addto_chunk calls write stream_len bytes from stream_list. While stream_len itself wraps, the inconsistency between the allocated size (based on wrapped outlen/inlen) and what is written can lead to issues. More critically, outlen and inlen are also __u16 and computed as `(sizeof(struct) + stream_len)` which can also overflow, leading to a smaller allocation than the data actually written to the chunk.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a large stream_num (e.g., 32764), out=true, in=false, and a valid stream_list pointer.
stream_len is computed as stream_num * sizeof(__u16) = 32764 * 2 = 65528, stored in __u16. This fits in __u16.
outlen is computed as (sizeof(outreq) + stream_len) * out. sizeof(outreq) is typically 16 bytes. So outlen = 16 + 65528 = 65544. But 65544 > 65535 (__u16 max), so outlen wraps to 65544 - 65536 = 8.
inlen = 0 since in=false.
sctp_make_reconf allocates a chunk with space for SCTP_PAD4(8) + SCTP_PAD4(0) = 8 bytes of payload.

Since outlen (8) is non-zero, the code enters the outlen branch and calls sctp_addto_chunk(retval, sizeof(outreq), &outreq) writing 16 bytes of outreq header. This already exceeds the 8 bytes allocated.
Then sctp_addto_chunk(retval, stream_len, stream_list) writes 65528 bytes from stream_list, massively overflowing the buffer.

commit_message:
Bug 701785: fixed sanitizer heap-buffer-overflow in lprn_is_black().

In contrib/lips4/gdevlprn.c:lprn_is_black(), it seems that bpl is not
necessarily a multiple of lprn->nBw, so we need to explicitly avoid straying
into the next line's data.

This also avoids accessing beyond our buffer if we are already on the last
line, and so fixes the sanitizer error.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=lips2p ../bug-701785.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in lprn_is_black() in contrib/lips4/gdevlprn.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

vulnerabilities.description: If `r + h - bh` is negative, the C `%` operator preserves the sign, so `y0` will be a negative value. This leads to a negative index into `lprn->ImageBuf`, causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with parameters where `r + h` is less than `bh` (e.g., r=0, h=1, and lprn->nBh (bh) is larger, say 8).
maxY is computed as `(lprn->BlockLine / lprn->nBh) * lprn->nBh`. Assuming BlockLine >= nBh, maxY is a positive value, e.g. 64.
y0 is computed as `(r + h - bh) % maxY = (0 + 1 - 8) % 64 = (-7) % 64`. In C, this yields -7 (sign of dividend is preserved).
The loop begins with y=0. The index into ImageBuf is `(y0 + y) * bpl + bx * lprn->nBw = (-7 + 0) * bpl + bx * nBw`, which is a negative offset.
The pointer `p` points before the start of `lprn->ImageBuf`. The subsequent read `p[x] != 0` performs an out-of-bounds read from memory before the buffer.

commit_message:
atlantic: Fix OOB read and write in hw_atl_utils_fw_rpc_wait

This bug report shows up when running our research tools. The
reports is SOOB read, but it seems SOOB write is also possible
a few lines below.

In details, fw.len and sw.len are inputs coming from io. A len
over the size of self->rpc triggers SOOB. The patch fixes the
bugs by adding sanity checks.

The bugs are triggerable with compromised/malfunctioning devices.
They are potentially exploitable given they first leak up to
0xffff bytes and able to overwrite the region later.

The patch is tested with QEMU emulater.
This is NOT tested with a real device.

Attached is the log we found by fuzzing.

commit_message:
Improve fix for avoiding huge number of tiny dashes

Some pathological cases were not caught by the previous fix.

Fixes: QTBUG-95239
Pick-to: 6.2 6.1 5.15
Change-Id: I0337ee3923ff93ccb36c4d7b810a9c0667354cc5
Reviewed-by: Robert Löhning <robert.loehning@qt.io>
cve_desc: Qt 5.x before 5.15.6 and 6.x through 6.1.2 has an out-of-bounds write in QOutlineMapper::convertPath (called from QRasterPaintEngine::fill and QPaintEngineEx::stroke).

vulnerabilities.description: When a CurveToElement is encountered in the types array, the code accesses points[0] through points[5] (6 qreal values, i.e., 3 coordinate pairs). However, the while loop only checks `points < lastPoint` at the beginning of each iteration, which only guarantees that at least one coordinate pair (2 qreals) remains. If a malformed QVectorPath has a CurveToElement type near the end of the path but fewer than 3 remaining points, this results in an out-of-bounds read of up to 4 qreal values past the buffer. This pattern appears in both the non-cosmetic and cosmetic pen code paths.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a QVectorPath `path` that has element types. Suppose the path has elementCount() == 2 (so 2 elements, meaning 4 qreal values in the points array), and types[0] is MoveToElement, types[1] is CurveToElement.
The first iteration of the while loop: points < lastPoint is true. types[0] is MoveToElement, so moveTo(points[0], points[1]) is called, then points += 2, types += 1.
Second iteration: points < lastPoint check passes (original_points + 2 < original_points + 4). types[1] is CurveToElement, so the code attempts to access points[0] through points[5].

state.value: 2 (only points[0] and points[1] are in bounds)
out-of-bounds read of 4 qreal values beyond the allocated buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: The out-of-bounds memory is read and passed to cubicTo(), potentially leaking memory contents or causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.description: When the types array contains an unrecognized element type, the default case in the switch statement does not advance either the `points` or `types` pointer. This causes the while loop (`while (points < lastPoint)`) to loop infinitely on the same unrecognized type, resulting in a denial-of-service (infinite loop / hang).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a QVectorPath with a types array containing an invalid/unrecognized element type value at some index.

state.value: some value not matching MoveToElement, LineToElement, or CurveToElement

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop condition `points < lastPoint` is true, so the loop body executes. The switch falls to the default case.
Since neither `points` nor `types` is advanced, the loop condition remains true and the same default case is hit indefinitely, causing an infinite loop.

commit_message:
Bluetooth: virtio_bt: fix memory leak in virtbt_rx_handle()

On the reception of packets with an invalid packet type, the memory of
the allocated socket buffers is never freed. Add a default case that frees
these to avoid a memory leak.

Fixes: afd2daa26c7a ("Bluetooth: Add support for virtio transport driver")
Signed-off-by: Soenke Huster <soenke.huster@eknoes.de>
Signed-off-by: Marcel Holtmann <marcel@holtmann.org>
cve_desc: drivers/bluetooth/virtio_bt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.3 has a memory leak (socket buffers have memory allocated but not freed).

commit_message:
Fix possible stack corruption with XM instrument headers claiming a size of less than 4

Closes #275
cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

vulnerabilities.description: When header->ver is 0x104 and instr[y].size is read from the file as a value less than 4 (e.g., 0, 1, 2, or 3), the expression `instr[y].size - 4` underflows (unsigned integer underflow) to a very large value. This causes `f.read` to attempt to read up to ~4GB into a 29-byte stack buffer, resulting in a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The file header is parsed and header->ver is set to 0x104.
Execution enters the ver==0x104 instrument loading block. For instrument y, instr[y].size is read from the file.
The condition `instr[y].size < 29` is true, so we enter the branch with a 29-byte stack buffer.
The expression `instr[y].size - 4` is computed. Since instr[y].size is an unsigned 32-bit integer with value 2, this underflows to 0xFFFFFFFE.
f.read(buffer, 1, 0xFFFFFFFE) attempts to read ~4GB into the 29-byte stack buffer, causing a massive stack buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: During pattern data parsing, the index `pc` into the heap-allocated `buffer` (of size phead[y].patdata) is never bounds-checked. The outer loops iterate over rows*channels which is determined by phead[y].rows and header->channum, while the buffer size is determined by phead[y].patdata. A crafted file can provide a small patdata value but large rows/channum values, causing `pc` to exceed the buffer bounds, resulting in a heap buffer over-read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Pattern header is read. phead[y].rows and phead[y].patdata are read from the file. An attacker sets rows*channum to require more data than patdata bytes.
A buffer of size phead[y].patdata (10 bytes) is heap-allocated and filled from the file.
The nested loop iterates over rows (256) * channels (32) = 8192 slots. Each slot consumes 1-5 bytes from buffer via pc. After a few iterations, pc exceeds 10.
buffer[pc] is read out of bounds of the heap allocation, causing a heap buffer over-read.

vulnerabilities.description: The `nbu` array is populated from file data and contains byte values (0-255). These values are used as indices into `smpReloc[MP_MAXINSSAMPS]`. If MP_MAXINSSAMPS is less than 256 (typically 96 for XM format), a crafted file can set nbu values >= MP_MAXINSSAMPS, causing an out-of-bounds read on the smpReloc stack array. In the v0x102 path, smpReloc is also not fully initialized, compounding the issue.

step_by_step_execution.description: Instrument data is read. nbu is filled from insData which comes from file: memcpy(nbu, insDataPtr, MP_MAXINSSAMPS). An attacker sets nbu bytes to values >= MP_MAXINSSAMPS (e.g., 200).
smpReloc is an array of MP_MAXINSSAMPS elements on the stack. In the v0x102 path only indices 0..instr[y].samp-1 are written.
The loop accesses smpReloc[nbu[sc]] where nbu[sc]=200, which is beyond the array bounds.

commit_message:
atlantic: Fix OOB read and write in hw_atl_utils_fw_rpc_wait

This bug report shows up when running our research tools. The
reports is SOOB read, but it seems SOOB write is also possible
a few lines below.

In details, fw.len and sw.len are inputs coming from io. A len
over the size of self->rpc triggers SOOB. The patch fixes the
bugs by adding sanity checks.

The bugs are triggerable with compromised/malfunctioning devices.
They are potentially exploitable given they first leak up to
0xffff bytes and able to overwrite the region later.

The patch is tested with QEMU emulater.
This is NOT tested with a real device.

Attached is the log we found by fuzzing.

BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in
	hw_atl_utils_fw_upload_dwords+0x393/0x3c0 [atlantic]
Read of size 4 at addr ffff888016260b08 by task modprobe/213

commit_message: Added missing allocation checks
cve_desc: Array index error in the (1) dtoa implementation in dtoa.c (aka pdtoa.c) and the (2) gdtoa (aka new dtoa) implementation in gdtoa/misc.c in libc, as used in multiple operating systems and products including in FreeBSD 6.4 and 7.2, NetBSD 5.0, OpenBSD 4.5, Mozilla Firefox 3.0.x before 3.0.15 and 3.5.x before 3.5.4, K-Meleon 1.5.3, SeaMonkey 1.1.8, and other products, allows context-dependent attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via a large precision value in the format argument to a printf function, which triggers incorrect memory allocation and a heap-based buffer overflow during conversion to a floating-point number.

vulnerabilities.description: If MALLOC returns NULL (allocation failure), the code unconditionally dereferences `rv` to set `rv->sign` and `rv->wds`, resulting in a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with some value of k.
The mutex is locked and freelist[k] is checked. Assume freelist[k] is NULL (no cached entry).

state.value: NULL (freelist[k] is NULL, so the if-condition is false)

step_by_step_execution.description: In the else branch, x is computed and MALLOC is called. If the system is out of memory, MALLOC returns NULL.
The mutex is unlocked, and rv->sign and rv->wds are written through the NULL pointer, causing a NULL pointer dereference / crash.

vulnerabilities.description: The parameter `k` is used without bounds checking as an index into the `freelist` array, and to compute `1 << k`. A negative or overly large value of `k` leads to an out-of-bounds array access on `freelist` and undefined behavior from the shift operation.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with an attacker-influenced or unchecked value of k (e.g., k = 50 or k = -1).
freelist[k] is accessed. Since k is not bounds-checked, this reads out-of-bounds memory from the freelist array.

state.value: out of range for freelist array

step_by_step_execution.description: If the out-of-bounds read returned NULL or a falsy value, the else branch computes x = 1 << k. For k >= 31 (on 32-bit int), this is undefined behavior (signed integer overflow). For k < 0, shifting by a negative amount is also undefined.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-3qx3-cg72-wrh9
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming RTCP BYE message contains a reason's length, this declared length is not checked against the actual received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP. A malicious actor can send a RTCP BYE message with an invalid reason length. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

commit_message:
Improve fix for avoiding huge number of tiny dashes

Some pathological cases were not caught by the previous fix.

Fixes: QTBUG-95239
Pick-to: 6.2 6.1 5.15
Change-Id: I0337ee3923ff93ccb36c4d7b810a9c0667354cc5
Reviewed-by: Robert Löhning <robert.loehning@qt.io>
cve_desc: Qt 5.x before 5.15.6 and 6.x through 6.1.2 has an out-of-bounds write in QOutlineMapper::convertPath (called from QRasterPaintEngine::fill and QPaintEngineEx::stroke).

vulnerabilities.description: When the path's element types array contains a CurveToElement, the code accesses 6 consecutive qreal values (points[0]..points[5]) and advances the types pointer by 3. However, the loop guard only checks `points < lastPoint`, which guarantees at least 1 remaining value but not 6. A malformed QVectorPath whose elementCount does not account for enough points for all CurveToElements will cause an out-of-bounds read. This occurs in both the non-cosmetic and cosmetic pen code paths.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a QVectorPath `path`. Suppose path.elementCount() returns 2 and the types array is {CurveToElement, CurveToDataElement}.
lastPoint is computed as points + (pointCount << 1), i.e. points + 4. The points buffer has 4 qreal values (2 elements * 2 coordinates).
The while loop checks `points < lastPoint`. Since points < points+4, the condition is true and the loop body executes.

The switch matches CurveToElement. The code attempts to read points[0] through points[5], i.e. 6 qreal values. However, only 4 values (indices 0-3) are within the buffer. Accessing points[4] and points[5] is an out-of-bounds read.
The types pointer is also advanced by 3, but only 2 elements exist in the types array, causing an out-of-bounds read on the types array as well.

state.value: advanced 1 past the end of the types array of size 2

vulnerabilities.description: If the types array contains an unexpected element type (not MoveTo, LineTo, or CurveTo), the default case executes `break` without advancing either the `points` or `types` pointer. Since the loop condition `while (points < lastPoint)` remains true, this leads to an infinite loop, causing a denial of service.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a QVectorPath whose types array contains an invalid/unexpected element type value (e.g., CurveToDataElement at a position where it is not preceded by CurveToElement).
The while loop checks `points < lastPoint`, which is true.
The switch statement does not match MoveToElement, LineToElement, or CurveToElement. The default case runs `break`, exiting the switch but not advancing `points` or `types`.
The loop condition is re-evaluated. Since neither `points` nor `types` changed, `points < lastPoint` is still true. The loop repeats indefinitely.

commit_message: check max member count in class
cve_desc: sqclass.cpp in Squirrel through 2.2.5 and 3.x through 3.1 allows an out-of-bounds read (in the core interpreter) that can lead to Code Execution. If a victim executes an attacker-controlled squirrel script, it is possible for the attacker to break out of the squirrel script sandbox even if all dangerous functionality such as File System functions has been disabled. An attacker might abuse this bug to target (for example) Cloud services that allow customization via SquirrelScripts, or distribute malware through video games that embed a Squirrel Engine.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0011: reading beyond the end of the line with put command

Problem:    Reading beyond the end of the line with put command.
Solution:   Adjust the end mark position.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

code_snippets:
totlen = count * (yanklen + spaces) + bd.startspaces + bd.endspaces;
	    newp = alloc(totlen + oldlen + 1);
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in buffer size calculation. In both the MBLOCK and MCHAR (y_size==1) code paths, 'totlen' is validated to not exceed INT_MAX, but the subsequent allocation size 'totlen + oldlen + 1' is computed using 'int' arithmetic, which can overflow when 'totlen' is close to INT_MAX and 'oldlen' (length of the current line) is non-trivial. This results in a small buffer being allocated, followed by writes that exceed the allocated size (heap buffer overflow).

code_snippets:
mch_memmove(ptr, oldp + bd.textcol + delcount,
				(size_t)(oldlen - bd.textcol - delcount + 1));

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a block-mode yank register (y_type == MBLOCK) and a large 'count' value.

state.value: large value, e.g., close to INT_MAX / (yanklen + spaces)

step_by_step_execution.description: The overflow guard checks that count <= (INT_MAX - (bd.startspaces + bd.endspaces)) / (yanklen + spaces). This passes, so totlen is computed as an int value near INT_MAX.
oldlen is computed as STRLEN(oldp), the length of the current line in the buffer. This can be a non-trivial positive value.

The allocation size 'totlen + oldlen + 1' is computed in int arithmetic. Since totlen is close to INT_MAX and oldlen > 0, the addition overflows the int type, wrapping to a small positive or negative value. When passed to alloc() (which takes size_t), a small positive value results in a small heap allocation.

state.value: overflowed int, e.g. (INT_MAX - 10) + 100 + 1 wraps to ~90 (small value)
points to a heap buffer of ~90 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: Subsequent mch_memmove calls write bd.textcol bytes from oldp, then bd.startspaces spaces, then count * yanklen bytes of yank data, then bd.endspaces spaces, then remaining old line data — all far exceeding the allocated buffer size, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: totlen + oldlen + 1 (the true, non-overflowed value, close to INT_MAX + 91)

commit_message:
ip6_gre: fix ip6gre_err() invalid reads

Andrey Konovalov reported out of bound accesses in ip6gre_err()

If GRE flags contains GRE_KEY, the following expression
*(((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1)

accesses data ~40 bytes after the expected point, since
grehlen includes the size of IPv6 headers.

Let's use a "struct gre_base_hdr *greh" pointer to make this
code more readable.

p[1] becomes greh->protocol.
grhlen is the GRE header length.

Fixes: c12b395a4664 ("gre: Support GRE over IPv6")
Signed-off-by: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Reported-by: Andrey Konovalov <andreyknvl@google.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: The ip6gre_err function in net/ipv6/ip6_gre.c in the Linux kernel allows remote attackers to have unspecified impact via vectors involving GRE flags in an IPv6 packet, which trigger an out-of-bounds access.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when computing the GRE key for tunnel lookup. The variable `grehlen` includes the full offset from the start of the skb data (i.e., `offset + GRE header fields`), but the pointer `p` already starts at `skb->data + offset`. The expression `((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1` effectively double-counts `offset`, reading far past the validated data. For example, if `offset` is 40, `grehlen` with GRE_KEY (no CSUM) is 48, so the code reads at `p + (48/4 - 1) = p + 44 bytes = skb->data + 84`, but `pskb_may_pull` only guaranteed 48 bytes are available.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry: `offset` parameter represents the byte offset of the GRE header from `skb->data`. For an IPv6 header, this is typically 40.
Pointer `p` is set to point at the start of the GRE header within skb->data.
Flags are read from p[0]. Assume GRE_KEY is set but GRE_CSUM, GRE_VERSION, GRE_ROUTING are not set.
Since GRE_KEY is set, grehlen is increased by 4. GRE_CSUM is not set so no further increase.
pskb_may_pull(skb, grehlen) is called, ensuring 48 bytes are linearly accessible from skb->data. This succeeds. Pointers ipv6h and p are refreshed.

The GRE key is read via `*(((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1)`. This computes: ((__be32 *)p) + (48/4) - 1 = ((__be32 *)p) + 11, which is p + 44 bytes = skb->data + 84. However, only 48 bytes from skb->data are guaranteed to be accessible, so this reads 36 bytes past the end of validated data.

commit_message:
bpf, ringbuf: Deny reserve of buffers larger than ringbuf

A BPF program might try to reserve a buffer larger than the ringbuf size.
If the consumer pointer is way ahead of the producer, that would be
successfully reserved, allowing the BPF program to read or write out of
the ringbuf allocated area.

Reported-by: Ryota Shiga (Flatt Security)
Fixes: 457f44363a88 ("bpf: Implement BPF ring buffer and verifier support for it")
Signed-off-by: Thadeu Lima de Souza Cascardo <cascardo@canonical.com>
Signed-off-by: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Acked-by: Andrii Nakryiko <andrii@kernel.org>
Acked-by: Alexei Starovoitov <ast@kernel.org>
cve_desc: The eBPF RINGBUF bpf_ringbuf_reserve() function in the Linux kernel did not check that the allocated size was smaller than the ringbuf size, allowing an attacker to perform out-of-bounds writes within the kernel and therefore, arbitrary code execution. This issue was fixed via commit 4b81ccebaeee ("bpf, ringbuf: Deny reserve of buffers larger than ringbuf") (v5.13-rc4) and backported to the stable kernels in v5.12.4, v5.11.21, and v5.10.37. It was introduced via 457f44363a88 ("bpf: Implement BPF ring buffer and verifier support for it") (v5.8-rc1).

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4646: using buffer line after it has been freed

Problem:    Using buffer line after it has been freed in old regexp engine.
Solution:   After getting mark get the line again.
cve_desc: Use after free in utf_ptr2char in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4646.

vulnerabilities.description: In the MULTIBYTECODE case, `rex.input += len` is executed unconditionally after both the composing-character path and the non-composing byte-comparison path, regardless of whether status is RA_MATCH or RA_NOMATCH. When the match fails (RA_NOMATCH), `rex.input` is still advanced by `len` (the length of the operand, not the input), which can move the pointer past the NUL terminator of the input string. Subsequent reads through `rex.input` in the regex engine then access out-of-bounds memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: The MULTIBYTECODE case is entered. `opnd = OPERAND(scan)` gets the operand bytes, and `len = (*mb_ptr2len)(opnd)` computes the operand's multibyte character length (e.g., 3 for a 3-byte UTF-8 character).

state.value: e.g., 3 (length of the multibyte operand character)
pointer to the operand bytes in the compiled regex program

step_by_step_execution.description: The code checks `enc_utf8 && utf_iscomposing(opndc)`. Assuming this is false (the operand is a regular multibyte character, not a composing character), we enter the `else` branch for byte-by-byte comparison.

state.value: true or false, but utf_iscomposing(opndc) is false
points to current position in input string, e.g., a short string like "a\0"

step_by_step_execution.description: The byte-comparison loop `for (i = 0; i < len; ++i)` compares `opnd[i]` with `rex.input[i]`. When `rex.input[i]` is NUL (end of string) but `opnd[i]` is not, the comparison fails, status is set to RA_NOMATCH, and the inner for-loop breaks.

Execution reaches `rex.input += len` unconditionally. Since `len` is the operand length (e.g., 3) but the input string from the current position has only 1 valid byte before NUL, `rex.input` is advanced 3 bytes past its current position, going past the NUL terminator.

state.value: now points 2 bytes past the NUL terminator of the input string (out of bounds)

step_by_step_execution.description: Although status is RA_NOMATCH causing the inner loop to break, `rex.input` is now pointing to out-of-bounds memory. Depending on the state-machine handling (e.g., if a restore doesn't happen before the next read, or in the composing-character path where RA_NOMATCH leaves `len` as the operand length), subsequent accesses to `*rex.input` or `rex.input[...]` perform out-of-bounds reads.

state.value: out-of-bounds pointer past end of input buffer

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4646: using buffer line after it has been freed

Problem:    Using buffer line after it has been freed in old regexp engine.
Solution:   After getting mark get the line again.
cve_desc: Use after free in utf_ptr2char in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4646.

vulnerabilities.description: In the MULTIBYTECODE case, `rex.input += len` is executed unconditionally after the match attempt, even when `status` has been set to `RA_NOMATCH`. If `rex.input` points near the end of the input buffer (e.g., at or near the NUL terminator) and `len >= 2`, the pointer is advanced past the NUL terminator, resulting in an out-of-bounds read on any subsequent dereference of `rex.input`. This affects both the composing-character path (where `status` stays `RA_NOMATCH` if no composing character matches, but `len` retains the original `mb_ptr2len(opnd)` value) and the byte-by-byte comparison path.

step_by_step_execution.description: The MULTIBYTECODE case is entered with `has_mbyte` true.
The operand length is computed via `mb_ptr2len(opnd)`, yielding len >= 2 (otherwise we'd break early with RA_NOMATCH).

state.value: pointer to multibyte operand in compiled pattern

step_by_step_execution.description: We enter the non-composing path (the `else` branch). rex.input points to the NUL terminator at the end of the current line, so rex.input[0] == NUL.

state.value: false, so we take the else branch

step_by_step_execution.description: The byte-by-byte comparison loop runs: opnd[0] != rex.input[0] is true (since opnd[0] is a non-NUL multibyte byte), so status is set to RA_NOMATCH and the for loop breaks at i=0.
Execution falls through to `rex.input += len`, which unconditionally advances the input pointer by `len` (>= 2) bytes past the NUL terminator, creating an out-of-bounds pointer.

state.value: points 2+ bytes past the NUL terminator (out of bounds)

commit_message:
bpf, ringbuf: Deny reserve of buffers larger than ringbuf

A BPF program might try to reserve a buffer larger than the ringbuf size.
If the consumer pointer is way ahead of the producer, that would be
successfully reserved, allowing the BPF program to read or write out of
the ringbuf allocated area.

Reported-by: Ryota Shiga (Flatt Security)
Fixes: 457f44363a88 ("bpf: Implement BPF ring buffer and verifier support for it")
Signed-off-by: Thadeu Lima de Souza Cascardo <cascardo@canonical.com>
Signed-off-by: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Acked-by: Andrii Nakryiko <andrii@kernel.org>
Acked-by: Alexei Starovoitov <ast@kernel.org>
cve_desc: The eBPF RINGBUF bpf_ringbuf_reserve() function in the Linux kernel did not check that the allocated size was smaller than the ringbuf size, allowing an attacker to perform out-of-bounds writes within the kernel and therefore, arbitrary code execution. This issue was fixed via commit 4b81ccebaeee ("bpf, ringbuf: Deny reserve of buffers larger than ringbuf") (v5.13-rc4) and backported to the stable kernels in v5.12.4, v5.11.21, and v5.10.37. It was introduced via 457f44363a88 ("bpf: Implement BPF ring buffer and verifier support for it") (v5.8-rc1).

commit_message: check max member count in class
cve_desc: sqclass.cpp in Squirrel through 2.2.5 and 3.x through 3.1 allows an out-of-bounds read (in the core interpreter) that can lead to Code Execution. If a victim executes an attacker-controlled squirrel script, it is possible for the attacker to break out of the squirrel script sandbox even if all dangerous functionality such as File System functions has been disabled. An attacker might abuse this bug to target (for example) Cloud services that allow customization via SquirrelScripts, or distribute malware through video games that embed a Squirrel Engine.

commit_message:
io_uring: fix off-by-one in sync cancelation file check

The passed in index should be validated against the number of registered
files we have, it needs to be smaller than the index value to avoid going
one beyond the end.

Fixes: 78a861b94959 ("io_uring: add sync cancelation API through io_uring_register()")
Reported-by: Luo Likang <luolikang@nsfocus.com>
Signed-off-by: Jens Axboe <axboe@kernel.dk>
cve_desc: off-by-one in io_uring module.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

commit_message:
video: fbdev: pxa3xx-gcu: Fix integer overflow in pxa3xx_gcu_write

In pxa3xx_gcu_write, a count parameter of type size_t is passed to words of
type int.  Then, copy_from_user() may cause a heap overflow because it is used
as the third argument of copy_from_user().

Signed-off-by: Hyunwoo Kim <imv4bel@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Helge Deller <deller@gmx.de>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel before 5.19. In pxa3xx_gcu_write in drivers/video/fbdev/pxa3xx-gcu.c, the count parameter has a type conflict of size_t versus int, causing an integer overflow and bypassing the size check. After that, because it is used as the third argument to copy_from_user(), a heap overflow may occur. NOTE: the original discoverer disputes that the overflow can actually happen.

vulnerabilities.description: There is a TOCTOU race condition between the check of `priv->free` (done without holding the spinlock) and the subsequent use of `priv->free` under the spinlock. If another thread/writer consumes the last free buffer between `pxa3xx_gcu_wait_free` returning and the spinlock acquisition, `priv->free` can be NULL. Then `buffer` is set to NULL, and `buffer->next` is a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: Two threads (T1 and T2) enter pxa3xx_gcu_write concurrently. Only one free buffer remains in priv->free.

state.value: points to last free buffer (single element, next is NULL)

step_by_step_execution.description: T1 checks `!priv->free`, finds it non-NULL, so skips waiting.
T2 also checks `!priv->free`, finds it non-NULL, so also skips waiting.
T1 acquires spinlock, sets buffer = priv->free (the last free buffer), sets priv->free = buffer->next (NULL), releases spinlock.

state.value: points to the last free buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: T2 acquires spinlock, sets buffer = priv->free which is now NULL.
T2 dereferences buffer->next (NULL pointer dereference) when executing `priv->free = buffer->next`.

commit_message:
video: fbdev: pxa3xx-gcu: Fix integer overflow in pxa3xx_gcu_write

In pxa3xx_gcu_write, a count parameter of type size_t is passed to words of
type int.  Then, copy_from_user() may cause a heap overflow because it is used
as the third argument of copy_from_user().

Signed-off-by: Hyunwoo Kim <imv4bel@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Helge Deller <deller@gmx.de>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel before 5.19. In pxa3xx_gcu_write in drivers/video/fbdev/pxa3xx-gcu.c, the count parameter has a type conflict of size_t versus int, causing an integer overflow and bypassing the size check. After that, because it is used as the third argument to copy_from_user(), a heap overflow may occur. NOTE: the original discoverer disputes that the overflow can actually happen.

vulnerabilities.description: There is a TOCTOU (Time-of-Check-Time-of-Use) race condition on `priv->free`. The check `if (!priv->free)` and the subsequent `pxa3xx_gcu_wait_free()` are performed without holding `priv->spinlock`. Between the point where `priv->free` is observed to be non-NULL (or after `pxa3xx_gcu_wait_free` returns indicating a buffer is available) and the point where the spinlock is actually acquired, another concurrent call to `pxa3xx_gcu_write` could consume the last free buffer, setting `priv->free` to NULL. The code then executes `buffer = priv->free` (NULL) followed by `priv->free = buffer->next`, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: Thread A enters pxa3xx_gcu_write. `priv->free` points to a single remaining free buffer (non-NULL).
Thread A evaluates `if (!priv->free)` — the condition is false, so it skips the wait.
Before Thread A acquires the spinlock, Thread B enters pxa3xx_gcu_write concurrently. Thread B also sees `priv->free` as non-NULL (same buffer) and skips the wait.
Thread B acquires the spinlock first, sets `buffer = priv->free` (the last buffer), and sets `priv->free = buffer->next` which is NULL. Thread B releases the spinlock.
Thread A now acquires the spinlock. It executes `buffer = priv->free`, which is now NULL.
Thread A dereferences the NULL `buffer` pointer via `priv->free = buffer->next`, causing a NULL pointer dereference (kernel panic/oops).

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5123: using invalid index when looking for spell suggestions

Problem:    Using invalid index when looking for spell suggestions.
Solution:   Do not decrement the index when it is zero.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: In STATE_REP, when a REP replacement's 'to' string is longer than its 'from' string, the content of fword[] is shifted right via STRMOVE without any bounds check on the total length of fword. Since REP replacements can occur at multiple depths during the trie walk, the cumulative growth (tracked by repextra but never bounded) can cause fword to overflow its buffer. The fword parameter is typically a stack-allocated buffer of MAXWLEN bytes in the caller.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives fword as a char_u* parameter, typically pointing to a stack buffer of MAXWLEN bytes.

state.value: pointer to buffer of MAXWLEN bytes containing the bad word

step_by_step_execution.description: During the main loop, execution reaches STATE_REP. A REP item from the .aff file is matched where ft_to is longer than ft_from.

Since fl != tl and tl > fl, STRMOVE(p + tl, p + fl) shifts all remaining content of fword right by (tl - fl) bytes, expanding the string in-place without checking if the result still fits in the MAXWLEN buffer.

state.value: original length + 3, may approach or exceed MAXWLEN

step_by_step_execution.description: mch_memmove copies the replacement 'to' string into fword at position p.

The trie walk continues, and subsequent iterations can hit STATE_REP again with additional REP items, each time potentially growing fword further. After multiple such replacements, the cumulative repextra growth causes STRMOVE to write past the end of the MAXWLEN-sized fword buffer.

state.value: cumulative growth from multiple REP replacements, unbounded
content extends beyond allocated MAXWLEN bytes — buffer overflow

vulnerabilities.description: The length of preword (which is MAXWLEN*3 = ~762 bytes) is cast to char_u (unsigned 8-bit, max 255) before being stored in ts_prewordlen. When preword exceeds 255 bytes (possible via compound words and prefix concatenation), ts_prewordlen wraps around to a small value. Subsequent writes using the truncated offset (e.g., STRCPY(preword + sp->ts_prewordlen, ...)) write to an incorrect position, corrupting preword and potentially causing further out-of-bounds access.

step_by_step_execution.description: preword is declared as a local buffer of MAXWLEN*3 bytes (~762). Through compound word building and prefix handling, preword accumulates content.

state.value: buffer of MAXWLEN*3 bytes on stack
grows with each compound/split word appended

step_by_step_execution.description: When the actual length of preword exceeds 255 bytes (via compound words, splits, and prefixes), the cast to char_u truncates the length.

On the next iteration, code writes to preword + sp->ts_prewordlen (offset 4 instead of 260). Operations like STRCPY or make_case_word overwrite earlier content. With continued accumulation, the effective written length can exceed MAXWLEN*3 since length tracking is broken.

state.value: points to offset 4 instead of 260, subsequent appends can accumulate unboundedly

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0011: reading beyond the end of the line with put command

Problem:    Reading beyond the end of the line with put command.
Solution:   Adjust the end mark position.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the allocation size computation `totlen + oldlen + 1` in the MCHAR/y_size==1 path. The overflow check only ensures `count * yanklen` fits in INT_MAX, but the subsequent addition of `oldlen` (the length of the existing line) can cause the `int` expression `totlen + oldlen + 1` to wrap around to a small positive value. This results in a small heap allocation followed by writes of `totlen + oldlen + 1` bytes, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is entered with y_type == MCHAR and y_size == 1. count and yanklen are such that count * yanklen is just under INT_MAX (e.g., count=INT_MAX/100, yanklen=100).
The overflow check `count > INT_MAX / yanklen` passes because count * yanklen fits within INT_MAX.
totlen is computed as count * yanklen, which is a large value near INT_MAX.
oldp = ml_get(lnum) retrieves the current line. oldlen = STRLEN(oldp) is the length of that line, which can be a non-trivial positive value (e.g., 100).
The expression `totlen + oldlen + 1` is computed as int arithmetic: ~2147483600 + 100 + 1 overflows a 32-bit signed int, wrapping to a small positive value (e.g., ~53).
alloc() allocates a buffer of the small wrapped size (~53 bytes).

mch_memmove writes col bytes from oldp, then count*yanklen bytes of yanked text, then the remainder of oldp. Total written is totlen + oldlen + 1 (~2147483701 bytes) into a ~53 byte buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

code_snippets:
totlen = count * (yanklen + spaces) + bd.startspaces + bd.endspaces;
newp = alloc(totlen + oldlen + 1);
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the MBLOCK path allocation `alloc(totlen + oldlen + 1)`. The overflow check ensures `count * (yanklen + spaces) + bd.startspaces + bd.endspaces` fits in INT_MAX, but adding `oldlen` can overflow the `int` computation, leading to a small allocation and subsequent heap buffer overflow when the old and new text are copied in.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters the MBLOCK path with count and yanklen chosen so that totlen is near INT_MAX.
The overflow check passes: count * (yanklen + spaces) + bd.startspaces + bd.endspaces <= INT_MAX.
oldlen is the length of the current line, a positive value.
totlen + oldlen + 1 overflows 32-bit int, wrapping to a small value. alloc returns a small buffer.
Subsequent mch_memmove calls write totlen + oldlen + 1 actual bytes into the undersized buffer, causing heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
drivers: hamradio: 6pack: fix UAF bug caused by mod_timer()

When a 6pack device is detaching, the sixpack_close() will act to cleanup
necessary resources. Although del_timer_sync() in sixpack_close()
won't return if there is an active timer, one could use mod_timer() in
sp_xmit_on_air() to wake up timer again by calling userspace syscall such
as ax25_sendmsg(), ax25_connect() and ax25_ioctl().

This unexpected waked handler, sp_xmit_on_air(), realizes nothing about
the undergoing cleanup and may still call pty_write() to use driver layer
resources that have already been released.

One of the possible race conditions is shown below:

      (USE)                      |      (FREE)
ax25_sendmsg()                   |
 ax25_queue_xmit()               |
  ...                            |
  sp_xmit()                      |
   sp_encaps()                   | sixpack_close()
    sp_xmit_on_air()             |  del_timer_sync(&sp->tx_t)
     mod_timer(&sp->tx_t,...)    |  ...
                                 |  unregister_netdev()
                                 |  ...
     (wait a while)              | tty_release()
                                 |  tty_release_struct()
                                 |   release_tty()
    sp_xmit_on_air()             |    tty_kref_put(tty_struct) //FREE
     pty_write(tty_struct) //USE |    ...

The corresponding fail log is shown below:
===============================================================
BUG: KASAN: use-after-free in __run_timers.part.0+0x170/0x470
Write of size 8 at addr ffff88800a652ab8 by task swapper/2/0
...
Call Trace:
  ...
  queue_work_on+0x3f/0x50
  pty_write+0xcd/0xe0pty_write+0xcd/0xe0
  sp_xmit_on_air+0xb2/0x1f0
  call_timer_fn+0x28/0x150
  __run_timers.part.0+0x3c2/0x470
  run_timer_softirq+0x3b/0x80
  __do_softirq+0xf1/0x380
  ...

This patch reorders the del_timer_sync() after the unregister_netdev()
to avoid UAF bugs. Because the unregister_netdev() is well synchronized,
it flushs out any pending queues, waits the refcount of net_device
decreases to zero and removes net_device from kernel. There is not any
running routines after executing unregister_netdev(). Therefore, we could
not arouse timer from userspace again.

Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Reviewed-by: Lin Ma <linma@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A use-after-free vulnerabilitity was discovered in drivers/net/hamradio/6pack.c of linux that allows an attacker to crash linux kernel by simulating ax25 device using 6pack driver from user space.

commit_message:
media: em28xx: initialize refcount before kref_get

The commit 47677e51e2a4("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct
em28xx after finishing all extensions") adds kref_get to many init
functions (e.g., em28xx_audio_init). However, kref_init is called too
late in em28xx_usb_probe, since em28xx_init_dev before will invoke
those init functions and call kref_get function. Then refcount bug
occurs in my local syzkaller instance.

Fix it by moving kref_init before em28xx_init_dev. This issue occurs
not only in dev but also dev->dev_next.

Fixes: 47677e51e2a4 ("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct em28xx after finishing all extensions")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
cve_desc: A flaw use after free in the Linux kernel video4linux driver was found in the way user triggers em28xx_usb_probe() for the Empia 28xx based TV cards. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

commit_message:
ovl: prevent private clone if bind mount is not allowed

Add the following checks from __do_loopback() to clone_private_mount() as
well:

 - verify that the mount is in the current namespace

 - verify that there are no locked children

Reported-by: Alois Wohlschlager <alois1@gmx-topmail.de>
Fixes: c771d683a62e ("vfs: introduce clone_private_mount()")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # v3.18
Signed-off-by: Miklos Szeredi <mszeredi@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the Linux kernel's OverlayFS subsystem in the way the user mounts the TmpFS filesystem with OverlayFS. This flaw allows a local user to gain access to hidden files that should not be accessible.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-3qx3-cg72-wrh9
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming RTCP BYE message contains a reason's length, this declared length is not checked against the actual received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP. A malicious actor can send a RTCP BYE message with an invalid reason length. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The reason length is read from the packet byte at offset 8 and clamped only against the destination buffer size, but never against the actual remaining packet size (size - 9). An attacker can craft a packet where the declared reason length exceeds the available data, causing pj_memcpy to read beyond the bounds of the packet buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a crafted RTCP BYE packet. For example, size = 10 (only 1 byte of reason payload after offset 9).
The check `size > 8` passes, so we enter the if-block.
The reason length byte at pkt+8 is read. An attacker sets this to, e.g., 200.

reason.slen is computed as PJ_MIN(sizeof(peer_sdes_buf_), 200). Assuming peer_sdes_buf_ is at least 200 bytes, reason.slen becomes 200. Even if peer_sdes_buf_ is smaller (e.g., 128), reason.slen = 128, which still exceeds the 1 byte of valid data.
pj_memcpy reads reason.slen bytes from pkt+9. Only 1 byte (size - 9 = 1) is within bounds of the packet buffer, so up to 127+ bytes are read out-of-bounds.

  sp_xmit()                      |
   sp_encaps()                   | sixpack_close()
    sp_xmit_on_air()             |  del_timer_sync(&sp->tx_t)
     mod_timer(&sp->tx_t,...)    |  ...
                                 |  unregister_netdev()
                                 |  ...
     (wait a while)              | tty_release()
                                 |  tty_release_struct()
                                 |   release_tty()
    sp_xmit_on_air()             |    tty_kref_put(tty_struct) //FREE
     pty_write(tty_struct) //USE |    ...

The corresponding fail log is shown below:
===============================================================
BUG: KASAN: use-after-free in __run_timers.part.0+0x170/0x470
Write of size 8 at addr ffff88800a652ab8 by task swapper/2/0
...
Call Trace:
  ...
  queue_work_on+0x3f/0x50
  pty_write+0xcd/0xe0pty_write+0xcd/0xe0
  sp_xmit_on_air+0xb2/0x1f0
  call_timer_fn+0x28/0x150
  __run_timers.part.0+0x3c2/0x470
  run_timer_softirq+0x3b/0x80
  __do_softirq+0xf1/0x380
  ...

This patch reorders the del_timer_sync() after the unregister_netdev()
to avoid UAF bugs. Because the unregister_netdev() is well synchronized,
it flushs out any pending queues, waits the refcount of net_device
decreases to zero and removes net_device from kernel. There is not any
running routines after executing unregister_netdev(). Therefore, we could
not arouse timer from userspace again.

Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Reviewed-by: Lin Ma <linma@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A use-after-free vulnerabilitity was discovered in drivers/net/hamradio/6pack.c of linux that allows an attacker to crash linux kernel by simulating ax25 device using 6pack driver from user space.

io_uring: fix uninitialized field in rw io_kiocb

io_rw_init_file does not initialize kiocb->private, so when iocb_bio_iopoll
reads kiocb->private it can contain uninitialized data.

Fixes: 3e08773c3841 ("block: switch polling to be bio based")
Signed-off-by: Joseph Ravichandran <jravi@mit.edu>
Signed-off-by: Jens Axboe <axboe@kernel.dk>

commit_message:
Remove double free() in contrib/shpsrt, issue #39

This fixes issue #39
cve_desc: A double-free condition exists in contrib/shpsort.c of shapelib 1.5.0 and older releases. This issue may allow an attacker to cause a denial of service or have other unspecified impact via control over malloc.

vulnerabilities.description: When the first realloc call fails (returns NULL) during the first iteration (i=0), `result` is also NULL, so the condition `!tmp && result` evaluates to false. The error is not handled, `result` remains NULL, and the code proceeds to dereference it via `result[i++] = dupstr(cptr)`, causing a NULL pointer dereference / write.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters, `result` is initialized to NULL and `i` to 0.
First iteration of the for loop: strtok returns a valid token, so `cptr` is non-NULL.
realloc(result, sizeof *result * 1) is called, which is realloc(NULL, ...). Suppose this fails and returns NULL.
The condition `!tmp && result` is evaluated: `!NULL && NULL` => `true && false` => false. The error-handling block is skipped.
`result = tmp` assigns NULL to result. Then `result[i++] = dupstr(cptr)` writes to address NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

vulnerabilities.description: In the error-handling path of the second realloc (after the for loop), `free(copy)` is called, but `copy` was already freed at the end of the for loop. This is a double-free vulnerability.

step_by_step_execution.description: The for loop completes successfully, producing at least one token (i > 0).
`free(copy)` is called after the for loop, freeing the duplicated input string.
The condition `if (i)` is true, so a second realloc is attempted to add space for the NULL terminator. Suppose this realloc fails and returns NULL.
In the error block, after freeing result entries and result itself, `free(copy)` is called again on the already-freed pointer, causing a double-free.

commit_message:
Initialize the max upload file size when quotas are enabled

Due to an unwanted check, files causing the quota to be exceeded
were deleted after the upload, but not during the upload.

The bug was introduced in 2009 in version 1.0.23

Spotted by @DroidTest, thanks!
cve_desc: In Pure-FTPd before 1.0.50, an incorrect max_filesize quota mechanism in the server allows attackers to upload files of unbounded size, which may lead to denial of service or a server hang. This occurs because a certain greater-than-zero test does not anticipate an initial -1 value. (Versions 1.0.23 through 1.0.49 are affected.)

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one error in the FTPWHO filename copy causes an unsigned integer underflow. When `sl` equals `sizeof shm_data_cur->filename`, the expression `sl - sizeof shm_data_cur->filename - 1U` evaluates to `0 - 1` in unsigned arithmetic, wrapping to SIZE_MAX. This results in `&name[SIZE_MAX]`, an out-of-bounds read that can crash the process or leak memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a filename `name`. Assume all earlier checks pass and the code reaches the FTPWHO block with `shm_data_cur != NULL`.

state.value: a string of length exactly equal to sizeof shm_data_cur->filename

step_by_step_execution.description: `sl` is computed as `strlen(name)`.
The condition `sl < sizeof shm_data_cur->filename` is evaluated. Since `sl == sizeof shm_data_cur->filename`, this is false, so the else branch is taken.

In the else branch, the offset is computed: `sl - sizeof shm_data_cur->filename - 1U`. Since both operands are `size_t` (unsigned), `sl - sizeof shm_data_cur->filename` equals 0, and `0 - 1U` wraps around to SIZE_MAX.
`memcpy` reads `sizeof shm_data_cur->filename` bytes from `&name[SIZE_MAX]`, which is a wildly out-of-bounds memory address, causing a crash or information disclosure.

vulnerabilities.description: After `close(f)` is called, the file descriptor `f` is reused in `FSTATFS(f, &statfsbuf)` (when SHOW_REAL_DISK_SPACE is defined). Using a closed file descriptor in fstatfs is undefined behavior and could operate on a recycled file descriptor if another thread opens a file between the close and fstatfs calls.

step_by_step_execution.description: The upload completes and `close(f)` is called to close the file descriptor.
When SHOW_REAL_DISK_SPACE is defined, `FSTATFS(f, &statfsbuf)` is called using the already-closed file descriptor `f`.
If another thread or operation has opened a new file descriptor that reuses the same integer value, FSTATFS operates on the wrong file, potentially leaking information about a different filesystem.

state.value: may contain data from wrong filesystem

commit_message:
ipf: release unhandled packets from the batch

Since 640d4db788ed ("ipf: Fix a use-after-free error, ...") the ipf
framework unconditionally allocates a new dp_packet to track
individual fragments.  This prevents a use-after-free.  However, an
additional issue was present - even when the packet buffer is cloned,
if the ip fragment handling code keeps it, the original buffer is
leaked during the refill loop.  Even in the original processing code,
the hardcoded dnsteal branches would always leak a packet buffer from
the refill loop.

This can be confirmed with valgrind:

==717566== 16,672 (4,480 direct, 12,192 indirect) bytes in 8 blocks are definitely lost in loss record 390 of 390
==717566==    at 0x484086F: malloc (vg_replace_malloc.c:380)
==717566==    by 0x537BFD: xmalloc__ (util.c:137)
==717566==    by 0x537BFD: xmalloc (util.c:172)
==717566==    by 0x46DDD4: dp_packet_new (dp-packet.c:153)
==717566==    by 0x46DDD4: dp_packet_new_with_headroom (dp-packet.c:163)
==717566==    by 0x550AA6: netdev_linux_batch_rxq_recv_sock.constprop.0 (netdev-linux.c:1262)
==717566==    by 0x5512AF: netdev_linux_rxq_recv (netdev-linux.c:1511)
==717566==    by 0x4AB7E0: netdev_rxq_recv (netdev.c:727)
==717566==    by 0x47F00D: dp_netdev_process_rxq_port (dpif-netdev.c:4699)
==717566==    by 0x47FD13: dpif_netdev_run (dpif-netdev.c:5957)
==717566==    by 0x4331D2: type_run (ofproto-dpif.c:370)
==717566==    by 0x41DFD8: ofproto_type_run (ofproto.c:1768)
==717566==    by 0x40A7FB: bridge_run__ (bridge.c:3245)
==717566==    by 0x411269: bridge_run (bridge.c:3310)
==717566==    by 0x406E6C: main (ovs-vswitchd.c:127)

The fix is to delete the original packet when it isn't able to be
reinserted into the packet batch.  Subsequent valgrind runs show that
the packets are not leaked from the batch any longer.

Fixes: 640d4db788ed ("ipf: Fix a use-after-free error, and remove the 'do_not_steal' flag.")
Fixes: 4ea96698f667 ("Userspace datapath: Add fragmentation handling.")
Reported-by: Wan Junjie <wanjunjie@bytedance.com>
Reported-at: https://github.com/openvswitch/ovs-issues/issues/226
Signed-off-by: Aaron Conole <aconole@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Tested-by: Wan Junjie <wanjunjie@bytedance.com>
Signed-off-by: Alin-Gabriel Serdean <aserdean@ovn.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak was found in Open vSwitch (OVS) during userspace IP fragmentation processing. An attacker could use this flaw to potentially exhaust available memory by keeping sending packet fragments.

commit_message:
fuse: fix live lock in fuse_iget()

Commit 5d069dbe8aaf ("fuse: fix bad inode") replaced make_bad_inode()
in fuse_iget() with a private implementation fuse_make_bad().

The private implementation fails to remove the bad inode from inode
cache, so the retry loop with iget5_locked() finds the same bad inode
and marks it bad forever.

kmsg snip:

[ ] rcu: INFO: rcu_sched self-detected stall on CPU
...
[ ]  ? bit_wait_io+0x50/0x50
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ? find_inode.isra.32+0x60/0xb0
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ilookup5_nowait+0x65/0x90
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ilookup5.part.36+0x2e/0x80
[ ]  ? fuse_init_file_inode+0x70/0x70
[ ]  ? fuse_inode_eq+0x20/0x20
[ ]  iget5_locked+0x21/0x80
[ ]  ? fuse_inode_eq+0x20/0x20
[ ]  fuse_iget+0x96/0x1b0

Fixes: 5d069dbe8aaf ("fuse: fix bad inode")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # 5.10+
Signed-off-by: Amir Goldstein <amir73il@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Miklos Szeredi <mszeredi@redhat.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in fs/fuse/fuse_i.h in the Linux kernel before 5.11.8. A "stall on CPU" can occur because a retry loop continually finds the same bad inode, aka CID-775c5033a0d1.

commit_message:
CamelGpgContext: Enclose email addresses in brackets.

The recipient list for encrypting can be specified by either key ID or
email address.  Enclose email addresses in brackets to ensure an exact
match, as per the gpg man page:

HOW TO SPECIFY A USER ID

       ...

       By exact match on an email address.
              This is indicated by enclosing the email address in the
              usual way with left and right angles.

         <heinrichh@uni-duesseldorf.de>

Without the brackets gpg uses a substring match, which risks selecting
the wrong recipient.
cve_desc: The gpg_ctx_add_recipient function in camel/camel-gpg-context.c in GNOME Evolution 3.8.4 and earlier and Evolution Data Server 3.9.5 and earlier does not properly select the GPG key to use for email encryption, which might cause the email to be encrypted with the wrong key and allow remote attackers to obtain sensitive information.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

commit_message:
CVE-2022-31214: fixing the fix, one more time

the previous commit "CVE-2022-31214: fixing the fix"
made private-etc=fonts,fonts and similar commands
fail with an error

fix that regression by tolerating already existing
directories
cve_desc: A Privilege Context Switching issue was discovered in join.c in Firejail 0.9.68. By crafting a bogus Firejail container that is accepted by the Firejail setuid-root program as a join target, a local attacker can enter an environment in which the Linux user namespace is still the initial user namespace, the NO_NEW_PRIVS prctl is not activated, and the entered mount namespace is under the attacker's control. In this way, the filesystem layout can be adjusted to gain root privileges through execution of available setuid-root binaries such as su or sudo.

commit_message:
Initialize the max upload file size when quotas are enabled

Due to an unwanted check, files causing the quota to be exceeded
were deleted after the upload, but not during the upload.

The bug was introduced in 2009 in version 1.0.23

Spotted by @DroidTest, thanks!
cve_desc: In Pure-FTPd before 1.0.50, an incorrect max_filesize quota mechanism in the server allows attackers to upload files of unbounded size, which may lead to denial of service or a server hang. This occurs because a certain greater-than-zero test does not anticipate an initial -1 value. (Versions 1.0.23 through 1.0.49 are affected.)

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow in the FTPWHO filename copy. When `sl` equals `sizeof shm_data_cur->filename`, the expression `sl - sizeof shm_data_cur->filename - 1U` underflows (unsigned arithmetic wraps around to SIZE_MAX), causing an out-of-bounds read from `name` at a massive offset.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a `name` parameter. The name passes the sanity check `checknamesanity`.
The upload proceeds normally until the FTPWHO block. `sl` is computed as `strlen(name)`.
The condition `sl < sizeof shm_data_cur->filename` is evaluated. If `sl == sizeof shm_data_cur->filename`, the condition is false and the else branch is taken.

In the else branch, the index `sl - sizeof shm_data_cur->filename - 1U` is computed. Since all operands are unsigned (size_t), `sl - sizeof shm_data_cur->filename` yields 0, and `0 - 1U` wraps around to SIZE_MAX.
memcpy reads `sizeof shm_data_cur->filename` bytes from `&name[SIZE_MAX]`, which is a massively out-of-bounds memory read, likely causing a crash or information disclosure.

state.value: name + SIZE_MAX, far out of bounds

vulnerabilities.description: The file descriptor `f` is closed via `close(f)`, and then used in `FSTATFS(f, &statfsbuf)`. This is a use-after-close: the file descriptor is no longer valid and may have been reused for another file, leading to incorrect results or operating on the wrong file.

step_by_step_execution.description: After the upload completes (ul_send/ul_exit), `close(f)` is called to close the file descriptor.
Immediately after, when SHOW_REAL_DISK_SPACE is defined, `FSTATFS(f, &statfsbuf)` is called using the same closed file descriptor `f`.

state.value: closed file descriptor, possibly reused by another open() call in closedata() or elsewhere

step_by_step_execution.description: FSTATFS operates on an invalid or reused file descriptor, which may return data about a different file or fail unpredictably.

state.value: potentially contains data from wrong file system or garbage

patch 8.2.5162: reading before the start of the line with BS in Replace mode
