commit_message:
Fix #73280: Stack Buffer Overflow in GD dynamicGetbuf

We make sure to never pass a negative `rlen` as size to memcpy().

Cf. <https://github.com/libgd/libgd/commit/53110871>.
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the dynamicGetbuf function in gd_io_dp.c in the GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) through 2.2.3, as used in PHP before 5.6.28 and 7.x before 7.0.13, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (stack-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted imagecreatefromstring call.

commit_message:
[bgpd] cleanup, compact and consolidate capability parsing code

2007-07-26 Paul Jakma <paul.jakma@sun.com>

	* (general) Clean up and compact capability parsing slightly.
	  Consolidate validation of length and logging of generic TLV, and
	  memcpy of capability data, thus removing such from cap specifc
	  code (not always present or correct).
	* bgp_open.h: Add structures for the generic capability TLV header
	  and for the data formats of the various specific capabilities we
	  support.  Hence remove the badly named, or else misdefined, struct
	  capability.
	* bgp_open.c: (bgp_capability_vty_out) Use struct capability_mp_data.
	  Do the length checks *before* memcpy()'ing based on that length
	  (stored capability - should have been validated anyway on input,
	  but..).
	  (bgp_afi_safi_valid_indices) new function to validate (afi,safi)
	  which is about to be used as index into arrays, consolidates
	  several instances of same, at least one of which appeared to be
	  incomplete..
	  (bgp_capability_mp) Much condensed.
	  (bgp_capability_orf_entry) New, process one ORF entry
	  (bgp_capability_orf) Condensed. Fixed to process all ORF entries.
	  (bgp_capability_restart) Condensed, and fixed to use a
	  cap-specific type, rather than abusing capability_mp.
	  (struct message capcode_str) added to aid generic logging.
	  (size_t cap_minsizes[]) added to aid generic validation of
	  capability length field.
	  (bgp_capability_parse) Generic logging and validation of TLV
	  consolidated here. Code compacted as much as possible.
	* bgp_packet.c: (bgp_open_receive) Capability parsers now use
	  streams, so no more need here to manually fudge the input stream
	  getp.
	  (bgp_capability_msg_parse) use struct capability_mp_data. Validate
	  lengths /before/ memcpy. Use bgp_afi_safi_valid_indices.
	  (bgp_capability_receive) Exported for use by test harness.
	* bgp_vty.c: (bgp_show_summary) fix conversion warning
	  (bgp_show_peer) ditto
	* bgp_debug.h: Fix storage 'extern' after type 'const'.
        * lib/log.c: (mes_lookup) warning about code not being in
          same-number array slot should be debug, not warning. E.g. BGP
          has several discontigious number spaces, allocating from
          different parts of a space is not uncommon (e.g. IANA
          assigned versus vendor-assigned code points in some number
          space).
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read in the BGP daemon of FRRouting FRR before 8.4 may lead to a segmentation fault and denial of service. This occurs in bgp_capability_msg_parse in bgpd/bgp_packet.c.

vulnerabilities.description: The memcpy copies a fixed `sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)` bytes from the packet buffer at `pnt + 3`, but the bounds check only validates that `pnt + hdr->length + 3 <= end`. If `hdr->length` is smaller than `sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)`, the memcpy will read past the validated region of the buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read. This memcpy executes for every capability entry, not just MP ones.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a peer-supplied capability message. `pnt` points to the beginning of the data and `end = pnt + length`.

state.value: e.g., 4 (small but valid to pass initial checks)

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop begins; the check `pnt + 3 > end` passes because 3 <= 4.
action is read from *pnt (valid value like CAPABILITY_ACTION_SET), and hdr is set to pnt+1. hdr->length is attacker-controlled, e.g. 0 or 1.
The length check `(pnt + hdr->length + 3) > end` evaluates to `(pnt + 0 + 3) > (pnt + 4)`, which is false, so the check passes.

memcpy copies sizeof(struct capability_mp_data) bytes (typically 4 bytes: 2 for afi, 1 reserved, 1 for safi) from pnt+3. But only 1 byte (end - (pnt+3)) is within bounds. This reads 3 bytes beyond the buffer.

commit_message:
Fix #73280: Stack Buffer Overflow in GD dynamicGetbuf

We make sure to never pass a negative `rlen` as size to memcpy().

Cf. <https://github.com/libgd/libgd/commit/53110871>.
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the dynamicGetbuf function in gd_io_dp.c in the GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) through 2.2.3, as used in PHP before 5.6.28 and 7.x before 7.0.13, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (stack-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted imagecreatefromstring call.

commit_message:
[bgpd] cleanup, compact and consolidate capability parsing code

2007-07-26 Paul Jakma <paul.jakma@sun.com>

	* (general) Clean up and compact capability parsing slightly.
	  Consolidate validation of length and logging of generic TLV, and
	  memcpy of capability data, thus removing such from cap specifc
	  code (not always present or correct).
	* bgp_open.h: Add structures for the generic capability TLV header
	  and for the data formats of the various specific capabilities we
	  support.  Hence remove the badly named, or else misdefined, struct
	  capability.
	* bgp_open.c: (bgp_capability_vty_out) Use struct capability_mp_data.
	  Do the length checks *before* memcpy()'ing based on that length
	  (stored capability - should have been validated anyway on input,
	  but..).
	  (bgp_afi_safi_valid_indices) new function to validate (afi,safi)
	  which is about to be used as index into arrays, consolidates
	  several instances of same, at least one of which appeared to be
	  incomplete..
	  (bgp_capability_mp) Much condensed.
	  (bgp_capability_orf_entry) New, process one ORF entry
	  (bgp_capability_orf) Condensed. Fixed to process all ORF entries.
	  (bgp_capability_restart) Condensed, and fixed to use a
	  cap-specific type, rather than abusing capability_mp.
	  (struct message capcode_str) added to aid generic logging.
	  (size_t cap_minsizes[]) added to aid generic validation of
	  capability length field.
	  (bgp_capability_parse) Generic logging and validation of TLV
	  consolidated here. Code compacted as much as possible.
	* bgp_packet.c: (bgp_open_receive) Capability parsers now use
	  streams, so no more need here to manually fudge the input stream
	  getp.
	  (bgp_capability_msg_parse) use struct capability_mp_data. Validate
	  lengths /before/ memcpy. Use bgp_afi_safi_valid_indices.
	  (bgp_capability_receive) Exported for use by test harness.
	* bgp_vty.c: (bgp_show_summary) fix conversion warning
	  (bgp_show_peer) ditto
	* bgp_debug.h: Fix storage 'extern' after type 'const'.
        * lib/log.c: (mes_lookup) warning about code not being in
          same-number array slot should be debug, not warning. E.g. BGP
          has several discontigious number spaces, allocating from
          different parts of a space is not uncommon (e.g. IANA
          assigned versus vendor-assigned code points in some number
          space).
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read in the BGP daemon of FRRouting FRR before 8.4 may lead to a segmentation fault and denial of service. This occurs in bgp_capability_msg_parse in bgpd/bgp_packet.c.

vulnerabilities.description: The code copies `sizeof(struct capability)` bytes from `pnt + 1` into `cap` before validating the full capability length. At this point, only 3 bytes of availability from `pnt` have been confirmed (`pnt + 3 <= end`), meaning only 2 bytes are guaranteed from `pnt + 1`. If `sizeof(struct capability)` is larger than 2 bytes (which it is, given it contains at minimum `code`, `length`, and a `mpc` sub-struct with `afi` and `safi`), the `memcpy` reads past the end of the input buffer. The subsequent bounds check `pnt + (cap.length + 3) > end` occurs too late, after the over-read has already happened.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a `pnt` buffer and `length`. `end` is computed as `pnt + length`.

state.value: e.g., 3 (minimum to pass the first bounds check)

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop begins. The check `pnt + 3 > end` passes because `pnt + 3 == end`, so there are exactly 3 bytes available starting from `pnt` (i.e., 2 bytes starting from `pnt + 1`).

state.value: false (check passes, 3 bytes available from pnt)

step_by_step_execution.description: The action byte is read: `action = *pnt;`. This is safe (1 byte within bounds).

`memcpy(&cap, pnt + 1, sizeof(struct capability))` is executed. `sizeof(struct capability)` includes `code` (1 byte), `length` (1 byte), and a union containing at least `mpc` (with `afi` (2 bytes) and `safi` (1 byte)), so it is at least 5 bytes. Only 2 bytes are guaranteed available from `pnt + 1`, causing a read of at least 3 bytes beyond the buffer boundary.

state.value: at least 3 bytes past end of buffer

commit_message:
CVE-2022-31214: fixing the fix, one more time

the previous commit "CVE-2022-31214: fixing the fix"
made private-etc=fonts,fonts and similar commands
fail with an error

fix that regression by tolerating already existing
directories
cve_desc: A Privilege Context Switching issue was discovered in join.c in Firejail 0.9.68. By crafting a bogus Firejail container that is accepted by the Firejail setuid-root program as a join target, a local attacker can enter an environment in which the Linux user namespace is still the initial user namespace, the NO_NEW_PRIVS prctl is not activated, and the entered mount namespace is under the attacker's control. In this way, the filesystem layout can be adjusted to gain root privileges through execution of available setuid-root binaries such as su or sudo.

vulnerabilities.description: TOCTOU race condition and symlink-following vulnerability. When `mkdir` fails with `EEXIST` (the path already exists), the code proceeds to call `chmod` on `dst` without verifying it is actually a directory and not a symlink. An attacker who controls a component of the `dst` path can place a symlink there before this function runs, causing `chmod` to follow the symlink and change permissions on an arbitrary file. Even when `mkdir` succeeds, there is a race window between `mkdir` and `chmod` where an attacker could replace the newly created directory with a symlink.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins iterating through path components of `src` starting at `src + src_prefix_len + 1`, with `q` tracking the corresponding position in `dst`.
A path component boundary is found (a '/' or '\0' in src). The src path is temporarily null-terminated, and `stat(src, &s)` confirms it is a directory.
The dst path is constructed by writing '\0' at *q and restoring '/' at *r. The code calls `mkdir(dst, 0700)`.

state.value: constructed path to the target directory component

step_by_step_execution.description: If an attacker has already placed a symlink at the `dst` path, `mkdir` fails with `errno == EEXIST`. The error is suppressed because EEXIST is explicitly allowed.

`chmod(dst, s.st_mode)` is called. Since `dst` is a symlink placed by the attacker, `chmod` follows the symlink and applies the permissions from the `src` directory's mode to an arbitrary attacker-chosen file.

commit_message:
io_uring: fix off-by-one in sync cancelation file check

The passed in index should be validated against the number of registered
files we have, it needs to be smaller than the index value to avoid going
one beyond the end.

Fixes: 78a861b94959 ("io_uring: add sync cancelation API through io_uring_register()")
Reported-by: Luo Likang <luolikang@nsfocus.com>
Signed-off-by: Jens Axboe <axboe@kernel.dk>
cve_desc: off-by-one in io_uring module.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one error in the bounds check: `fd > ctx->nr_user_files` should be `fd >= ctx->nr_user_files`. When `fd` equals `ctx->nr_user_files`, the check passes, but the index is out of range. `array_index_nospec` then clamps the value to 0, causing the code to operate on fixed file slot 0 instead of returning -EBADF. This allows a user to cancel I/O operations associated with the wrong file descriptor.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_FD and IORING_ASYNC_CANCEL_FD_FIXED flags set, and fd equal to ctx->nr_user_files (e.g., nr_user_files = 10, fd = 10).
The bounds check `fd > ctx->nr_user_files` is evaluated. Since 10 > 10 is false, the check does not trigger and execution continues past it.
`array_index_nospec(fd, ctx->nr_user_files)` is called with index=10, size=10. Since index is not less than size, it clamps the return value to 0 to prevent speculative out-of-bounds access.
The code reads `file_ptr` from fixed file slot 0, which is the wrong slot. The user intended to reference slot 10 (which doesn't exist), but the code now uses slot 0's file.

state.value: pointer to the file at slot 0

step_by_step_execution.description: __io_async_cancel is called with cd->file pointing to the file from slot 0, potentially cancelling I/O operations associated with a different file than the user intended or should have access to cancel.

commit_message: fix #2963 - oob write (1 byte) in pyc/marshal.c
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds write when getting data from PYC(python) files. A user opening a malicious PYC file could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number 68948017423a12786704e54227b8b2f918c2fd27 contains a patch.

vulnerabilities.description: When the second length read (n2) results in an error or overflow, the function returns NULL without freeing previously allocated `ret` and `s1`, causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: ret is allocated via RZ_NEW0
n1 is read successfully from buffer, no error or overflow
s1 is allocated and filled successfully

state.value: valid heap pointer with n1+1 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: n2 is read from buffer, but get_st32/get_ut8 sets error=true, or UT32_ADD_OVFCHK(n2, 1) is true
Function returns NULL without freeing ret or s1

vulnerabilities.description: When the allocation of s1 fails, the function returns NULL without freeing the previously allocated `ret`, causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: ret is allocated via RZ_NEW0
n1 is read successfully
malloc(n1 + 1) fails, s1 is NULL
Function returns NULL without freeing ret

vulnerabilities.description: When the allocation of s2 fails, the function returns NULL without freeing `ret` or `s1`, causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: ret and s1 are allocated and valid
n2 is read successfully, no error
malloc(n2 + 1) fails, s2 is NULL
Function returns NULL without freeing ret or s1

Update nft_data_init() to report EINVAL if chain is already bound.

Reported-by: Gwangun Jung <exsociety@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Pablo Neira Ayuso <pablo@netfilter.org>

commit_message: use mkstemp for sdb
cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. NOTE: this CVE Record is about sdb.cc and similar files in the Singular interface that have predictable /tmp pathnames; this CVE Record is not about the lack of a safe temporary-file creation capability in the Singular language.

vulnerabilities.description: The EDITOR (or VISUAL) environment variable is read and passed directly into a system() call without any sanitization. An attacker who controls the environment variable can inject arbitrary shell commands. For example, setting EDITOR to 'vi; rm -rf /' (which contains a space, triggering the system() branch) causes the shell to interpret the semicolon as a command separator.

step_by_step_execution.description: getenv("EDITOR") is called, returning an attacker-controlled string such as "vi; malicious_command"
editor is duplicated via omStrDup
The procedure body is written to the temp file and the file is closed
fork() is called; in the child process pid==0
strchr(editor,' ') is checked; since the editor string contains a space, the else branch is taken
A buffer p is allocated with strlen(editor)+strlen(filename)+2 and sprintf formats "%s %s" with editor and filename

system(p) is called, which passes the string to /bin/sh. The shell interprets the semicolon as a command separator, executing both 'vi' (which fails without a terminal or runs) and 'malicious_command /tmp/sdABCDEF' as a separate command.

commit_message:
SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill: Fix crash on broken file

Issue #802
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in Poppler through 0.78.0. There is a divide-by-zero error in the function SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill at SplashOutputDev.cc.

code_snippets:
result_width = tBitmap->getWidth() * imgData.repeatX;
  result_height = tBitmap->getHeight() * imgData.repeatY;
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow when computing result_width and result_height from the product of bitmap dimensions and repeat counts. Both operands are int, and no overflow check is performed. The overflowed values are then passed to splash->drawImage() as image dimensions, potentially causing heap buffer overflows or other memory corruption in the rendering pipeline.

code_snippets: retValue = splash->drawImage(&tilingBitmapSrc, nullptr, &imgData, colorMode, true, result_width, result_height, matc, false, true) == splashOk;

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with attacker-controlled PDF parameters including large x0, y0, x1, y1 values and crafted bbox, mat, ptm values.
width and height are computed from bbox. xStep == width and yStep == height check passes.
CTM is manipulated and checked for finite values. kx and ky are computed; the code enters the branch where fabs(kx) < 1 && fabs(ky) < 1.
In this branch, surface_width and surface_height are recalculated to small values (e.g. 2-3), but repeatX and repeatY are set directly to x1-x0 and y1-y0 with no bounds check.

The check surface_width == 0 || surface_height == 0 || repeatX * repeatY <= 4 is evaluated. repeatX * repeatY = 10^10, which overflows int (but the overflowed value is unlikely <= 4), so the check likely passes or the overflow itself is UB.
A bitmap of size surface_width x surface_height is allocated (small, succeeds). The pattern is rendered into it.
result_width = tBitmap->getWidth() * imgData.repeatX computes 2 * 100000 = 200000 (or with larger values can overflow int). result_height similarly overflows.

The overflowed (or very large) result_width and result_height are passed to splash->drawImage which uses them to compute memory accesses, potentially leading to heap buffer overflow or other memory corruption.

vulnerabilities.description: Division by zero when both ctm[0] and ctm[1] are zero. The condition splashAbs(ctm[1]) > splashAbs(ctm[0]) is false (0 > 0 is false), so the else branch executes and divides by ctm[0] which is 0. The isfinite check only rejects NaN/Inf in individual ctm values, not zero. The same pattern repeats for matc later in the function.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with mat values crafted such that after concatCTM operations, ctm[0] and ctm[1] are both 0. The isfinite check passes since 0.0 is finite.
The condition splashAbs(ctm[1]) > splashAbs(ctm[0]) evaluates to false (0 > 0 is false), entering the else branch.

Division by ctm[0] (which is 0.0) occurs. For floating point, this produces Inf or NaN rather than a crash, but leads to undefined/incorrect computation of result_width and result_height which are cast to int, causing undefined behavior.

state.value: undefined (int cast of Inf/NaN is UB)

commit_message:
patch 8.2.3564: invalid memory access when scrolling without valid screen

Problem:    Invalid memory access when scrolling without a valid screen.
Solution:   Do not set VALID_BOTLINE in w_valid.
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Heap-based Buffer Overflow

commit_message: fix #2963 - oob write (1 byte) in pyc/marshal.c
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds write when getting data from PYC(python) files. A user opening a malicious PYC file could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number 68948017423a12786704e54227b8b2f918c2fd27 contains a patch.

vulnerabilities.description: When n1 is obtained from get_st32 and equals 0xFFFFFFFF (i.e., -1 interpreted as ut32), the expression `n1 + 1` wraps to 0. malloc(0) can return a non-NULL pointer to a zero-size allocation, and then rz_buf_read attempts to write up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into it, causing a massive heap buffer overflow. The same issue applies to n2.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with pyc->magic_int having lower 16 bits > 62061, so the get_st32 path is taken for n1.
get_st32 returns -1 from buffer, which is stored in ut32 n1 as 0xFFFFFFFF. error remains false.
malloc(n1 + 1) is called. n1 + 1 = 0xFFFFFFFF + 1 = 0 due to unsigned 32-bit overflow. malloc(0) returns a non-NULL pointer to a zero-size (or minimally-sized) heap buffer.
rz_buf_read(buffer, s1, n1) attempts to read 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into the zero-size buffer pointed to by s1, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: After reading n2, if error is true, the function returns NULL without freeing s1 and ret, leaking both allocations. While primarily a resource leak, s1 contains attacker-controlled data that persists on the heap.

step_by_step_execution.description: s1 is successfully allocated and filled with data from the buffer. ret was also allocated.
The second call to get_ut8 or get_st32 for n2 sets error to true.
The error check returns NULL without freeing s1 or ret, causing a memory leak.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/4988
cve_desc: ImageMagick 7.1.0-27 is vulnerable to Buffer Overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: The quantum pixel buffer (`pixels`) is allocated internally by `AcquireQuantumInfo` and sized based on `GetQuantumExtent`. However, the actual read length `length` is computed independently via `GetBytesPerRow` using attacker-controlled `image->depth` (from `cin.image.channel[0].bits_per_pixel`, an unsigned char 0-255) and `image->columns`. The value of `extent` is explicitly discarded with `(void) extent;`. If `length` exceeds the quantum pixel buffer size, `ReadBlobStream` will write `length` bytes into the `pixels` buffer (when the blob is file-backed), causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Image is opened and CIN header is parsed, including channel information from the file.

state.value: attacker-controlled unsigned char, e.g. 32 or any value 0-255

step_by_step_execution.description: Image dimensions and depth are set directly from attacker-controlled header fields without validation of depth.
Quantum info is allocated. The internal pixel buffer is sized based on GetQuantumExtent which uses quantum_type (RGB) and the quantum info settings (quantum=32, pack=false).

state.value: allocated with internal buffer sized for quantum extent

step_by_step_execution.description: extent is computed via GetQuantumExtent but explicitly discarded.

state.value: computed buffer size for quantum pixels, then cast to void and ignored

step_by_step_execution.description: length is computed independently via GetBytesPerRow using image->columns, 3 channels, image->depth, and padding=true. With certain depth values (e.g., a large bits_per_pixel), length can exceed the quantum pixel buffer size.

state.value: GetBytesPerRow(image->columns, 3, image->depth, MagickTrue) — can differ from quantum extent
pointer to quantum internal buffer, sized for extent not length

step_by_step_execution.description: In the row-reading loop, ReadBlobStream reads `length` bytes. When the blob is file-backed (not memory-mapped), it reads into the `pixels` buffer. If length > quantum buffer size, this overflows the heap buffer.

state.value: potentially larger than the allocated pixels buffer
heap buffer that may be overflowed

vulnerabilities.description: image->depth is assigned directly from cin.image.channel[0].bits_per_pixel (an unsigned char, range 0-255) with no validation that it is a supported or reasonable bit depth. A depth of 0 could cause GetBytesPerRow to return 0 (infinite loop or logic error), and unusual large depths could cause integer overflow in row-size calculations, leading to undersized buffer allocations.

step_by_step_execution.description: CIN header is parsed; bits_per_pixel is read as a single byte from the file.
image->depth is set directly without any bounds check.

GetBytesPerRow is called with the unchecked depth. Unusual depth values (0, or very large like 255) can cause incorrect length computation — either 0 (leading to infinite progress with no data read) or an overflowed/unexpected value.

state.value: result of GetBytesPerRow with potentially invalid depth, could be 0 or overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: The computed length is used to read data into the pixel buffer and to feed ImportQuantumPixels, which may process data incorrectly or access memory out of bounds based on the mismatched depth.

state.value: bytes actually read, compared against potentially wrong length

commit_message:
patch 8.2.3564: invalid memory access when scrolling without valid screen

Problem:    Invalid memory access when scrolling without a valid screen.
Solution:   Do not set VALID_BOTLINE in w_valid.

commit_message: use mkstemp for sdb
cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. NOTE: this CVE Record is about sdb.cc and similar files in the Singular interface that have predictable /tmp pathnames; this CVE Record is not about the lack of a safe temporary-file creation capability in the Singular language.

vulnerabilities.description: Buffer overflow when writing PID into the filename buffer. The buffer is allocated as a duplicate of "/tmp/sd000000" (14 bytes total including null). Writing starts at offset 7, leaving only 7 bytes (6 chars + null). PIDs can be up to 7 digits (e.g., 4194304), requiring 8 bytes including the null terminator, causing a 1-byte heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: filename is allocated as a heap copy of "/tmp/sd000000"
sprintf writes the PID as a decimal string starting at filename+7. If PID is e.g. 4194304 (7 digits), it writes 7 characters + null terminator = 8 bytes.
sprintf writes "4194304\0" (8 bytes) into a 7-byte region, overflowing the heap buffer by 1 byte.

vulnerabilities.description: Command injection via the EDITOR (or VISUAL) environment variable. When the EDITOR value contains a space, the code concatenates it with the filename and passes it to system(), which interprets shell metacharacters. An attacker controlling the EDITOR variable can inject arbitrary shell commands (e.g., EDITOR="vi ; malicious_command").

step_by_step_execution.description: The EDITOR environment variable is read from the environment.
editor contains a space, so strchr(editor,' ') != NULL, and the else branch is taken.
The editor string is concatenated with the filename into buffer p using sprintf.
system(p) is called, which passes the string to /bin/sh, executing the injected command.

state.value: "vi ; rm -rf / /tmp/sd12345" — shell interprets ';' as command separator

commit_message: Fixed bug #22965 (Crash in gd lib's ImageFillToBorder()).
cve_desc: Stack consumption vulnerability in GD in PHP before 5.6.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a crafted imagefilltoborder call.

vulnerabilities.description: The function clamps x and y for upper bounds (>= sx, >= sy) but never checks if x or y are negative. A negative y value will be passed directly to gdImageGetPixel and gdImageSetPixel, causing out-of-bounds memory read and write operations.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with y = -1 and a valid x (e.g., x = 0) on a valid image.
border is checked to be >= 0, passes the check.
x >= im->sx check: 0 >= 100 is false, so x is not clamped.
y >= im->sy check: -1 >= 100 is false, so y is not clamped. No check for y < 0 exists.
The leftward-seeking loop begins with i = 0. gdImageGetPixel(im, 0, -1) is called, reading memory before the pixel row buffer (out-of-bounds read).
If the out-of-bounds read does not equal border, gdImageSetPixel(im, 0, -1, color) is called, writing to memory before the pixel row buffer (out-of-bounds write).

commit_message:
Make ZlibInStream more robust against failures

Move the checks around to avoid missing cases where we might access
memory that is no longer valid. Also avoid touching the underlying
stream implicitly (e.g. via the destructor) as it might also no
longer be valid.

A malicious server could theoretically use this for remote code
execution in the client.

Issue found by Pavel Cheremushkin from Kaspersky Lab
cve_desc: TigerVNC version prior to 1.10.1 is vulnerable to stack use-after-return, which occurs due to incorrect usage of stack memory in ZRLEDecoder. If decoding routine would throw an exception, ZRLEDecoder may try to access stack variable, which has been already freed during the process of stack unwinding. Exploitation of this vulnerability could potentially result into remote code execution. This attack appear to be exploitable via network connectivity.

vulnerabilities.description: In both packed-pixel and palette-RLE modes, the index used to access the `palette` array is not validated against `palSize`. The `palette` array has 128 entries on the stack, but only `palSize` entries are initialized. An attacker-controlled index (up to 127) can read uninitialized stack memory, which is then written into the pixel buffer and potentially displayed, leading to information disclosure of stack contents.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads a tile's mode byte from the zlib stream. Suppose mode = 3 (rle=false, palSize=3).
Only palette[0], palette[1], palette[2] are initialized from the stream. palette[3]..palette[127] remain uninitialized stack memory.
Since palSize=3 and rle=false, packed pixels mode is entered. bppp is calculated as 2 (since palSize > 2 but not > 4).
For each pixel, an index in range 0-3 is extracted from the stream byte: index = (byte >> nbits) & 0x3 & 127. An attacker can craft the byte so index = 3.
palette[3] is accessed, reading uninitialized stack data. This value is written to buf and later to the pixel buffer via pb->imageRect().

commit_message:
udf: Restore i_lenAlloc when inode expansion fails

When we fail to expand inode from inline format to a normal format, we
restore inode to contain the original inline formatting but we forgot to
set i_lenAlloc back. The mismatch between i_lenAlloc and i_size was then
causing further problems such as warnings and lost data down the line.

Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: 7e49b6f2480c ("udf: Convert UDF to new truncate calling sequence")
Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Signed-off-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>
cve_desc: A flaw null pointer dereference in the Linux kernel UDF file system functionality was found in the way user triggers udf_file_write_iter function for the malicious UDF image. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system. Actual from Linux kernel 4.2-rc1 till 5.17-rc2.

commit_message: Fixed bug #22965 (Crash in gd lib's ImageFillToBorder()).
cve_desc: Stack consumption vulnerability in GD in PHP before 5.6.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a crafted imagefilltoborder call.

vulnerabilities.description: The function does not validate that the initial `x` and `y` parameters are within the image bounds (0 <= x < im->sx, 0 <= y < im->sy) before using them in gdImageGetPixel and gdImageSetPixel calls. If a caller passes out-of-bounds coordinates (e.g., y < 0, y >= im->sy, or x >= im->sx), this leads to out-of-bounds read/write on the image pixel data.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with y = -1 (or any negative value, or y >= im->sy). No bounds check on y exists at function entry.
The border >= 0 check passes, so execution continues.
The leftward seek loop begins: for (i = x; (i >= 0); i--). Since x >= 0, the loop body executes.
gdImageGetPixel(im, i, y) is called with y = -1, causing an out-of-bounds read on the pixel array.
If the pixel read does not match border, gdImageSetPixel(im, i, y, color) is called with y = -1, causing an out-of-bounds write on the pixel array.

vulnerabilities.description: The function is recursive with no depth limit. A specially crafted image (e.g., large area with no border pixels) can cause extremely deep recursion, exhausting the call stack and causing a stack overflow / denial of service.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called on a large image where no pixel matches the border color. The leftward and rightward scans fill the entire row.
The 'Above' section iterates across the full row and finds pixels that are neither border nor color, recursively calling gdImageFillToBorder with y-1.

Each recursive call again fills a full row and recurses upward/downward. With a large image (e.g., 1000x1000), recursion can reach hundreds or thousands of frames deep, each frame also potentially branching, exhausting the stack.

state.value: up to im->sy levels deep, with branching

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/pull/5034
cve_desc: In ImageMagick, there is load of misaligned address for type 'double', which requires 8 byte alignment and for type 'float', which requires 4 byte alignment at MagickCore/property.c. Whenever crafted or untrusted input is processed by ImageMagick, this causes a negative impact to application availability or other problems related to undefined behavior.

commit_message:
Bug 790784 - (CVE-2017-17784) heap overread in gbr parser / load_image.

We were assuming the input name was well formed, hence was
nul-terminated. As any data coming from external input, this has to be
thorougly checked.
Similar to commit 06d24a79af94837d615d0024916bb95a01bf3c59 but adapted
to older gimp-2-8 code.
cve_desc: In GIMP 2.8.22, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in load_image in plug-ins/common/file-gbr.c in the gbr import parser, related to mishandling of UTF-8 data.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the allocation size when a pattern follows a 1-byte brush. The initial sanitization check validates `G_MAXSIZE / bh.width / bh.height / bh.bytes < 1` with `bh.bytes == 1`, ensuring `width * height` fits in `gsize`. However, when a valid pattern header is found, the code allocates `4 * bh.width * bh.height` bytes without re-checking for overflow. On a 32-bit system (where gsize is 32 bits), this multiplication can wrap around, resulting in a small heap allocation. Subsequent writes into `brush_buf` via the read loop will then overflow the heap buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: File is opened and the BrushHeader is read. Fields are converted from network byte order.

Sanitization check: `G_MAXSIZE / bh.width / bh.height / bh.bytes < 1` is evaluated. On a 32-bit system, G_MAXSIZE is 4294967295. 4294967295 / 32768 / 32768 / 1 = 3, which is >= 1, so the check passes.
The initial brush data buffer is allocated: `size = bh.width * bh.height * bh.bytes = 1073741824`. `brush_buf = g_malloc(size)` allocates 1 GB. Data is read into brush_buf.
In the `bh.bytes == 1` case, a PatternHeader is read. A crafted file provides valid pattern header with ph.width=32768, ph.height=32768, ph.bytes=3, matching bh dimensions.
The code computes `4 * bh.width * bh.height` for the new allocation. On a 32-bit system: 4 * 32768 * 32768 = 4294967296, which overflows a 32-bit gsize to 0.
`g_malloc(0)` allocates a minimal buffer (implementation-defined, often 1 byte or a small block). The old brush_buf is saved as plain_brush.

The loop iterates `ph.width * ph.height` (1073741824) times, writing to `brush_buf[i * 4]` through `brush_buf[i * 4 + 3]`. Since brush_buf is essentially 0 bytes, this massively overflows the heap buffer.

commit_message:
ALSA: control: Re-order bounds checking in get_ctl_id_hash()

These two checks are in the reverse order so it might read one element
beyond the end of the array.  First check if the "i" is within bounds
before using it.

Fixes: 6ab55ec0a938 ("ALSA: control: Fix an out-of-bounds bug in get_ctl_id_hash()")
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/YwjgNh/gkG1hH7po@kili
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds access issue was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem. It could occur when the 'id->name' provided by the user did not end with '\0'. A privileged local user could pass a specially crafted name through ioctl() interface and crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

commit_message:
Fix integer overflow in string search causing oobread ##crash

* Reported by @greatergoodest via huntrdev
* BountyID: 8a3dc5cb-08b3-4807-82b2-77f08c137a04
* Reproducer bfileovf
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0.

code_snippets: int res = r_charset_encode_str (ch, out, outlen, buf, len);
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow when computing `outlen`. The variable `len` (of type `st64`) is validated to be at most `ST32_MAX` (2147483647), but `len * 4` can exceed `INT_MAX` (2147483647) when `len > 536870911`. Storing this result in `int outlen` causes signed integer overflow, yielding a negative or unexpectedly small value. This corrupted `outlen` is then passed as the output buffer size limit to `r_charset_encode_str`. If that function interprets the negative value as an unsigned large value, it could write beyond intended bounds; if it treats it as a small positive, the resulting `res` (and thus `len`) would be incorrect, leading to the main scanning loop processing uninitialized/zeroed buffer data with incorrect assumptions about content length.

code_snippets: int res = r_charset_encode_str (ch, out, outlen, buf, len);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function computes `len` as `to - from`, which is validated to be between 1 and ST32_MAX (2147483647).
A buffer `buf` is allocated with `calloc(len, 1)` and data is read into it.
The RABIN2_CHARSET environment variable is set, so the charset encoding path is taken.
`outlen` is computed as `len * 4`. With `len = 0x60000000`, `len * 4 = 0x180000000` which overflows a 32-bit `int`, resulting in a negative or wrapped value.

state.value: 0x180000000 truncated to int = -2147483648 (or similar negative/wrong value)

step_by_step_execution.description: `calloc(len, 4)` allocates `len * 4` bytes correctly using `size_t` arithmetic, so the allocation is the correct size.

`r_charset_encode_str(ch, out, outlen, buf, len)` is called with the overflowed `outlen` as the output buffer size limit. The function may misinterpret this corrupted size, potentially writing beyond intended limits or producing a corrupted `res` value.

state.value: negative or incorrectly small integer due to overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: `len` is reassigned to `res` and `buf` is replaced with `out`. The main scanning loop then operates with a potentially incorrect `len` while iterating up to the original `to` value.

state.value: out buffer with potentially incorrect/incomplete data

commit_message:
media: em28xx: initialize refcount before kref_get

The commit 47677e51e2a4("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct
em28xx after finishing all extensions") adds kref_get to many init
functions (e.g., em28xx_audio_init). However, kref_init is called too
late in em28xx_usb_probe, since em28xx_init_dev before will invoke
those init functions and call kref_get function. Then refcount bug
occurs in my local syzkaller instance.

Fix it by moving kref_init before em28xx_init_dev. This issue occurs
not only in dev but also dev->dev_next.

Fixes: 47677e51e2a4 ("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct em28xx after finishing all extensions")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
cve_desc: A flaw use after free in the Linux kernel video4linux driver was found in the way user triggers em28xx_usb_probe() for the Empia 28xx based TV cards. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.description: After `usb_set_intfdata(intf, dev)` stores a pointer to `dev` in the USB interface, if `em28xx_init_dev()` (or any subsequent operation) fails and jumps to `err_free`, the code frees `dev` via `kfree(dev)` without first clearing the interface data pointer (i.e., no `usb_set_intfdata(intf, NULL)`). This leaves a dangling pointer in the USB interface structure, which can be dereferenced later (e.g., during disconnect), resulting in a use-after-free.

step_by_step_execution.description: A USB device is plugged in and em28xx_usb_probe is called. A free device slot `nr` is found and allocated.
`dev` is allocated with kzalloc and `dev->alt_max_pkt_size_isoc` is allocated with kcalloc. Endpoints are checked, and has_video/has_dvb/has_vendor_audio are set such that at least one is true.
`usb_set_intfdata(intf, dev)` is called, storing the pointer to `dev` inside the USB interface structure.
`em28xx_init_dev(dev, udev, intf, nr)` is called and returns a non-zero error code.
Execution jumps to `err_free`. `kfree(dev->alt_max_pkt_size_isoc)` and `kfree(dev)` are called, freeing the device memory. However, `usb_set_intfdata(intf, NULL)` is never called.

state.value: dangling pointer to freed dev memory

step_by_step_execution.description: Any subsequent access to the interface data (e.g., during USB disconnect callback) will dereference the dangling pointer, causing a use-after-free.

vulnerabilities.description: When the board has dual TS and `em28xx_duplicate_dev` succeeds, a second `em28xx_init_dev` is called for `dev->dev_next`. If this second init fails, execution jumps to `err_free`, which only does `kfree(dev->alt_max_pkt_size_isoc)` and `kfree(dev)`. The first `em28xx_init_dev(dev, ...)` had already succeeded and likely allocated sub-resources and registered components; these are never torn down. Additionally, `dev->dev_next` (allocated by `em28xx_duplicate_dev`) is never freed, causing a memory leak and potential use-after-free of resources initialized by the first `em28xx_init_dev`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Probe proceeds normally: dev is allocated, endpoints checked, first em28xx_init_dev succeeds.
dev->board.has_dual_ts is true, em28xx_duplicate_dev(dev) returns 0 (success), allocating dev->dev_next.
em28xx_init_dev(dev->dev_next, ...) is called and fails, returning non-zero.

Execution jumps to err_free. Only dev->alt_max_pkt_size_isoc and dev are freed. dev->dev_next is leaked. Resources from the first successful em28xx_init_dev are not torn down, and dev is freed while those resources may still reference it.

state.value: freed without proper teardown of init_dev resources

commit_message:
Bug 790784 - (CVE-2017-17784) heap overread in gbr parser / load_image.

We were assuming the input name was well formed, hence was
nul-terminated. As any data coming from external input, this has to be
thorougly checked.
Similar to commit 06d24a79af94837d615d0024916bb95a01bf3c59 but adapted
to older gimp-2-8 code.
cve_desc: In GIMP 2.8.22, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in load_image in plug-ins/common/file-gbr.c in the gbr import parser, related to mishandling of UTF-8 data.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the allocation for the combined brush+pattern buffer. The initial sanitization check validates `G_MAXSIZE / bh.width / bh.height / bh.bytes < 1` with `bh.bytes == 1`. However, when a valid pattern follows, `bh.bytes` is changed to 4 and a new buffer is allocated with `g_malloc(4 * bh.width * bh.height)`. This multiplication can overflow (especially on 32-bit platforms), resulting in a small heap allocation, which is then filled with `width * height * 4` bytes of data, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: File is opened and BrushHeader is read. After byte-swapping, sanitization checks pass with bh.bytes == 1. For example, on a 32-bit system: bh.width = 32768, bh.height = 32768, bh.bytes = 1.
The overflow check `G_MAXSIZE / bh.width / bh.height / bh.bytes < 1` evaluates to `4294967295 / 32768 / 32768 / 1 = 4`, which is >= 1, so the check passes.
The initial brush_buf is allocated: `size = 32768 * 32768 * 1 = 1073741824` bytes, and brush data is read.
Since bh.bytes == 1, the code enters the pattern-following path. A PatternHeader is read, and its fields match the conditions (magic, version, dimensions, bytes==3).

The code sets bh.bytes = 4 and allocates a new buffer: `g_malloc(4 * bh.width * bh.height)`. The multiplication `4 * 32768 * 32768` = `4 * 1073741824` = `4294967296`, which overflows a 32-bit gsize to 0.
The loop iterates `ph.width * ph.height = 1073741824` times, writing to `brush_buf + i * 4` at each iteration. Since the buffer is effectively 0 bytes, this causes a massive heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

commit_message: check vertexIndex which might be -1 for broken DXF
cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the expression `4*maxVertices`. `maxVertices` is derived from user-controlled input via `toInt(groupValue)`. If `maxVertices` is large enough (e.g., 0x20000001 on a 32-bit int), `4*maxVertices` overflows, causing a small buffer allocation while the subsequent initialization loop iterates based on the original large `maxVertices`, writing far beyond the allocated heap buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: groupCode is 90, so we enter the first branch.
maxVertices is set from user input via toInt(groupValue). Attacker provides a large value like 0x20000001 (536870913).
maxVertices > 0 check passes, so we proceed to allocation.

The expression `4*maxVertices` is computed as a 32-bit int multiplication: 4 * 0x20000001 = 0x80000004, which overflows to 4 (or a small/negative value depending on signed interpretation). The `new double[4*maxVertices]` allocates a very small buffer.

state.value: pointer to heap buffer of ~4 doubles (32 bytes)

step_by_step_execution.description: The for loop iterates from i=0 to i<536870913, writing vertices[i*4], vertices[i*4+1], vertices[i*4+2], vertices[i*4+3]. This massively overflows the allocated buffer, corrupting heap memory.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5123: using invalid index when looking for spell suggestions

Problem:    Using invalid index when looking for spell suggestions.
Solution:   Do not decrement the index when it is zero.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: In STATE_REP, REP replacements are applied in-place to the `fword` buffer. When `ft_to` is longer than `ft_from`, `STRMOVE` expands `fword`. Since the trie walk recurses to deeper levels while the replacement is active, multiple REP replacements can stack, each growing `fword` further. The cumulative growth tracked by `repextra` is never checked against `fword`'s buffer size (typically MAXWLEN), leading to an out-of-bounds write on the `fword` buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives `fword`, a pointer to a buffer (typically sized MAXWLEN, e.g., 254 bytes) containing the bad word to match.

state.value: pointer to a buffer of MAXWLEN bytes containing the misspelled word

step_by_step_execution.description: During trie traversal, STATE_REP_INI is reached. A REP item is found where ft_to is longer than ft_from (e.g., ft_from="x" len=1, ft_to="abc" len=3).
STRMOVE(p + tl, p + fl) shifts the remainder of fword by (tl - fl) = 2 bytes to the right, growing the content of fword. No bounds check against fword's buffer size is performed.
The replacement string is copied in: mch_memmove(p, ftp->ft_to, tl). Depth is incremented and traversal continues with the expanded fword.
At the deeper level, traversal eventually reaches STATE_REP_INI again at a different position in the now-expanded fword. Another REP item with tl > fl is matched and applied.

This process can repeat at each depth level. With many REP rules where ft_to is longer than ft_from, the cumulative growth can cause writes past the end of the fword buffer, since repextra is never checked against the buffer boundary.

state.value: written past its allocated MAXWLEN buffer

vulnerabilities.description: The length of `preword` (a buffer of MAXWLEN*3 = 762 bytes) is cast to `char_u` (unsigned char, 0-255) before being stored in `ts_prewordlen`. When `preword` exceeds 255 bytes through compound word or split concatenation, the length wraps around, causing subsequent writes via `preword + sp->ts_prewordlen` to target incorrect (earlier) positions in the buffer. This corrupts the preword content and can lead to incorrect memory operations.

step_by_step_execution.description: preword is declared as char_u preword[MAXWLEN * 3] (762 bytes). Through repeated compound word building or splits, content is appended to preword.
The code executes sp->ts_prewordlen = (char_u)STRLEN(preword). The cast to char_u truncates the value.

Subsequent operations use the truncated ts_prewordlen. For example, make_case_word(tword + sp->ts_splitoff, preword + sp->ts_prewordlen, c) writes the case-adjusted word at offset 44 instead of offset 300, overwriting existing compound word data in preword.
This corrupted preword is then used in add_suggestion() and other comparisons, producing incorrect suggestions. Further concatenation based on the wrong offset corrupts the data structure.

state.value: corrupted content due to mid-buffer overwrites

commit_message: 0.16.1: fix for dcraw ljpeg_start() vulnerability
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the ljpeg_start function in dcraw 7.00 and earlier allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted image, which triggers a buffer overflow, related to the len variable.

vulnerabilities.description: The `len` variable is of type `ushort`. When the JPEG marker length field contains the value 0 or 1, subtracting 2 causes an unsigned integer underflow, wrapping `len` to 65534 or 65535. This causes `fread` to read a massive amount of file data into the stack buffer, and all subsequent switch-case parsing processes data far beyond the actual marker boundaries.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the do-while loop and reads 4 bytes from the file for tag and length.
Tag is computed and validated to be > 0xff00.
len is computed as (data[2] << 8 | data[3]) - 2. If attacker sets data[2]=0 and data[3]=0, the expression evaluates to 0 - 2 = -2, which is stored as ushort.

fread(data, 1, len, ifp) attempts to read 65534 bytes from the file into the data buffer. The buffer is 0x10000 (65536) bytes, so no stack overflow occurs, but the function reads far beyond the marker's actual data, consuming subsequent markers and arbitrary file content.

state.value: Filled with up to 65534 bytes of unintended file content

step_by_step_execution.description: The switch cases then parse this data. For example, in case 0xffc0, attacker-controlled bytes set jh->bits, jh->high, jh->wide, jh->clrs from file content that was not part of this marker. In case 0xffc4, the loop iterates with dp bounded by data+65534, calling make_decoder_ref with attacker-controlled data far beyond the original marker, potentially causing excessive heap allocations or further memory corruption in the decoder.

vulnerabilities.description: In the 0xffda handler, data[0] (number of scan components) is used to compute array indices without validation against `len`. An attacker can set data[0] to a large value, causing reads of indices up to 513 within the stack buffer. If `len` is small, these reads access stack data that was not populated by fread (uninitialized or stale from a prior loop iteration), leading to use of uninitialized memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop reads tag and length for a 0xffda marker. The length field is small, e.g., data[2]=0, data[3]=8 giving len=6.
fread(data, 1, 6, ifp) reads 6 bytes into data[0..5]. Bytes data[6..65535] are uninitialized or contain stale values from a previous iteration.
Code computes index 1 + data[0]*2 = 1 + 255*2 = 511 and accesses data[511], which was never written by fread for this marker. Similarly accesses data[513].
jh->psv and jh->bits are set from uninitialized stack memory, which could be leveraged to influence subsequent image decoding with attacker-favorable values.

state.value: Modified by uninitialized value from stack

commit_message:
Fix UAF in aaaa on arm/thumb switching ##crash

* Reported by @peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer tests_65185
* This is a logic fix, but not the fully safe as changes in the code
  can result on UAF again, to properly protect r2 from crashing we
  need to break the ABI and add refcounting to RRegItem, which can't
  happen in 5.6.x because of abi-compat rules
cve_desc: Use After Free in op_is_set_bp in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {
vulnerabilities.description: When `stack_rev` is true, the loop variable `i` is of type `size_t` (unsigned). When `to` is 0, the loop condition `i >= to` is equivalent to `i >= 0`, which is always true for an unsigned type. After `i` reaches 0 and is decremented via `i--`, it wraps around to SIZE_MAX, causing an integer underflow. The loop then continues with an extremely large index, leading to out-of-bounds reads when `i` is passed to functions like `r_type_func_args_type`.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with valid parameters, and `*sign == '+'` so the '+' branch is taken. `isarg` is true.
A function name `fname` is successfully guessed. `stack_rev` is determined to be true because the calling convention's arg place is "stack_rev".
`cnt` (the number of function args from the type database) is 0 or any value, and `r_anal_cc_max_arg` returns 0, setting `to = 0`. `from` is set based on `cnt`.
The for-loop begins with `i = 0`. The condition `i >= to` evaluates to `0 >= 0` which is true. The loop body executes.

Assume `r_type_func_args_type` returns a non-NULL value and `sum_sz != frame_off`, so the iteration completes normally and `i--` is executed. Since `i` is `size_t` (unsigned), decrementing 0 wraps to SIZE_MAX.

The loop condition `i >= to` is checked: `SIZE_MAX >= 0` is always true for unsigned. The loop continues with a massive index value, passing it to `r_type_func_args_type(anal->sdb_types, fname, i)`, causing out-of-bounds access or undefined behavior.

commit_message: Fix oob write for dwarf with abbrev with count 0 (Fix #2083) (#2086)
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. In versions up to and including 0.3.1 there is a heap-based out of bounds write in parse_die() when reversing an AMD64 ELF binary with DWARF debug info. When a malicious AMD64 ELF binary is opened by a victim user, Rizin may crash or execute unintended actions. No workaround are known and users are advised to upgrade.

commit_message: check vertexIndex which might be -1 for broken DXF
cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the expression `4*maxVertices` when `maxVertices` is a large value parsed from user input. Since `maxVertices` is an `int`, the multiplication `4*maxVertices` is performed in `int` arithmetic and can wrap around to a small value. This causes a small heap buffer to be allocated, while the subsequent initialization loop iterates based on the original (large) `maxVertices`, writing far beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: groupCode is 90, so we enter the first branch to allocate vertices.
maxVertices is set from user-controlled input via toInt(groupValue). An attacker provides a large value such as 0x20000001 (536870913).
maxVertices > 0 check passes, so we proceed to allocate.

The expression `4*maxVertices` is computed in int arithmetic: 4 * 0x20000001 = 0x80000004, which overflows a 32-bit signed int. Depending on implementation, this wraps to a small or negative value. With unsigned interpretation or a slightly different value like 0x30000001, 4*0x30000001 = 0xC0000004 truncated to int. For maxVertices = 0x40000001, 4*0x40000001 = 0x100000004, truncated to 4 as 32-bit int. The small value is then zero/sign-extended to size_t for the new[] call.
A very small buffer is allocated: `new double[4]` — only 32 bytes on the heap.

The initialization loop runs from i=0 to i<maxVertices (1073741825 iterations), writing to vertices[i*4], vertices[i*4+1], etc. This massively overflows the 32-byte heap buffer, corrupting heap memory.

state.value: ~32 GB worth of writes past a 32-byte buffer

Problem:    Reading before the start of the line.
Solution:   When displaying "$" check the column is not negative.
cve_desc: Improper Validation of Specified Quantity in Input in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0218.

commit_message:
Fix UAF in aaaa on arm/thumb switching ##crash

* Reported by @peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer tests_65185
* This is a logic fix, but not the fully safe as changes in the code
  can result on UAF again, to properly protect r2 from crashing we
  need to break the ABI and add refcounting to RRegItem, which can't
  happen in 5.6.x because of abi-compat rules
cve_desc: Use After Free in op_is_set_bp in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {
vulnerabilities.description: When `stack_rev` is true, the loop counter `i` (of type `size_t`, which is unsigned) is decremented. When `i` reaches 0 and matches `to` (which is 0), the loop body executes, then `i--` wraps `i` to `SIZE_MAX`. The condition `i >= to` (i.e., `SIZE_MAX >= 0`) remains true, causing the loop to continue with an extremely large index value. This leads to out-of-bounds reads when `i` is passed to functions like `r_type_func_args_type` and `r_type_func_args_name`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with `sign` pointing to '+', so the `*sign == '+'` branch is taken. `ptr` is non-zero and `isarg` is true.
A function name is guessed via `r_type_func_guess`, returning a valid `fname`.
`place` is determined via `r_anal_cc_arg` and equals "stack_rev", so `stack_rev` is set to true.
`from` is set to `cnt - 1` (or `cnt` if 0) and `to` is set to `r_anal_cc_max_arg(anal, fcn->cc)` which returns 0 (or `fcn->cc` is NULL making `to = 0`).
Loop begins: condition `i >= to` is `0 >= 0` which is true, so the loop body executes. After the body (assuming `sum_sz != frame_off`), `i--` is evaluated.
After `i--`, since `i` is `size_t` (unsigned), it wraps around to `SIZE_MAX` (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF on 64-bit).

Loop condition `i >= to` evaluates to `SIZE_MAX >= 0` which is true, so the loop continues. `r_type_func_args_type(anal->sdb_types, fname, SIZE_MAX)` is called with an astronomically large index, leading to out-of-bounds access or undefined behavior.

commit_message:
Calcualte max nspace declarations correctly for XML writer

(raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common): Calculate max including for
each attribute a potential name and value.

Fixes Issues #0000617 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=617
and #0000618 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=618
cve_desc: raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common in raptor_xml_writer.c in Raptor RDF Syntax Library 2.0.15 miscalculates the maximum nspace declarations for the XML writer, leading to heap-based buffer overflows (sometimes seen in raptor_qname_format_as_xml).

code_snippets:
for(j = 0; j < nspace_declarations_count; j++)
            if(nspace_declarations[j].nspace == element->attributes[j]->nspace) {
vulnerabilities.description: The duplicate namespace check inside the attribute loop uses `element->attributes[j]` instead of `element->attributes[i]`. Since `nspace_declarations_count` can exceed `element->attribute_count` (it includes the element's own namespace declaration and previously processed attributes), the index `j` can go out of bounds of the `element->attributes` array, causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with an element that has a namespace not in scope and at least one attribute with a different namespace also not in scope.
nspace_max_count is calculated and nspace_declarations array is allocated.
The element's own namespace is not in scope, so it's added to nspace_declarations at index 0.

The attribute loop starts with i=0. The attribute at index 0 has a namespace not in scope and different from element's namespace. The inner deduplication loop runs for j=0 to nspace_declarations_count-1 (j=0).

The check accesses element->attributes[j] (j=0) instead of element->attributes[i] (i=0). In this case j==i so it's fine. The namespace is declared at nspace_declarations[1], and then the attribute itself is added at nspace_declarations[2]. nspace_declarations_count becomes 3.

If there's a second attribute (attribute_count >= 2) with a namespace not in scope, the inner loop iterates j from 0 to 2. When j=2, element->attributes[2] is accessed, but if attribute_count is 2, valid indices are only 0 and 1. This is an out-of-bounds read.

code_snippets:
size_t buf_length = XML_LANG_PREFIX_LEN + lang_len + 1;
unsigned char* buffer = RAPTOR_MALLOC(unsigned char*, buf_length + 1);
vulnerabilities.description: When building the xml:lang attribute string, the buffer is sized based on the raw string length (lang_len) without accounting for XML escape expansion. The escape function is passed `buf_length` (lang_len + 11) as the available output space, but actual remaining space after the prefix is only `lang_len + 2`. If the language string contains characters that get escaped (expanding in size), `raptor_xml_escape_string` can write past the end of the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with an element that has xml_language set to a string containing characters requiring XML escaping, e.g., '&' characters.
buf_length is calculated as XML_LANG_PREFIX_LEN + lang_len + 1 = 10 + 3 + 1 = 14. Buffer is allocated with buf_length + 1 = 15 bytes.
The prefix 'xml:lang="' (10 bytes) is copied into the buffer. Pointer p is set to buffer + 10, leaving 5 bytes of space.

raptor_xml_escape_string is called with the output buffer at p, and buf_length (14) as the available size parameter. The '&' in 'a&b' expands to '&' (5 chars), so total output is 'a&b' = 7 bytes. The function believes it has 14 bytes available but only 5 bytes actually remain. It writes 7 bytes starting at p, overflowing the buffer by 2 bytes.

commit_message:
lib/iov_iter: initialize "flags" in new pipe_buffer

The functions copy_page_to_iter_pipe() and push_pipe() can both
allocate a new pipe_buffer, but the "flags" member initializer is
missing.

Fixes: 241699cd72a8 ("new iov_iter flavour: pipe-backed")
To: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
To: linux-fsdevel@vger.kernel.org
To: linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Max Kellermann <max.kellermann@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

commit_message: ITS#9454 fix issuerAndThisUpdateCheck
cve_desc: In OpenLDAP through 2.4.57 and 2.5.x through 2.5.1alpha, an assertion failure in slapd can occur in the issuerAndThisUpdateCheck function via a crafted packet, resulting in a denial of service (daemon exit) via a short timestamp. This is related to schema_init.c and checkTime.

vulnerabilities.description: In the loop scanning for the closing double-quote of the issuer value, when is->bv_len equals x.bv_len - 1 and is->bv_val[is->bv_len] is a double-quote, the code reads is->bv_val[is->bv_len + 1] without verifying that is->bv_len + 1 is still within the bounds of x.bv_len. This results in a one-byte out-of-bounds read past the logical end of the buffer being parsed.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives an input berval `in` that passes the minimum length check and the '{'/'}' brace check. For example: {issuer rdnSequence:"...data...",thisUpdate "YYMMDDhhmmssZ"} where the issuer content ends with a double-quote character positioned such that it falls at the last index of x's remaining length.
x is set up by stripping the outer braces. x.bv_val points one past '{', x.bv_len = in->bv_len - 2.

The parser matches 'issuer', advances past it, skips a space, matches 'rdnSequence:', advances past it, matches a double-quote, and advances x.bv_val and decreases x.bv_len accordingly. is->bv_val is set to x.bv_val (start of issuer content) and is->bv_len to 0.

state.value: points to start of issuer quoted content

step_by_step_execution.description: The scanning loop iterates: for (; is->bv_len < x.bv_len; ). When is->bv_len reaches x.bv_len - 1 and is->bv_val[is->bv_len] happens to be a '"' character (the issuer content itself contains a quote near the boundary), the loop body enters the quote-check branch.

The code reads is->bv_val[is->bv_len + 1] which equals is->bv_val[x.bv_len]. This index is one past the logical end of the x buffer (x.bv_val + x.bv_len), constituting an out-of-bounds read. While it reads into the '}' of the original in buffer (which happens to be allocated), the access is beyond the intended parsing boundary and no bounds check guards it.

state.value: x.bv_len (one past the end of logical buffer)

commit_message:
random32: update the net random state on interrupt and activity

This modifies the first 32 bits out of the 128 bits of a random CPU's
net_rand_state on interrupt or CPU activity to complicate remote
observations that could lead to guessing the network RNG's internal
state.

Note that depending on some network devices' interrupt rate moderation
or binding, this re-seeding might happen on every packet or even almost
never.

In addition, with NOHZ some CPUs might not even get timer interrupts,
leaving their local state rarely updated, while they are running
networked processes making use of the random state.  For this reason, we
also perform this update in update_process_times() in order to at least
update the state when there is user or system activity, since it's the
only case we care about.

Reported-by: Amit Klein <aksecurity@gmail.com>
Suggested-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
Cc: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Cc: "Jason A. Donenfeld" <Jason@zx2c4.com>
Cc: Andy Lutomirski <luto@kernel.org>
Cc: Kees Cook <keescook@chromium.org>
Cc: Thomas Gleixner <tglx@linutronix.de>
Cc: Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: Willy Tarreau <w@1wt.eu>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The Linux kernel through 5.7.11 allows remote attackers to make observations that help to obtain sensitive information about the internal state of the network RNG, aka CID-f227e3ec3b5c. This is related to drivers/char/random.c and kernel/time/timer.c.

commit_message:
red fixes (#468)

* red fixes

* remove magic number
cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

vulnerabilities.description: The pipePath string (from user-controlled endpoint.name()) is copied into the fixed-size sun_path buffer (typically 108 bytes on Linux) using strcpy without any length check, leading to a stack buffer overflow if pipePath exceeds the buffer size.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives an endpoint parameter and extracts the pipe path name.

state.value: arbitrary string from endpoint.name(), can be longer than sizeof(local.sun_path) (~108 bytes)

step_by_step_execution.description: A sockaddr_un struct is declared on the stack, which contains a fixed-size sun_path buffer (typically 108 bytes).

state.value: uninitialized, fixed-size char array of ~108 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: strcpy copies the entire pipePath into local.sun_path without bounds checking. If pipePath length exceeds ~107 characters, it overflows the sun_path buffer and corrupts adjacent stack memory.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0025: accessing beyond allocated memory with the cmdline window

Problem:    Accessing beyond allocated memory when using the cmdline window in
            Ex mode.
Solution:   Use "*" instead of "'<,'>" for Visual mode.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Write in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.description: When has_visual_range is TRUE, use_plus_cmd is TRUE, and eap->cmd > cmd_start (modifiers were parsed), the code moves content from cmd_start (orig_cmd + 5) back to orig_cmd and then appends the 7-byte string "'<,'>+\0" at orig_cmd + len. The original buffer held 5 + len + 1 bytes of content (the "'<,'>" prefix plus the len-length remainder plus NUL). The new content requires len + 6 + 1 = len + 7 bytes. Since len + 7 > len + 6, this writes 1 byte past the end of the original string's storage, causing a heap/stack buffer overflow (off-by-one).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with eap->cmd pointing to a buffer starting with "'<,'>" followed by command modifiers but no actual command, while in ex mode (exmode_active is true).

state.value: points to buffer containing e.g. "'<,'>silent\0" (total 12 bytes including NUL)

step_by_step_execution.description: The code detects the "'<,'>" prefix and advances eap->cmd by 5, saving cmd_start.
In the for loop, "silent" is parsed as a command modifier, advancing eap->cmd past it.
The loop encounters NUL while exmode_active is true, sets use_plus_cmd = TRUE and breaks.
Post-loop: has_visual_range is TRUE and eap->cmd > cmd_start, so the first branch is entered. use_plus_cmd is TRUE, so the special case executes.
len = STRLEN(cmd_start) computes the length of the string from cmd_start to NUL.
mch_memmove(orig_cmd, cmd_start, len) moves 6 bytes ("silent") to orig_cmd. Buffer now: "silentilent\0" (first 6 bytes overwritten).

STRCPY(orig_cmd + len, "'<,'>+") writes 7 bytes ("'<,'>+\0") starting at orig_cmd + 6. This needs bytes at offsets 6 through 12, but the original buffer only held 12 bytes (offsets 0-11). Offset 12 is one byte past the buffer.

state.value: 1 byte written at offset 12, past end of buffer

commit_message: Fixed an issue affecting recursions in JIT
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the get_recurse_data_length() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This issue affects recursions in JIT-compiled regular expressions caused by duplicate data transfers.

commit_message:
xfrm: xfrm_policy: fix a possible double xfrm_pols_put() in xfrm_bundle_lookup()

xfrm_policy_lookup() will call xfrm_pol_hold_rcu() to get a refcount of
pols[0]. This refcount can be dropped in xfrm_expand_policies() when
xfrm_expand_policies() return error. pols[0]'s refcount is balanced in
here. But xfrm_bundle_lookup() will also call xfrm_pols_put() with
num_pols == 1 to drop this refcount when xfrm_expand_policies() return
error.

This patch also fix an illegal address access. pols[0] will save a error
point when xfrm_policy_lookup fails. This lead to xfrm_pols_put to resolve
an illegal address in xfrm_bundle_lookup's error path.

Fix these by setting num_pols = 0 in xfrm_expand_policies()'s error path.

Fixes: 80c802f3073e ("xfrm: cache bundles instead of policies for outgoing flows")
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.18.14. xfrm_expand_policies in net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c can cause a refcount to be dropped twice.

commit_message: Fixed an issue affecting recursions in JIT
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the get_recurse_data_length() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This issue affects recursions in JIT-compiled regular expressions caused by duplicate data transfers.

commit_message:
random32: update the net random state on interrupt and activity

This modifies the first 32 bits out of the 128 bits of a random CPU's
net_rand_state on interrupt or CPU activity to complicate remote
observations that could lead to guessing the network RNG's internal
state.

Note that depending on some network devices' interrupt rate moderation
or binding, this re-seeding might happen on every packet or even almost
never.

In addition, with NOHZ some CPUs might not even get timer interrupts,
leaving their local state rarely updated, while they are running
networked processes making use of the random state.  For this reason, we
also perform this update in update_process_times() in order to at least
update the state when there is user or system activity, since it's the
only case we care about.

Reported-by: Amit Klein <aksecurity@gmail.com>
Suggested-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
Cc: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Cc: "Jason A. Donenfeld" <Jason@zx2c4.com>
