commit_message:
mount: sanitize paths from non-root users

 $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
 mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

this is too promiscuous. It seems better to ignore on command line
specified paths which are not resolve-able for non-root users.

Fixed version:

  $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
  mount: /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2: Permission denied

  $ mount /dev/sda2
  mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

Note that this bug has no relation to mount(2) permissions evaluation
in suid mode. The way how non-root user specifies paths on command
line is completely irrelevant for comparison with fstab entries.

Signed-off-by: Karel Zak <kzak@redhat.com>
cve_desc: (a) mount and (b) umount in util-linux 2.14.1, 2.17.2, and probably other versions allow local users to determine the existence of restricted directories by (1) using the --guess-fstype command-line option or (2) attempting to mount a non-existent device, which generates different error messages depending on whether the directory exists.

commit_message:
red fixes (#468)

* red fixes

* remove magic number
cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

vulnerabilities.description: strncpy does not null-terminate the destination buffer when the source string length is >= sizeof(local.sun_path) (typically 108 bytes). If pipePath is long enough, local.sun_path will not be null-terminated, and subsequent uses (unlink, bind, chmod) will read beyond the buffer boundary looking for a null terminator, causing out-of-bounds reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives an endpoint whose name() returns a string of length >= sizeof(local.sun_path) (typically 108 bytes).
A sockaddr_un struct 'local' is declared on the stack. Its sun_path field is a fixed-size char array (typically 108 bytes).

strncpy copies at most sizeof(local.sun_path) bytes from pipePath into local.sun_path. Because pipePath length >= 108, all 108 bytes are filled with non-null characters, and strncpy does NOT write a null terminator.

unlink(local.sun_path) is called, which expects a null-terminated string. It reads past the 108-byte sun_path buffer into adjacent stack memory until it finds a null byte, constituting an out-of-bounds read. The resulting path string is unpredictable.

state.value: not null-terminated, read goes out of bounds

step_by_step_execution.description: bind() and chmod() similarly consume the non-null-terminated sun_path, also reading out of bounds.

this is too promiscuous. It seems better to ignore on command line

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

vulnerabilities.description: The local buffer `drc_name` is not null-terminated before being passed to `strchr`. `memcpy` copies exactly `nbytes` bytes but does not append a null terminator. Since `drc_name` is an uninitialized stack buffer, `strchr` will read past the copied data into uninitialized stack memory looking for '\n' or '\0'. This can cause an out-of-bounds read on the stack. Worse, if `strchr` finds a '\n' byte in the uninitialized region beyond the array boundary, `end` will point outside `drc_name`, and `*end = '\0'` will perform an out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `buf` containing data with no '\n' character and no '\0' character within `nbytes` bytes. `nbytes` is less than MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN (e.g., nbytes = MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN - 1).

state.value: contains no '\n' or '\0' in its first nbytes bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: The size check passes because nbytes < MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN.

`memcpy(drc_name, buf, nbytes)` copies nbytes bytes into `drc_name`. The buffer is MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN bytes long, so the last byte at index MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN-1 remains uninitialized. No null terminator is written.

`strchr(drc_name, '\n')` is called. Since the copied region has no '\n' and no '\0', strchr continues scanning into the uninitialized bytes of drc_name and potentially beyond the array boundary into other stack memory.

state.value: past copied data, into uninitialized stack memory, potentially beyond drc_name bounds

step_by_step_execution.description:
If strchr does not find '
' or '\0' within the drc_name array, it reads out of bounds (CWE-125). If it finds '
' at an address beyond the drc_name array, `end` points outside the buffer, and `*end = '\0'` writes out of bounds (CWE-787). If no '
' is found and NULL is returned, the `if (!end)` branch sets end = &drc_name[nbytes] which is safe, but this only happens if '\0' is encountered before '
'.

state.value: potentially points beyond drc_name array boundary

commit_message:
udf: Restore i_lenAlloc when inode expansion fails

When we fail to expand inode from inline format to a normal format, we
restore inode to contain the original inline formatting but we forgot to
set i_lenAlloc back. The mismatch between i_lenAlloc and i_size was then
causing further problems such as warnings and lost data down the line.

Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: 7e49b6f2480c ("udf: Convert UDF to new truncate calling sequence")
Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Signed-off-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>
cve_desc: A flaw null pointer dereference in the Linux kernel UDF file system functionality was found in the way user triggers udf_file_write_iter function for the malicious UDF image. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system. Actual from Linux kernel 4.2-rc1 till 5.17-rc2.

vulnerabilities.description: If iinfo->i_lenAlloc is greater than PAGE_SIZE (e.g., from a crafted/corrupt UDF filesystem image), the memset size calculation (PAGE_SIZE - iinfo->i_lenAlloc) underflows to a very large value, and the memcpy writes beyond the page boundary. There is no validation that i_lenAlloc <= PAGE_SIZE before these operations.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with an inode from a (possibly crafted) UDF filesystem. iinfo->i_lenAlloc is read from on-disk metadata and is not validated against PAGE_SIZE.

state.value: A value larger than PAGE_SIZE, e.g., PAGE_SIZE + 0x100 (from corrupt/crafted UDF image)

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `if (!iinfo->i_lenAlloc)` is false because i_lenAlloc is non-zero, so execution continues past the early return.
i_data_sem is released and a page is found/created successfully. The page is not uptodate, so we enter the if block.
kmap_atomic maps the page, giving a kernel virtual address to a PAGE_SIZE buffer.

memset(kaddr + iinfo->i_lenAlloc, 0x00, PAGE_SIZE - iinfo->i_lenAlloc) is called. Since i_lenAlloc > PAGE_SIZE, the size argument underflows (unsigned subtraction) to a very large value, causing a massive out-of-bounds write past the page.

state.value: wraps to ~(size_t)0 - 0xFF, a huge value (unsigned underflow)

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy(kaddr, iinfo->i_data + iinfo->i_lenEAttr, iinfo->i_lenAlloc) copies i_lenAlloc bytes into the PAGE_SIZE buffer, overflowing it by 0x100 bytes.

vulnerabilities.description: In the error recovery path, inode->i_size is used as the copy length into iinfo->i_data without checking if it fits in the available space (the inode's inline data area). A crafted filesystem could set inode->i_size to a value larger than the inline data buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is entered with a crafted UDF inode where i_lenAlloc is valid (fits in a page) but inode->i_size has been set to a large value (e.g. by modifying inode->i_size between the initial copy and the error path, or from corrupt metadata).

state.value: large value exceeding inline data area capacity

step_by_step_execution.description: The normal path executes: data is copied to the page, i_lenAlloc is zeroed, alloc type changed, page is dirtied and unlocked.
filemap_fdatawrite fails, entering the error recovery path.
memcpy copies inode->i_size bytes from the page into iinfo->i_data + iinfo->i_lenEAttr. Since inode->i_size may exceed the available space in iinfo->i_data, this overflows the inode info buffer.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/4988
cve_desc: ImageMagick 7.1.0-27 is vulnerable to Buffer Overflow.

commit_message: do not attempt to write a null image list (thanks to Vinay Rohila)
cve_desc: In ImageMagick, a crafted file could trigger an assertion failure when a call to WriteImages was made in MagickWand/operation.c, due to a NULL image list. This could potentially cause a denial of service. This was fixed in upstream ImageMagick version 7.1.0-30.

commit_message: Fix oob write for dwarf with abbrev with count 0 (Fix #2083) (#2086)
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. In versions up to and including 0.3.1 there is a heap-based out of bounds write in parse_die() when reversing an AMD64 ELF binary with DWARF debug info. When a malicious AMD64 ELF binary is opened by a victim user, Rizin may crash or execute unintended actions. No workaround are known and users are advised to upgrade.

code_snippets:
buf = parse_attr_value(buf, buf_end - buf, &abbrev->defs[i],
				&die->attr_values[i], hdr, debug_str, debug_str_len, big_endian);
vulnerabilities.description: If parse_attr_value returns NULL (e.g., on a parse error or end-of-buffer condition), the returned NULL pointer is used in the next loop iteration without any check. On the next iteration, 'buf_end - buf' (where buf is NULL) is undefined behavior, and NULL is passed as the buffer to parse_attr_value, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

code_snippets:
buf = parse_attr_value(buf, buf_end - buf, &abbrev->defs[i],
				&die->attr_values[i], hdr, debug_str, debug_str_len, big_endian);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with abbrev->count >= 2, so the loop will iterate at least once.
parse_attr_value is called with buf and (buf_end - buf) as the size. If parsing fails or the buffer is exhausted, it may return NULL.

The loop continues to the next iteration (i=1). buf_end - buf is computed where buf is NULL, which is undefined behavior. Then NULL is passed as the buffer pointer to parse_attr_value, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4418: crash when using special multi-byte character

Problem:    Crash when using special multi-byte character.
Solution:   Don't use isalpha() for an arbitrary character.
cve_desc: Use of Out-of-range Pointer Offset in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4418.

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow: the buffer `buf` is allocated with size `STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5`, assuming directory entry names will not exceed BASENAMELEN. However, on certain filesystems (e.g., network mounts), `dp->d_name` from `readdir()` can exceed BASENAMELEN. When `STRCPY(s, dp->d_name)` is called with a long filename, it overflows `buf`. Furthermore, even with names exactly BASENAMELEN long, the subsequent `STRCPY(buf + len, "/**")` and `STRCPY(buf + len + 3, path_end)` appends additional characters that may exceed the allocated buffer size, depending on the relationship between the original wildcard component length and the replacement filename length.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a path like "/**" (length 3), wildoff=0, flags with some value, didstar=FALSE.
Buffer is allocated: STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5 = 3 + 255 + 5 = 263 bytes.

The loop parses the path. The '/' is found first, then '**' is found as a wildcard. path_end points to the trailing NUL or further path components. s points to buf+1 (just after '/'), e points to buf+3.

state.value: points to NUL at end of path or remaining path after '**' component

step_by_step_execution.description: Directory is opened. readdir() returns an entry with d_name longer than BASENAMELEN (possible on certain filesystems like network FS, or a filesystem with a NAME_MAX > 255).

state.value: a filename of length > BASENAMELEN, e.g. 300 characters

step_by_step_execution.description: STRCPY(s, dp->d_name) copies the filename into buf starting at offset s. Since s = buf+1 and dp->d_name is 300 chars, it writes 301 bytes starting at buf+1, totaling 302 bytes from buf start, exceeding the 263-byte allocation.

state.value: 263 bytes allocated, but 302 bytes written — heap buffer overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: Even if the filename fits exactly within BASENAMELEN (255 chars), subsequent operations like STRCPY(buf + len, "/**") append 3 more characters, and STRCPY(buf + len + 3, path_end) appends the remaining path, which together can exceed the buffer when the replaced wildcard component (e.g., 2 chars for '**') was shorter than the actual filename.

state.value: prefix_len + 255 (filename) + 3 (/**) + remaining_path_len, potentially exceeding allocated size

commit_message: Fix segfault in older versions of OpenSSL (before 0.9.8i)
cve_desc: The openssl_encrypt function in ext/openssl/openssl.c in PHP 5.3.9 through 5.3.13 does not initialize a certain variable, which allows remote attackers to obtain sensitive information from process memory by providing zero bytes of input data.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of the output buffer size. If `data_len` is sufficiently large (close to INT_MAX), adding `EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type)` can cause an integer overflow, resulting in a small allocation via `emalloc`. The subsequent `EVP_DecryptUpdate` then writes far more data than the allocated buffer can hold, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `raw_input = 1` and a very large `data_len` (close to INT_MAX, e.g., 0x7FFFFFF0). The base64 decode path is skipped since raw_input is true.
A valid cipher is looked up, e.g. AES-128-CBC with block size 16.
The output buffer length is calculated: `outlen = data_len + EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type)`. This overflows the signed int, wrapping to a small or negative value.

state.value: 0x7FFFFFF0 + 16 = 0x80000000 (signed integer overflow, wraps to negative or small value)

step_by_step_execution.description: `outbuf = emalloc(outlen + 1)` allocates a very small buffer (or behavior is undefined due to negative size being cast to size_t — either way the allocation is not large enough for the actual data).
`EVP_DecryptUpdate` is called with the original large `data_len`, writing up to `data_len + block_size` bytes into the undersized `outbuf`, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
- Fix Integer Overflows in Size Calculations,
  reported by X41 D-Sec.
cve_desc: Unbound before 1.9.5 allows an integer overflow in a size calculation in dnscrypt/dnscrypt.c. NOTE: The vendor disputes that this is a vulnerability. Although the code may be vulnerable, a running Unbound installation cannot be remotely or locally exploited

commit_message: Fix segfault in older versions of OpenSSL (before 0.9.8i)
cve_desc: The openssl_encrypt function in ext/openssl/openssl.c in PHP 5.3.9 through 5.3.13 does not initialize a certain variable, which allows remote attackers to obtain sensitive information from process memory by providing zero bytes of input data.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the calculation of 'outlen = data_len + EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type)'. If data_len is sufficiently large (close to INT_MAX), the addition can wrap around to a small or negative value, causing a small heap buffer to be allocated via emalloc. The subsequent EVP_EncryptUpdate call then writes the full encrypted output into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: User calls openssl_encrypt with a very large data string. zend_parse_parameters parses arguments successfully.
cipher_type is resolved and block size is retrieved. For AES, block size is 16.
outlen is calculated as data_len + block_size. This overflows the int type, wrapping to a small or negative value.
emalloc(outlen + 1) is called. Depending on how the wrapped value is interpreted (possibly cast to size_t), a small buffer or unexpected size is allocated.

state.value: Points to a small heap-allocated buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: EVP_EncryptUpdate writes encrypted data for the full data_len bytes into outbuf, overflowing the undersized heap buffer.

state.value: Heap buffer overflow: writes ~data_len bytes into a much smaller buffer

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1540
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-40 Q16, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the function WritePNMImage of coders/pnm.c, which allows an attacker to cause a denial of service or possibly information disclosure via a crafted image file. This is related to SetGrayscaleImage in MagickCore/quantize.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When image->storage_class is PseudoClass, colormap_index is allocated with only (image->colors+1) elements. However, in the final pixel-rewriting loop, the array is indexed by ScaleQuantumToMap(GetPixelIndex(image,q)), which maps a Quantum value to the Map range (0..MaxMap, typically up to 65535). If image->colors is small, this index can far exceed the allocated buffer size, causing a heap-based out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a PseudoClass image that has a small number of colors (e.g., image->colors = 10).
colormap_index is allocated with (image->colors+1) elements, i.e., 11 ssize_t entries.
Because storage_class is PseudoClass, the block that handles non-PseudoClass images (which allocates MaxColormapSize+1 entries) is skipped entirely.
The sorting/dedup section executes, writing to colormap_index at indices corresponding to image->colormap[i].alpha (which are 0..image->colors-1), so indices 0..9. This is within bounds.

The final loop iterates over all pixels. For each pixel, it computes ScaleQuantumToMap(GetPixelIndex(image,q)). ScaleQuantumToMap maps a Quantum value to the range 0..MaxMap (e.g., 65535). Even a modest pixel index value, when scaled, can produce an index far exceeding 11.

colormap_index is accessed at an index potentially up to 65535, while only 11 elements were allocated. This is a heap out-of-bounds read, and the result is used to set the pixel index, potentially also corrupting image data.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4418: crash when using special multi-byte character

Problem:    Crash when using special multi-byte character.
Solution:   Don't use isalpha() for an arbitrary character.
cve_desc: Use of Out-of-range Pointer Offset in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4418.

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow when a directory entry name (dp->d_name) exceeds BASENAMELEN. The buffer is allocated as STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5, assuming filenames from readdir() fit within BASENAMELEN bytes. However, POSIX does not guarantee that d_name length is bounded by BASENAMELEN; on certain filesystems (e.g., network filesystems), names can be longer. When STRCPY(s, dp->d_name) copies such a long name into buf, it overflows the heap buffer. The subsequent STRCPY operations that append "/**" and path_end further exacerbate the overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Buffer buf is allocated with size STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5.

state.value: STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5 (e.g., if path is "/dir/*", size = 6 + 255 + 5 = 266)

step_by_step_execution.description: The path is parsed: s points to the wildcard component start within buf, e points to its end. For path "/dir/*", s points to buf+5 (the '*'), e points to buf+6.

state.value: points to NUL (nothing after the wildcard)

step_by_step_execution.description: Directory /dir/ is opened and readdir() returns an entry with a name longer than BASENAMELEN (e.g., 300 bytes on a filesystem that supports long names).
STRCPY(s, dp->d_name) copies 300 bytes starting at s (buf+5). This writes to buf[5] through buf[304], but buf is only 266 bytes, causing a heap buffer overflow of ~39 bytes.
If starstar is true, STRCPY(buf + len, "/**") and STRCPY(buf + len + 3, path_end) write even further past the end of the buffer.

commit_message:
Calcualte max nspace declarations correctly for XML writer

(raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common): Calculate max including for
each attribute a potential name and value.

Fixes Issues #0000617 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=617
and #0000618 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=618
cve_desc: raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common in raptor_xml_writer.c in Raptor RDF Syntax Library 2.0.15 miscalculates the maximum nspace declarations for the XML writer, leading to heap-based buffer overflows (sometimes seen in raptor_qname_format_as_xml).

code_snippets:
for(j = 0; j < nspace_declarations_count; j++)
            if(nspace_declarations[j].nspace == element->attributes[j]->nspace) {
vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on element->attributes[j]. The inner loop iterates j up to nspace_declarations_count, but indexes element->attributes[j] instead of element->attributes[i]. Since nspace_declarations_count can exceed element->attribute_count (due to element namespace and attribute value entries incrementing the count), j can go past the bounds of the attributes array.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with an element that has element->name->nspace set and not in scope, and element->attribute_count >= 2 with at least the second attribute having a namespace.
nspace_max_count is calculated and nspace_declarations array is allocated.
Element namespace is not in scope, so it is added to nspace_declarations.

Attribute loop begins. i=0: attribute has a namespace not in scope, different from element namespace. Inner dedup loop runs j=0..0. j=0 compares nspace_declarations[0].nspace == element->attributes[0]->nspace — happens to be in-bounds. declare_me remains 1, namespace declaration added.
Attribute i=0's value is added unconditionally.

Attribute loop continues. i=1: attribute has a namespace not in scope. Inner dedup loop runs j=0..2. When j=2, the code accesses element->attributes[2], but attribute_count is only 2, so valid indices are 0 and 1. This is an out-of-bounds read.

code_snippets:
size_t buf_length = XML_LANG_PREFIX_LEN + lang_len + 1;
unsigned char* buffer = RAPTOR_MALLOC(unsigned char*, buf_length + 1);
vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow when writing the xml:lang attribute. The buffer is allocated with size buf_length+1 = lang_len+12 bytes. After writing 10 bytes for "xml:lang=\"", pointer p is at buffer+10 with only lang_len+2 bytes remaining. However, raptor_xml_escape_string is told the output buffer size is buf_length (= lang_len+11), allowing it to write up to lang_len+11 bytes starting at p, overflowing the heap buffer. XML escape expansion (e.g., '"' to '"') makes the output longer than lang_len.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with nstack non-null and element->xml_language containing characters that need XML escaping, e.g., a string of '&' characters.
buf_length is computed as XML_LANG_PREFIX_LEN + lang_len + 1 = 10 + 5 + 1 = 16. Buffer of buf_length + 1 = 17 bytes is allocated.
10 bytes "xml:lang=\"" are written to buffer. p points to buffer+10. Remaining space from p to end of buffer is 7 bytes.

raptor_xml_escape_string is called with output buffer p and size limit buf_length=16. Each '&' expands to '&' (5 bytes). 5 ampersands expand to 25 bytes. Even bounded by buf_length=16, the function may write up to 16 bytes starting at p, but only 7 bytes of space remain, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: up to 16 (passed as limit), but only 7 bytes available
up to 9 bytes past buffer end

commit_message:
net: xilinx_emaclite: Do not print real IOMEM pointer

Printing kernel pointers is discouraged because they might leak kernel
memory layout.  This fixes smatch warning:

drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c:1191 xemaclite_of_probe() warn:
 argument 4 to %08lX specifier is cast from pointer

Signed-off-by: YueHaibing <yuehaibing@huawei.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.13.3 makes it easier for attackers to defeat an ASLR protection mechanism because it prints a kernel pointer (i.e., the real IOMEM pointer).

commit_message: fix #2964 - double-free in bin_qnx.c
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to a double free in bobj.c:rz_bin_reloc_storage_free() when freeing relocations generated from qnx binary plugin. A user opening a malicious qnx binary could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. commit_message:
Make ZlibInStream more robust against failures

Move the checks around to avoid missing cases where we might access
memory that is no longer valid. Also avoid touching the underlying
stream implicitly (e.g. via the destructor) as it might also no
longer be valid.

A malicious server could theoretically use this for remote code
execution in the client.

Issue found by Pavel Cheremushkin from Kaspersky Lab
cve_desc: TigerVNC version prior to 1.10.1 is vulnerable to stack use-after-return, which occurs due to incorrect usage of stack memory in ZRLEDecoder. If decoding routine would throw an exception, ZRLEDecoder may try to access stack variable, which has been already freed during the process of stack unwinding. Exploitation of this vulnerability could potentially result into remote code execution. This attack appear to be exploitable via network connectivity.

vulnerabilities.description: In both the packed-pixels and palette-RLE decoding paths, the palette index derived from attacker-controlled compressed stream data is not validated against palSize before being used to access the palette array. An attacker can craft data where the index exceeds palSize, causing a read of uninitialized stack memory from the palette[128] array, which is then written into the pixel buffer. This leaks stack contents to the display.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads the mode byte for a tile. Suppose mode = 50 (rle=false, palSize=50).
50 palette entries are read from the stream and stored in palette[0..49]. palette[50..127] remain uninitialized on the stack.
Since palSize=50 > 16, bppp is set to 8 in the packed pixels path.
A byte is read from the stream. The index is computed as (byte >> 0) & 0xFF & 127. If byte is e.g. 100, index = 100.
palette[100] is read, but only indices 0-49 were initialized. Uninitialized stack data is read and written to buf, then later to the pixel buffer via pb->imageRect().

vulnerabilities.description: In both the plain RLE and palette RLE decoding paths, the run length is accumulated in a signed int variable by repeatedly adding byte values from the attacker-controlled stream. An attacker can send a very long sequence of 255 bytes, causing signed integer overflow (undefined behavior in C++). This can result in len wrapping to an unexpected value, potentially bypassing the subsequent bounds check.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the plain RLE path (rle=true, palSize=0). A pixel value is read, then the run length accumulation loop begins with len=1.
The attacker sends approximately 8,421,504 consecutive bytes of value 255 through the compressed stream. Each iteration adds 255 to len.
One more byte of 255 is read, causing len += 255 to overflow INT_MAX (2147483647). This is signed integer overflow, which is undefined behavior.

state.value: undefined (signed integer overflow); on typical 2's complement: wraps to negative or other arbitrary value

step_by_step_execution.description: The bounds check 'if (end - ptr < len)' may not trigger if len has wrapped to a negative or otherwise unexpected value, since end - ptr is positive and a negative len would not satisfy the condition.

state.value: negative due to overflow; check evaluates to false

commit_message:
input-bmp: Increase header buffer in some cases

Signed-off-by: Peter Lemenkov <lemenkov@gmail.com>
cve_desc: AutoTrace v0.40.0 was discovered to contain a heap overflow via the ReadImage function at input-bmp.c:660.

vulnerabilities.description: When fopen() fails, fd is NULL and the code jumps to the cleanup label which calls fclose(fd). Passing NULL to fclose() is undefined behavior and will likely cause a NULL pointer dereference crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: fopen() is called and returns NULL because the file cannot be opened.
The code checks !fd, which is true, so it logs an error and calls at_exception_fatal.
The code executes 'goto cleanup', jumping to the cleanup label.
At cleanup, fclose(fd) is called with fd == NULL, which is undefined behavior per the C standard.

vulnerabilities.description: Bitmap_Head.biWidth and biHeight are 32-bit integers that can be larger than 65535 (the max for unsigned short). The cast to unsigned short truncates the value, causing the bitmap metadata to record a much smaller dimension than the actual allocated image buffer. This mismatch can lead to out-of-bounds access by callers who use the truncated dimensions.

step_by_step_execution.description: A valid BMP file with Windows 3.x header (biSize == 40) is parsed. biWidth is read as a 32-bit value, e.g. 70000.
Sanity checks pass: biWidth > 0, biHeight != 0, biPlanes == 1, and overflow checks pass since 70000 * 24 < 0x7fffffff and 70000 < 0x7fffffff / 100 / 4.
ReadImage allocates and fills a buffer for the full 70000-pixel width.
at_bitmap_init is called with (unsigned short)70000 = 4464 as the width, creating a bitmap struct that claims to be 4464 pixels wide but has data for 70000 pixels wide.

commit_message:
input-bmp: Increase header buffer in some cases

Signed-off-by: Peter Lemenkov <lemenkov@gmail.com>
cve_desc: AutoTrace v0.40.0 was discovered to contain a heap overflow via the ReadImage function at input-bmp.c:660.

vulnerabilities.description: When biSize is 108 or 124 (BMP Version 4 or 5), the code reads `biSize - 4` bytes (104 or 120 bytes) into a 64-byte stack buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A BMP file is opened and its magic bytes and initial header are read successfully.
The biSize field is read from the file. An attacker crafts a BMP with biSize = 108 or biSize = 124.
The code enters the branch for biSize == 108 || biSize == 124 and calls ReadOK(fd, buffer, Bitmap_File_Head.biSize - 4), which attempts to read 104 or 120 bytes.
ReadOK writes 104 or 120 bytes into the 64-byte buffer, overwriting adjacent stack memory (return address, saved registers, other local variables).

vulnerabilities.description: If fopen() fails and returns NULL, the code jumps to the cleanup label which calls fclose(NULL), resulting in undefined behavior.

step_by_step_execution.description: fopen fails and returns NULL.
The NULL check triggers and execution jumps to 'goto cleanup'.
At the cleanup label, fclose(fd) is called with NULL, which is undefined behavior.

vulnerabilities.description: Bitmap_Head.biWidth and biHeight (32-bit integers) are cast to unsigned short (16-bit), causing truncation. A large valid width/height (e.g. 65537) would be truncated to 1, resulting in a mismatch between allocated image data and reported dimensions.

step_by_step_execution.description: A valid BMP file is parsed with biWidth or biHeight larger than 65535 but passing all sanity checks.
ReadImage allocates and processes image data based on the full 32-bit width and height values.
The width is cast to unsigned short, truncating 65537 to 1. The bitmap metadata now misrepresents the actual buffer size.

commit_message:
lib/iov_iter: initialize "flags" in new pipe_buffer

The functions copy_page_to_iter_pipe() and push_pipe() can both
allocate a new pipe_buffer, but the "flags" member initializer is
missing.

Fixes: 241699cd72a8 ("new iov_iter flavour: pipe-backed")
To: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
To: linux-fsdevel@vger.kernel.org
To: linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Max Kellermann <max.kellermann@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.description: The pipe buffer's `flags` field is never cleared when initializing a new buffer entry. If a previous use of this pipe buffer slot left `PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE` set in `buf->flags`, the flag persists. Because the page being inserted is a page-cache page (via `get_page`), a subsequent `pipe_write` operation will see `PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE` and append user-controlled data directly into the page cache page, overwriting file contents even without write permissions. This is the "Dirty Pipe" vulnerability (CVE-2022-0847).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a page-cache page, an offset, a byte count, and a pipe iov_iter. `bytes` is clamped to `i->count` and validated to be non-zero.
`off` is read from `i->iov_offset`. Assume `off == 0`, meaning we are not continuing from a partial buffer — the merge path is skipped and `i_head` is not incremented.
`pipe_full()` is checked and returns false, so execution continues to initialize the buffer.

The buffer is initialized: `buf->ops`, `buf->page`, `buf->offset`, and `buf->len` are set. However, `buf->flags` is NOT written to. If a previous splice/pipe operation on this same ring slot set `PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE`, that flag remains.

state.value: stale value from previous use, potentially PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE

step_by_step_execution.description: Later, when userspace calls `write()` on the pipe, `pipe_write` sees `PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE` on the last buffer and directly copies user data into `buf->page` (the page-cache page), bypassing all file permission checks and corrupting cached file data.

state.value: page-cache page now writable by attacker

commit_message:
Fix oobread in dyldcache ##crash

* Reported by @hdthky via huntr.dev
* Reproducers: poc1
* BountyID: 8ae2c61a-2220-47a5-bfe8-fe6d41ab1f82
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of inducing denial of service.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < hdr->imagesCount; j++) {
				bool printing = !deps[j];
vulnerabilities.description: The `deps` array is allocated based on `cache->hdr->imagesCount` (i.e., cache->hdr[0].imagesCount), but the inner loop iterates using `hdr->imagesCount` where `hdr = &cache->hdr[i]` for each outer loop iteration. If a subsequent header (i > 0) has a larger `imagesCount` than the first header, accessing `deps[j]` results in an out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `deps` array is allocated with size `cache->hdr->imagesCount`, which is `cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`.
The outer loop iterates over all headers: `for (i = 0; i < cache->n_hdr; i++)`.
When i > 0, `hdr = &cache->hdr[i]` may have a larger `imagesCount` than `cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`.
The inner loop runs `for (j = 0; j < hdr->imagesCount; j++)` and accesses `deps[j]` which is out of bounds when j >= cache->hdr[0].imagesCount.

vulnerabilities.description: The `dep_index` value derived from `depArray[k] & 0x7fff` (up to 32767) is used to index both `deps` (sized `cache->hdr->imagesCount`) and `img` (sized `hdr->imagesCount`) without any bounds checking. A crafted or corrupted cache file can cause out-of-bounds array access.

step_by_step_execution.description: depArray is read from the cache file buffer and depArray[k] can contain arbitrary values.
dep_index is computed as depArray[k] & 0x7fff, which can be up to 0x7fff (32767).
deps is indexed with dep_index. deps has size cache->hdr->imagesCount which can be much smaller than 32767, causing out-of-bounds write.
Similarly, img[dep_index] is accessed via get_lib_name, causing out-of-bounds read if dep_index >= hdr->imagesCount.

vulnerabilities.description: The loop over depArray uses a sentinel value (0xffff) to terminate but does not check that `k` remains within the bounds of the `depArray` allocation (size `cache->accel->depListCount`). A malformed cache file without the sentinel could cause an out-of-bounds read past the depArray buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: depArray is allocated with cache->accel->depListCount elements.
extras[j].dependentsStartArrayIndex is read from the file and used as the starting index k.
The loop increments k until depArray[k] == 0xffff, but never checks k < cache->accel->depListCount. If the sentinel is missing or startIndex is already out of bounds, this reads past the buffer.

commit_message:
Avoid possible segfault when handling networkstatus vote with bad flavor

Fix for 6530; fix on 0.2.2.6-alpha.
cve_desc: Use-after-free vulnerability in dns.c in Tor before 0.2.2.38 might allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (daemon crash) via vectors related to failed DNS requests.

vulnerabilities.description: When tok->n_args is exactly 2 (i.e., > 1), the code accesses tok->args[2] in the error message, which is an out-of-bounds read. The parsed flavor comes from tok->args[1], but the error log references tok->args[2]. With only 2 arguments (indices 0 and 1), index 2 is out of bounds.

code_snippets:
log_warn(LD_DIR, "Can't parse document with unknown flavor %s",
               escaped(tok->args[2]));

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a network status string s. It tokenizes the header.
find_by_keyword locates the K_NETWORK_STATUS_VERSION token. The token has exactly 2 arguments (e.g., 'network-status-version 3 bogus_flavor').
The condition tok->n_args > 1 is true, so the code enters the branch and calls networkstatus_parse_flavor_name(tok->args[1]).
networkstatus_parse_flavor_name returns -1 for the unrecognized flavor string, so flavor < 0 is true.
The error log accesses tok->args[2], but only args[0] and args[1] exist (indices 0..1). This is an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message: Fix use-after-free in PlayerGeneric destructor
cve_desc: PlayerGeneric.cpp in MilkyTracker through 1.02.00 has a use-after-free in the PlayerGeneric destructor.

vulnerabilities.description: After `mixer` is deleted, the code dereferences it via `mixer->isActive()` and `mixer->isDeviceRemoved(player)` and `mixer->removeDevice(player)` inside the `if (player)` block, resulting in a use-after-free.

step_by_step_execution.description: The destructor is entered with both `mixer` and `player` being non-null pointers.
`mixer` is non-null, so `delete mixer` is executed, freeing the memory pointed to by `mixer`.
`player` is non-null, so the code enters the `if (player)` block.
`mixer->isActive()` is called on the already-freed `mixer` object, causing a use-after-free.

commit_message:
Fix oobread in dyldcache ##crash

* Reported by @hdthky via huntr.dev
* Reproducers: poc1
* BountyID: 8ae2c61a-2220-47a5-bfe8-fe6d41ab1f82
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of inducing denial of service.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < hdr->imagesCount; j++) {
				bool printing = !deps[j];
vulnerabilities.description: The `deps` array is allocated using `cache->hdr->imagesCount` (i.e., `cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`), but the inner loop iterates `j` from 0 to `hdr->imagesCount` where `hdr = &cache->hdr[i]` for each outer loop iteration `i`. If a subsequent header (i > 0) has a larger `imagesCount` than the first header, accessing `deps[j]` will be an out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `deps` array is allocated based on `cache->hdr->imagesCount`, which is `cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`.
The outer loop iterates over all headers: `for (i = 0; i < cache->n_hdr; i++)`.
For i > 0, `hdr = &cache->hdr[i]` may have a larger `imagesCount` than `cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`.
The inner loop iterates `j` from 0 to `hdr->imagesCount - 1`, and accesses `deps[j]` which is out of bounds when `j >= cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`.

vulnerabilities.description: The `dep_index` value derived from `depArray[k] & 0x7fff` (range 0-32767) is used to index both `deps` and `img` arrays without any bounds check against `hdr->imagesCount`. A crafted cache file can cause out-of-bounds memory access.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `depArray` is read from the cache file buffer, containing attacker-controlled ut16 values.
`dep_index` is computed as `depArray[k] & 0x7fff`, giving a range of 0 to 32767.
`deps[dep_index]++` is executed. `deps` was allocated with `cache->hdr->imagesCount` elements, which could be much smaller than 32767.
`img[dep_index]` is accessed in `get_lib_name`. `img` has `hdr->imagesCount` elements, also potentially much smaller than `dep_index`.

state.value: up to 32767, potentially out of bounds

vulnerabilities.description: The loop over `depArray` uses a sentinel value (0xffff) to terminate but never checks that `k` remains within the bounds of the allocated `depArray` (size `cache->accel->depListCount`). A malformed cache file missing the sentinel can cause an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: `depArray` is allocated with `cache->accel->depListCount` ut16 elements.
`extras[j].dependentsStartArrayIndex` provides the starting index `k`, read from the cache file.
The loop increments `k` until `depArray[k] == 0xffff`, but never checks `k < cache->accel->depListCount`. If the sentinel is missing or `k` starts beyond the array, this reads out of bounds.

commit_message:
Fixed int overflow in PresentationContext_new

Thanks to hac425 CVE-2020-11038
cve_desc: In FreeRDP less than or equal to 2.0.0, an Integer Overflow to Buffer Overflow exists. When using /video redirection, a manipulated server can instruct the client to allocate a buffer with a smaller size than requested due to an integer overflow in size calculation. With later messages, the server can manipulate the client to write data out of bound to the previously allocated buffer. This has been patched in 2.1.0.

vulnerabilities.description: The expression `width * height * 4` is computed using UINT32 arithmetic and can overflow, resulting in a much smaller allocation than expected. Subsequent operations that write decoded video frame data (width*height*4 bytes) into `surfaceData` would cause a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with attacker-controlled width and height values, e.g. width=65536, height=65536.
The expression `width * height * 4` is evaluated as UINT32 multiplication: 65536 * 65536 * 4. 65536 * 65536 = 0x100000000, which wraps to 0 in 32-bit arithmetic. 0 * 4 = 0.
BufferPool_Take allocates a buffer of size 0 (or a minimal allocation), far smaller than the actual 16 GB needed.

state.value: pointer to a 0-byte or minimal allocation

step_by_step_execution.description: Later, when decoded video data (expected to be width*height*4 = 16 GB worth of pixel data) is written into ret->surfaceData, a massive heap buffer overflow occurs.

state.value: heap buffer overflow when frame data is written

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0018: going over the end of the typahead

Problem:    Going over the end of the typahead.
Solution:   Put a NUL after the typeahead.
cve_desc: Integer Overflow or Wraparound in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.description: When buf is not NULL, tp points to buf + offset and len = *buflen - offset. The write tp[len] = NUL writes to buf[*buflen]. If *buflen equals bufsize (the allocated size of buf), this is a one-byte out-of-bounds write (off-by-one). The bufsize parameter is available but is never checked before this NUL termination.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a non-NULL buf, bufsize indicating the allocated size, and *buflen potentially equal to bufsize (buffer is full).

state.value: non-NULL pointer to a buffer of size bufsize

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop begins with offset = 0. Since buf != NULL and offset < *buflen, we enter the else branch.
The code writes a NUL terminator at tp[len], which is buf[bufsize]. This is one byte past the end of the allocated buffer.

commit_message: SERVER-59071 Treat '$sample' as unsharded when connecting directly to shards
cve_desc: An authorized user may trigger an invariant which may result in denial of service or server exit if a relevant aggregation request is sent to a shard. Usually, the requests are sent via mongos and special privileges are required in order to know the address of the shards and to log in to the shards of an auth enabled environment.

Signed-off-by: Jonathon Jongsma <jjongsma@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Ján Tomko <jtomko@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Ján Tomko <jtomko@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in libvirt in the virConnectListAllNodeDevices API in versions before 7.0.0. It only affects hosts with a PCI device and driver that supports mediated devices (e.g., GRID driver). This flaw could be used by an unprivileged client with a read-only connection to crash the libvirt daemon by executing the 'nodedev-list' virsh command. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

vulnerabilities.description: Operator precedence bug: `*mdev_types[i]` is parsed as `*(mdev_types[i])` (array subscript binds tighter than dereference), instead of the intended `(*mdev_types)[i]`. When `i > 0`, `mdev_types[i]` reads memory at offsets beyond the single `mdev_types` pointer parameter on the caller's stack/memory, leading to an out-of-bounds read and likely use of an invalid pointer passed to `virMediatedDeviceTypeFree`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with `mdev_types` pointing to a caller-owned `virMediatedDeviceTypePtr *` variable, and `*nmdev_types` is >= 2 (indicating a refresh with at least two existing entries).

state.value: pointer to a single virMediatedDeviceTypePtr* variable on the caller side

step_by_step_execution.description: The for loop begins. For i=0, the expression `*mdev_types[0]` is evaluated as `*(mdev_types[0])` which equals `*(*mdev_types)` — this is equivalent to `(*mdev_types)[0]`, so the first iteration works correctly by coincidence.

For i=1, the expression `*mdev_types[1]` is evaluated as `*(mdev_types[1])`. `mdev_types` is a pointer to a single pointer variable; `mdev_types[1]` reads memory one pointer-size past that variable, which is out-of-bounds.

state.value: out-of-bounds read past the caller's pointer variable

step_by_step_execution.description: The out-of-bounds value is dereferenced and passed to `virMediatedDeviceTypeFree`, which may corrupt memory or crash.

commit_message:
openvswitch: fix OOB access in reserve_sfa_size()

Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and
reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, if next_offset is
greater than MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE, the function reserve_sfa_size() does
not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, but it allocates MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE
bytes increasing actions_len by req_size. This can then lead to an OOB
write access, especially when further actions need to be copied.

Fix it by rearranging the flow action size check.

KASAN splat below:

==================================================================
BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in reserve_sfa_size+0x1ba/0x380 [openvswitch]
Write of size 65360 at addr ffff888147e4001c by task handler15/836

CPU: 1 PID: 836 Comm: handler15 Not tainted 5.18.0-rc1+ #27
...

 ? __mod_lruvec_page_state+0x2e3/0x470
 ? __kasan_kmalloc_large+0x90/0xe0

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: f28cd2af22a0 ("openvswitch: fix flow actions reallocation")
Signed-off-by: Paolo Valerio <pvalerio@redhat.com>
Acked-by: Eelco Chaudron <echaudro@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An integer coercion error was found in the openvswitch kernel module. Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, the reserve_sfa_size() function does not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, potentially leading to an out-of-bounds write access. This flaw allows a local user to crash or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer signedness/overflow issue: next_offset is declared as int, and it's computed from (*sfa)->actions_len. If actions_len is large enough, next_offset could overflow or be large. When compared in `req_size <= (ksize(*sfa) - next_offset)`, if next_offset exceeds ksize(*sfa), the subtraction wraps around to a very large value (since ksize returns size_t, an unsigned type), making the check pass incorrectly. This allows skipping the reallocation and writing beyond the allocated buffer at the `out` label.

step_by_step_execution.description: next_offset is computed as offsetof(struct sw_flow_actions, actions) + (*sfa)->actions_len

state.value: A large value such that next_offset > ksize(*sfa)
offsetof(...) + actions_len, exceeding the actual allocation size

step_by_step_execution.description: The size check `req_size <= (ksize(*sfa) - next_offset)` is evaluated. Since ksize returns size_t (unsigned) and next_offset is implicitly converted to size_t, if next_offset > ksize(*sfa) the subtraction wraps to a very large unsigned value.

state.value: Wraps to a very large size_t value due to unsigned underflow

step_by_step_execution.description: The comparison succeeds (req_size <= huge_value), so execution jumps to the `out` label, skipping any reallocation.
At the out label, actions_len is incremented and a pointer past the allocated buffer is returned, enabling an out-of-bounds write by the caller.

state.value: (*sfa) + next_offset, which is beyond the allocated buffer

commit_message:
Bug 702582, CVE 2020-15900 Memory Corruption in Ghostscript 9.52

Fix the 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size to give the correct
size.  Previous calculation would result in a size that was too large,
and could underflow to max uint32_t. Also fix 'rsearch' to return the
correct 'pre' string with empty string match.

A future change may 'undefine' this undocumented, non-standard operator
during initialization as we do with the many other non-standard internal
PostScript operators and procedures.
cve_desc: A memory corruption issue was found in Artifex Ghostscript 9.50 and 9.52. Use of a non-standard PostScript operator can allow overriding of file access controls. The 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size resulted in a size that was too large, and could underflow to max uint32_t. This was fixed in commit 5d499272b95a6b890a1397e11d20937de000d31b.

vulnerabilities.description: In the backward search case, the size calculated for the 'post' (suffix) string is incorrect. The formula `count + (size - 1)` does not correctly represent the number of bytes remaining after the match. For backward search, when a match is found, `count` equals the match position offset. The correct suffix length is `initial_count - count` (i.e., `r_size(op1) - size - count`), but the code computes `count + size - 1`. When `size > 1`, this yields a value larger than the actual remaining bytes, allowing subsequent reads past the end of the original string buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with forward=false (backward search). op1 is the source string (e.g., "abcdef", length 6), op is the pattern (e.g., "cd", size 2).
count is computed as r_size(op1) - size. ptr is set to op1->value.bytes + count (start of last possible match position).
Loop iteration 1: *ptr='e' != 'c', no match. ptr -= 1, count-- (post-decrement, old value 4 is truthy).
Loop iteration 2: *ptr='d' != 'c', no match. ptr -= 1, count-- (old value 3 is truthy).
Loop iteration 3: *ptr='c' == 'c' and memcmp(ptr, pat, 2) matches "cd". goto found.

In the 'found' block, after push(2) and setting up match/pre strings, the post string size is calculated as count + (size - 1) = 2 + 1 = 3. The actual suffix after the match is "ef" which has length 2. The post string is assigned 3 bytes of size starting at ptr+size, reading 1 byte beyond the original string.

state.value: 3 bytes from position 'e', so 'e', 'f', and 1 byte out-of-bounds

commit_message:
Bug 702582, CVE 2020-15900 Memory Corruption in Ghostscript 9.52

Fix the 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size to give the correct
size.  Previous calculation would result in a size that was too large,
and could underflow to max uint32_t. Also fix 'rsearch' to return the
correct 'pre' string with empty string match.

A future change may 'undefine' this undocumented, non-standard operator
during initialization as we do with the many other non-standard internal
PostScript operators and procedures.
cve_desc: A memory corruption issue was found in Artifex Ghostscript 9.50 and 9.52. Use of a non-standard PostScript operator can allow overriding of file access controls. The 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size resulted in a size that was too large, and could underflow to max uint32_t. This was fixed in commit 5d499272b95a6b890a1397e11d20937de000d31b.

commit_message:
Fixed int overflow in PresentationContext_new

Thanks to hac425 CVE-2020-11038
cve_desc: In FreeRDP less than or equal to 2.0.0, an Integer Overflow to Buffer Overflow exists. When using /video redirection, a manipulated server can instruct the client to allocate a buffer with a smaller size than requested due to an integer overflow in size calculation. With later messages, the server can manipulate the client to write data out of bound to the previously allocated buffer. This has been patched in 2.1.0.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of buffer size `s`. The expression `width * height` is evaluated using 32-bit unsigned arithmetic (both operands are UINT32), which can overflow/wrap around before the result is promoted to 64-bit for the multiplication by `4ULL`. For example, if width=65536 and height=65536, `width * height` wraps to 0 in 32-bit arithmetic, resulting in `s = 0`. This passes the `s > INT32_MAX` check, causing a zero-size (or undersized) buffer to be allocated via `BufferPool_Take`, while the h264/YUV decoders are initialized for the full 65536x65536 dimensions, leading to a heap buffer overflow when decoded frame data is written to `surfaceData`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with width=65536 and height=65536 (both valid UINT32 values).
The size `s` is computed. `width * height` is evaluated as UINT32 multiplication: 65536 * 65536 = 2^32, which wraps to 0 in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic. Then 0 * 4ULL = 0.
The check `s > INT32_MAX` evaluates to false (0 <= INT32_MAX), so execution continues.
h264_context_reset is called with the original full dimensions (65536x65536), setting up the decoder for a large frame.
BufferPool_Take allocates a buffer of size 0 (or a minimal allocation) for surfaceData.

yuv_context_reset is called with full 65536x65536 dimensions. When decoding later writes into ret->surfaceData, it writes up to 65536*65536*4 bytes into the 0-byte buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
i2c-ddc: fix oob read

Suggested-by: Michael Hanselmann <public@hansmi.ch>
Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>

cve_desc: QEMU, through version 2.10 and through version 3.1.0, is vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read of up to 128 bytes in the hw/i2c/i2c-ddc.c:i2c_ddc() function. A local attacker with permission to execute i2c commands could exploit this to read stack memory of the qemu process on the host.

vulnerabilities.description: If s->reg is not properly bounded, repeated calls to i2c_ddc_rx will increment s->reg beyond the size of s->edid_blob, leading to an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called, and s->reg holds the current index into s->edid_blob.

state.value: any value from 0 up to and beyond the size of edid_blob after repeated calls

step_by_step_execution.description: s->edid_blob is accessed at index s->reg without any bounds check.
s->reg is incremented unconditionally, so subsequent calls will access further out-of-bounds memory.

state.value: previous value + 1, no wrapping or clamping

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0240: crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file

Problem:    Crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file.
Solution:   Check for an empty word tree.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0240.

vulnerabilities.description: There is no bounds check on `depth` before incrementing it when descending into the trie. If the trie depth reaches MAXWLEN, the post-increment on `depth` causes out-of-bounds writes to `arridx[MAXWLEN]`, `curi[MAXWLEN]`, `wordcount[MAXWLEN]`, and potentially `tword[MAXWLEN]` on subsequent iterations. A maliciously crafted spell file with a word longer than MAXWLEN characters triggers stack buffer overflows.

step_by_step_execution.description: Arrays `arridx`, `curi`, `tword`, and `wordcount` are declared on the stack, each with size MAXWLEN. `depth` is initialized to 0.
The while loop iterates, traversing the trie from `slang->sl_fbyts` / `slang->sl_fidxs`. Each time a non-NUL byte `c` is encountered, the else branch is taken, incrementing depth.
When depth equals MAXWLEN-1, `tword[depth++] = c` writes to `tword[MAXWLEN-1]` (last valid index) and increments depth to MAXWLEN.
`arridx[depth] = idxs[n]` writes to `arridx[MAXWLEN]`, which is one element past the end of the stack-allocated array. Similarly, `curi[depth]` and `wordcount[depth]` are written out of bounds.

state.value: written with idxs[n] — stack buffer overflow

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0240: crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file

Problem:    Crash when using ":mkspell" with an empty .dic file.
Solution:   Check for an empty word tree.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0240.

vulnerabilities.description: When traversing the trie, the `depth` variable is incremented without any bounds check against MAXWLEN. If the slang trie contains a path deeper than MAXWLEN levels, `depth` will exceed the array bounds of `arridx`, `curi`, `tword`, and `wordcount` (all declared with size MAXWLEN), leading to stack buffer overflows.

step_by_step_execution.description: Arrays are declared on the stack with fixed size MAXWLEN.
depth is initialized to 0, and traversal of the trie begins.

In the else branch (normal character), depth is incremented: tword[depth++] = c. If the trie is crafted (e.g., from a malicious spell file) with a chain of non-NUL bytes longer than MAXWLEN, depth keeps incrementing.
When depth reaches MAXWLEN-1, the line tword[depth++] = c writes to tword[MAXWLEN-1] (valid) and increments depth to MAXWLEN.
Immediately after, arridx[depth], curi[depth], and wordcount[depth] are written with depth == MAXWLEN, which is one past the end of all these arrays, causing a stack-based out-of-bounds write.

commit_message:
Fix bug 697459 Buffer overflow in fill_threshold_buffer

There was an overflow check for ht_buffer size, but none for the larger
threshold_buffer. Note that this file didn't fail on Windows because the
combination of the ht_buffer and the size of the (miscalculated due to
overflow) threshold_buffer would have exceeded the 2Gb limit.
cve_desc: The fill_threshhold_buffer function in base/gxht_thresh.c in Artifex Software, Inc. Ghostscript 9.20 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted PostScript document.

commit_message:
- Fix Integer Overflows in Size Calculations,
  reported by X41 D-Sec.
cve_desc: Unbound before 1.9.5 allows an integer overflow in a size calculation in dnscrypt/dnscrypt.c. NOTE: The vendor disputes that this is a vulnerability. Although the code may be vulnerable, a running Unbound installation cannot be remotely or locally exploited

code_snippets:
rrlen = strlen(dnscenv->provider_name) +
                         strlen(ttl_class_type) +
                         4 * sizeof(struct SignedCert) + // worst case scenario
                         1 + // trailing double quote
                         1;
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in uint16_t rrlen calculation leads to a heap buffer overflow. The guard check compares against 0xffff0000, which is far too large for a uint16_t (max 65535). A provider_name of moderate length (e.g., ~65000 bytes) causes the sum to exceed 65535, wrapping rrlen to a small value. A tiny buffer is then allocated via malloc(rrlen), and the subsequent snprintf calls write far beyond its bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function iterates over signed_certs. For each non-rotated cert, it computes rrlen.
The integer overflow guard checks if strlen(provider_name) >= 0xffff0000. On a system with 32-bit unsigned, 0xffff0000 is ~4.29 billion. The length 65100 passes this check.
rrlen is computed as a uint16_t. Assuming sizeof(struct SignedCert) is 124: 65100 + 15 + 496 + 1 + 1 = 65613. This overflows uint16_t (max 65535), wrapping to 65613 - 65536 = 77.
malloc(rrlen) allocates a buffer of only 77 bytes.

snprintf writes provider_name (65100 chars) plus the TXT prefix into the 77-byte buffer. While snprintf itself is bounded by rrlen-1=76, the size argument 'rrlen - 1 - strlen(rr)' in subsequent calls will underflow (unsigned wrap) because strlen(rr) quickly reaches 76, and further calls compute a very large size_t value, leading to writes well beyond the allocated buffer.

state.value: 77 bytes allocated, but content logically needs ~65100+ bytes

commit_message:
io_uring: reexpand under-reexpanded iters

[   74.211232] BUG: KASAN: stack-out-of-bounds in iov_iter_revert+0x809/0x900
[   74.212778] Read of size 8 at addr ffff888025dc78b8 by task
syz-executor.0/828
[   74.214756] CPU: 0 PID: 828 Comm: syz-executor.0 Not tainted
5.14.0-rc3-next-20210730 #1
[   74.216525] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996),
BIOS rel-1.14.0-0-g155821a1990b-prebuilt.qemu.org 04/01/2014
[   74.219033] Call Trace:
[   74.219683]  dump_stack_lvl+0x8b/0xb3
[   74.220706]  print_address_description.constprop.0+0x1f/0x140
[   74.224226]  kasan_report.cold+0x7f/0x11b
[   74.226085]  iov_iter_revert+0x809/0x900
[   74.227960]  io_write+0x57d/0xe40
[   74.232647]  io_issue_sqe+0x4da/0x6a80
[   74.242578]  __io_queue_sqe+0x1ac/0xe60
[   74.245358]  io_submit_sqes+0x3f6e/0x76a0
[   74.248207]  __do_sys_io_uring_enter+0x90c/0x1a20
[   74.257167]  do_syscall_64+0x3b/0x90
[   74.257984]  entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x44/0xae

old_size = iov_iter_count();
...
iov_iter_revert(old_size - iov_iter_count());

If iov_iter_revert() is done base on the initial size as above, and the
iter is truncated and not reexpanded in the middle, it miscalculates
borders causing problems. This trace is due to no one reexpanding after
generic_write_checks().

Now iters store how many bytes has been truncated, so reexpand them to
the initial state right before reverting.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Reported-by: Palash Oswal <oswalpalash@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Sudip Mukherjee <sudipm.mukherjee@gmail.com>
Reported-and-tested-by: syzbot+9671693590ef5aad8953@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Signed-off-by: Pavel Begunkov <asml.silence@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read flaw was found in the Linux kernel’s io_uring module in the way a user triggers the io_read() function with some special parameters. This flaw allows a local user to read some memory out of bounds.

commit_message: ITS#9454 fix issuerAndThisUpdateCheck
cve_desc: In OpenLDAP through 2.4.57 and 2.5.x through 2.5.1alpha, an assertion failure in slapd can occur in the issuerAndThisUpdateCheck function via a crafted packet, resulting in a denial of service (daemon exit) via a short timestamp. This is related to schema_init.c and checkTime.

vulnerabilities.description: An unsigned integer underflow in x.bv_len can occur when the issuer scanning loop consumes all remaining characters via double-quote pairs (is->bv_len += 2) without finding a lone closing quote. When is->bv_len equals x.bv_len after consuming a trailing double-quote pair, the subsequent subtraction `x.bv_len -= is->bv_len + 1` underflows (since is->bv_len + 1 > x.bv_len), causing x.bv_len to wrap to a very large value. This leads to out-of-bounds reads in subsequent parsing loops.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input is provided, e.g. `{thisUpdate "20200101000000Z",issuer rdnSequence:"""}`; length check passes (> 39 chars), first/last char check passes ('{' and '}').
Strip '{}': x.bv_val points to content after '{', x.bv_len reduced by 2.
First iteration of do-while: parse thisUpdate field, extracting the generalized time value. have becomes HAVE_THISUPDATE. Comma is found and consumed.
Second iteration: match 'issuer', consume 'issuer' + space + 'rdnSequence:' + opening '"'. x now points to the two remaining characters '""' before the '}'.
Issuer scanning loop iteration: is->bv_len=0, is->bv_val[0]='"'. Check is->bv_val[1]='"' (second quote). It's a double-quote pair, so numdquotes++ and is->bv_len += 2.
Loop condition: is->bv_len (2) < x.bv_len (2) is false. Loop exits without finding a closing quote.
Subtraction x.bv_len -= is->bv_len + 1 = 2 - 3. Since bv_len is unsigned (ber_len_t), this underflows to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF on 32-bit or 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF on 64-bit).
Subsequent space-eating loop checks x.bv_val[0] and iterates with the enormous x.bv_len, causing out-of-bounds reads far past the original input buffer.

commit_message:
vhost: fix FD leak with inflight messages

Even if unlikely, a buggy vhost-user master might attach fds to inflight
messages. Add checks like for other types of vhost-user messages.

Fixes: d87f1a1cb7b6 ("vhost: support inflight info sharing")
Cc: stable@dpdk.org

Signed-off-by: David Marchand <david.marchand@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Maxime Coquelin <maxime.coquelin@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in dpdk. This flaw allows a malicious vhost-user master to attach an unexpected number of fds as ancillary data to VHOST_USER_GET_INFLIGHT_FD / VHOST_USER_SET_INFLIGHT_FD messages that are not closed by the vhost-user slave. By sending such messages continuously, the vhost-user master exhausts available fd in the vhost-user slave process, leading to a denial of service.

code_snippets:
for (i = 0; i < num_queues; i++) {
		vq = dev->virtqueue[i];
vulnerabilities.description: The loop iterates up to `num_queues` (attacker-controlled value from the message payload) and accesses `dev->virtqueue[i]` without checking that `num_queues` does not exceed `dev->nr_vring` (the actual number of allocated virtqueues). An attacker can send a message with a large `num_queues` value, causing an out-of-bounds read on the `dev->virtqueue` array.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function validates the message fd count and basic message size, which pass.
Fields are read from the attacker-controlled message payload, including `num_queues`.

state.value: actual number of allocated virtqueues, e.g. 2

step_by_step_execution.description: The mmap succeeds, returning a valid `addr`.
The loop begins iterating with `i` from 0 to `num_queues - 1`. When `i >= dev->nr_vring`, `dev->virtqueue[i]` is an out-of-bounds access on the virtqueue array.

state.value: ranges from 0 to num_queues-1 (e.g. up to 65534)

step_by_step_execution.description: The out-of-bounds pointer `vq` is dereferenced (if non-NULL) to write `desc_num`, or the read itself already accesses memory beyond the array, potentially leading to a crash or memory corruption.

commit_message: do not attempt to write a null image list (thanks to Vinay Rohila)
cve_desc: In ImageMagick, a crafted file could trigger an assertion failure when a call to WriteImages was made in MagickWand/operation.c, due to a NULL image list. This could potentially cause a denial of service. This was fixed in upstream ImageMagick version 7.1.0-30.

vulnerabilities.description: When the 'set' option handler is reached after percent escape interpretation already occurred at the top of the function, the previously allocated arg1 (and arg2) are overwritten without being freed, causing a memory leak. Furthermore, after the 'set' handler calls DestroyString on its own allocations (setting arg1/arg2 to NULL), the cleanup code at the bottom of the function tests `arg1 != arg1n` which is true (NULL != original non-NULL pointer), and then calls DestroyString(NULL), leading to a NULL pointer dereference or assertion failure.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with option="-set", arg1n="option:somekey" (non-NULL), arg2n="somevalue" (non-NULL). Percent escape interpretation flags are set.

Percent escape block executes: arg1 is assigned to result of InterpretImageProperties (a newly allocated string, call it stringA). arg2 is assigned to result of InterpretImageProperties (a newly allocated string, call it stringC).
The 'set' option handler is entered. It calls InterpretImageProperties again for arg1, overwriting arg1 with a new allocation (stringB). stringA is leaked (never freed).
Inside the 'option:' sub-handler, arg2 is set to NULL, leaking stringC. Then for IfNormalOp, arg2 is re-assigned to a new InterpretImageProperties result (stringD).
The handler calls arg1=DestroyString(arg1) freeing stringB, arg1 becomes NULL. Then arg2=DestroyString(arg2) freeing stringD, arg2 becomes NULL. Then 'break' exits the do-while loop.
Cleanup code runs: `if (arg1 != arg1n)` evaluates to (NULL != "option:somekey") which is true. DestroyString(NULL) is called, causing a NULL pointer dereference or assertion failure crash.

commit_message:
crypto: ccp - fix resource leaks in ccp_run_aes_gcm_cmd()

There are three bugs in this code:

1) If we ccp_init_data() fails for &src then we need to free aad.
   Use goto e_aad instead of goto e_ctx.
2) The label to free the &final_wa was named incorrectly as "e_tag" but
   it should have been "e_final_wa".  One error path leaked &final_wa.
3) The &tag was leaked on one error path.  In that case, I added a free
   before the goto because the resource was local to that block.

Fixes: 36cf515b9bbe ("crypto: ccp - Enable support for AES GCM on v5 CCPs")
Reported-by: "minihanshen(沈明航)" <minihanshen@tencent.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: John Allen <john.allen@amd.com>
Tested-by: John Allen <john.allen@amd.com>
Signed-off-by: Herbert Xu <herbert@gondor.apana.org.au>
cve_desc: A memory leak flaw was found in the Linux kernel in the ccp_run_aes_gcm_cmd() function in drivers/crypto/ccp/ccp-ops.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption). This vulnerability is similar with the older CVE-2019-18808.

vulnerabilities.description: In the decryption path, if aes->src_len is smaller than authsize, the subtraction underflows because ilen is an unsigned int. This results in a very large value (close to UINT_MAX) that is subsequently used for scatterwalk_ffwd, ccp_init_data, and length calculations, leading to out-of-bounds memory access or other undefined behavior.

code_snippets: ret = ccp_init_data(&src, cmd_q, p_inp, ilen, AES_BLOCK_SIZE, in_place ? DMA_BIDIRECTIONAL : DMA_TO_DEVICE);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function validates the key length and authsize but does not validate aes->src_len against authsize.

state.value: user-controlled, could be 0 or any small value less than authsize

step_by_step_execution.description: The action is CCP_AES_ACTION_DECRYPT (not encrypt), so the else branch is taken.
ilen is computed as aes->src_len - authsize. Since both are unsigned and src_len < authsize, this wraps around to a very large value.
scatterwalk_ffwd is called with the massive ilen value, attempting to advance past a huge offset in the input scatterlist, causing out-of-bounds access.
If execution continues, ilen > 0 is true (huge unsigned value), so ccp_init_data is called with this massive length, leading to further out-of-bounds DMA operations or memory corruption.

commit_message:
can: mcba_usb: mcba_usb_start_xmit(): fix double dev_kfree_skb in error path

There is no need to call dev_kfree_skb() when usb_submit_urb() fails
because can_put_echo_skb() deletes original skb and
can_free_echo_skb() deletes the cloned skb.

Fixes: 51f3baad7de9 ("can: mcba_usb: Add support for Microchip CAN BUS Analyzer")
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/all/20220311080208.45047-1-hbh25y@gmail.com
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Marc Kleine-Budde <mkl@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: mcba_usb_start_xmit in drivers/net/can/usb/mcba_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.17.1 has a double free.

commit_message: Improve handling of Gopher responses (#1022)
cve_desc: In Squid 3.x through 3.5.28, 4.x through 4.17, and 5.x before 5.6, due to improper buffer management, a Denial of Service can occur when processing long Gopher server responses.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one error when null-terminating the `line` buffer. The code writes the null terminator at `line[llen + 1]` instead of `line[llen]`. When `llen` reaches `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1` (the maximum value allowed by the bounds check), `line[llen + 1]` writes at index `TEMP_BUF_SIZE`, which is one byte past the end of the stack-allocated `line` buffer. Additionally, `line[llen]` is left uninitialized/stale, meaning the string includes a garbage byte before the null terminator.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `line` buffer is allocated on the stack with size TEMP_BUF_SIZE via LOCAL_ARRAY.
Enter the while loop. Assume `gopherState->len` is 0 (no leftover from previous call). A complete line (ending with '\n') is found in inbuf with length close to TEMP_BUF_SIZE.

state.value: length of complete line from pos to lpos

step_by_step_execution.description: The bounds check `if (gopherState->len + llen >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE)` triggers, truncating llen to `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 0 - 1 = TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1`.
Since `lpos` is not NULL (complete line found), we proceed past the incomplete-line branch. Since `gopherState->len == 0`, we enter the else branch and copy llen bytes into line.
The code executes `line[llen + 1] = '\0'`, which writes at index `(TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1) + 1 = TEMP_BUF_SIZE`. This is one byte past the end of the buffer, causing a stack-based out-of-bounds write.

state.value: written with '\0' — 1 byte past buffer end
