The vulnerability is triggered by specially-crafted TPM2 command packets that then trigger the issue when the state of the TPM2's volatile state is written. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability. This issue affects libtpms versions before 0.8.5, before 0.7.9 and before 0.6.6.

commit_message:
Sanitize inputs to bookmark-cmd (#591)

Newsbeuter didn't properly shell-escape the arguments passed to
bookmarking command, which allows a remote attacker to perform remote
code execution by crafting an RSS item whose title and/or URL contain
something interpretable by the shell (most notably subshell
invocations.)

This has been reported by Jeriko One <jeriko.one@gmx.us>, complete with
PoC and a patch.

This vulnerability was assigned CVE-2017-12904.
cve_desc: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command in bookmarking function of Newsbeuter versions 0.7 through 2.9 allows remote attackers to perform user-assisted code execution by crafting an RSS item that includes shell code in its title and/or URL.

commit_message:
nfc: nfcmrvl: main: reorder destructive operations in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev to avoid bugs

There are destructive operations such as nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort and
gpio_free in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev. The resources such as firmware,
gpio and so on could be destructed while the upper layer functions such as
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start and nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame is executing, which leads
to double-free, use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs.

There are three situations that could lead to double-free bugs.

The first situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 release_firmware()           |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  kfree(fw) //(1)             |    fw_dnld_over
                              |     release_firmware
  ...                         |      kfree(fw) //(2)
                              |     ...

The second situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |
 mod_timer                    |
 (wait a time)                |
 fw_dnld_timeout              |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
   fw_dnld_over               |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
    release_firmware          |    fw_dnld_over
     kfree(fw) //(1)          |     release_firmware
     ...                      |      kfree(fw) //(2)

The third situation is shown below:

       (Thread 1)               |       (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame          |
 if(..->fw_download_in_progress)|
  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_recv_frame    |
   queue_work                   |
                                |
fw_dnld_rx_work                 | nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 fw_dnld_over                   |  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  release_firmware              |   fw_dnld_over
   kfree(fw) //(1)              |    release_firmware
                                |     kfree(fw) //(2)

The firmware struct is deallocated in position (1) and deallocated
in position (2) again.

The crash trace triggered by POC is like below:

BUG: KASAN: double-free or invalid-free in fw_dnld_over
Call Trace:
  kfree
  fw_dnld_over
  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
  nci_uart_tty_close
  tty_ldisc_kill
  tty_ldisc_hangup
  __tty_hangup.part.0
  tty_release
  ...

What's more, there are also use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs
in nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start. If we deallocate firmware struct, gpio or
set null to the members of priv->fw_dnld in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev,
then, we dereference firmware, gpio or the members of priv->fw_dnld in
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start, the UAF or NPD bugs will happen.

This patch reorders destructive operations after nci_unregister_device
in order to synchronize between cleanup routine and firmware download
routine.

The nci_unregister_device is well synchronized. If the device is
detaching, the firmware download routine will goto error. If firmware
download routine is executing, nci_unregister_device will wait until
firmware download routine is finished.

Fixes: 3194c6870158 ("NFC: nfcmrvl: add firmware download support")
Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A flaw in Linux Kernel found in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev() in drivers/nfc/nfcmrvl/main.c can lead to use after free both read or write when non synchronized between cleanup routine and firmware download routine.

vulnerabilities.description: nci_unregister_device(ndev) frees the nfc_dev associated with the nci_dev. After that call, the code dereferences priv->ndev->nfc_dev->fw_download_in_progress, which accesses already-freed memory (use-after-free).

step_by_step_execution.description: nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev is called with a valid priv pointer.
ndev is assigned from priv->ndev.

state.value: valid pointer to nci_dev (same as priv->ndev)

step_by_step_execution.description: nci_unregister_device(ndev) is called, which internally calls nfc_unregister_device and nfc_free_device, freeing ndev->nfc_dev.
The code dereferences priv->ndev->nfc_dev->fw_download_in_progress, accessing memory that was freed in the previous step.

commit_message: squash heap-buffer-overflow, PoC TIFF from Hardik
cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow issue was found in ImageMagick. When an application processes a malformed TIFF file, it could lead to undefined behavior or a crash causing a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the calculation of 'extent' for strip pixel buffer allocation. The multiplication `4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*TIFFStripSize(tiff)` can wrap around on 32-bit or even 64-bit systems when TIFFStripSize returns a large value. If `extent` wraps to a small value, a small buffer is allocated, but `TIFFReadEncodedStrip` later reads up to `TIFFStripSize(tiff)` bytes into it, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted TIFF file is opened and parsed. Method is determined to be ReadStripMethod because rows_per_strip is set.
samples_per_pixel is read from the TIFF header. It can be up to 32 (MaxPixelChannels).
TIFFStripSize(tiff) returns a large value derived from crafted TIFF header fields (width, rows_per_strip, bits_per_sample, samples_per_pixel).

state.value: large value, e.g. close to SIZE_MAX/132 or larger

step_by_step_execution.description: extent is computed as 4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*TIFFStripSize(tiff). With samples_per_pixel=32, the multiplier is 132. This multiplication overflows size_t, wrapping to a small value.

state.value: small value due to integer overflow wrapping

step_by_step_execution.description: AcquireQuantumMemory allocates a small buffer based on the overflowed extent value.

state.value: points to a small heap buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: TIFFReadEncodedStrip reads up to TIFFStripSize(tiff) bytes (the original large value) into the small strip_pixels buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: up to the original large TIFFStripSize value written into small buffer

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the calculation of 'extent' for tile pixel buffer allocation. The multiplications `rows*TIFFTileRowSize(tiff)` and the outer `4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*...` can overflow, resulting in a small allocation. TIFFReadTile then writes more data than the buffer can hold, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted tiled TIFF file is opened. TIFFIsTiled returns true, so method is set to ReadTileMethod.
Tile dimensions (columns, rows) and samples_per_pixel are read from TIFF header.

extent is computed: 4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*MagickMax(rows*TIFFTileRowSize(tiff), TIFFTileSize(tiff)). The inner multiplication rows*TIFFTileRowSize can overflow, and/or the outer multiplication can overflow size_t.

state.value: small value due to integer overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: AcquireQuantumMemory allocates a small buffer based on the overflowed extent.
TIFFReadTile writes actual tile data (potentially much larger than allocated buffer) into tile_pixels, causing heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4436: crash with weird 'vartabstop' value

Problem:    Crash with weird 'vartabstop' value.
Solution:   Check for running into the end of the line.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4436.

vulnerabilities.description: In the VREPLACE mode handling at the end of the function, `new_line` is allocated via `vim_strsave(ml_get_curline())`, which has a size of `strlen(curline) + 1`. The code then writes `NUL` at index `curwin->w_cursor.col` without checking that this index is within the bounds of `new_line`. If `curwin->w_cursor.col` exceeds the length of the current line (which can happen when the cursor position is computed to be beyond the actual string content, e.g. via the `new_cursor_col += curwin->w_cursor.col` addition or the space-insertion logic failing due to allocation failure), this results in a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is entered with State having VREPLACE_FLAG set. `orig_line` is saved and `orig_col` records the cursor column.

`new_cursor_col` is computed as `curwin->w_cursor.col`, then `beginline(BL_WHITE)` moves the cursor to the first non-blank. `new_cursor_col -= curwin->w_cursor.col` gives offset from first non-blank. If cursor was after the first non-blank, `new_cursor_col >= 0`.
Indent is changed (increased) via `set_indent` or `shift_line`. Cursor is placed on the new first non-blank column.

state.value: new first non-blank column (increased due to added indent)

step_by_step_execution.description: Since `new_cursor_col >= 0`, the code computes `new_cursor_col += curwin->w_cursor.col`. If the indent was increased significantly and the original cursor offset was large, `new_cursor_col` can exceed the actual length of the current line content.

state.value: curwin->w_cursor.col (new first non-blank) + original offset, potentially > strlen(ml_get_curline())

step_by_step_execution.description: `curwin->w_cursor.col` is set to `(colnr_T)new_cursor_col` since `new_cursor_col > 0`.

state.value: potentially beyond end of current line string

step_by_step_execution.description: In the VREPLACE block, `new_line = vim_strsave(ml_get_curline())` allocates a buffer of `strlen(curline) + 1` bytes.
`new_line[curwin->w_cursor.col] = NUL` writes a NUL byte past the end of the allocated heap buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
ax25: Fix UAF bugs in ax25 timers

There are race conditions that may lead to UAF bugs in
ax25_heartbeat_expiry(), ax25_t1timer_expiry(), ax25_t2timer_expiry(),
ax25_t3timer_expiry() and ax25_idletimer_expiry(), when we call
ax25_release() to deallocate ax25_dev.

One of the UAF bugs caused by ax25_release() is shown below:

      (Thread 1)                    |      (Thread 2)
ax25_dev_device_up() //(1)          |
...                                 | ax25_kill_by_device()
ax25_bind()          //(2)          |
ax25_connect()                      | ...
 ax25_std_establish_data_link()     |
  ax25_start_t1timer()              | ax25_dev_device_down() //(3)
   mod_timer(&ax25->t1timer,..)     |
                                    | ax25_release()
   (wait a time)                    |  ...
                                    |  ax25_dev_put(ax25_dev) //(4)FREE
   ax25_t1timer_expiry()            |
    ax25->ax25_dev->values[..] //USE|  ...
     ...                            |

We increase the refcount of ax25_dev in position (1) and (2), and
decrease the refcount of ax25_dev in position (3) and (4).
The ax25_dev will be freed in position (4) and be used in
ax25_t1timer_expiry().

The fail log is shown below:
==============================================================

[  106.116942] BUG: KASAN: use-after-free in ax25_t1timer_expiry+0x1c/0x60
[  106.116942] Read of size 8 at addr ffff88800bda9028 by task swapper/0/0
[  106.116942] CPU: 0 PID: 0 Comm: swapper/0 Not tainted 5.17.0-06123-g0905eec574
[  106.116942] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996), BIOS rel-14
[  106.116942] Call Trace:
...
[  106.116942]  ax25_t1timer_expiry+0x1c/0x60
[  106.116942]  call_timer_fn+0x122/0x3d0
[  106.116942]  __run_timers.part.0+0x3f6/0x520
[  106.116942]  run_timer_softirq+0x4f/0xb0
[  106.116942]  __do_softirq+0x1c2/0x651
...

This patch adds del_timer_sync() in ax25_release(), which could ensure
that all timers stop before we deallocate ax25_dev.

Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: Paolo Abeni <pabeni@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the Linux kernel’s Amateur Radio AX.25 protocol functionality in the way a user connects with the protocol. This flaw allows a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.description: In the AX25_STATE_3 / AX25_STATE_4 case, `ax25->ax25_dev->values[AX25_VALUES_PROTOCOL]` is dereferenced without a NULL check. Earlier in the function, `ax25_dev` is assigned from `ax25->ax25_dev`, and it is only checked for NULL much later (`if (ax25_dev)`). If `ax25->ax25_dev` is NULL when the socket is in STATE_3 or STATE_4, this leads to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins; `sk` is obtained from `sock->sk` and is non-NULL, so execution continues.
`ax25` is obtained via `sk_to_ax25(sk)`, and `ax25_dev` is set to `ax25->ax25_dev`.
The socket type is SOCK_SEQPACKET, and `ax25->state` is AX25_STATE_3 or AX25_STATE_4, so execution enters that case branch.
The code dereferences `ax25->ax25_dev->values[AX25_VALUES_PROTOCOL]` without checking whether `ax25->ax25_dev` is NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

There are race conditions that may lead to UAF bugs in
ax25_heartbeat_expiry(), ax25_t1timer_expiry(), ax25_t2timer_expiry(),
ax25_t3timer_expiry() and ax25_idletimer_expiry(), when we call

One of the UAF bugs caused by ax25_release() is shown below:

We increase the refcount of ax25_dev in position (1) and (2), and

cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the Linux kernel’s Amateur Radio AX.25 protocol functionality in the way a user connects with the protocol. This flaw allows a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.description: NULL pointer dereference when ax25->ax25_dev is NULL. The variable ax25_dev is assigned from ax25->ax25_dev at the top of the function but is only checked for NULL later (after the switch statement). In states AX25_STATE_3 and AX25_STATE_4, the code dereferences ax25->ax25_dev->values without first verifying it is non-NULL.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins and retrieves the sock pointer and checks it for NULL.
ax25 and ax25_dev are retrieved from the socket.

state.value: ax25->ax25_dev, which can be NULL if no device is associated

In case AX25_STATE_3/AX25_STATE_4, ax25_clear_queues and n2count reset happen, then the code dereferences ax25->ax25_dev->values[AX25_VALUES_PROTOCOL] without a NULL check.

commit_message:
i2c: ismt: prevent memory corruption in ismt_access()

The "data->block[0]" variable comes from the user and is a number
between 0-255.  It needs to be capped to prevent writing beyond the end
of dma_buffer[].

Fixes: 5e9a97b1f449 ("i2c: ismt: Adding support for I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL")
Reported-and-tested-by: Zheyu Ma <zheyuma97@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Mika Westerberg <mika.westerberg@linux.intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel Intel’s iSMT SMBus host controller driver in the way it handled the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case (via the ioctl I2C_SMBUS) with malicious input data. This flaw could allow a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write case, `dma_size` is a `u8` computed as `data->block[0] + 1`. If `data->block[0]` is 255, then `data->block[0] + 1` overflows the u8 to 0. Subsequently, `memcpy(&dma_buffer[1], &data->block[1], dma_size - 1)` is called where `dma_size` is 0. When promoted to int for the subtraction, `0 - 1 = -1`, which is then implicitly cast to `size_t` (a very large value), causing a massive buffer over-read/over-write.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `size = I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA` and `read_write = I2C_SMBUS_WRITE`, and `data->block[0]` is 255.
Execution enters the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write branch. `dma_size` is assigned `data->block[0] + 1`. Since `dma_size` is `u8`, the value 256 overflows to 0.
`desc->wr_len_cmd` is set to `dma_size` which is 0.

`memcpy(&dma_buffer[1], &data->block[1], dma_size - 1)` is called. `dma_size` (u8, value 0) is promoted to int for the subtraction, yielding -1. This is implicitly converted to `size_t`, resulting in a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF on 64-bit).
memcpy copies an enormous amount of data from `data->block[1]` into `dma_buffer[1]`, causing a massive heap/stack buffer overflow, corrupting memory well beyond `priv->buffer`.

vulnerabilities.description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case, `data->block[0]` is used directly as the size argument to memcpy without any bounds validation. If `data->block[0]` exceeds the size of the DMA buffer (priv->buffer), this causes an out-of-bounds write into `dma_buffer`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `size = I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL`. `data->block[0]` contains a value larger than the DMA buffer can hold (e.g., 255).
Execution enters the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL branch. No validation is performed on `data->block[0]`.

`memcpy(&dma_buffer[1], &data->block[1], data->block[0])` copies 255 bytes starting at `dma_buffer[1]`, writing 256 bytes total into a buffer that is likely much smaller (priv->buffer is typically sized for SMBus max of 32 data bytes plus overhead).

commit_message:
Sanitize inputs to bookmark-cmd (#591)

Newsbeuter didn't properly shell-escape the arguments passed to
bookmarking command, which allows a remote attacker to perform remote
code execution by crafting an RSS item whose title and/or URL contain
something interpretable by the shell (most notably subshell
invocations.)

This has been reported by Jeriko One <jeriko.one@gmx.us>, complete with
PoC and a patch.

This vulnerability was assigned CVE-2017-12904.
cve_desc: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command in bookmarking function of Newsbeuter versions 0.7 through 2.9 allows remote attackers to perform user-assisted code execution by crafting an RSS item that includes shell code in its title and/or URL.

code_snippets:
std::string cmdline = strprintf::fmt("%s '%s' %s %s %s",
		                                       bookmark_cmd,
		                                       utils::replace_all(url,"'", "%27"),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(title)),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(description)),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(feed_title)));
vulnerabilities.description: The title, description, and feed_title parameters are inserted into a shell command line without proper shell escaping. While the URL is wrapped in single quotes (with internal single quotes replaced), the other three parameters are only passed through stfl::quote (an STFL UI escaping function, not a shell escaping function) and quote_empty. An attacker who controls an RSS feed's title, item title, or item description can inject arbitrary shell commands (e.g., using $(malicious_command) or backticks).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with attacker-controlled feed data, e.g., title = "$(touch /tmp/pwned)"
bookmark_cmd is read from config and is non-empty, so the command construction proceeds.

The command line is constructed. The URL is single-quoted and sanitized. However, title is passed through stfl::quote (which escapes for STFL display, not for shell) and quote_empty, but is NOT wrapped in shell-safe single quotes in the format string (%s not '%s').
The constructed command line is executed via /bin/sh -c, causing the shell to interpret $(touch /tmp/pwned) as a command substitution and execute 'touch /tmp/pwned'.

commit_message:
gsocketclient: Fix criticals

This ensures the parent GTask is kept alive as long as an enumeration
is running and trying to connect.

Closes #1646
Closes #1649
cve_desc: gio/gsocketclient.c in GNOME GLib 2.59.2 does not ensure that a parent GTask remains alive during the execution of a connection-attempting enumeration, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (g_socket_client_connected_callback mishandling and application crash) via a crafted web site, as demonstrated by GNOME Web (aka Epiphany).

vulnerabilities.description: When data is NULL (which the comment explicitly states is possible: "data is NULL once the task is completed"), and the connection succeeds (g_socket_connection_connect_finish returns TRUE), the code falls through to dereference data without a NULL check, causing a NULL pointer dereference. The failure path correctly checks `if (data)` before using it, but the success path does not.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called as a callback with attempt->data potentially being NULL, as indicated by the comment 'data is NULL once the task is completed'.
The first cancellation check is skipped because data is NULL, so the condition `data && g_task_return_error_if_cancelled(data->task)` short-circuits to false.
The timeout source cleanup proceeds (operates on attempt, not data, so no issue).
g_socket_connection_connect_finish succeeds (returns TRUE), so the error-handling block is skipped entirely. Note that the error-handling block correctly checks `if (data)` before dereferencing it.
Execution reaches `data->socket = g_steal_pointer(&attempt->socket)` which dereferences the NULL data pointer, causing a crash.

commit_message:
rgw: reject control characters in response-header actions

S3 GetObject permits overriding response header values, but those inputs
need to be validated to insure only characters that are valid in an HTTP
header value are present.

Credit: Initial vulnerability discovery by William Bowling (@wcbowling)
Credit: Further vulnerability discovery by Robin H. Johnson <rjohnson@digitalocean.com>
Signed-off-by: Robin H. Johnson <rjohnson@digitalocean.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the Ceph Object Gateway, where it supports request sent by an anonymous user in Amazon S3. This flaw could lead to potential XSS attacks due to the lack of proper neutralization of untrusted input.

commit_message:
Fix: Worked around buffer overflows in URL auth's cURL interface

This is only a workaround that keeps compatibility with 2.4.x mainline.
A real fix has been implemented in 2.5.x (master).
cve_desc: A buffer overflow was discovered in the URL-authentication backend of the Icecast before 2.4.4. If the backend is enabled, then any malicious HTTP client can send a request for that specific resource including a crafted header, leading to denial of service and potentially remote code execution.

commit_message: Avoid maxrlvls more than upper bound to cause heap-buffer-overflow
cve_desc: There's a flaw in jasper's jpc encoder in versions prior to 2.0.23. Crafted input provided to jasper by an attacker could cause an arbitrary out-of-bounds write. This could potentially affect data confidentiality, integrity, or application availability.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in hsteplcm/vsteplcm computation. These uint_fast32_t values are multiplied in a loop for each image component without overflow checks. The overflowed values are later used to multiply cp->imgareatlx and cp->imgareatly, which feed into reference grid and tile calculations, potentially causing incorrect geometry values, bypassing subsequent validation checks, and leading to incorrect numtiles computation.

step_by_step_execution.description: hsteplcm and vsteplcm are initialized to 1.

Loop iterates over image components, multiplying hsteplcm by each component's horizontal step. For an image with multiple components having large step values (e.g., 3 components each with hstep=2000), the multiplication overflows uint_fast32_t.
User provides imgareatlx via OPT_IMGAREAOFFX option, e.g. atoi returns some value like 10.
cp->imgareatlx is multiplied by the overflowed hsteplcm. Since hsteplcm overflowed to a small or unexpected value, imgareatlx becomes an incorrect value.
cp->refgrdwidth is computed as cp->imgareatlx + image_width, which can itself overflow or be inconsistent.

state.value: incorrect value due to overflowed imgareatlx

step_by_step_execution.description: Tile dimensions and tile grid calculations proceed with corrupted geometry values. cp->numhtiles and cp->numvtiles are computed via JPC_CEILDIV with potentially incorrect values, and cp->numtiles = cp->numhtiles * cp->numvtiles may itself overflow, leading to an undersized numtiles value used for downstream memory allocations.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in numtiles calculation. When an attacker specifies very small tile dimensions (tilewidth=1, tileheight=1) with a large image, numhtiles and numvtiles can be very large, and their product overflows uint_fast32_t. This incorrect numtiles value is used for tile-related memory allocations downstream, leading to heap buffer overflows.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides tilewidth=1 and tileheight=1 via options.
Default imgareatlx and imgareatly are 0 (UINT_FAST32_MAX sentinel kept). refgrdwidth and refgrdheight are set to image dimensions, e.g. 70000 x 70000.
numhtiles = CEILDIV(70000 - 0, 1) = 70000, numvtiles = CEILDIV(70000 - 0, 1) = 70000
numtiles = 70000 * 70000 = 4,900,000,000 which overflows a 32-bit uint_fast32_t (max ~4.29 billion), wrapping to a small value.

state.value: overflowed value (~605,032,704 or similar wrapped value on 32-bit platforms)

S3 GetObject permits overriding response header values, but those inputs
need to be validated to insure only characters that are valid in an HTTP

Credit: Further vulnerability discovery by Robin H. Johnson <rjohnson@digitalocean.com>
Signed-off-by: Robin H. Johnson <rjohnson@digitalocean.com>

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1337
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-13 Q16, there is an infinite loop in the ReadBMPImage function of the coders/bmp.c file. Remote attackers could leverage this vulnerability to cause a denial of service via a crafted bmp file.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in bytes_per_line calculation. On 32-bit platforms, `image->columns * bmp_info.bits_per_pixel` can overflow `size_t`, producing a truncated (small) `bytes_per_line`. This causes a small `length`, which passes the `length/8 > GetBlobSize` sanity check. The subsequent pixel buffer allocation may be undersized relative to the actual data layout. Later pixel-processing loops (e.g., case 32) recalculate `bytes_per_line = 4*(image->columns)` independently, producing a correct (large) stride. Pointer arithmetic like `p=pixels+(image->rows-y-1)*bytes_per_line` then accesses memory far beyond the allocated buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted BMP file is read with large width (e.g. 0x10000000) and bits_per_pixel=32 on a 32-bit platform.
bytes_per_line is calculated: image->columns * bmp_info.bits_per_pixel = 0x10000000 * 32 = 0x200000000, which overflows 32-bit size_t to 0. Then 4*((0+31)/32) = 4.

length is calculated as bytes_per_line * image->rows = 4 * 2 = 8. The check `(length/8) > GetBlobSize(image)` evaluates to `1 > GetBlobSize(image)` which is false for any valid file, so the check passes.

Buffer is allocated via AcquireVirtualMemory(image->rows, MagickMax(bytes_per_line, image->columns+256UL)). MagickMax(4, 0x10000100) = 0x10000100. Total allocation = 2 * 0x10000100 = 0x20000200 bytes.
ReadBlob reads `length` (8) bytes into the pixels buffer. count == 8 == length, so the check passes despite the buffer being largely uninitialized.

In the case 32 pixel processing, bytes_per_line is recalculated as 4*(image->columns) = 4*0x10000000 = 0x40000000. For y=0 (last row): p = pixels + (2-0-1)*0x40000000 = pixels + 0x40000000. This is far beyond the allocated buffer of 0x20000200 bytes, causing an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
KVM: PPC: Book3S: Fix H_RTAS rets buffer overflow

The kvmppc_rtas_hcall() sets the host rtas_args.rets pointer based on
the rtas_args.nargs that was provided by the guest. That guest nargs
value is not range checked, so the guest can cause the host rets pointer
to be pointed outside the args array. The individual rtas function
handlers check the nargs and nrets values to ensure they are correct,
but if they are not, the handlers store a -3 (0xfffffffd) failure
indication in rets[0] which corrupts host memory.

Fix this by testing up front whether the guest supplied nargs and nret
would exceed the array size, and fail the hcall directly without storing
a failure indication to rets[0].

Also expand on a comment about why we kill the guest and try not to
return errors directly if we have a valid rets[0] pointer.

Fixes: 8e591cb72047 ("KVM: PPC: Book3S: Add infrastructure to implement kernel-side RTAS calls")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v3.10+
Reported-by: Alexey Kardashevskiy <aik@ozlabs.ru>
Signed-off-by: Nicholas Piggin <npiggin@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
cve_desc: arch/powerpc/kvm/book3s_rtas.c in the Linux kernel through 5.13.5 on the powerpc platform allows KVM guest OS users to cause host OS memory corruption via rtas_args.nargs, aka CID-f62f3c20647e.

commit_message: Fix bug #79037 (global buffer-overflow in `mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar`)
cve_desc: When using certain mbstring functions to convert multibyte encodings, in PHP versions 7.2.x below 7.2.27, 7.3.x below 7.3.14 and 7.4.x below 7.4.2 it is possible to supply data that will cause function mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar to read past the allocated buffer. This may lead to information disclosure or crash.

vulnerabilities.description: If the loop completes without finding a matching range (i.e., no `break` is executed), `k` will be equal to the table size (`sizeof(cp950_pua_tbl)/(sizeof(unsigned short)*4)`). The code then accesses `cp950_pua_tbl[k]` without checking whether `k` is still within bounds, resulting in an out-of-bounds read on the `cp950_pua_tbl` array.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters with filter->status == 0, filter->from->no_encoding == mbfl_no_encoding_cp950, and c is a valid lead byte (e.g., 0xFA). c1 is set to 0x80, and since c > 0x80 && c < 0xFF, filter->status is set to 1, filter->cache = c.
Function is called again with the second byte. filter->status == 1, so we enter case 1. c1 = filter->cache = 0xFA. Suppose c = 0x40 (satisfies c > 0x39 && c < 0x7f).

w is computed from big5_ucs_table. Assume w <= 0 (no valid mapping found). Since filter is cp950 encoding and c1=0xFA falls in range 0xFA-0xFE, and c=0x40 satisfies (c > 0x39 && c < 0x7f), we enter the CP950 PUA block.

The for-loop iterates through all entries of cp950_pua_tbl looking for c2 in range [cp950_pua_tbl[k][2], cp950_pua_tbl[k][3]]. If c2=0xFA40 does not match any entry, the loop completes without breaking, and k equals the table size.

state.value: sizeof(cp950_pua_tbl)/(sizeof(unsigned short)*4) — one past last valid index

step_by_step_execution.description: The code accesses cp950_pua_tbl[k][2] with k out of bounds, reading memory beyond the array. This value is used in subsequent calculations and assigned to w.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4151: reading beyond the end of a line

Problem:    Reading beyond the end of a line.
Solution:   For block insert only use the offset for correcting the length.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in vim/vim prior to 8.2.

commit_message: Fix bug #79037 (global buffer-overflow in `mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar`)
cve_desc: When using certain mbstring functions to convert multibyte encodings, in PHP versions 7.2.x below 7.2.27, 7.3.x below 7.3.14 and 7.4.x below 7.4.2 it is possible to supply data that will cause function mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar to read past the allocated buffer. This may lead to information disclosure or crash.

vulnerabilities.description: If `is_in_cp950_pua(c1, c)` returns true but none of the entries in `cp950_pua_tbl` match the computed `c2` value, the for-loop completes without breaking, and `k` equals the table size. The subsequent access to `cp950_pua_tbl[k][2]` and other indices is an out-of-bounds read on the table.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with filter->from->no_encoding == mbfl_no_encoding_cp950 and filter->status == 1 (processing the second byte of a DBCS character).
filter->status is set to 0, and c1 is loaded from filter->cache (the lead byte stored during status 0 processing).
The condition `(c > 0x39 && c < 0x7f) | (c > 0xa0 && c < 0xff)` is satisfied (e.g. c=0x40), so we enter the DBCS decoding block.
w is calculated as (c1-0xa1)*157 + (c-0x40). For the chosen values, w is looked up in big5_ucs_table and yields 0 or a value <= 0 (unmapped character).
Since w <= 0, is_in_cp950_pua(c1, c) is checked and returns true (the values fall within a PUA range check).

The for-loop iterates over all entries of cp950_pua_tbl. If the is_in_cp950_pua check is broader than the table ranges, or if there is an inconsistency, no entry satisfies `c2 >= cp950_pua_tbl[k][2] && c2 <= cp950_pua_tbl[k][3]`, and the loop finishes without break.

state.value: sizeof(cp950_pua_tbl)/(sizeof(unsigned short)*4) — one past the last valid index

step_by_step_execution.description: cp950_pua_tbl[k][2] is read out-of-bounds, causing undefined behavior / out-of-bounds memory read. Further accesses to cp950_pua_tbl[k][0] and cp950_pua_tbl[k][3] also occur.

commit_message: imptcp: fix Segmentation Fault when octet count is to high
cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in rsyslog in the imptcp module. An attacker could send a specially crafted message to the imptcp socket, which would cause rsyslog to crash. Versions before 8.27.0 are vulnerable.

vulnerabilities.description: In the eInOctetCnt state, when digit characters are received, each digit is written into pThis->pMsg at offset pThis->iMsg and iMsg is incremented. There is no bounds check on iMsg against iMaxLine or the allocated size of pMsg. An attacker can send a very long sequence of digit characters (without a space delimiter) to overflow the pMsg buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with pThis->inputState == eAtStrtFram, pThis->bSuppOctetFram is true, and the character c is a digit (e.g., '1').
Because bSuppOctetFram is true and c is a digit, state transitions to eInOctetCnt, iOctetsRemain is set to 0, and framing is set to TCP_FRAMING_OCTET_COUNTING.

Execution enters the eInOctetCnt block. c is a digit and iOctetsRemain (0) <= 200000000, so iOctetsRemain is updated and the digit is written to pMsg[iMsg] without any bounds check. iMsg is incremented.

The function is called repeatedly with more digit characters. Each time, iOctetsRemain <= 200000000 passes (the value grows but stays under the limit for many digits), and each digit is written to pMsg[iMsg++] with no bounds check.

state.value: growing with each digit character received

step_by_step_execution.description: After iOctetsRemain exceeds 200000000, framing changes to OCTET_STUFFING but iMsg is already well past the pMsg buffer size. Even before that threshold, if the attacker sends enough digits (e.g., more than iMaxLine digits), the writes to pMsg overflow the buffer.

state.value: points past allocated buffer — heap buffer overflow

commit_message:
af_key: Do not call xfrm_probe_algs in parallel

When namespace support was added to xfrm/afkey, it caused the
previously single-threaded call to xfrm_probe_algs to become
multi-threaded.  This is buggy and needs to be fixed with a mutex.

Reported-by: Abhishek Shah <abhishek.shah@columbia.edu>
Fixes: 283bc9f35bbb ("xfrm: Namespacify xfrm state/policy locks")
Signed-off-by: Herbert Xu <herbert@gondor.apana.org.au>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: A race condition was found in the Linux kernel's IP framework for transforming packets (XFRM subsystem) when multiple calls to xfrm_probe_algs occurred simultaneously. This flaw could allow a local attacker to potentially trigger an out-of-bounds write or leak kernel heap memory by performing an out-of-bounds read and copying it into a socket.

commit_message: LibVNCClient: really fix #250
cve_desc: LibVNC before commit 7b1ef0ffc4815cab9a96c7278394152bdc89dc4d contains heap out-of-bound write vulnerability inside structure in VNC client code that can result remote code execution

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the buffer size check allows an attacker-controlled nSubrects value to wrap around when multiplied by (4 + BPP/8), bypassing the RFB_BUFFER_SIZE guard. The subsequent loop then reads past the end of client->buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: hdr.nSubrects is read from the network and byte-swapped. An attacker controls this 32-bit value.

state.value: attacker-controlled uint32_t, e.g. a value near UINT32_MAX / (4 + BPP/8) + 1

step_by_step_execution.description: The overflow check computes hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8)). For example with BPP=8, the stride is 5. If nSubrects = 0x33333334, then 0x33333334 * 5 = 0x100000004 which truncates to 4 in 32-bit arithmetic, passing the > RFB_BUFFER_SIZE check.
ReadFromRFBServer reads only 4 bytes (the wrapped result) into client->buffer.
The for-loop iterates hdr.nSubrects (0x33333334) times, incrementing ptr by (4 + BPP/8) = 5 bytes each iteration, reading far beyond the 4 bytes that were actually placed in client->buffer.

state.value: increments well past end of client->buffer

commit_message:
net/mlx5: prevent memory leak in mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq

In mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq if mlx5_vector2eqn fails the allocated
memory should be released.

Fixes: 537a50574175 ("net/mlx5: FPGA, Add high-speed connection routines")
Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Saeed Mahameed <saeedm@mellanox.com>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq() function in drivers/net/ethernet/mellanox/mlx5/core/fpga/conn.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering mlx5_vector2eqn() failures, aka CID-c8c2a057fdc7.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-2qpg-f6wf-w984
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming STUN message contains an ERROR-CODE attribute, the header length is not checked before performing a subtraction operation, potentially resulting in an integer underflow scenario. This issue affects all users that use STUN. A malicious actor located within the victim’s network may forge and send a specially crafted UDP (STUN) message that could remotely execute arbitrary code on the victim’s machine. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when `attr->hdr.length` (parsed from the attacker-controlled buffer) is less than 4. The code accesses `buf[6]` and `buf[7]` unconditionally, but these offsets fall outside the attribute's declared value region (and potentially outside the actual buffer) when the length field is small. The check `if (value.slen < 0)` only clamps the string length later and does not prevent the preceding out-of-bounds access.

step_by_step_execution.description: The attribute header is parsed from the buffer via GETATTRHDR, populating `attr->hdr.length` with the 16-bit length value from the attacker-controlled buffer.

The code unconditionally accesses `buf[6]` and `buf[7]` to compute the error code. With ATTR_HDR_LEN = 4, index 6 is at ATTR_HDR_LEN + 2 and index 7 is at ATTR_HDR_LEN + 3, both within the attribute value payload. However, the attribute value payload is only `attr->hdr.length` bytes long (starting at offset 4). When `attr->hdr.length` is 0, 1, 2, or 3, indices 6 and/or 7 exceed the attribute's actual data boundary.

The out-of-bounds values are used to compute `attr->err_code`, and execution continues. The later check `if (value.slen < 0)` only clamps the string length and does not prevent the already-occurred OOB read.

commit_message:
cgroup-v1: Require capabilities to set release_agent

The cgroup release_agent is called with call_usermodehelper.  The function
call_usermodehelper starts the release_agent with a full set fo capabilities.
Therefore require capabilities when setting the release_agaent.

Reported-by: Tabitha Sable <tabitha.c.sable@gmail.com>
Tested-by: Tabitha Sable <tabitha.c.sable@gmail.com>
Fixes: 81a6a5cdd2c5 ("Task Control Groups: automatic userspace notification of idle cgroups")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v2.6.24+
Signed-off-by: "Eric W. Biederman" <ebiederm@xmission.com>
Signed-off-by: Tejun Heo <tj@kernel.org>
cve_desc: A vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel’s cgroup_release_agent_write in the kernel/cgroup/cgroup-v1.c function. This flaw, under certain circumstances, allows the use of the cgroups v1 release_agent feature to escalate privileges and bypass the namespace isolation unexpectedly.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0018: going over the end of the typahead

Problem:    Going over the end of the typahead.
Solution:   Put a NUL after the typeahead.
cve_desc: Integer Overflow or Wraparound in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.description: In both the vertical and horizontal scrollbar event loops (FEAT_GUI, !USE_ON_FLY_SCROLL), the loop condition accesses tp[j], tp[j+1], and tp[j+2] without verifying that j+2 is within the bounds of `len` (the valid input length). As the loop iterates and `j` grows, it can read past the valid data boundary and potentially past the allocated buffer, leading to out-of-bounds reads.

code_snippets:
for (i = 0; tp[j] == CSI && tp[j + 1] == KS_VER_SCROLLBAR
					     && tp[j + 2] != NUL; ++i)

for (i = 0; tp[j] == CSI && tp[j + 1] == KS_HOR_SCROLLBAR
					     && tp[j + 2] != NUL; ++i)

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with buf != NULL. tp is set to buf + offset, and len = *buflen - offset represents the valid input data length.
A termcode match is found where key_name[0] == KS_VER_SCROLLBAR (or KS_HOR_SCROLLBAR). The variable `slen` is set based on the matched termcode length.
The vertical scrollbar loop begins with j=0. The loop condition checks tp[j], tp[j+1], tp[j+2] without any comparison of j against len.
On the first iteration the condition matches (first 3 bytes are CSI, KS_VER_SCROLLBAR, non-NUL). j is advanced by 3 plus additional bytes from get_bytes_from_buf and get_long_from_buf. slen = j.

The loop condition is checked again: tp[j], tp[j+1], tp[j+2] are accessed. If j >= len, this reads beyond the valid data. If j+2 >= bufsize - offset, this reads beyond the allocated buffer entirely, causing an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4563: "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line

Problem:    "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line.
Solution:   Adjust "badlen".
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow occurs in vim in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4563.

vulnerabilities.description: Stack-based buffer overflow in `wcopy`. The buffer is `MAXWLEN + 2` bytes, but when appending the remainder of the bad word, the code writes at offset `stp->st_wordlen` without checking that `stp->st_wordlen + (sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen)` fits within `MAXWLEN + 2`. If `st_wordlen` is close to MAXWLEN and the remainder is non-trivial, the write overflows the stack buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the suggestion display loop. `wcopy` is a stack buffer of size `MAXWLEN + 2`.

state.value: uninitialized stack buffer of size MAXWLEN + 2

step_by_step_execution.description: For each suggestion `stp`, the word is copied into `wcopy` via `vim_strncpy(wcopy, stp->st_word, MAXWLEN)`, which copies up to MAXWLEN bytes plus a NUL terminator.

state.value: contains st_word, up to MAXWLEN chars + NUL

step_by_step_execution.description: The code checks if `sug.su_badlen > stp->st_orglen`. If the bad word is longer than what this suggestion replaces, the remainder is appended.

state.value: portion of bad word replaced by suggestion, e.g. 10

step_by_step_execution.description: The remainder is written at `wcopy + stp->st_wordlen`. With `st_wordlen` up to MAXWLEN and a nonzero remainder, the total bytes written is `st_wordlen + remainder`, which can exceed `MAXWLEN + 2`.
This causes a stack buffer overflow, writing up to ~38 bytes past the end of `wcopy`, potentially overwriting return addresses or other stack data.

state.value: overflowed by ~38 bytes past its MAXWLEN+2 boundary

commit_message: Fix bug #72750: wddx_deserialize null dereference
cve_desc: The php_wddx_pop_element function in ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid base64 binary value, as demonstrated by a wddx_deserialize call that mishandles a binary element in a wddxPacket XML document.

vulnerabilities.description: php_base64_decode can return NULL if the input is not valid base64 or memory allocation fails. The return value is assigned directly to Z_STRVAL_P(ent1->data) without a NULL check. This NULL string value is then used in subsequent operations (e.g., when adding to a hash table or when the zval is later accessed), leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with name == EL_BINARY and stack->top >= 1.
wddx_stack_top retrieves ent1 from the top of the stack. ent1->data is non-NULL and holds a zval of type IS_STRING with invalid base64 content.
The code enters the EL_BINARY branch and calls php_base64_decode on the invalid input, which returns NULL.
STR_FREE frees the old string value, then Z_STRVAL_P(ent1->data) is set to NULL (new_str).

Execution continues past the binary block. When stack->top > 1, the code merges ent1->data into a parent hash table or otherwise uses the zval whose string value is NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference when the string is accessed.

state.value: zval with IS_STRING type but NULL string pointer

commit_message:
SERVER-59071 Treat '$sample' as unsharded when connecting directly to shards

(cherry picked from commit f3604b901d688c194de5e430c7fbab060c9dc8e0)
cve_desc: An authorized user may trigger an invariant which may result in denial of service or server exit if a relevant aggregation request is sent to a shard. Usually, the requests are sent via mongos and special privileges are required in order to know the address of the shards and to log in to the shards of an auth enabled environment.

Based on a reported DoS vulnerability reported by Team82 (Claroty
Research).

An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending an unlimited number of huge chunks (e.g. 2GB each) without sending the Final closing chunk.

commit_message:
net/mlx5: prevent memory leak in mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq

In mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq if mlx5_vector2eqn fails the allocated
memory should be released.

Fixes: 537a50574175 ("net/mlx5: FPGA, Add high-speed connection routines")
Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Saeed Mahameed <saeedm@mellanox.com>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq() function in drivers/net/ethernet/mellanox/mlx5/core/fpga/conn.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering mlx5_vector2eqn() failures, aka CID-c8c2a057fdc7.

vulnerabilities.description: When mlx5_vector2eqn() fails, the code jumps to err_cqwq which only calls mlx5_wq_destroy but does not free the memory allocated by kvzalloc for 'in', causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: mlx5_cqwq_create succeeds, so execution continues past the first error check.
kvzalloc succeeds and allocates memory for 'in'.

state.value: non-NULL pointer to allocated memory of size inlen

step_by_step_execution.description: mlx5_vector2eqn is called and returns an error.
The error check triggers a goto err_cqwq, skipping over kvfree(in).

state.value: still points to allocated memory, never freed

step_by_step_execution.description: err_cqwq label: mlx5_wq_destroy is called but 'in' is never freed, resulting in a memory leak.

commit_message:
cadence_gem: switch to use qemu_receive_packet() for loopback

This patch switches to use qemu_receive_packet() which can detect
reentrancy and return early.

This is intended to address CVE-2021-3416.

Cc: Prasad J Pandit <ppandit@redhat.com>
Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Alexander Bulekov <alxndr@bu.edu>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.

commit_message: imptcp: fix Segmentation Fault when octet count is to high
cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in rsyslog in the imptcp module. An attacker could send a specially crafted message to the imptcp socket, which would cause rsyslog to crash. Versions before 8.27.0 are vulnerable.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in octet count parsing. The octet count is accumulated without any upper bound check during digit parsing. A malicious sender can supply a very long digit string causing `iOctetsRemain` to overflow (signed integer overflow is undefined behavior in C, and can wrap to a negative value). When this negative value is later used as `octatesToCopy` and passed to `memcpy` (which interprets its `size_t` parameter as unsigned), it results in a massive heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called repeatedly for each byte of input. The session starts in `eAtStrtFram` state. A digit character is received, transitioning to `eInOctetCnt` state with `iOctetsRemain = 0`.

The attacker sends many digit characters (e.g., '9' repeated many times). Each call multiplies `iOctetsRemain` by 10 and adds the digit value. There is no bounds check on the accumulated value. After enough digits, `iOctetsRemain` overflows the signed int range, resulting in undefined behavior that typically wraps to a negative value.

state.value: negative due to signed integer overflow (e.g., after parsing '99999999999' or similar)

step_by_step_execution.description: The attacker sends a space character to terminate the octet count. The code checks `iOctetsRemain < 1` but only logs an error and does NOT abort or reset the state. Execution continues and sets `inputState = eInMsg`.
On the next call, the code enters the `TCP_FRAMING_OCTET_COUNTING` branch. `octatesToCopy` is set to the negative `iOctetsRemain`.
The check `buffLen < octatesToCopy` compares a positive int with a negative int. Since buffLen is positive, it is not less than a negative number, so this check is skipped.
The check `octatesToCopy + pThis->iMsg > iMaxLine` evaluates to a negative sum which is not greater than iMaxLine, so truncation logic is skipped.

`memcpy(pThis->pMsg + pThis->iMsg, *buff, octatesToCopy)` is called. The negative `octatesToCopy` (int) is implicitly converted to `size_t` (unsigned), becoming a very large value (e.g., ~4GB on 32-bit or ~18EB on 64-bit), causing a massive heap buffer overflow write.

state.value: very large unsigned value (~4GB or more)

commit_message:
study_chunk: avoid mutating regexp program within GOSUB

gh16947 and gh17743: studying GOSUB may restudy in an inner call
(via a mix of recursion and enframing) something that an outer call
is in the middle of looking at.  Let the outer frame deal with it.

(CVE-2020-12723)

(cherry picked from commit c4033e740bd18d9fbe3456a9db2ec2053cdc5271)
cve_desc: regcomp.c in Perl before 5.30.3 allows a buffer overflow via a crafted regular expression because of recursive S_study_chunk calls.

commit_message:
tipc: fix size validations for the MSG_CRYPTO type

The function tipc_crypto_key_rcv is used to parse MSG_CRYPTO messages
to receive keys from other nodes in the cluster in order to decrypt any
further messages from them.
This patch verifies that any supplied sizes in the message body are
valid for the received message.

Fixes: 1ef6f7c9390f ("tipc: add automatic session key exchange")
Signed-off-by: Max VA <maxv@sentinelone.com>
Acked-by: Ying Xue <ying.xue@windriver.com>
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
Acked-by: Jon Maloy <jmaloy@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/tipc/crypto.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.16. The Transparent Inter-Process Communication (TIPC) functionality allows remote attackers to exploit insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes for the MSG_CRYPTO message type.

vulnerabilities.description: The sanity check on skey->keylen occurs AFTER the memcpy that uses it. An attacker-controlled message can set keylen to a value larger than the allocated buffer (size bytes), causing a heap buffer overflow when copying key material into skey->key. The allocation is kmalloc(size, GFP_ATOMIC) but the copy length is skey->keylen which is read directly from the untrusted message data without prior validation.

step_by_step_execution.description: msg_data_sz(hdr) returns the data size from the received message, stored in 'size'. An attacker crafts a message with a small data size, e.g. size = 48.
skey is allocated with kmalloc(size, GFP_ATOMIC), so a 48-byte heap buffer is allocated.
skey->keylen is read from the message data at offset TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME. The attacker sets this field to a large value, e.g., 0x1000 (4096).
memcpy copies TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME bytes of alg_name — this fits within the buffer, no issue yet.
memcpy copies skey->keylen (4096) bytes into skey->key. The skey buffer is only 48 bytes total, so this writes far beyond the allocated heap buffer, causing a heap overflow.

state.value: 4096 bytes written into a buffer with only ~48 - offsetof(key) bytes available

step_by_step_execution.description: The sanity check 'size != tipc_aead_key_size(skey)' executes after the overflow has already occurred. The damage is done before the check can prevent it.

commit_message:
KVM: PPC: Book3S: Fix H_RTAS rets buffer overflow

The kvmppc_rtas_hcall() sets the host rtas_args.rets pointer based on
the rtas_args.nargs that was provided by the guest. That guest nargs
value is not range checked, so the guest can cause the host rets pointer
to be pointed outside the args array. The individual rtas function
handlers check the nargs and nrets values to ensure they are correct,
but if they are not, the handlers store a -3 (0xfffffffd) failure
indication in rets[0] which corrupts host memory.

Fix this by testing up front whether the guest supplied nargs and nret
would exceed the array size, and fail the hcall directly without storing
a failure indication to rets[0].

Also expand on a comment about why we kill the guest and try not to
return errors directly if we have a valid rets[0] pointer.

Fixes: 8e591cb72047 ("KVM: PPC: Book3S: Add infrastructure to implement kernel-side RTAS calls")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v3.10+
Reported-by: Alexey Kardashevskiy <aik@ozlabs.ru>
Signed-off-by: Nicholas Piggin <npiggin@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
cve_desc: arch/powerpc/kvm/book3s_rtas.c in the Linux kernel through 5.13.5 on the powerpc platform allows KVM guest OS users to cause host OS memory corruption via rtas_args.nargs, aka CID-f62f3c20647e.

vulnerabilities.description: The guest-controlled value args.nargs is used as an array index into the fixed-size args.args[] array without any bounds check. If a malicious guest provides a large nargs value, args.rets will point outside the args structure (which lives on the kernel stack), and the subsequent handler invocation can read/write out-of-bounds memory via the rets pointer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The guest physical address of the RTAS args is obtained from guest register r4.
The entire rtas_args structure is copied from guest memory into the local stack variable 'args'. All fields including nargs are guest-controlled.
args.rets is set to &args.args[be32_to_cpu(args.nargs)] with no bounds check. If nargs exceeds the array size (e.g., 16), this pointer goes past the end of the args structure on the stack.

state.value: e.g. 0xFFFFFFFF or any value > array size
points well beyond the args.args[] array, into other stack data or beyond the stack frame

step_by_step_execution.description: If a matching token is found, d->handler->handler(vcpu, &args) is called. The handler will typically write a return status into args.rets[0], causing an out-of-bounds stack write through the corrupted rets pointer.

commit_message:
sctp: Fix kernel panic while process protocol violation parameter

Since call to function sctp_sf_abort_violation() need paramter 'arg' with
'struct sctp_chunk' type, it will read the chunk type and chunk length from
the chunk_hdr member of chunk. But call to sctp_sf_violation_paramlen()
always with 'struct sctp_paramhdr' type's parameter, it will be passed to
sctp_sf_abort_violation(). This may cause kernel panic.

   sctp_sf_violation_paramlen()
     |-- sctp_sf_abort_violation()
        |-- sctp_make_abort_violation()

This patch fixed this problem. This patch also fix two place which called
sctp_sf_violation_paramlen() with wrong paramter type.

Signed-off-by: Wei Yongjun <yjwei@cn.fujitsu.com>
Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: The Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel before 2.6.27 does not properly handle a protocol violation in which a parameter has an invalid length, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) via unspecified vectors, related to sctp_sf_violation_paramlen, sctp_sf_abort_violation, sctp_make_abort_violation, and incorrect data types in function calls.

commit_message:
USB: gadget: validate interface OS descriptor requests

Stall the control endpoint in case provided index exceeds array size of
MAX_CONFIG_INTERFACES or when the retrieved function pointer is null.

Signed-off-by: Szymon Heidrich <szymon.heidrich@gmail.com>
Cc: stable@kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in drivers/usb/gadget/composite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.10. The USB Gadget subsystem lacks certain validation of interface OS descriptor requests (ones with a large array index and ones associated with NULL function pointer retrieval). Memory corruption might occur.

vulnerabilities.description: If bos_desc() returns a negative error code, the cast to (u16) in the min() macro converts it to a large positive number (e.g., -ENOMEM = -12 becomes 65524 as u16). The min() then selects w_length, making 'value' positive. This causes the check_value path to queue a response of w_length bytes from req->buf, potentially leaking uninitialized or stale kernel heap data to the USB host.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a USB_REQ_GET_DESCRIPTOR request with w_value >> 8 == USB_DT_BOS, and w_length set to some reasonable value (e.g., 512).
The code enters the USB_DT_BOS case. gadget_is_superspeed() or gadget->lpm_capable is true, so bos_desc(cdev) is called.
bos_desc() fails and returns a negative error code, e.g., -ENOMEM (-12).
The negative value is cast to (u16) in min(w_length, (u16) value). -12 cast to u16 becomes 65524. min(512, 65524) = 512. value is now set to 512.

Execution falls through to check_value. Since value (512) >= 0 and != USB_GADGET_DELAYED_STATUS, req->length is set to 512 and the request buffer is queued to the host, sending 512 bytes of potentially uninitialized/stale heap data.

state.value: uninitialized or stale data (bos_desc failed, buffer was not properly filled)
