commit_message:
patch 8.2.4563: "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line

Problem:    "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line.
Solution:   Adjust "badlen".
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow occurs in vim in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4563.

vulnerabilities.description: Stack buffer overflow in `wcopy`. The buffer is `MAXWLEN + 2` bytes, but the code writes `stp->st_word` (up to MAXWLEN bytes) starting at offset 0, then appends the remainder of the bad word at offset `stp->st_wordlen`. If `stp->st_wordlen + (sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen)` exceeds `MAXWLEN + 2`, a stack-based buffer overflow occurs. The `badlen` value derived from a Visual selection is not bounded by MAXWLEN, so `sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen` can be arbitrarily large.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry. `wcopy` is a stack buffer of size `MAXWLEN + 2`.

VIsual_active is true. `badlen` is computed from the visual selection columns: `badlen = abs(curwin->w_cursor.col - VIsual.col) + 1`. For a large visual selection on a single line, `badlen` can be much larger than MAXWLEN.

`spell_find_suggest` is called with this `badlen`. The suggestion engine sets `sug.su_badlen` based on `badlen`. A suggestion `stp` may have `stp->st_orglen` small (e.g., 5) and `stp->st_wordlen` close to MAXWLEN (e.g., MAXWLEN).
`vim_strncpy(wcopy, stp->st_word, MAXWLEN)` copies up to MAXWLEN bytes to `wcopy`. This itself is fine.

state.value: filled with st_word, up to MAXWLEN bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: Condition `sug.su_badlen > stp->st_orglen` is true (500 > 5). The code calls `vim_strncpy(wcopy + stp->st_wordlen, ..., sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen)` which writes up to 495 bytes starting at offset MAXWLEN in `wcopy`.
The write exceeds the `wcopy` buffer by ~494 bytes, causing a stack-based buffer overflow that can overwrite the return address and other stack variables.

commit_message:
Fix possible NULL dereference (savannah bug #55369)

* src/sparse.c (pax_decode_header): Check return from find_next_block.
cve_desc: pax_decode_header in sparse.c in GNU Tar before 1.32 had a NULL pointer dereference when parsing certain archives that have malformed extended headers.

commit_message:
USB: gadget: validate interface OS descriptor requests

Stall the control endpoint in case provided index exceeds array size of
MAX_CONFIG_INTERFACES or when the retrieved function pointer is null.

Signed-off-by: Szymon Heidrich <szymon.heidrich@gmail.com>
Cc: stable@kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in drivers/usb/gadget/composite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.10. The USB Gadget subsystem lacks certain validation of interface OS descriptor requests (ones with a large array index and ones associated with NULL function pointer retrieval). Memory corruption might occur.

vulnerabilities.description: In the USB_DT_BOS descriptor handler, if bos_desc() returns a negative error code, the result is cast to (u16) without first checking for a negative return value. This converts the negative error (e.g., -1 becomes 65535) into a large positive number. The min() with w_length then yields a positive value, causing the code at check_value to queue a response of up to w_length bytes from req->buf, potentially leaking uninitialized or stale kernel heap memory to the USB host. Other descriptor cases (USB_DT_CONFIG, USB_DT_STRING) correctly guard with 'if (value >= 0)' before the min/cast, but USB_DT_BOS does not.

step_by_step_execution.description: A USB host sends a GET_DESCRIPTOR request for USB_DT_BOS with a non-zero w_length (e.g., 255). The w_length passes the USB_COMP_EP0_BUFSIZ check since it is small enough.
The code enters the USB_DT_BOS case. gadget_is_superspeed or lpm_capable is true, so bos_desc(cdev) is called.
bos_desc(cdev) returns a negative error code, e.g., -ENOMEM (-12). No valid descriptor data has been written to req->buf.
The code executes 'value = min(w_length, (u16) value)'. The cast (u16)(-12) produces 65524. min(255, 65524) = 255.

Execution reaches check_value. Since value (255) >= 0 and != USB_GADGET_DELAYED_STATUS, req->length is set to 255 and the request is queued via composite_ep0_queue, sending 255 bytes of potentially uninitialized/stale heap data from req->buf to the USB host.

commit_message:
Submitted by: Tomas Hoger <thoger@redhat.com>

Fix for CVE-2010-0433 where some kerberos enabled versions of OpenSSL
could be crashed if the relevant tables were not present (e.g. chrooted).
cve_desc: The kssl_keytab_is_available function in ssl/kssl.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8n, when Kerberos is enabled but Kerberos configuration files cannot be opened, does not check a certain return value, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and daemon crash) via SSL cipher negotiation, as demonstrated by a chroot installation of Dovecot or stunnel without Kerberos configuration files inside the chroot.

commit_message: Fix bug #72606: heap-buffer-overflow (write) simplestring_addn simplestring.c
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the simplestring_addn function in simplestring.c in xmlrpc-epi through 0.54.2, as used in PHP before 5.5.38, 5.6.x before 5.6.24, and 7.x before 7.0.9, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a long first argument to the PHP xmlrpc_encode_request function.

vulnerabilities.description: The function dereferences `target` to read `target->size` on the very first line, before the null check `if(target && source)` on line 3. If `target` is NULL, this is a null pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with `target` being NULL.
`target->size` is dereferenced to initialize `newsize`, before any null check is performed.
The null check `if(target && source)` on line 3 would have caught this, but it comes too late.

commit_message:
Fix oobread segfault in java arith8.class ##crash

* Reported by Cen Zhang via huntr.dev
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.2.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when reading `number_of_classes` from the buffer at offset 6. There is no check that `sz >= 8` before this read. The bounds check `offset + 8 > sz` only occurs inside the for loop, after `number_of_classes` has already been read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a small buffer, e.g. sz = 7.
r_bin_java_default_attr_new is called and returns a non-NULL attr. offset is set to 6.
R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT(buffer, offset) reads 2 bytes at buffer[6..7], but sz is only 7, meaning buffer[7] is out of bounds.

vulnerabilities.description: Memory leak: when icattr->name cannot be resolved and icattr is freed directly with free(), the previously allocated icattr->flags_str (from retrieve_class_method_access_string) and the just-assigned icattr->name (from r_str_dup) are not freed, leading to memory leaks.

step_by_step_execution.description: An icattr is allocated and populated. flags_str is allocated via retrieve_class_method_access_string.
Both attempts to resolve icattr->name via r_bin_java_get_item_name_from_bin_cp_list fail, returning NULL.
icattr->name is set to r_str_dup(NULL, "NULL"), allocating a new string.
free(icattr) is called, but icattr->flags_str and icattr->name are not freed beforehand, causing memory leaks.

commit_message: Fix bug #72606: heap-buffer-overflow (write) simplestring_addn simplestring.c
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the simplestring_addn function in simplestring.c in xmlrpc-epi through 0.54.2, as used in PHP before 5.5.38, 5.6.x before 5.6.24, and 7.x before 7.0.9, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a long first argument to the PHP xmlrpc_encode_request function.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the size check `target->len + add_len + 1` can wrap to a negative value when `add_len` is very large, causing the reallocation branch to be skipped. The subsequent `memcpy` then writes `add_len` bytes into a buffer that is too small, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a valid target (e.g., target->len=100, target->size=200, target->str allocated to 200 bytes) and a large add_len.
Both target and source are non-null, target->str is non-null, so initialization is skipped.
The overflow check computes target->len + add_len + 1. Since all operands are int: 100 + 2147483647 + 1 overflows signed int, wrapping to a negative value (e.g., -2147483548 on two's complement).
The comparison `(negative_value > target->size)` i.e. `-2147483548 > 200` is false, so the entire reallocation block is skipped. The buffer remains at its original 200-byte size.
Execution enters `if(target->str)` which is true, and `if(add_len)` which is true (INT_MAX != 0).
memcpy copies INT_MAX bytes from source into target->str + 100, far exceeding the 200-byte buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Remove 'umask' calls from 'mkdir'.

Fixes <http://bugs.gnu.org/24659>.

* libguile/filesys.c (SCM_DEFINE): Remove calls to 'umask' when MODE is
unbound; instead, use 0777 as the mode.  Update docstring to clarify
this.
* doc/ref/posix.texi (File System): Adjust accordingly.
* NEWS: Mention it.
cve_desc: The mkdir procedure of GNU Guile temporarily changed the process' umask to zero. During that time window, in a multithreaded application, other threads could end up creating files with insecure permissions. For example, mkdir without the optional mode argument would create directories as 0777. This is fixed in Guile 2.0.13. Prior versions are affected.

commit_message:
grep -F: fix a heap buffer (read) overrun

grep's read buffer is often filled to its full size, except when
reading the final buffer of a file.  In that case, the number of
bytes read may be far less than the size of the buffer.  However, for
certain unusual pattern/text combinations, grep -F would mistakenly
examine bytes in that uninitialized region of memory when searching
for a match.  With carefully chosen inputs, one can cause grep -F to
read beyond the end of that buffer altogether.  This problem arose via
commit v2.18-90-g73893ff with the introduction of a more efficient
heuristic using what is now the memchr_kwset function. The use of
that function in bmexec_trans could leave TP much larger than EP,
and the subsequent call to bm_delta2_search would mistakenly access
beyond end of the main input read buffer.

* src/kwset.c (bmexec_trans): When TP reaches or exceeds EP,
do not call bm_delta2_search.
* tests/kwset-abuse: New file.
* tests/Makefile.am (TESTS): Add it.
* THANKS.in: Update.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.

Prior to this patch, this command would trigger a UMR:

  printf %0360db 0 | valgrind src/grep -F $(printf %019dXb 0)

  Use of uninitialised value of size 8
     at 0x4142BE: bmexec_trans (kwset.c:657)
     by 0x4143CA: bmexec (kwset.c:678)
     by 0x414973: kwsexec (kwset.c:848)
     by 0x414DC4: Fexecute (kwsearch.c:128)
     by 0x404E2E: grepbuf (grep.c:1238)
     by 0x4054BF: grep (grep.c:1417)
     by 0x405CEB: grepdesc (grep.c:1645)
     by 0x405EC1: grep_command_line_arg (grep.c:1692)
     by 0x4077D4: main (grep.c:2570)

See the accompanying test for how to trigger the heap buffer overrun.

Thanks to Nima Aghdaii for testing and finding numerous
ways to break early iterations of this patch.
cve_desc: The bmexec_trans function in kwset.c in grep 2.19 through 2.21 allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds heap read and crash) via crafted input when using the -F option.

vulnerabilities.description: kwset->mind is assigned to an `int` variable `len`. If `kwset->mind` is a large unsigned/size_t value, it could overflow or truncate when stored in `int len`, potentially bypassing the `len > size` safety check and leading to out-of-bounds memory accesses when `len` is used to compute pointers like `tp = text + len` and array indices.

step_by_step_execution.description: kwset->mind is read and assigned to int len. If mind is a large value (e.g., greater than INT_MAX), integer truncation/overflow occurs.
The check `if (len == 0)` does not trigger since len is negative.

The check `if (len > size)` compares int to size_t. A negative len is converted to a large unsigned value, so this check may or may not trigger depending on size. However if size is also large enough, it passes.

state.value: could be >= converted len, passing the check

step_by_step_execution.description: tp = text + len is computed with the original int len (negative), producing a pointer before the text buffer.

state.value: text + negative offset = pointer before text buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: Subsequent accesses like d1[U(tp[-1])] read out-of-bounds memory.

commit_message: Fix ultra rare but potential memory-leak
cve_desc: ImageMagick before 7.0.8-50 has a memory leak vulnerability in the function ReadBMPImage in coders/bmp.c.

vulnerabilities.description: In the 1-bit per pixel case, pixel index values (0 or 1) are written without validating them against the colormap size. If number_colors is set to 1 (which passes the validation check since 1 <= 2), the colormap has only 1 entry (index 0). When a pixel bit is set, index 1 is stored. The subsequent SyncImage() call resolves PseudoClass pixel indexes via the colormap, causing an out-of-bounds read on image->colormap[1]. The 4-bit and 8-bit cases use IsValidColormapIndex for validation, but the 1-bit case does not.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted BMP file is read with bits_per_pixel=1 and number_colors=1.
The validation check passes because number_colors (1) <= (1 << bits_per_pixel) which is (1 << 1) = 2.
image->colors is set to bmp_info.number_colors (1), and AcquireImageColormap allocates a colormap with exactly 1 entry (index 0 only).
In the 1-bit scanline conversion (case 1), for each bit in the pixel data, the index is set to 0 or 1 depending on the bit value. No IsValidColormapIndex check is performed.
When a pixel bit is 1, SetPixelIndex stores index value 1 into the index queue.

SyncImage() is called, which resolves PseudoClass indexes by reading image->colormap[index]. For index=1, this reads image->colormap[1] which is beyond the allocated colormap array of size 1, resulting in a heap out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
grep -F: fix a heap buffer (read) overrun

grep's read buffer is often filled to its full size, except when
reading the final buffer of a file.  In that case, the number of
bytes read may be far less than the size of the buffer.  However, for
certain unusual pattern/text combinations, grep -F would mistakenly
examine bytes in that uninitialized region of memory when searching
for a match.  With carefully chosen inputs, one can cause grep -F to
read beyond the end of that buffer altogether.  This problem arose via
commit v2.18-90-g73893ff with the introduction of a more efficient
heuristic using what is now the memchr_kwset function. The use of
that function in bmexec_trans could leave TP much larger than EP,
and the subsequent call to bm_delta2_search would mistakenly access
beyond end of the main input read buffer.

* src/kwset.c (bmexec_trans): When TP reaches or exceeds EP,
do not call bm_delta2_search.
* tests/kwset-abuse: New file.
* tests/Makefile.am (TESTS): Add it.
* THANKS.in: Update.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.

Prior to this patch, this command would trigger a UMR:

  printf %0360db 0 | valgrind src/grep -F $(printf %019dXb 0)

  Use of uninitialised value of size 8
     at 0x4142BE: bmexec_trans (kwset.c:657)
     by 0x4143CA: bmexec (kwset.c:678)
     by 0x414973: kwsexec (kwset.c:848)
     by 0x414DC4: Fexecute (kwsearch.c:128)
     by 0x404E2E: grepbuf (grep.c:1238)
     by 0x4054BF: grep (grep.c:1417)
     by 0x405CEB: grepdesc (grep.c:1645)
     by 0x405EC1: grep_command_line_arg (grep.c:1692)
     by 0x4077D4: main (grep.c:2570)

See the accompanying test for how to trigger the heap buffer overrun.

Thanks to Nima Aghdaii for testing and finding numerous
ways to break early iterations of this patch.
cve_desc: The bmexec_trans function in kwset.c in grep 2.19 through 2.21 allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds heap read and crash) via crafted input when using the -F option.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable `len` is declared as `int` but `kwset->mind` could be a large unsigned value (e.g., `size_t`). If `kwset->mind` is larger than `INT_MAX`, the assignment truncates/wraps the value, potentially making `len` negative or a small positive number. This would bypass the `len > size` check and cause subsequent pointer arithmetic (`tp = text + len`, `sp = kwset->target + len`) to use an incorrect offset, leading to out-of-bounds reads throughout the function.

step_by_step_execution.description: kwset->mind is assigned to `int len`. If kwset->mind is a large value (e.g. greater than INT_MAX), integer truncation occurs.

state.value: negative value due to truncation, e.g. -2147483647

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `len == 0` is false since len is negative.

The check `len > size` compares int to size_t. With a negative len, this comparison (after implicit conversion) may behave unexpectedly depending on platform, but on common platforms where int is promoted to size_t (unsigned), a negative int becomes a very large unsigned value, so this check passes and returns -1. However, if mind fits in int but is crafted so that `len` is small/positive after truncation while actual pattern data is different, subsequent pointer arithmetic is wrong.
tp = text + len uses the truncated len, pointing to an incorrect position. Subsequent reads via d1[U(tp[-1])] access memory based on this incorrect pointer.

state.value: out-of-bounds read if len doesn't match actual data

commit_message:
tipc: fix size validations for the MSG_CRYPTO type

The function tipc_crypto_key_rcv is used to parse MSG_CRYPTO messages
to receive keys from other nodes in the cluster in order to decrypt any
further messages from them.
This patch verifies that any supplied sizes in the message body are
valid for the received message.

Fixes: 1ef6f7c9390f ("tipc: add automatic session key exchange")
Signed-off-by: Max VA <maxv@sentinelone.com>
Acked-by: Ying Xue <ying.xue@windriver.com>
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
Acked-by: Jon Maloy <jmaloy@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/tipc/crypto.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.16. The Transparent Inter-Process Communication (TIPC) functionality allows remote attackers to exploit insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes for the MSG_CRYPTO message type.

commit_message:
auth: Don't crash expanding %variables when username isn't set.

This continues the auth-policy fix in
c3d3faa4f72a676e183f34be960cff13a5a725ae
cve_desc: The auth component in Dovecot before 2.2.27, when auth-policy is configured, allows a remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) by aborting authentication without setting a username.

commit_message:
Submitted by: Tomas Hoger <thoger@redhat.com>

Fix for CVE-2010-0433 where some kerberos enabled versions of OpenSSL
could be crashed if the relevant tables were not present (e.g. chrooted).
cve_desc: The kssl_keytab_is_available function in ssl/kssl.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8n, when Kerberos is enabled but Kerberos configuration files cannot be opened, does not check a certain return value, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and daemon crash) via SSL cipher negotiation, as demonstrated by a chroot installation of Dovecot or stunnel without Kerberos configuration files inside the chroot.

code_snippets:
krb5rc = krb5_sname_to_principal(krb5context, NULL, 
                                     kssl_ctx->service_name ? kssl_ctx->service_name: KRB5SVC,
                                     KRB5_NT_SRV_HST, &princ);

    krb5rc = krb5_kt_get_entry(krb5context, krb5keytab, 
                                princ,
                                0 /* IGNORE_VNO */,
                                0 /* IGNORE_ENCTYPE */,
                                &entry);
vulnerabilities.description: The return value of krb5_sname_to_principal is not checked before calling krb5_kt_get_entry. If krb5_sname_to_principal fails, princ may remain NULL, and a NULL principal is then passed to krb5_kt_get_entry, which can lead to a NULL pointer dereference.

code_snippets:
krb5rc = krb5_kt_get_entry(krb5context, krb5keytab, 
                                princ,
                                0 /* IGNORE_VNO */,
                                0 /* IGNORE_ENCTYPE */,
                                &entry);

step_by_step_execution.description: krb5_init_context succeeds, krb5context is initialized.
A keytab is resolved or the default is obtained successfully.
krb5_sname_to_principal is called but fails (returns non-zero). The error code is stored in krb5rc, but there is no check or goto exit.
Execution falls through to krb5_kt_get_entry, which is called with princ still set to NULL, leading to a NULL pointer dereference inside the function.

commit_message:
sctp: correct bounds check in sctp_setsockopt_auth_key

The bonds check to prevent buffer overlflow was not exactly
right.  It still allowed overflow of up to 8 bytes which is
sizeof(struct sctp_authkey).

Since optlen is already checked against the size of that struct,
we are guaranteed not to cause interger overflow either.

Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the sctp_setsockopt_auth_key function in net/sctp/socket.c in the Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel 2.6.24-rc1 through 2.6.26.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted sca_keylength field associated with the SCTP_AUTH_KEY option.

vulnerabilities.description: The validation of sca_keylength checks against the full optlen, but the key data in the flexible array member of sctp_authkey starts at an offset of sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) within the allocated buffer. If sca_keylength is set to a value close to optlen, sctp_auth_set_key will read sca_keylength bytes starting from the key field (which is at offset sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)), reading past the end of the heap-allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: User calls setsockopt with optlen set to, e.g., sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) + 1, passing the initial size check.
kmalloc allocates a buffer of optlen bytes.
copy_from_user copies optlen bytes from user space into authkey. The user sets sca_keylength to optlen (sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) + 1).
The check 'authkey->sca_keylength > optlen' is evaluated. Since sca_keylength == optlen, the check passes (is false), so execution continues.

sctp_auth_set_key is called. It reads sca_keylength bytes from authkey->sca_key (the flexible array at the end of the struct). The key field starts at offset sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) within the buffer, but only 1 byte of actual key data exists. The function reads (sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) + 1) bytes starting from that offset, reading sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) bytes beyond the allocated heap buffer.

state.value: sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) bytes past end of allocation

commit_message:
Prevent over long nonces in ChaCha20-Poly1305

ChaCha20-Poly1305 is an AEAD cipher, and requires a unique nonce input for
every encryption operation. RFC 7539 specifies that the nonce value (IV)
should be 96 bits (12 bytes). OpenSSL allows a variable nonce length and
front pads the nonce with 0 bytes if it is less than 12 bytes. However it
also incorrectly allows a nonce to be set of up to 16 bytes. In this case
only the last 12 bytes are significant and any additional leading bytes are
ignored.

It is a requirement of using this cipher that nonce values are unique.
Messages encrypted using a reused nonce value are susceptible to serious
confidentiality and integrity attacks. If an application changes the
default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes and then makes a change to
the leading bytes of the nonce expecting the new value to be a new unique
nonce then such an application could inadvertently encrypt messages with a
reused nonce.

Additionally the ignored bytes in a long nonce are not covered by the
integrity guarantee of this cipher. Any application that relies on the
integrity of these ignored leading bytes of a long nonce may be further
affected.

Any OpenSSL internal use of this cipher, including in SSL/TLS, is safe
because no such use sets such a long nonce value. However user
applications that use this cipher directly and set a non-default nonce
length to be longer than 12 bytes may be vulnerable.

CVE-2019-1543

Fixes #8345

Reviewed-by: Paul Dale <paul.dale@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Richard Levitte <levitte@openssl.org>
(Merged from https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/8406)

(cherry picked from commit 2a3d0ee9d59156c48973592331404471aca886d6)
cve_desc: ChaCha20-Poly1305 is an AEAD cipher, and requires a unique nonce input for every encryption operation. RFC 7539 specifies that the nonce value (IV) should be 96 bits (12 bytes). OpenSSL allows a variable nonce length and front pads the nonce with 0 bytes if it is less than 12 bytes. However it also incorrectly allows a nonce to be set of up to 16 bytes. In this case only the last 12 bytes are significant and any additional leading bytes are ignored. It is a requirement of using this cipher that nonce values are unique. Messages encrypted using a reused nonce value are susceptible to serious confidentiality and integrity attacks. If an application changes the default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes and then makes a change to the leading bytes of the nonce expecting the new value to be a new unique nonce then such an application could inadvertently encrypt messages with a reused nonce. Additionally the ignored bytes in a long nonce are not covered by the integrity guarantee of this cipher. Any application that relies on the integrity of these ignored leading bytes of a long nonce may be further affected. Any OpenSSL internal use of this cipher, including in SSL/TLS, is safe because no such use sets such a long nonce value. However user applications that use this cipher directly and set a non-default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes may be vulnerable. OpenSSL versions 1.1.1 and 1.1.0 are affected by this issue. Due to the limited scope of affected deployments this has been assessed as low severity and therefore we are not creating new releases at this time. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1c (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1b). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.0k (Affected 1.1.0-1.1.0j).

commit_message:
sftp: Fix bug in sftp_mkdir not returning on error.

resolves: #84
(cherry picked from commit a92c97b2e17715c1b3cdd693d14af6c3311d8e44)
cve_desc: Multiple double free vulnerabilities in the (1) agent_sign_data function in agent.c, (2) channel_request function in channels.c, (3) ssh_userauth_pubkey function in auth.c, (4) sftp_parse_attr_3 function in sftp.c, and (5) try_publickey_from_file function in keyfiles.c in libssh before 0.5.3 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via unspecified vectors.

vulnerabilities.description: When any of the buffer_add or sftp_packet_write calls fail, `ssh_buffer_free(buffer)` and `ssh_string_free(path)` are called inside the if-block, and then called again unconditionally after the if-block, resulting in a double-free. Additionally, the error path lacks a `return -1`, so execution continues into the `while` loop using already-freed resources.

step_by_step_execution.description: A buffer and path string are allocated successfully.
The id is obtained and buffer_add_u32 (or one of the subsequent calls) returns < 0, entering the error branch of the if statement.
Inside the if-block, ssh_buffer_free(buffer) and ssh_string_free(path) are called, freeing both objects.
Execution falls through to the unconditional ssh_buffer_free(buffer) and ssh_string_free(path) calls after the if-block, freeing the same pointers a second time (double-free).

commit_message:
Prevent an invalid memory writes in compileRule

Thanks to Han Zheng for reporting it

Fixes #1214
cve_desc: Liblouis 3.21.0 has an out-of-bounds write in compileRule in compileTranslationTable.c, as demonstrated by lou_trace.

vulnerabilities.description: During macro expansion, when substituting arguments into tmpFile.line, there is no bounds check on tmpFile.linelen against MAXSTRING. While the code checks for MAXSTRING when copying individual definition characters, the argument substitution loop writes characters without any length check, allowing a heap or stack buffer overflow of tmpFile.line.

code_snippets:
for (int j = 0; j < arg.length; j++)
									tmpFile.line[tmpFile.linelen++] = arg.chars[j];

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads a token that matches a macro name, entering the macro expansion branch.
Arguments are read from the file and stored in the malloc'd arguments array.

state.value: a large value, e.g. close to MAXSTRING

step_by_step_execution.description: The macro expansion loop begins. Definition characters are copied one by one into tmpFile.line with a MAXSTRING bounds check.
The loop reaches a substitution point (subst < m->substitution_count). The argument's characters are copied into tmpFile.line in a loop WITHOUT any bounds check on tmpFile.linelen.

state.value: large enough to push linelen past MAXSTRING

step_by_step_execution.description: tmpFile.linelen exceeds the size of tmpFile.line, causing an out-of-bounds write on the stack (FileInfo is a local struct).

state.value: > MAXSTRING, out of bounds write has occurred

vulnerabilities.description: When the number of arguments read is less than expected (argument_count < m->argument_count), the function returns 0 without freeing the 'arguments' array, causing a memory leak. Similarly, several other error return paths within the macro expansion do not free 'arguments'.

step_by_step_execution.description: A macro is matched and arguments array is allocated via malloc.

state.value: heap-allocated pointer of size m->argument_count * sizeof(CharsString)

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop attempts to read arguments but getToken fails before all arguments are read.
The error condition is detected and compileError is called, then the function returns 0 without calling free(arguments).

commit_message:
Prevent over long nonces in ChaCha20-Poly1305

ChaCha20-Poly1305 is an AEAD cipher, and requires a unique nonce input for
every encryption operation. RFC 7539 specifies that the nonce value (IV)
should be 96 bits (12 bytes). OpenSSL allows a variable nonce length and
front pads the nonce with 0 bytes if it is less than 12 bytes. However it
also incorrectly allows a nonce to be set of up to 16 bytes. In this case
only the last 12 bytes are significant and any additional leading bytes are
ignored.

It is a requirement of using this cipher that nonce values are unique.
Messages encrypted using a reused nonce value are susceptible to serious
confidentiality and integrity attacks. If an application changes the
default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes and then makes a change to
the leading bytes of the nonce expecting the new value to be a new unique
nonce then such an application could inadvertently encrypt messages with a
reused nonce.

Additionally the ignored bytes in a long nonce are not covered by the
integrity guarantee of this cipher. Any application that relies on the
integrity of these ignored leading bytes of a long nonce may be further
affected.

Any OpenSSL internal use of this cipher, including in SSL/TLS, is safe
because no such use sets such a long nonce value. However user
applications that use this cipher directly and set a non-default nonce
length to be longer than 12 bytes may be vulnerable.

CVE-2019-1543

Fixes #8345

Reviewed-by: Paul Dale <paul.dale@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Richard Levitte <levitte@openssl.org>
(Merged from https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/8406)

(cherry picked from commit 2a3d0ee9d59156c48973592331404471aca886d6)
cve_desc: ChaCha20-Poly1305 is an AEAD cipher, and requires a unique nonce input for every encryption operation. RFC 7539 specifies that the nonce value (IV) should be 96 bits (12 bytes). OpenSSL allows a variable nonce length and front pads the nonce with 0 bytes if it is less than 12 bytes. However it also incorrectly allows a nonce to be set of up to 16 bytes. In this case only the last 12 bytes are significant and any additional leading bytes are ignored. It is a requirement of using this cipher that nonce values are unique. Messages encrypted using a reused nonce value are susceptible to serious confidentiality and integrity attacks. If an application changes the default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes and then makes a change to the leading bytes of the nonce expecting the new value to be a new unique nonce then such an application could inadvertently encrypt messages with a reused nonce. Additionally the ignored bytes in a long nonce are not covered by the integrity guarantee of this cipher. Any application that relies on the integrity of these ignored leading bytes of a long nonce may be further affected. Any OpenSSL internal use of this cipher, including in SSL/TLS, is safe because no such use sets such a long nonce value. However user applications that use this cipher directly and set a non-default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes may be vulnerable. OpenSSL versions 1.1.1 and 1.1.0 are affected by this issue. Due to the limited scope of affected deployments this has been assessed as low severity and therefore we are not creating new releases at this time. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1c (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1b). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.0k (Affected 1.1.0-1.1.0j).

commit_message:
Remove 'umask' calls from 'mkdir'.

Fixes <http://bugs.gnu.org/24659>.

* libguile/filesys.c (SCM_DEFINE): Remove calls to 'umask' when MODE is
unbound; instead, use 0777 as the mode.  Update docstring to clarify
this.
* doc/ref/posix.texi (File System): Adjust accordingly.
* NEWS: Mention it.
cve_desc: The mkdir procedure of GNU Guile temporarily changed the process' umask to zero. During that time window, in a multithreaded application, other threads could end up creating files with insecure permissions. For example, mkdir without the optional mode argument would create directories as 0777. This is fixed in Guile 2.0.13. Prior versions are affected.

vulnerabilities.description: There is a TOCTOU race condition on the process-wide umask. The code calls umask(0) to read the current umask (which also sets it to 0), then immediately restores it with umask(mask). In a multi-threaded program, between the umask(0) call and the umask(mask) call, another thread could create files with no permission restrictions (umask of 0), or observe the wrong umask value. This is a classic non-atomic read-modify-restore pattern on shared process state.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called without the optional mode argument, so SCM_UNBNDP(mode) is true.
umask(0) is called, which atomically sets the process umask to 0 and returns the old value. The process umask is now 0.

Between this point and the next umask() call, another thread in the same process can create files or directories. Because the process umask is 0, those files may be created with overly permissive permissions.
umask(mask) is called to restore the original umask.
mkdir is called with 0777 ^ mask, creating the directory with the intended permissions, but the window of vulnerability for other threads has already passed.

commit_message: libclamav/mspack.c: fix Quantum decompressor (bb#1771)
cve_desc: The qtm_decompress function in libclamav/mspack.c in ClamAV before 0.96 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption and application crash) via a crafted CAB archive that uses the Quantum (aka .Q) compression format.  NOTE: some of these details are obtained from third party information.

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow in the match copy path when match_offset > window_posn. When the match wraps around the window, there is no check that match_length does not exceed the remaining space in the window buffer (window_size - window_posn). A crafted input can cause rundest to write past the end of the allocated window buffer. Additionally, window_posn += match_length uses the original unclamped match_length in both the wrap and non-wrap branches, potentially causing window_posn to exceed window_size and leading to further out-of-bounds accesses in subsequent iterations.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters with valid qtm stream and positive out_bytes. Local state is restored, including window_posn which could be near window_size (e.g., window_size - 2).
The outer while loop condition is satisfied (more bytes needed). frame_end is computed. The inner while loop begins with window_posn < frame_end.
QTM_GET_SYMBOL returns selector=6 (variable length match). A sym is decoded from model6len, and match_length is computed as length_base[sym] + extra + 5, resulting in a large value, e.g., 20.
A sym is decoded from model6 for the match offset. match_offset is computed and is greater than window_posn (e.g., match_offset = 32770), causing the wrap-around path to be taken.

rundest is set to &window[32766]. i is set to match_length (20). j = match_offset - window_posn = 4. j is not greater than window_size, so no error is returned. runsrc = &window[32768 - 4] = &window[32764].

Since j (4) < i (20), the split copy is performed: first copy 4 bytes (j), advancing rundest to &window[32770]. Then i becomes 16 and runsrc is reset to &window[0]. There is NO check that the remaining 16 bytes fit within the window buffer.

state.value: &window[32770] — 2 bytes past the allocated window buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: The second copy loop writes 16 bytes starting at rundest=&window[32770], writing up to 16 bytes past the end of the window buffer. This is a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: up to 18 bytes (from 32770 to 32785, window ends at 32767)

step_by_step_execution.description: window_posn += match_length sets window_posn to 32786, which exceeds window_size (32768), corrupting state for subsequent iterations.

commit_message:
CPU time limit patch / setrlimit(RLIMIT_CPU, 0) cheat fix

As discovered here today, the change in Kernel 2.6.17 intended to inhibit
users from setting RLIMIT_CPU to 0 (as that is equivalent to unlimited) by
"cheating" and setting it to 1 in such a case, does not make a difference,
as the check is done in the wrong place (too late), and only applies to the
profiling code.

On all systems I checked running kernels above 2.6.17, no matter what the
hard and soft CPU time limits were before, a user could escape them by
issuing in the shell (sh/bash/zsh) "ulimit -t 0", and then the user's
process was not ever killed.

Attached is a trivial patch to fix that.  Simply moving the check to a
slightly earlier location (specifically, before the line that actually
assigns the limit - *old_rlim = new_rlim), does the trick.

Do note that at least the zsh (but not ash, dash, or bash) shell has the
problem of "caching" the limits set by the ulimit command, so when running
zsh the fix will not immediately be evident - after entering "ulimit -t 0",
"ulimit -a" will show "-t: cpu time (seconds) 0", even though the actual
limit as returned by getrlimit(...) will be 1.  It can be verified by
opening a subshell (which will not have the values of the parent shell in
cache) and checking in it, or just by running a CPU intensive command like
"echo '65536^1048576' | bc" and verifying that it dumps core after one
second.

Regardless of whether that is a misfeature in the shell, perhaps it would
be better to return -EINVAL from setrlimit in such a case instead of
cheating and setting to 1, as that does not really reflect the actual state
of the process anymore.  I do not however know what the ground for that
decision was in the original 2.6.17 change, and whether there would be any
"backward" compatibility issues, so I preferred not to touch that right
now.

Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: Linux kernel 2.6.17, and other versions before 2.6.22, does not check when a user attempts to set RLIMIT_CPU to 0 until after the change is made, which allows local users to bypass intended resource limits.

vulnerabilities.description: Time-of-check to time-of-use (TOCTOU) race condition: the permission check on old_rlim->rlim_max is performed without holding the task_lock, but the actual write to *old_rlim happens later under the lock. In a multi-threaded process, two threads can concurrently call setrlimit. Thread A can read old_rlim->rlim_max (e.g., 100), pass the privilege check with new_rlim.rlim_max=80, and before it writes, Thread B writes a lower rlim_max (e.g., 50). Thread A then writes rlim_max=80, effectively raising rlim_max above 50 without CAP_SYS_RESOURCE. This allows an unprivileged process to bypass the rlim_max privilege check.

step_by_step_execution.description: Thread A enters sys_setrlimit. old_rlim points to current->signal->rlim[resource]. The current rlim_max is 100.
Thread A checks (new_rlim.rlim_max > old_rlim->rlim_max): 80 > 100 is false, so the permission check passes without requiring CAP_SYS_RESOURCE.

Thread B (same process) concurrently calls sys_setrlimit for the same resource with new_rlim.rlim_max = 50. It reads old_rlim->rlim_max = 100, passes the check (50 <= 100), acquires task_lock, and writes rlim_max = 50.

Thread A now acquires task_lock and writes *old_rlim = new_rlim, setting rlim_max to 80. This effectively raises rlim_max from 50 to 80 without CAP_SYS_RESOURCE, bypassing the privilege check which was evaluated against the stale value of 100.

state.value: 80 (overwritten by Thread A, raised from 50 without privilege)

commit_message:
CPU time limit patch / setrlimit(RLIMIT_CPU, 0) cheat fix

As discovered here today, the change in Kernel 2.6.17 intended to inhibit
users from setting RLIMIT_CPU to 0 (as that is equivalent to unlimited) by
"cheating" and setting it to 1 in such a case, does not make a difference,
as the check is done in the wrong place (too late), and only applies to the
profiling code.

On all systems I checked running kernels above 2.6.17, no matter what the
hard and soft CPU time limits were before, a user could escape them by
issuing in the shell (sh/bash/zsh) "ulimit -t 0", and then the user's
process was not ever killed.

Attached is a trivial patch to fix that.  Simply moving the check to a
slightly earlier location (specifically, before the line that actually
assigns the limit - *old_rlim = new_rlim), does the trick.

Do note that at least the zsh (but not ash, dash, or bash) shell has the
problem of "caching" the limits set by the ulimit command, so when running
zsh the fix will not immediately be evident - after entering "ulimit -t 0",
"ulimit -a" will show "-t: cpu time (seconds) 0", even though the actual
limit as returned by getrlimit(...) will be 1.  It can be verified by
opening a subshell (which will not have the values of the parent shell in
cache) and checking in it, or just by running a CPU intensive command like
"echo '65536^1048576' | bc" and verifying that it dumps core after one
second.

Regardless of whether that is a misfeature in the shell, perhaps it would
be better to return -EINVAL from setrlimit in such a case instead of
cheating and setting to 1, as that does not really reflect the actual state
of the process anymore.  I do not however know what the ground for that
decision was in the original 2.6.17 change, and whether there would be any
"backward" compatibility issues, so I preferred not to touch that right
now.

Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: Linux kernel 2.6.17, and other versions before 2.6.22, does not check when a user attempts to set RLIMIT_CPU to 0 until after the change is made, which allows local users to bypass intended resource limits.

vulnerabilities.description: There is a TOCTOU race condition between the privilege check reading old_rlim->rlim_max (without any lock) and the later write of *old_rlim = new_rlim (under task_lock). Two threads in the same thread group can race: Thread A (privileged) lowers rlim_max via a concurrent sys_setrlimit call. Thread B (unprivileged) reads old_rlim->rlim_max before Thread A's write completes, sees the old higher value, passes the capability check, then overwrites the rlimit with a value whose rlim_max exceeds the intended limit. This allows an unprivileged thread to raise the hard resource limit beyond what should be permitted.

step_by_step_execution.description: Thread B (unprivileged) enters sys_setrlimit with a new_rlim where rlim_max is set to, say, a high value H. Meanwhile, the current hard limit (old_rlim->rlim_max) is also H because Thread A (privileged) previously raised it.

state.value: H (set previously by privileged thread A)

step_by_step_execution.description: Thread B reads old_rlim->rlim_max without holding any lock and compares it with new_rlim.rlim_max. Since new_rlim.rlim_max (H) is not greater than old_rlim->rlim_max (H), the check passes and EPERM is not returned.
Concurrently, Thread A (privileged) calls sys_setrlimit, acquires task_lock, and writes a lower rlim_max value L to *old_rlim, then releases task_lock. The shared rlim_max is now L.

Thread B now acquires task_lock and writes *old_rlim = new_rlim, setting rlim_max back to H, which is greater than the intended limit L. The unprivileged thread has successfully raised the hard limit past L without CAP_SYS_RESOURCE.

commit_message:
Avoid an integer overflow in the xbm loader

At the same time, reject some silly input, such as negative
width or height.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=672811
cve_desc: Multiple integer overflows in the read_bitmap_file_data function in io-xbm.c in gdk-pixbuf before 2.26.1 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) via a negative (1) height or (2) width in an XBM file, which triggers a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the calculation `size = bytes_per_line * hh`. Both `ww` and `hh` are parsed from user-controlled file input. A negative `value` from `sscanf` cast to `guint` produces a large unsigned width/height. The multiplication of `bytes_per_line` (int) by `hh` (guint) can overflow, resulting in a small allocation via `g_malloc` while the returned `*width` and `*height` values reflect the original large dimensions. This causes the caller to believe the buffer is much larger than it actually is, leading to heap-based buffer overflows/over-reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: The file contains a #define line setting width. sscanf parses `value` as an int. For example, value = 524281 (a valid positive int). ww is set to 524281.
The file contains a #define line setting height. For example, value = 65537. hh is set to 65537.
The static data line is matched (non-version10p). The code computes bytes_per_line = (524281+7)/8 + 0 = 65536.
size = bytes_per_line * hh = 65536 * 65537. On 32-bit int/guint, this is 4295032832 which overflows. The truncated 32-bit result is 65536 (0x10000). This small value is stored in `size`.
g_malloc(size) allocates only 65536 bytes for `bits`.
The non-version10p loop writes exactly `size` (65536) bytes into the 65536-byte buffer. No overflow within this function's write loop.

The function returns successfully, setting *data = bits (65536-byte buffer), *width = 524281, *height = 65537. The caller will compute the expected bitmap size as (524281+7)/8 * 65537 = 4295032832 bytes, but only 65536 bytes are allocated, leading to a massive heap buffer over-read or overflow when the caller accesses the data.

This continues the auth-policy fix in

commit_message:
Add a test for GENERAL_NAME_cmp

Based on a boringssl test contributed by David Benjamin

Reviewed-by: Tomas Mraz <tmraz@fedoraproject.org>
cve_desc: The X.509 GeneralName type is a generic type for representing different types of names. One of those name types is known as EDIPartyName. OpenSSL provides a function GENERAL_NAME_cmp which compares different instances of a GENERAL_NAME to see if they are equal or not. This function behaves incorrectly when both GENERAL_NAMEs contain an EDIPARTYNAME. A NULL pointer dereference and a crash may occur leading to a possible denial of service attack. OpenSSL itself uses the GENERAL_NAME_cmp function for two purposes: 1) Comparing CRL distribution point names between an available CRL and a CRL distribution point embedded in an X509 certificate 2) When verifying that a timestamp response token signer matches the timestamp authority name (exposed via the API functions TS_RESP_verify_response and TS_RESP_verify_token) If an attacker can control both items being compared then that attacker could trigger a crash. For example if the attacker can trick a client or server into checking a malicious certificate against a malicious CRL then this may occur. Note that some applications automatically download CRLs based on a URL embedded in a certificate. This checking happens prior to the signatures on the certificate and CRL being verified. OpenSSL's s_server, s_client and verify tools have support for the "-crl_download" option which implements automatic CRL downloading and this attack has been demonstrated to work against those tools. Note that an unrelated bug means that affected versions of OpenSSL cannot parse or construct correct encodings of EDIPARTYNAME. However it is possible to construct a malformed EDIPARTYNAME that OpenSSL's parser will accept and hence trigger this attack. All OpenSSL 1.1.1 and 1.0.2 versions are affected by this issue. Other OpenSSL releases are out of support and have not been checked. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1i (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1h). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2x (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2w).

commit_message:
Prevent an invalid memory writes in compileRule

Thanks to Han Zheng for reporting it

Fixes #1214
cve_desc: Liblouis 3.21.0 has an out-of-bounds write in compileRule in compileTranslationTable.c, as demonstrated by lou_trace.

vulnerabilities.description: During macro expansion, when substituting arguments into the expanded line, argument characters are copied into tmpFile.line without checking whether tmpFile.linelen exceeds MAXSTRING. While individual definition characters have a bounds check (tmpFile.linelen >= MAXSTRING), the argument substitution loop has no such check, leading to a heap or stack buffer overflow on tmpFile.line.

code_snippets:
for (int j = 0; j < arg.length; j++)
									tmpFile.line[tmpFile.linelen++] = arg.chars[j];

step_by_step_execution.description: A token is read and matched to a macro name in the inScopeMacros list.

state.value: pointer to a Macro with substitution_count > 0 and argument_count > 0

step_by_step_execution.description: A FileInfo tmpFile is created on the stack with linelen initialized to 0. tmpFile.line is a fixed-size buffer of MAXSTRING widechar elements.
Arguments are read from the file. An argument with a large length (up to the maximum CharsString length) is stored.

state.value: CharsString with length close to MAXSTRING

step_by_step_execution.description: The macro definition is iterated. Individual definition characters are copied with a MAXSTRING bounds check, so tmpFile.linelen could be just under MAXSTRING before a substitution point.
A substitution point is reached. The argument's characters are copied into tmpFile.line in a loop with no bounds check on tmpFile.linelen against MAXSTRING.

state.value: large value, e.g. several hundred characters
incremented past MAXSTRING, writing out of bounds

step_by_step_execution.description: The write tmpFile.line[tmpFile.linelen++] = arg.chars[j] writes beyond the allocated size of tmpFile.line, causing a buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: When the macro does not receive enough arguments, the function returns 0 without freeing the 'arguments' array that was allocated with malloc, resulting in a memory leak. Additionally, when the macro expansion succeeds (returns 1) or fails during compileRule recursion, 'arguments' is also never freed.

step_by_step_execution.description: A macro is matched. Memory is allocated for arguments.
The code attempts to read arguments from the file. If not enough arguments are available, getToken returns false and the loop breaks early.
The condition argument_count < m->argument_count is true. The function calls compileError and returns 0 without calling free(arguments).

commit_message: squash heap-buffer-overflow, PoC TIFF from Hardik
cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow issue was found in ImageMagick. When an application processes a malformed TIFF file, it could lead to undefined behavior or a crash causing a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of 'extent' in the ReadStripMethod path. The multiplication of 4 * ((image->depth+7)/8) * (samples_per_pixel+1) * TIFFStripSize(tiff) can overflow a size_t on 32-bit platforms, resulting in a small allocation. Subsequently, TIFFReadEncodedStrip writes up to TIFFStripSize(tiff) bytes into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: TIFF image is opened and fields are read. bits_per_sample is set (e.g., 32), samples_per_pixel is set (e.g., 32, max allowed is MaxPixelChannels=32).
The method is determined to be ReadStripMethod because TIFFGetField for TIFFTAG_ROWSPERSTRIP succeeds.

Extent is computed: 4 * ((32+7)/8) * (32+1) * TIFFStripSize(tiff) = 4 * 4 * 33 * TIFFStripSize = 528 * TIFFStripSize. On a 32-bit platform (size_t is 32-bit), if TIFFStripSize returns e.g. 0x400000 (4MB), then 528 * 4194304 = 2,214,592,512 which fits. But with TIFFStripSize ~= 0xC00000 (12MB), 528 * 12582912 = 6,643,777,536 which overflows uint32 to a small value.

state.value: ~12MB (a large strip from a crafted TIFF)

step_by_step_execution.description: strip_pixels is allocated with the overflowed small extent value via AcquireQuantumMemory.

state.value: heap buffer of overflowed small size

step_by_step_execution.description: TIFFReadEncodedStrip is called with the third argument TIFFStripSize(tiff) (~12MB), which is the maximum bytes to decode. The decoded strip data (up to 12MB) is written into strip_pixels, which was allocated with a much smaller size due to the integer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of 'extent' in the ReadTileMethod path. The multiplication of 4 * (samples_per_pixel+1) * MagickMax(rows*TIFFTileRowSize, TIFFTileSize) can overflow a size_t on 32-bit platforms. This leads to a small allocation for tile_pixels, but TIFFReadTile writes a full tile's worth of data into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: TIFF image is opened and determined to be tiled. samples_per_pixel is read from the TIFF (up to 32).

Tile dimensions are read (e.g., large tile). extent is computed: 4 * (32+1) * MagickMax(rows*TIFFTileRowSize(tiff), TIFFTileSize(tiff)) = 132 * TIFFTileSize. On 32-bit, if TIFFTileSize is large enough (~32MB), 132 * 33554432 overflows uint32.
tile_pixels is allocated with the overflowed small extent via AcquireQuantumMemory.

state.value: heap buffer of overflowed small size

step_by_step_execution.description: TIFFReadTile writes a full tile of data into tile_pixels, which exceeds the allocated buffer size.

state.value: tile data written beyond buffer bounds

commit_message:
MDEV-25787 Bug report: crash on SELECT DISTINCT thousands_blob_fields

fix a debug assert to account for not opened temp tables
cve_desc: MariaDB through 10.5.9 allows an application crash via certain long SELECT DISTINCT statements that improperly interact with storage-engine resource limitations for temporary data structures.

commit_message:
MDEV-25636: Bug report: abortion in sql/sql_parse.cc:6294

The asserion failure was caused by this query

  select /*id=1*/ from t1
  where
   col= ( select /*id=2*/ from ... where corr_cond1
          union
          select /*id=4*/ from ... where corr_cond2)

Here,
- select with id=2 was correlated due to corr_cond1.
- select with id=4 was initially correlated due to corr_cond2, but then
  the optimizer optimized away the correlation, making the select with id=4
  uncorrelated.

However, since select with id=2 remained correlated, the execution had to
re-compute the whole UNION. When it tried to execute select with id=4, it
hit an assertion  (join buffer already free'd).

This is because select with id=4 has freed its execution structures after
it has been executed once. The select is uncorrelated, so it did not expect
it would need to be executed for the second time.

Fixed this by adding this logic in
st_select_lex::optimize_unflattened_subqueries():

  If a member of a UNION is correlated, mark all its members as
  correlated, so that they are prepared to be executed multiple times.
cve_desc: MariaDB through 10.5.9 allows an application crash in sub_select_postjoin_aggr for a NULL value of aggr.

commit_message:
MDEV-28095 crash in multi-update and implicit grouping

disallow implicit grouping in multi-update.
explicit GROUP BY is not allowed by the grammar.
cve_desc: There is an Assertion failure in MariaDB Server v10.9 and below via 'node->pcur->rel_pos == BTR_PCUR_ON' at /row/row0mysql.cc.

commit_message: MDEV-24176 fixup: GCC -Wmaybe-uninitialized
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6.5 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component Item_args::walk_arg, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

commit_message:
MDEV-26402: A SEGV in Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order...

When doing condition pushdown from HAVING into WHERE,
Item_equal::create_pushable_equalities() calls
item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL) for constant items.
Then, Item::cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor() checks for this flag
to see if it should call item->cleanup() or leave the item as-is.

The failure happens when a constant item has a non-constant one inside it,
like:

   (tbl.col=0 AND impossible_cond)

item->walk(cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor) works in a bottom-up
way so it
1. will call Item_func_eq(tbl.col=0)->cleanup()
2. will not call Item_cond_and->cleanup (as the AND is constant)

This creates an item tree where a fixed Item has an un-fixed Item inside
it which eventually causes an assertion failure.

Fixed by introducing this rule: instead of just calling

  item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL);

we call Item::walk() to set the flag for all sub-items of the item.
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.7 and below was discovered to contain a segmentation fault via the component Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order.

commit_message: fix oob write bug inside libmobi
cve_desc: libmobi is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Write

vulnerabilities.description: When offset is negative, the only bounds check verifies that `buf->offset >= aoffset` (preventing source pointer underflow). However, there is no check that `buf->offset + len <= buf->maxlen` for the destination, nor that `(buf->offset - aoffset) + len <= buf->maxlen` for the source read. This allows an out-of-bounds read and write via memmove when `len` is large enough to exceed the buffer boundary.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a negative offset (e.g., offset = -4) and a large `len` (e.g., len such that buf->offset + len > buf->maxlen).
`aoffset` is computed as `(size_t) abs(offset)`, which is 4. `source` is set to `buf->data + buf->offset`.
Since offset < 0, the else branch is taken. The check `buf->offset < aoffset` evaluates to `8 < 4`, which is false, so execution continues.
`source -= aoffset` sets source to `buf->data + 4`. No further bounds check is performed on `len`.
`memmove(buf->data + 8, buf->data + 4, 100)` is called. The destination write extends to offset 108, which exceeds buf->maxlen (100), causing an out-of-bounds write.

vulnerabilities.description: If `offset` is `INT_MIN` (-2147483648 on 32-bit int), `abs(INT_MIN)` is undefined behavior in C (typically returns INT_MIN). Casting this negative value to `size_t` produces a very large value, which would bypass the intended `buf->offset < aoffset` check in the negative branch and cause an out-of-bounds source pointer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with offset = INT_MIN (-2147483648).
`abs(INT_MIN)` triggers undefined behavior. On typical implementations it returns INT_MIN. Casting to size_t produces a very large value (e.g., 2147483648 on 32-bit or 0xFFFFFFFF80000000 on 64-bit).

Since offset < 0, the else branch is taken. The check `buf->offset < aoffset` is true (buf->offset is typically much smaller), so MOBI_BUFFER_END is returned. However, on some platforms/compilers UB from abs(INT_MIN) could produce unexpected results that bypass this check.

commit_message:
flxdec: Don't unref() parent in the chain function

We don't own the reference here, it is owned by the caller and given to
us for the scope of this function. Leftover mistake from 0.10 porting.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=774897
cve_desc: The gst_decode_chain_free_internal function in the flxdex decoder in gst-plugins-good in GStreamer before 1.10.2 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (invalid memory read and crash) via an invalid file, which triggers an incorrect unref call.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of flxdec->size from attacker-controlled width and height values read from the FLX file header. If width * height overflows a guint (32-bit), flxdec->size will be a small value, causing undersized allocations for frame_data, delta_data, and the output buffer. Subsequent operations (flx_decode_chunks, memcpy, flx_colorspace_convert) will write beyond the allocated buffers.

step_by_step_execution.description: The FLX header is read from the adapter and copied into flxdec->hdr. Width and height fields are attacker-controlled.
Header type check passes (attacker sets valid magic).

flxdec->size is computed as (guint)width * (guint)height. With width=65535 and height=65535, the product is 0xFFFE0001 which fits in 32-bit, but with slightly larger or different combinations (e.g. width=0x10000 if wider type, or width=65536, height=65537) it overflows. For 16-bit fields: width=65535, height=65535 yields 4294836225 which doesn't overflow, but flxdec->size * 4 in the output allocation WILL overflow a 32-bit value.

state.value: Overflows 32-bit: 0xFFF80004 -> wraps to a smaller value if guint is 32-bit

step_by_step_execution.description: gst_buffer_new_and_alloc(flxdec->size * 4) is called with the overflowed multiplication, allocating a much smaller buffer than needed.
flx_colorspace_convert writes width*height*4 bytes into the undersized output buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow when computing chunk size. If the attacker-controlled flxfh.size is smaller than FlxFrameChunkSize, the subtraction wraps to a very large unsigned value, potentially causing a massive allocation or read from the adapter.

step_by_step_execution.description: In the PLAYING state, the frame chunk header is read. flxfh.size is attacker-controlled.
The check 'avail < flxfh.size' passes since avail can be >= 2.
FlxFrameChunkSize bytes are flushed from the adapter.
gst_adapter_take is called with flxfh.size - FlxFrameChunkSize which underflows to a huge unsigned value (e.g. 0xFFFFFFxx).
This causes undefined behavior, potentially a massive heap allocation or out-of-bounds read from the adapter's internal buffers.

state.value: Result of reading far more data than available

commit_message:
flxdec: Don't unref() parent in the chain function

We don't own the reference here, it is owned by the caller and given to
us for the scope of this function. Leftover mistake from 0.10 porting.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=774897
cve_desc: The gst_decode_chain_free_internal function in the flxdex decoder in gst-plugins-good in GStreamer before 1.10.2 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (invalid memory read and crash) via an invalid file, which triggers an incorrect unref call.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow when computing the chunk size. If flxfh.size (read from the file) is less than FlxFrameChunkSize, the subtraction wraps around to a very large value because both operands are unsigned. The check `avail < flxfh.size` does not prevent this since flxfh.size could be a small positive value (e.g. 2) which is less than avail.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters the PLAYING state branch with sufficient data in the adapter.
A frame chunk header is read from the adapter. The attacker-controlled flxfh.size field is set to a value smaller than FlxFrameChunkSize (e.g., 2), and flxfh.id is FLX_FRAME_TYPE.
The check `avail < flxfh.size` is evaluated. Since avail >= FlxFrameChunkSize > 2, the check passes and execution continues.

The adapter flushes FlxFrameChunkSize bytes, then calls gst_adapter_take with `flxfh.size - FlxFrameChunkSize`. Since flxfh.size (2) < FlxFrameChunkSize, unsigned subtraction wraps around to a very large number (e.g., 0xFFFFFFxx).
gst_adapter_take attempts to allocate and copy a huge number of bytes, leading to heap buffer over-read or crash.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in computing flxdec->size (width * height) and subsequently flxdec->size * 4. Attacker-controlled width and height from the FLX header can cause overflow, leading to small heap allocations but large writes during frame decoding and colorspace conversion.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters the READ_HEADER branch with enough data. The header is parsed with attacker-controlled width and height values.
flxdec->size is computed as width * height. With large values, this overflows a 32-bit guint, resulting in a small value.

state.value: 65535 * 65537 mod 2^32 = 4294901760 * ... (small wrapped value, e.g. with width=65536, height=65537 -> 65536*65537 overflows to 65536)

step_by_step_execution.description: g_malloc allocates frame_data and delta_data based on the overflowed small size.
During PLAYING state, gst_buffer_new_and_alloc(flxdec->size * 4) is called, which can also overflow, allocating a small buffer.
flx_decode_chunks and flx_colorspace_convert write data based on the original (large) width*height dimensions into the undersized buffers, causing heap buffer overflow.

state.value: undersized buffer being written beyond bounds

commit_message:
MDEV-25636: Bug report: abortion in sql/sql_parse.cc:6294

The asserion failure was caused by this query

  select /*id=1*/ from t1
  where
   col= ( select /*id=2*/ from ... where corr_cond1
          union
          select /*id=4*/ from ... where corr_cond2)

Here,
- select with id=2 was correlated due to corr_cond1.
- select with id=4 was initially correlated due to corr_cond2, but then
  the optimizer optimized away the correlation, making the select with id=4
  uncorrelated.

However, since select with id=2 remained correlated, the execution had to
re-compute the whole UNION. When it tried to execute select with id=4, it
hit an assertion  (join buffer already free'd).

This is because select with id=4 has freed its execution structures after
it has been executed once. The select is uncorrelated, so it did not expect

Fixed this by adding this logic in
st_select_lex::optimize_unflattened_subqueries():

commit_message:
sctp: correct bounds check in sctp_setsockopt_auth_key

The bonds check to prevent buffer overlflow was not exactly
right.  It still allowed overflow of up to 8 bytes which is
sizeof(struct sctp_authkey).

Since optlen is already checked against the size of that struct,
we are guaranteed not to cause interger overflow either.

Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the sctp_setsockopt_auth_key function in net/sctp/socket.c in the Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel 2.6.24-rc1 through 2.6.26.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted sca_keylength field associated with the SCTP_AUTH_KEY option.

commit_message:
Fix possible NULL dereference (savannah bug #55369)

* src/sparse.c (pax_decode_header): Check return from find_next_block.
cve_desc: pax_decode_header in sparse.c in GNU Tar before 1.32 had a NULL pointer dereference when parsing certain archives that have malformed extended headers.

vulnerabilities.description: The return value of find_next_block() is used without a NULL check, both in the main function body and inside the COPY_BUF macro. If find_next_block() returns NULL (e.g., due to a truncated or malformed archive), the subsequent dereference of the pointer (accessing ->buffer) leads to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a tar_sparse_file where sparse_major > 0, entering the main code block.
set_next_block_after(current_header) is called, advancing past the current header block.
find_next_block() is called and its return value is stored in blk. If the archive is truncated or corrupted, find_next_block() may return NULL.
blk->buffer is dereferenced to assign to p. Since blk is NULL, this is a NULL pointer dereference.

Similarly, inside the COPY_BUF macro, when src reaches endp and a new block is needed, find_next_block() is called again and its result b is dereferenced without a NULL check, leading to the same issue at a different point in execution.

commit_message: libclamav/mspack.c: fix Quantum decompressor (bb#1771)
cve_desc: The qtm_decompress function in libclamav/mspack.c in ClamAV before 0.96 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption and application crash) via a crafted CAB archive that uses the Quantum (aka .Q) compression format.  NOTE: some of these details are obtained from third party information.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the match offset wrap-around check. When `match_offset` is much larger than `window_posn`, the unsigned subtraction `match_offset - window_posn` can produce a value exceeding INT_MAX. When stored in the signed `int j`, it becomes negative. The guard `j > (int) qtm->window_size` then fails (negative < positive), and the subsequent `window[qtm->window_size - j]` computes `window_size - (negative)` = `window_size + |j|`, a very large index, causing an out-of-bounds memory read and potential write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function restores local state including window_posn. Assume window_posn is 0 (e.g., at the start or after a window wrap).
A match selector (4, 5, or 6) is decoded. Through crafted input, sym indexes position_base to yield a large match_offset, e.g., 0x90000000.
The check `window_posn + match_length > qtm->window_size` passes since window_posn is 0 and match_length is small.

Since match_offset (0x90000000) > window_posn (0), the wrap-around branch is taken. j = match_offset - window_posn is computed as unsigned 0x90000000, then stored in signed int j, wrapping to a negative value.
The guard `j > (int) qtm->window_size` is checked. Since j is negative and window_size cast to int is positive, this comparison is false, so the check is bypassed.
runsrc is set to &window[qtm->window_size - j]. Since j is negative, this computes window_size + 1879048192, a huge index far beyond the window buffer, resulting in an out-of-bounds pointer.

The copy loop `while (i-- > 0) *rundest++ = *runsrc++;` reads from the out-of-bounds runsrc and writes into the window at rundest, causing out-of-bounds reads and potentially exploitable memory corruption.

When doing condition pushdown from HAVING into WHERE,

Then, Item::cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor() checks for this flag
to see if it should call item->cleanup() or leave the item as-is.

The failure happens when a constant item has a non-constant one inside it,
like:

   (tbl.col=0 AND impossible_cond)

item->walk(cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor) works in a bottom-up
way so it
1. will call Item_func_eq(tbl.col=0)->cleanup()
2. will not call Item_cond_and->cleanup (as the AND is constant)

This creates an item tree where a fixed Item has an un-fixed Item inside
it which eventually causes an assertion failure.

Fixed by introducing this rule: instead of just calling

  item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL);

we call Item::walk() to set the flag for all sub-items of the item.
